topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is CMOS Logic?

**CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) Logic** is a digital circuit design methodology that uses complementary pairs of **n-channel MOSFETs (NMOS)** and **p-channel MOSFETs (PMOS)** transistors to implement Boolean functions. For every input combination, exactly one of the two networks is conducting while the other is OFF, leading to a near-zero static power dissipation. This is the dominant digital design technology of the modern era.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CMOS logic refers to static CMOS gate families where a pull-up network (PUN) built from PMOS transistors and a pull-down network (PDN) built from NMOS transistors work in a complementary fashion to realize Boolean functions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Think of CMOS logic as a **two-person seesaw with opposing water taps**:

- Imagine two water taps on a pipe: one controlled by a **Green Button (PMOS)** and the other by a **Red Button (NMOS)**.
- When you press the **Green Button** (logic input = 0 for PMOS), water flows IN (pull-up to V_DD).
- When you press the **Red Button** (logic input = 1 for NMOS), water flows OUT (pull-down to GND).
- The brilliance is that **only one tap is open at a time**, so very little water is wasted.
- In electrical terms: the PMOS is ON when input is LOW, and NMOS is ON when input is HIGH. They **never conduct simultaneously** under steady-state conditions, eliminating static current flow.

> [!NOTE]
> This complementary behavior is the **fingerprint of CMOS** and is the primary reason CMOS is preferred over NMOS-only (ratioed) logic for low-power VLSI design.

## 1.3 Why CMOS? — Standard Metrics

| Metric | CMOS Performance | Physical Constant/Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Power Dissipation** | ~0 (ideal) | **< 1 μW per gate** |
| **Noise Margin** | High (≈ 40% of V_DD) | **V_DD = 1.8 V / 3.3 V / 5 V** |
| **Logic Swing** | Full rail-to-rail | **0 V to V_DD** |
| **Input Impedance** | Infinite (gate) | **> 10¹² Ω** |
| **Technology Node** | Modern standard | **180 nm to 3 nm** |

## 1.4 The CMOS Inverter — The Heart of All CMOS Logic

The **CMOS Inverter** is the most fundamental CMOS logic gate. It is the building block for all other complex gates.

**Transistor-level Structure:**
- One **PMOS transistor** connected between **V_DD** and the output node (PUN).
- One **NMOS transistor** connected between the output node and **GND** (PDN).
- The gates of both transistors are tied together to form the **Input (V_in)**.
- The drains of both transistors are tied together to form the **Output (V_out)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule for CMOS Inverter:**
> - When V_in = **V_DD** (logic 1) → NMOS is **ON**, PMOS is **OFF** → V_out = **0 V** (logic 0).
> - When V_in = **0 V** (logic 0) → NMOS is **OFF**, PMOS is **ON** → V_out = **V_DD** (logic 1).

## 1.5 Static CMOS Logic Family — General Structure

Every static CMOS logic gate (NAND, NOR, AND, OR, XOR, etc.) follows the **PUN/PDN complementary principle**:

- **PDN (Pull-Down Network):** Built using only **NMOS** transistors. It is ON when the Boolean function evaluates to **0**, providing a path from Output → GND.
- **PUN (Pull-Up Network):** Built using only **PMOS** transistors. It is ON when the Boolean function evaluates to **1**, providing a path from Output → V_DD.
- **Duality Property:** The PUN and PDN are **dual networks**. If the PDN has parallel branches of series transistors, the PUN has series branches of parallel transistors, and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Inverter Operation — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Identify the Operating Regions
For the CMOS inverter, at any input voltage V_in, the operating regions of the transistors change as V_in sweeps from 0 to V_DD.

**Phase 1: 0 ≤ V_in ≤ V_Tn** (NMOS in Cutoff, PMOS in Triode/Linear)
- V_GS_n = V_in < V_Tn ⇒ NMOS is **OFF**.
- V_SG_p = V_DD − V_in > |V_Tp| ⇒ PMOS is **ON** (in triode region).
- Output is being charged to V_DD through the ON PMOS. **V_out = V_DD** (logic 1).

**Phase 2: V_in = V_M (Switching Threshold)**
- Both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation** momentarily.
- This is the critical point where V_out = V_in.
- KTU High-Yield Concept: **V_M is the point of unity gain** in the VTC curve.

**Phase 3: V_Tn ≤ V_in ≤ V_DD − |V_Tp|** (Both Transistors in Saturation)
- NMOS is in saturation (since V_DS_n = V_out > V_GS_n − V_Tn).
- PMOS is in saturation (since V_SD_p = V_DD − V_out < V_SG_p − |V_Tp|).
- Both devices are current-matched, providing the **high-gain transition region**.

**Phase 4: V_DD − |V_Tp| ≤ V_in ≤ V_DD** (PMOS in Cutoff, NMOS in Triode)
- V_SG_p = V_DD − V_in < |V_Tp| ⇒ PMOS is **OFF**.
- V_GS_n = V_in > V_Tn ⇒ NMOS is **ON** (in triode region).
- Output is being discharged to GND. **V_out = 0 V** (logic 0).

### Step 2: Understand the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)
The VTC is a plot of **V_out vs V_in**. The ideal VTC should be a step function at V_in = V_DD/2. Real VTCs have a smooth transition due to MOSFET physics.

### Step 3: Determine Noise Margins
Noise margins quantify the **immunity of a gate to noise** at its input.

- **V_OH (Output High Voltage)** = Nominal high output = V_DD
- **V_OL (Output Low Voltage)** = Nominal low output = 0 V
- **V_IH (Input High Voltage)** = Minimum V_in recognized as logic 1
- **V_IL (Input Low Voltage)** = Maximum V_in recognized as logic 0
- **NM_H = V_OH − V_IH** (High Noise Margin)
- **NM_L = V_IL − V_OL** (Low Noise Margin)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **symmetric inverter** (matched drive strengths), V_IH = V_DD/2 + Δ and V_IL = V_DD/2 − Δ. KTU examiners love asking about this!

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_n = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |
| 2 | $k_p = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |
| 3 | $k_r = \frac{k_n}{k_p}$ | Transconductance ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Switching threshold of CMOS inverter | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $V_{IH} = \frac{V_{Tn} + r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert + V_{TP\_{sat}})}{1 + r}$ | Input high voltage (complex form) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $V_{IL} = \frac{V_{Tn} - V_{Tn} \cdot \sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Input low voltage (approx) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma (\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Body effect on threshold voltage | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic (switching) power dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 11 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static (leakage) power dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 12 | $P_{short} = I_{short} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Short-circuit power during switching | Watts (W) |
| 13 | $t_p = \frac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{2 k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | First-order propagation delay | seconds (s) |
| 14 | $f_{max} \approx \frac{1}{t_p}$ | Maximum operating frequency | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Body-effect coefficient γ**: 0.4 to 1.0 V^(1/2)
> - **Fermi potential φ_F**: 0.3 to 0.4 V
> - **Mobility ratio μ_n/μ_p**: ~2 to 3 (NMOS is faster than PMOS)

## 2.3 Engineering Applications of CMOS Logic

- **Microprocessors & CPUs** — Billions of CMOS gates integrated on a single die.
- **SRAM and DRAM Memory Cells** — 6T-SRAM cell uses CMOS cross-coupled inverters.
- **ASIC and FPGA Design** — CMOS is the standard technology for all custom digital ICs.
- **Watch and IoT Devices** — Ultra-low static power makes CMOS ideal for battery-powered systems.
- **RF and Mixed-Signal ICs** — CMOS LNAs, mixers, and ADCs are widely used in transceivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Switching Threshold Voltage (V_M) of CMOS Inverter

The **switching threshold V_M** is defined as the point on the VTC where **V_in = V_out**. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation** (when V_M lies in the middle of the transition region).

### Step 1: Write the Drain Currents in Saturation

For NMOS in saturation:
$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2} (V_{GSn} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

For PMOS in saturation (using source-at-V_DD convention):
$$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{SGp} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

### Step 2: Apply the CMOS Current-Equality Principle

At the switching threshold, the current through NMOS must equal the current through PMOS (KCL at the output node, ignoring capacitive currents during the brief switching moment):
$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

### Step 3: Simplify and Take the Square Root

Cancel the 1/2 factor on both sides:
$$k_n (V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Take the square root of both sides (positive root for V_M in the middle of V_DD):
$$\sqrt{k_n} (V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

### Step 4: Solve for V_M

Let $k_r = k_n / k_p$. Divide both sides by $\sqrt{k_p}$:
$$\sqrt{k_r} (V_M - V_{Tn}) = (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

Expand the left side:
$$\sqrt{k_r} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{k_r} \cdot V_{Tn} = V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

Collect V_M terms on the left:
$$\sqrt{k_r} \cdot V_M + V_M = V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert + \sqrt{k_r} \cdot V_{Tn}$$

Factor V_M:
$$V_M (1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_r} \cdot \vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

Rearrange the right-hand side:
$$V_M (1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Final V_M Expression:**
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** For a **symmetric inverter** (V_M = V_DD/2), set $k_r = 1$ by sizing $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$ to compensate for the mobility mismatch between NMOS and PMOS.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Dissipation in CMOS

### Dynamic Power (Switching Power)
When the output node of a CMOS gate transitions from 0 → 1 → 0, the load capacitance $C_L$ is charged and discharged. Energy drawn from V_DD per cycle = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$. Energy stored on capacitor = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 / 2$. Energy dissipated in PMOS = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 / 2$. Energy dissipated in NMOS = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 / 2$ (on discharge).

Total switching energy per clock cycle (one full transition):
$$E_{switch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

Power dissipation at switching frequency $f$ and switching activity factor $\alpha$:
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

### Static (Leakage) Power
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

### Short-Circuit Power
During the brief transition when both NMOS and PMOS are ON, a direct current flows from V_DD to GND:
$$P_{short} = I_{avg} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot (t_{rise} + t_{fall}) \cdot f$$

**Total CMOS Power Dissipation:**
$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Body Effect on Threshold Voltage

When the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$ is non-zero (source at a different potential than the body), the threshold voltage is **modulated**:

$$V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

Where:
- $V_{T0}$ = Zero-bias threshold voltage (when $V_{SB} = 0$)
- $\gamma$ = Body-effect coefficient ($\sqrt{2q\epsilon_{Si} N_A / C_{ox}}$)
- $\phi_F$ = Fermi potential of the substrate
- $V_{SB}$ = Source-to-body potential (must be ≥ 0 for NMOS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The body effect makes the PMOS in a transmission gate (where source is at varying voltage) exhibit a **variable threshold voltage** — a critical issue addressed in design.

## 3.4 Propagation Delay Derivation (First-Order)

The propagation delay $t_{pLH}$ (output going from LOW to HIGH) is the time for the PMOS to charge the load capacitor to 50% of V_DD:

For a simple RC charging model with on-resistance $R_p$:
$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} (1 - e^{-t/R_p C_L})$$

Setting V_out = V_DD/2 and solving:
$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{pLH}/R_p C_L}$$
$$e^{-t_{pLH}/R_p C_L} = 0.5$$
$$t_{pLH} = R_p C_L \ln 2 \approx 0.69 R_p C_L$$

For an NMOS in triode, $R_n = 1/[k_n(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})]$, giving:
$$t_p = \frac{0.69 C_L}{2 k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Transistor-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    VDD[VDD Supply]
    GND[Ground]
    VIN[Input Vin]
    VOUT[Output Vout]
    PMOS[PMOS Transistor M1]
    NMOS[NMOS Transistor M2]

    VDD -->|Source| PMOS
    PMOS -->|Drain| VOUT
    VIN -->|Gate| PMOS
    VIN -->|Gate| NMOS
    VOUT -->|Drain| NMOS
    NMOS -->|Source| GND
```

## 4.2 CMOS NAND Gate — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    VDD[VDD]
    GND[GND]
    A[Input A]
    B[Input B]
    OUT[Output Y = NOT AB]
    P1[PMOS M1]
    P2[PMOS M2]
    N1[NMOS M3]
    N2[NMOS M4]

    VDD -->|Source| P1
    P1 -->|Drain = Source| P2
    P2 -->|Drain| OUT
    A -->|Gate| P1
    B -->|Gate| P2
    A -->|Gate| N1
    B -->|Gate| N2
    OUT -->|Drain| N1
    N1 -->|Source| N2
    N2 -->|Source| GND
```

## 4.3 CMOS Logic Family Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TB
    START[Design a Boolean Function F]
    Q1{Static or Dynamic?}
    STATIC[Static CMOS]
    DYNAMIC[Dynamic CMOS]
    Q2{PDN Connection?}
    PUN[PUN with PMOS]
    PDN[PDN with NMOS]
    Q3{Use Duality Rule}
    RESULT[Realize PUN Dual of PDN]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Static| STATIC
    Q1 -->|Dynamic| DYNAMIC
    STATIC --> Q2
    Q2 --> PUN
    Q2 --> PDN
    PUN --> Q3
    PDN --> Q3
    Q3 --> RESULT
```

## 4.4 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) Block Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[V_in Rising from 0 to V_DD] --> B{V_in Region}
    B -->|Region 1: 0 to V_Tn| C[NMOS OFF, PMOS Triode - V_out = V_DD]
    B -->|Region 2: V_Tn to V_M| D[NMOS Sat, PMOS Sat - V_out Transitions]
    B -->|Region 3: V_M to V_DD - V_Tp| E[NMOS Sat, PMOS Sat - Sharp Transition]
    B -->|Region 4: V_DD - V_Tp to V_DD| F[NMOS Triode, PMOS OFF - V_out = 0]
    C --> G[Logic 1 Output]
    F --> H[Logic 0 Output]
```

## 4.5 Transmission Gate (TG) Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    IN[Input]
    OUT[Output]
    CTRL[Control Signal C]
    INV_CTRL[Inverted Control Cbar]
    N_MOS[NMOS M1]
    P_MOS[PMOS M2]

    IN --> N_MOS
    N_MOS --> OUT
    IN --> P_MOS
    P_MOS --> OUT
    CTRL -->|Gate| N_MOS
    INV_CTRL -->|Gate| P_MOS
```
