# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term "switching threshold" (V_M) of a CMOS inverter. Why is it important to design V_M = V_DD/2?

**Model Answer:**

The **switching threshold V_M** of a CMOS inverter is defined as the value of input voltage V_in at which the output voltage V_out equals V_in. Geometrically, it is the point on the VTC where the curve intersects the V_in = V_out line. Mathematically:
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$$

**Importance of V_M = V_DD/2:**
1. **Maximizes Noise Margins:** When V_M = V_DD/2, both NM_H and NM_L are equal and maximized, providing the best noise immunity.
2. **Symmetric Switching:** The gate has equal rise and fall delays (t_pLH = t_pHL).
3. **Improved Performance:** Lowest propagation delay and balanced drive strength.
4. **Achieved by Sizing:** Setting $(W/L)_p = 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$ (since μ_n ≈ 2.5 × μ_p).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** List the three components of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Which one dominates at high frequencies?

**Model Answer:**

The three components of CMOS power dissipation are:

1. **Dynamic (Switching) Power** — $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$. Caused by charging and discharging the load capacitance.
2. **Static (Leakage) Power** — $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$. Caused by sub-threshold and reverse-biased junction leakage currents.
3. **Short-Circuit Power** — $P_{short} = I_{short} \cdot V_{DD}$. Caused by direct current from V_DD to GND during the brief switching interval when both NMOS and PMOS are simultaneously ON.

> **At high frequencies, Dynamic Power dominates** because it scales linearly with switching frequency $f$, while static and short-circuit power remain relatively constant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate. Derive the condition for a symmetric switching threshold.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit Diagram and Operation

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**
A 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of:
- **PUN:** Two PMOS transistors (M1, M2) connected in **parallel** between V_DD and the output node.
- **PDN:** Two NMOS transistors (M3, M4) connected in **series** between the output node and GND.
- Inputs: A and B, connected to gates of corresponding PMOS and NMOS transistors.

**Operation Truth Table:**

| A | B | M1 (PMOS) | M2 (PMOS) | M3 (NMOS) | M4 (NMOS) | Output Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | V_DD (1) |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | V_DD (1) |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | V_DD (1) |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | GND (0) |

**Operation Explanation:**
- When **any input is LOW (0)**, the corresponding PMOS is ON, providing a path from V_DD to the output. The output is pulled HIGH to V_DD.
- When **both inputs are HIGH (1)**, both NMOS transistors are ON, forming a series path from output to GND. The output is pulled LOW.
- The output Y = $\overline{A \cdot B}$ (NAND function).

**Pull-Up Network (PUN) Analysis:**
The PUN is the **dual** of the PDN. Since the PDN has series NMOS, the PUN has parallel PMOS. This duality guarantees that **for any input combination, exactly one network is conducting**, eliminating static current.

**Pull-Down Network (PDN) Analysis:**
- Series NMOS means the PDN is ON only when **both A AND B are HIGH**.
- The output is pulled LOW only when A = 1 AND B = 1.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Symmetric Switching Threshold Derivation

For a NAND gate, the effective pull-down strength is reduced by a factor of 2 (two NMOS in series), while the effective pull-up strength is increased (two PMOS in parallel). The symmetric switching threshold requires:

For **symmetric V_M = V_DD/2**, the effective drive strengths must be equal:
$$k_{n,eff} = k_{p,eff}$$

For NAND gate:
- Effective NMOS width: $W_n / (2L_n)$ (since two NMOS in series, each transistor is twice as long effectively)
- Effective PMOS width: $2 W_p / L_p$ (two PMOS in parallel)

Equating:
$$\frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W_n}{2L_n} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{2 W_p}{L_p}$$

$$\frac{\mu_n W_n}{2 L_n} = \frac{2 \mu_p W_p}{L_p}$$

$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n L_p}{4 \mu_p L_n}$$

Assuming $L_p = L_n$ and $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$:
$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625$$

This means PMOS devices can be **smaller** in a NAND gate compared to an inverter to achieve symmetric switching.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget that series transistors in the PDN double the effective channel length, reducing drive strength by 2×.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Many students incorrectly assume all gates have the same W/L ratio as the inverter. This is WRONG — gate topology matters!
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Do not confuse the **W/L ratio for symmetric V_M** with the **W/L ratio for equal noise margins**. They are related but not identical.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Explain the sources of power dissipation in CMOS logic circuits. A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of 50 fF at 100 MHz with V_DD = 3.3 V. The switching activity factor is 0.2. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Sources of Power Dissipation

**Model Solution:**

There are **three main sources** of power dissipation in CMOS logic circuits:

**1. Dynamic (Switching) Power Dissipation:**
This is the dominant component and arises from charging and discharging the load and parasitic capacitances. When the output switches from 0 to 1, the load capacitor is charged from V_DD, drawing energy $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ from the supply. Half of this is stored on the capacitor, and the other half is dissipated in the PMOS. When the output switches from 1 to 0, the stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS.

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

Where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor (probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle).

**2. Static (Leakage) Power Dissipation:**
This arises from leakage currents even when the transistor is nominally OFF. Sources include:
- **Sub-threshold leakage:** Current flowing between source and drain when V_GS < V_T.
- **Reverse-biased junction leakage:** Current through reverse-biased p-n junctions.
- **Gate-oxide tunneling:** At very thin oxide layers (< 2 nm), electrons tunnel through the gate oxide.

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

**3. Short-Circuit Power Dissipation:**
During the brief transition of the input (typically 10-20% of the total time), both NMOS and PMOS transistors are simultaneously ON, creating a direct current path from V_DD to GND. This is non-zero only during switching.

$$P_{short} = I_{short} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Total Power Dissipation:**
$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short}$$

**Mitigation Techniques:**
- Reduce V_DD (most effective — quadratic reduction in P_dyn)
- Reduce C_L (smaller transistors, shorter wires)
- Reduce switching activity (clock gating, sleep transistors)
- Use low-leakage transistors (high-V_T, multi-V_T designs)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Calculation

**Given:**
- Load capacitance: $C_L = 50 \text{ fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- Switching frequency: $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$
- Switching activity factor: $\alpha = 0.2$

**Formula:**
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Substituting values:**
$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (3.3)^2 \times (100 \times 10^6)$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

First, compute $V_{DD}^2$:
$$(3.3)^2 = 10.89 \text{ V}^2$$

Next, multiply $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$:
$$50 \times 10^{-15} \times 10.89 = 544.5 \times 10^{-15} = 5.445 \times 10^{-13} \text{ (C·V)}$$

Multiply by frequency:
$$5.445 \times 10^{-13} \times 100 \times 10^6 = 5.445 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^8 = 5.445 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J/s}$$

Multiply by activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$:
$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 5.445 \times 10^{-5} = 1.089 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$P_{dyn} = 10.89 \text{ μW} \approx 10.9 \text{ μW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert **fF to F** ($1 \text{ fF} = 10^{-15}$ F) — this leads to an answer 15 orders of magnitude wrong.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **switching activity factor** α. Without α, the answer is 5× too large.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Including the factor of 0.5 (energy stored vs. dissipated) — the formula $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ already accounts for this through α.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Unit mismatch between MHz and Hz. Always convert to base SI units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for CMOS Logic:**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **CMOS Inverter** = 1 PMOS (PUN) + 1 NMOS (PDN); complementary operation.
- [x] **Static Logic Principle:** PUN (PMOS) and PDN (NMOS) are duals; exactly one is ON for any input.
- [x] **PUN ON when output = 1; PDN ON when output = 0.**
- [x] **CMOS NAND:** PMOS in parallel, NMOS in series.
- [x] **CMOS NOR:** PMOS in series, NMOS in parallel.

### Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] **V_M formula** (switching threshold): $V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$
- [x] **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_IH$ and $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$
- [x] **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$
- [x] **Propagation Delay:** $t_p \approx 0.69 C_L / (2 k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}))$
- [x] **Body Effect:** $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$

### VTC Operating Regions (5 Must-Know Points)
1. **Region 1 (0 to V_Tn):** NMOS OFF, PMOS Triode → V_out = V_DD
2. **Region 2 (V_Tn to V_M):** Both in Saturation → Sharp transition begins
3. **Region 3 (V_M to V_DD − |V_Tp|):** Both in Saturation → Maximum gain
4. **Region 4 (V_DD − |V_Tp| to V_DD):** PMOS OFF, NMOS Triode → V_out = 0
5. **V_M Point:** Unity-gain, V_in = V_out, ideal for symmetry

### Design Sizing Rules
- [x] **Symmetric Inverter:** $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$ to compensate for mobility.
- [x] **NAND Symmetric:** $(W/L)_p \approx 0.625 \times (W/L)_n$ (smaller PMOS allowed).
- [x] **NOR Symmetric:** $(W/L)_p \approx 5 \times (W/L)_n$ (larger PMOS needed).

### Power-Delay Trade-off
- [x] **Dynamic power scales as V_DD²** → reducing V_DD is the most effective power-saving technique.
- [x] **Delay scales as V_DD** → reducing V_DD hurts speed.
- [x] **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is the key Figure of Merit (FoM) for optimization.

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- [x] Derive V_M for CMOS inverter (10 marks) — appears almost every exam.
- [x] Draw and explain CMOS NAND/NOR — 7 marks, every semester.
- [x] Numerical on dynamic power dissipation — 4 to 7 marks.
- [x] Explain noise margins with VTC diagram — 7 marks.
- [x] Body effect and its impact on threshold — 5 marks, frequent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** Always draw the VTC plot with all five labeled regions and the V_M point. KTU examiners award **2-3 bonus marks** for clear, well-labeled diagrams even when the answer is partially correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Fabrication and Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Fabrication and Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) Fabrication** is the sequential physical and chemical process used to manufacture integrated circuits containing both **nMOS** and **pMOS** transistors on a single silicon substrate. The process involves creating regions of selective doping (n-type and p-type), growing thin insulating layers (gate oxide), depositing conductive layers (polysilicon, metal), and patterning them using **photolithography**.

**Layout** is the geometric representation of a circuit, drawn to scale, showing the exact placement and interconnections of every transistor, diffusion region, contact, and metal wire on the silicon die. The layout is a direct physical blueprint used to generate photolithographic **masks** for fabrication.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 emphasis is on understanding the *n-well CMOS process* step-by-step, the *lambda-based design rules* (commonly $\lambda = 0.25\mu m$ in modern sub-100nm nodes), and the conversion of schematic circuits into **stick diagrams** and finally into a complete **mask layout**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of CMOS fabrication like **building a multi-story house using stenciled cutouts on a vast concrete slab**:

- The **silicon wafer** is the empty plot of land (the foundation).
- **Oxidation** is pouring a thin protective concrete layer.
- **Photolithography** is using a stencil (the **mask**) and spraying paint (dopants) through it — only the cut-out pattern gets colored.
- Repeating this 10–15 times with different "paint colors" (n-type, p-type, metal, polysilicon) builds the transistors and wires.
- **Layout** is the architect's master floor plan that tells the workers exactly where to put each stencil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Truth of CMOS**: A CMOS inverter is the basic cell — it contains **one pMOS** (built in an n-well) and **one nMOS** (built directly in the p-substrate). Every more complex CMOS gate (NAND, NOR, flip-flop) is built by extending this same pattern, with pMOS devices in the n-well and nMOS devices in the substrate.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Material Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Silicon Atomic Density | **$5 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$** | Base doping reference |
| Intrinsic Carrier Concentration $n_i$ | **$1.45 \times 10^{10} / \text{cm}^3$** (at 300 K) | Determines doping thresholds |
| Gate Oxide ($SiO_2$) Thickness $t_{ox}$ | **2 nm – 10 nm** | Scales with technology node |
| Substrate Resistivity | **10–20 $\Omega$-cm** | For lightly doped p-substrate |
| Sheet Resistance of $n^+/p^+$ diffusion | **50–100 $\Omega/\square$** | For local interconnect |
| Sheet Resistance of Polysilicon | **20–40 $\Omega/\square$** | For gate material |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 CMOS Fabrication Process Technologies

There are **four major CMOS fabrication approaches**, each differing in how the well (tub) containing the pMOS or nMOS devices is created.

#### 2.1.1 The Four Process Variants

- **n-well Process (Most Common in Industry)**
  * Uses a **p-type silicon substrate** as the base.
  * The pMOS transistors are fabricated inside a **diffused n-well**.
  * The nMOS transistors are built directly in the p-substrate.
  * Why preferred? Because **electrons (nMOS carriers) have higher mobility** than holes, so it is better to keep nMOS in the uniform, high-quality bulk substrate and "build" the well only for the pMOS.

- **p-well Process (Older Technology)**
  * Uses an **n-type substrate**.
  * pMOS is in the bulk n-substrate; nMOS sits inside a **p-well**.
  * Disadvantage: poorer nMOS performance since the p-well is a diffused region with higher defect density.

- **Twin-Tub (Twin-Well) Process**
  * Uses a **lightly doped p- or n-substrate** ($n^-$ or $p^-$).
  * **Both n-well and p-well** are independently optimized by ion implantation.
  * Allows independent threshold voltage control for nMOS and pMOS.
  * Used in high-performance sub-micron processes.

- **Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI) Process**
  * Devices are built on a thin silicon film separated from the bulk by a **buried oxide (BOX)** layer.
  * Eliminates latch-up; reduces parasitic capacitance; ideal for low-power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** Out of these four, the **n-well process** is the most frequently asked. Memorize its cross-section, mask sequence, and which layer defines which feature.

### 2.2 The Eight Universal Photolithographic Steps

Every fabrication layer (oxide, poly, metal, contacts) is patterned using the same fundamental sequence. The KTU examiner **loves** asking these as direct short-answer questions.

1. **Oxidation** — Grow a thin layer of $SiO_2$ on the wafer (thermal or CVD).
2. **Photoresist (PR) Coating** — Spin-coat a light-sensitive polymer on the oxide.
3. **Soft Bake** — Drive off solvent to harden the photoresist.
4. **Mask Alignment & Exposure** — Place a **photomask** (a glass plate with chrome patterns) over the wafer and expose to **UV light** through the mask.
5. **Development** — Dissolve the exposed (positive PR) or unexposed (negative PR) regions using a chemical developer.
6. **Hard Bake** — Strengthen the remaining PR pattern.
7. **Etching** — Remove the underlying oxide or material from the *unprotected* regions (wet chemical or dry plasma).
8. **PR Strip & Clean** — Strip the remaining photoresist using solvents or plasma ashing, leaving the patterned layer.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sheet Resistance | $R_{\square} = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | Units: $\Omega/\square$ |
| Diffusion Sheet Resistance | $R_{diff} \approx 30{-}100\ \Omega/\square$ | For $n^+, p^+$ source/drain |
| Poly Sheet Resistance | $R_{poly} \approx 20{-}40\ \Omega/\square$ | For gate material |
| Lambda Design Rule Base | All dimensions expressed in $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{min}}{2}$ |
| Minimum Channel Length | $L = 2\lambda$ | For a $0.5\mu m$ process, $\lambda = 0.25\mu m$ |
| Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | $C_{ox}$ per unit area |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{th} = \phi_{ms} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_s q N_A 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect included; $N_A$ = doping |
| Intrinsic Fermi Potential | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | At 300 K, $\dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV |
| Inverter pMOS-to-nMOS Ratio | $\dfrac{W_p}{W_n} \approx 2$ to $3$ | To match rise/fall delays |
| Inverter Mask Count | **10 to 12 masks** for full CMOS | n-well process baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $\lambda$ scaling**: The lambda ($\lambda$) parameter is a *unitless scaling constant*. For a process with minimum drawn channel length $L = 2\lambda$, the actual physical dimension depends on the technology node (e.g., for a $0.18\mu m$ process, $\lambda = 0.09\mu m$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Why design rules matter in industry:** Foundries like TSMC publish **DRC (Design Rule Check)** decks in EDA tools (Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys IC Compiler) that enforce geometric and electrical rules. A violation means the chip cannot be manufactured with acceptable yield.
- **Why stick diagrams matter:** Before doing full mask layout (which is time-consuming), designers use stick diagrams as a **quick paper-and-pencil** tool to plan routing, check transistor count, and verify area efficiency.
- **Why lambda rules:** They are *scalable* — a layout designed in lambda rules can be migrated from one process node to another simply by changing $\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed n-Well CMOS Process Flow (14-Mask Standard)

Below is the **complete sequence** of the industry-standard n-well CMOS process, which is a guaranteed **14-mark KTU exam question**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy for answering:** Memorize the sequence, then for each step, write (i) *what is done*, (ii) *which mask is used*, and (iii) *what the cross-section looks like after the step*.

**Step 1 — Substrate Selection:**
Select a **p-type silicon wafer** with resistivity 10–20 $\Omega$-cm. Mark this in cross-section as the bottommost light-gray slab.

**Step 2 — Oxidation (Pad Oxide):**
Grow a thin pad oxide ($SiO_2$, ~ 50 nm) by **thermal oxidation** at 900–1100°C. This protects the silicon surface during the subsequent implant.

**Step 3 — n-Well Mask (Mask 1) and n-Well Formation:**
- Deposit **silicon nitride ($Si_3N_4$)** over the pad oxide.
- Pattern with the **n-well mask** (Mask #1). Etch nitride where the n-well should form.
- Perform a **phosphorus ion implant** ($N_D \approx 10^{16}$ /cm³) in the exposed regions.
- Drive in the phosphorus at high temperature (~1100°C) for several hours to form the **n-well** with depth $x_j \approx 1{-}3\ \mu m$.
- Strip the nitride and pad oxide.

$$
x_j \approx 2\sqrt{D \cdot t}
$$

where $D$ is the diffusivity of phosphorus in silicon at the drive-in temperature and $t$ is the diffusion time.

**Step 4 — Active Area (Thinox) Definition (Mask 2):**
- Grow a thin **sacrificial oxide** (~ 25 nm).
- Deposit a fresh **nitride layer** (~ 100 nm).
- Pattern with the **active area mask** (Mask #2). The active area defines the regions that will become **transistors** (sources, drains, channels) — i.e., the "thinox" areas.
- The regions *outside* the active area will become **field oxide (FOX)** — the thick isolation.

**Step 5 — Field Oxidation (LOCOS):**
- Use **Local Oxidation of Silicon (LOCOS)** technique: place the wafer in a steam ambient at ~1000°C for several hours.
- The nitride prevents oxidation; exposed areas grow a thick **field oxide ($SiO_2$, ~ 500–1000 nm)**.
- This FOX isolates adjacent transistors electrically.
- Strip the nitride. The active-area regions are now ready, separated by FOX.

**Step 6 — P-Channel Stop Implant (Mask 3):**
- Pattern the field with a **p-channel stop mask** (Mask #3).
- Perform a **boron implant** in the n-well field regions. This increases the p-type doping under the FOX *between* pMOS transistors to prevent **surface inversion** (which would create a parasitic n-channel between two n-wells).

**Step 7 — Threshold-Adjust Implant:**
- For the **nMOS** regions: a shallow **boron implant** to raise $V_{thn}$ to the target value (e.g., 0.5 V).
- For the **pMOS** regions: a shallow **phosphorus implant** to set $V_{thp}$ to target (e.g., -0.5 V).

**Step 8 — Gate Oxide Growth:**
- Strip the sacrificial oxide.
- Grow the **high-quality gate oxide** ($t_{ox} \approx 5{-}10$ nm for sub-micron process) by dry thermal oxidation.
- This is the most critical step — it determines transistor performance.

**Step 9 — Polysilicon Deposition and Doping:**
- Deposit a layer of **polycrystalline silicon (poly)** using **LPCVD** to a thickness of ~ 200–400 nm.
- Dope the poly heavily with **$n^+$ (phosphorus)** to make it conductive. The sheet resistance of $n^+$ poly is ~ 20–40 $\Omega/\square$.

**Step 10 — Poly (Gate) Mask (Mask 4):**
- Pattern the polysilicon layer with the **poly mask** (Mask #4).
- This defines the **gates of nMOS and pMOS transistors simultaneously** — a self-aligned process.

**Step 11 — n+ Source/Drain Implant (Mask 5):**
- Pattern the wafer with the **$n^+$ source/drain mask** (Mask #5), exposing the nMOS active areas and the poly regions in nMOS.
- Perform a heavy **phosphorus or arsenic implant** (dose $\sim 10^{15}$ /cm², energy 50–100 keV).
- The **polysilicon gate acts as a self-aligned mask** — S/D regions are formed on either side of the gate automatically.

**Step 12 — p+ Source/Drain Implant (Mask 6):**
- Pattern with the **$p^+$ source/drain mask** (Mask #6), exposing the pMOS active areas.
- Perform a heavy **boron implant** (dose $\sim 10^{15}$ /cm²).
- The poly gate self-aligns the pMOS S/D.

**Step 13 — Contact Cut Mask (Mask 7):**
- Deposit **borophosphosilicate glass (BPSG)** as the inter-layer dielectric (ILD) to planarize the wafer surface.
- Pattern with the **contact cut mask** (Mask #7) and etch contacts down to the source, drain, gate, and n-well tap regions.

**Step 14 — Metallization and Patterning (Mask 8):**
- Deposit a layer of **aluminum (or copper in advanced nodes)** by sputtering, ~ 0.5–1.0 $\mu m$ thick.
- Pattern with the **metal-1 mask** (Mask #8) and etch to form interconnects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern processes (sub-100 nm)** add additional masks: **lightly-doped drain (LDD)** implant, **silicide** formation, multiple metal layers (Metal 1, 2, 3, …), and **via** masks between metal layers. The KTU exam typically sticks to the **8-mask single-poly single-metal baseline**.

### 3.2 Stick Diagram Construction (Step-by-Step Derivation)

A **stick diagram** is an abstract representation using colored lines for each layer. Standard KTU color code:

| Layer | Color | Convention |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| n-diffusion | **Green** | For nMOS source/drain |
| p-diffusion | **Yellow/Brown** | For pMOS S/D and n-well indicator |
| Polysilicon | **Red** | Gates and poly interconnects |
| Metal 1 | **Blue** | First-level metal interconnect |
| Metal 2 | (varies) | Higher-level metal |
| Contact | **Black X** | Connects poly/metal to diffusion |

#### Derivation of the CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram

**Logical Structure of CMOS Inverter:**
- Input `A` is the common gate of both pMOS and nMOS.
- pMOS source is tied to $V_{DD}$.
- nMOS source is tied to $V_{SS}$ (GND).
- pMOS drain and nMOS drain are tied together to form output `Y`.

**Step 1:** Draw a horizontal **red line** (poly) to represent the common gate — this is the input `A`.

**Step 2:** Above the poly line (in the n-well region), draw a horizontal **yellow line** (p-diffusion). Place **two breaks** in the yellow line where it crosses the red poly line — these breaks represent the **channel regions** of the pMOS.

**Step 3:** Below the poly line (in the p-substrate), draw a horizontal **green line** (n-diffusion). Similarly, place two breaks at the red poly crossings to represent the nMOS channels.

**Step 4:** Label the three yellow segments (left of pMOS, between two pMOS, right of pMOS) as $V_{DD}$, internal connection, and output Y. Similarly label green segments.

**Step 5:** Add **black X's (contacts)** where metal-1 connects to diffusion. Add **blue (metal-1)** lines for $V_{DD}$ rail, $V_{SS}$ rail, and output Y wire.

**Step 6:** For the **n-well tap**, draw a small $p^+$ diffusion in the n-well region connected to $V_{DD}$. For the **substrate tap**, draw a small $n^+$ diffusion in the p-substrate connected to $V_{SS}$. This prevents latch-up by tying the body to a fixed potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **well/substrate taps**. The body of every MOSFET must be connected to the appropriate power rail via a low-resistance ohmic contact. Missing this is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty** in the KTU valuation key.

### 3.3 Derivation of Lambda Design Rules (Micron-Style Scaling)

**Why Lambda Rules Exist:**  
Lambda design rules were introduced by Mead and Conway (1980) to provide a *scalable* design methodology. The parameter $\lambda$ is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_{min}}{2}
$$

where $L_{min}$ is the minimum channel length (gate length) of the process. For a $0.5\ \mu m$ process, $L_{min} = 0.5\ \mu m$, hence $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$. All physical layout dimensions are expressed as integer multiples of $\lambda$.

**Example Lambda Design Rules (Sub-Micron Process):**

- Minimum poly width: **$2\lambda$**
- Minimum diffusion width: **$2\lambda$**
- Minimum contact size: **$2\lambda \times 2\lambda$**
- Poly-to-diffusion spacing: **$1\lambda$**
- Metal width: **$3\lambda$**
- Poly-to-metal spacing: **$1\lambda$**
- n-well to nMOS active area: **$3\lambda$**
- pMOS active area to nMOS active area: **$3\lambda$**
- Contact-to-poly spacing: **$1\lambda$**

**Micron (Absolute) Design Rules:**  
In modern sub-100 nm processes, absolute micron rules (e.g., 0.13 $\mu m$, 0.09 $\mu m$) are used because $\lambda$ does not scale uniformly (e.g., contact cuts scale differently from line widths). However, KTU 2024 syllabus still teaches **lambda rules** as the pedagogical foundation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters vs. Device Performance

| Process Step | Key Parameter | Effect on Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Well Depth $x_j$ | $\uparrow x_j$ | Deeper well $\rightarrow$ less latch-up risk, but larger S/D-to-well capacitance |
| Gate Oxide $t_{ox}$ | $\downarrow t_{ox}$ | Higher $C_{ox}$, more drive current, but higher leakage |
| S/D Doping Dose | $\uparrow$ dose | Lower S/D resistance, sharper junctions, more leakage |
| LDD Implant | Light dose | Reduces hot-carrier effect, slightly increases series resistance |
| Channel Length $L$ | $\downarrow L$ | Higher speed, but worse short-channel effects (Vth roll-off) |
| Channel Width $W$ | $\uparrow W$ | More drive current, larger input capacitance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The n-Well CMOS Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: p-type Si Wafer] --> B[Step 1: Grow Pad Oxide SiO2]
    B --> C[Step 2: Deposit Si3N4 Layer]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Mask 1 n-well, Etch Nitride]
    D --> E[Step 4: Phosphorus Implant for n-well]
    E --> F[Step 5: Drive-in Diffusion at 1100C]
    F --> G[Step 6: Strip Nitride and Pad Oxide]
    G --> H[Step 7: Grow New Pad Oxide, Deposit Nitride]
    H --> I[Step 8: Apply Mask 2 Active Area, Pattern Nitride]
    I --> J[Step 9: LOCOS Field Oxidation - Grow FOX]
    J --> K[Step 10: Strip Nitride]
    K --> L[Step 11: Apply Mask 3 Channel Stop, Boron Implant]
    L --> M[Step 12: Threshold Adjust Implants]
    M --> N[Step 13: Strip Sacrificial Oxide, Grow Gate Oxide]
    N --> O[Step 14: Deposit Polysilicon, Dope n+]
    O --> P[Step 15: Apply Mask 4 Poly Gate, Etch Poly]
    P --> Q[Step 16: Apply Mask 5 n+ S/D, Phosphorus Implant]
    Q --> R[Step 17: Apply Mask 6 p+ S/D, Boron Implant]
    R --> S[Step 18: Deposit BPSG Inter-Layer Dielectric]
    S --> T[Step 19: Apply Mask 7 Contact Cuts, Etch]
    T --> U[Step 20: Deposit Aluminum Metal 1]
    U --> V[Step 21: Apply Mask 8 Metal 1, Etch Interconnects]
    V --> W[End: Complete CMOS Inverter Wafer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram Layout Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WELL[n-well Region]
        direction LR
        VDD[VDD Rail Metal-1 Blue] --> TAP1[Well Tap p+ to VDD]
        TAP1 --> PD1[pMOS Source Yellow]
        PD1 --> CHP1[Channel Break]
        CHP1 --> PD2[pMOS Drain Yellow]
        PD2 --> CHP2[Channel Break]
        CHP2 --> PD3[pMOS Drain Yellow]
    end

    subgraph SUB[p-substrate Region]
        direction LR
        GND[VSS Rail Metal-1 Blue] --> TAP2[Substrate Tap n+ to VSS]
        TAP2 --> ND1[nMOS Source Green]
        ND1 --> CHN1[Channel Break]
        CHN1 --> ND2[nMOS Drain Green]
        ND2 --> CHN2[Channel Break]
        CHN2 --> ND3[nMOS Source Green]
    end

    IN[Input A on Red Poly Line] -.-> CHP1
    IN -.-> CHN1
    IN -.-> CHP2
    IN -.-> CHN2
    PD2 --> OUT[Output Y Metal-1 Blue]
    ND2 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Lambda Design Rule Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Lambda Design Rule System] --> POLY[Poly Layer Rules]
    ROOT --> DIFF[Diffusion Layer Rules]
    ROOT --> METAL[Metal Layer Rules]
    ROOT --> CONT[Contact Rules]
    ROOT --> WELL_R[Well Layer Rules]
    ROOT --> ACT[Active Area Rules]
    POLY --> P1[Min Poly Width 2 lambda]
    POLY --> P2[Poly Spacing 2 lambda]
    POLY --> P3[Poly to Diffusion 1 lambda]
    DIFF --> D1[Min Diff Width 3 lambda]
    DIFF --> D2[Diff Spacing 3 lambda]
    DIFF --> D3[Source to Gate 3 lambda]
    METAL --> M1[Min Metal Width 3 lambda]
    METAL --> M2[Metal Spacing 3 lambda]
    CONT --> C1[Contact Size 2x2 lambda]
    CONT --> C2[Contact Poly Enclosure 1 lambda]
    CONT --> C3[Contact Metal Enclosure 1 lambda]
    WELL_R --> W1[Min Well Width 6 lambda]
    WELL_R --> W2[Well to Active 3 lambda]
    ACT --> A1[Min Active Width 3 lambda]
    ACT --> A2[Active Spacing 3 lambda]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Photolithography Step Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PL[Start: Clean Wafer] --> O1[1. Thermal Oxidation Grow SiO2]
    O1 --> O2[2. Spin Coat Photoresist]
    O2 --> O3[3. Soft Bake at 90C]
    O3 --> O4[4. Align Mask, UV Exposure]
    O4 --> O5[5. Develop Pattern in Chemical Solution]
    O5 --> O6{Is Exposed Positive PR or Unexposed Negative PR Dissolved?}
    O6 -->|Positive| O7[Pattern Open in Resist]
    O6 -->|Negative| O8[Pattern Blocked in Resist]
    O7 --> O9[6. Hard Bake at 120C]
    O8 --> O9
    O9 --> O10[7. Etch Oxide Wet HF or Dry Plasma]
    O10 --> O11[8. Strip Photoresist with Acetone or Plasma Ash]
    O11 --> O12[End: Patterned Layer on Wafer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "photolithography" and list its main steps in CMOS fabrication.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Photolithography is the process of transferring geometric patterns from a photomask onto a silicon wafer using light-sensitive polymers (photoresist) and selective etching.

Main steps:
1. **Wafer cleaning and oxidation** — Prepare a clean $SiO_2$ surface.
2. **Photoresist coating and soft bake** — Spin-coat PR; bake to remove solvent.
3. **Mask alignment and UV exposure** — Project mask pattern onto PR.
4. **Development** — Dissolve the soluble PR regions in a developer.
5. **Hard bake** — Harden the remaining PR pattern.
6. **Etching** — Remove oxide/material from the exposed (PR-free) regions.
7. **Photoresist stripping** — Remove the residual PR; clean the wafer.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing steps with correct order)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is the difference between the n-well and twin-tub CMOS process? Which one is preferred and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | n-Well Process | Twin-Tub Process |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Substrate | p-type Si wafer | Lightly doped $n^-$ or $p^-$ wafer |
| nMOS | Built in p-substrate (bulk) | Built in p-tub (implanted) |
| pMOS | Built in n-well (diffused) | Built in n-tub (implanted) |
| Tub Formation | Only n-well diffused | Both tubs independently implanted |
| Threshold Control | Coupled to substrate | Independent for nMOS and pMOS |

The **n-well process is preferred in industry** because electrons (the carriers in nMOS) have **2–3 times higher mobility** than holes. By keeping nMOS in the uniform p-type bulk substrate, optimal nMOS performance is achieved, while the pMOS is built in the n-well where mobility demands are less stringent. *(3 marks: 1 for difference, 1 for preference, 1 for reason)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **n-well CMOS process** for fabricating a CMOS inverter. List the masks used and the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **CMOS inverter** contains one pMOS and one nMOS transistor. The **n-well process** fabricates the pMOS inside a diffused n-well and the nMOS directly in the p-substrate.

**Masks Required (8-mask baseline):**

| Mask # | Mask Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | n-well | Defines diffusion region for pMOS body |
| 2 | Active area (Thinox) | Defines transistor source/drain/channel regions |
| 3 | p-channel stop | Boron implant in n-well field to prevent surface inversion |
| 4 | Polysilicon | Defines gates of both nMOS and pMOS |
| 5 | $n^+$ S/D | Heavily dopes nMOS source, drain, gate (phosphorus) |
| 6 | $p^+$ S/D | Heavily dopes pMOS source, drain, gate (boron) |
| 7 | Contact cut | Opens vias to diffusion, poly, and well/substrate |
| 8 | Metal 1 | Defines first-level interconnects and I/O pads |

**Process Steps (with cross-section description):**

1. **Substrate selection:** p-type Si wafer, $\rho = 10{-}20\ \Omega$-cm. *(0.5 M)*
2. **Pad oxide growth and n-well mask (Mask 1):** Grow thin $SiO_2$ (~50 nm), deposit nitride, pattern with **Mask 1**, etch nitride over n-well region, perform **phosphorus implant** ($N_D \approx 10^{16}$/cm³), drive in at 1100°C to form n-well of depth $x_j \approx 2 \mu m$. *(1 M)*
3. **Active area definition (Mask 2):** Strip nitride; grow new pad oxide and deposit fresh nitride; pattern with **Mask 2** to define transistor active regions. *(0.5 M)*
4. **Field oxidation (LOCOS):** Expose wafer to steam at 1000°C; thick field oxide (~500–1000 nm) grows where nitride was removed, providing isolation between transistors. Strip nitride. *(1 M)*
5. **Channel stop implant (Mask 3):** Use **Mask 3** to protect the active regions; perform **boron implant** in the n-well field regions to prevent surface inversion (parasitic channel formation). *(0.5 M)*
6. **Threshold adjust and gate oxide:** Strip pad oxide, grow high-quality **gate oxide** ($t_{ox} \approx 5{-}10$ nm). *(0.5 M)*
7. **Polysilicon deposition and doping:** Deposit poly via LPCVD, dope with phosphorus to make it $n^+$ conductive ($R_{\square} \approx 30\ \Omega/\square$). *(0.5 M)*
8. **Poly gate definition (Mask 4):** Pattern poly with **Mask 4** to simultaneously form both transistor gates. The poly gates will act as **self-aligned masks** for the subsequent S/D implants. *(0.5 M)*
9. **$n^+$ S/D implant (Mask 5):** Mask the wafer exposing only nMOS active areas; implant phosphorus heavily. The poly gates self-align the S/D. *(0.5 M)*
10. **$p^+$ S/D implant (Mask 6):** Mask exposing only pMOS active areas; implant boron heavily. *(0.5 M)*
11. **Contact cut (Mask 7) and metal-1 (Mask 8):** Deposit BPSG ILD, etch contacts with **Mask 7**, deposit Al, pattern interconnects with **Mask 8**. *(0.5 M)*

The resulting cross-section shows a pMOS in the n-well, an nMOS in the p-substrate, each with its source, drain, and gate, interconnected by metal-1 to form the inverter.

---

**Q.A. (b)** Draw the **stick diagram** of a CMOS 2-input NAND gate using standard color conventions. List the masks and explain the function of the **well tap** and **substrate tap** contacts. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**2-Input NAND Gate:**  
$Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ — uses 2 pMOS in parallel + 2 nMOS in series.

**Stick Diagram Construction Steps:**

1. Draw two horizontal **yellow** diffusion lines in the n-well region (top, two parallel pMOS).
2. Draw a single horizontal **green** diffusion line in the p-substrate (bottom, two series nMOS).
3. Draw two vertical **red** poly strips crossing the diffusions — these are the inputs A (left) and B (right).
4. The nMOS diffusions are connected in series (output of nMOS1 drives input of nMOS2).
5. The pMOS diffusions are connected in parallel (both tied to $V_{DD}$).
6. Use **blue** metal-1 to connect: pMOS drains together and to nMOS drain (output Y); nMOS source to $V_{SS}$; pMOS sources to $V_{DD}$.
7. Add **black X** contacts at every metal-to-diffusion junction.
8. Add a **well tap** ($p^+$ in n-well, tied to $V_{DD}$) and **substrate tap** ($n^+$ in p-sub, tied to $V_{SS}$).

**Masks Used (8 masks, same as inverter):** n-well, active area, p-channel stop, poly, $n^+$ S/D, $p^+$ S/D, contact, metal 1.

**Function of Well/Substrate Taps (2 Marks):**
- The **body** of every MOSFET (the n-well for pMOS, the p-substrate for nMOS) must be connected to a fixed potential to:
  1. **Eliminate the body effect** (Vth depends on $V_{BS}$; tying body to source removes this dependency).
  2. **Prevent latch-up** (a parasitic thyristor in CMOS that can cause destructive failure). Without the taps, the body floats and is susceptible to triggering the latch.
- The **well tap** is a $p^+$ diffusion in the n-well, connected to $V_{DD}$, ensuring the pMOS body is at $V_{DD}$.
- The **substrate tap** is an $n^+$ diffusion in the p-substrate, connected to $V_{SS}$ (GND), ensuring the nMOS body is at $V_{SS}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common 2-Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Wrong color in stick diagram:** Using red for nMOS body and green for pMOS body is a *common fatal error*. KTU strictly follows the convention: **green = n-diff, yellow/brown = p-diff, red = poly, blue = metal-1, black X = contact**.
> 2. **Forgetting well tap or substrate tap:** Always include them. They are mandatory for a working CMOS layout.
> 3. **Wrong mask count:** Saying "only 5 masks" — the correct answer is **8 masks** for the standard n-well single-poly single-metal process (10–12 for advanced processes).

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B. (a)** With relevant equations, explain the **lambda ($\lambda$) design rules** for CMOS layout. Using a $0.5\ \mu m$ process (where $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$), determine the dimensions of: (i) minimum poly width, (ii) minimum diffusion width, (iii) minimum contact size, (iv) minimum spacing between two metal lines. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Lambda Design Rules – Definition (2 Marks):**

Lambda ($\lambda$) is a **scalable unit of length** used to express geometric design rules. It is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_{min}}{2}
$$

where $L_{min}$ is the minimum drawn channel length of the MOS transistor. For a $0.5\ \mu m$ process, $L_{min} = 0.5\ \mu m$, hence $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$. All layout dimensions (widths, spacings) are integer multiples of $\lambda$, which makes a layout *technology-independent* (it can be migrated to a different process node by simply changing $\lambda$).

**Standard Lambda Design Rules (3 Marks):**

| Rule | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Minimum poly width | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum diffusion width | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum contact size | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |
| Minimum contact to gate spacing | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing between two poly lines | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing between two metal lines | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum source-to-gate or drain-to-gate diffusion | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum metal-to-poly spacing | $1\lambda$ |
| Minimum n-well width | $6\lambda$ |
| n-well to nMOS active spacing | $3\lambda$ |
| pMOS active to nMOS active spacing | $3\lambda$ |

**Calculations for $0.5\ \mu m$ Process, $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$ (2 Marks):**

(i) Minimum poly width:

$$
W_{poly} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 0.5\ \mu m
$$

(ii) Minimum diffusion width:

$$
W_{diff} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 0.5\ \mu m
$$

(iii) Minimum contact size:

$$
\text{Contact} = 2\lambda \times 2\lambda = 0.5\ \mu m \times 0.5\ \mu m
$$

(iv) Minimum spacing between two metal lines:

$$
S_{metal} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 0.75\ \mu m
$$

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the following terms with CMOS fabrication context: (i) **LOCOS**, (ii) **Self-aligned gate process**, (iii) **LDD**, (iv) **Latch-up** and its prevention. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) LOCOS – Local Oxidation of Silicon (2 Marks):**
LOCOS is the **selective growth of a thick field oxide ($SiO_2$)** in non-active regions of the wafer to electrically isolate adjacent MOS transistors. The wafer is first covered with a patterned **silicon nitride ($Si_3N_4$)** layer that protects the active areas; the wafer is then exposed to a **steam (wet $O_2$) ambient at 1000°C**, causing $SiO_2$ to grow only in the nitride-free regions. The grown FOX is typically 500–1000 nm thick and provides robust inter-device isolation.

**(ii) Self-Aligned Gate Process (2 Marks):**
The self-aligned gate technique is a defining feature of modern CMOS. After depositing polysilicon, the **poly gate mask (Mask 4)** is used to etch the poly. The same etched poly then **physically acts as a mask** during the subsequent $n^+$ and $p^+$ source/drain implants. This ensures the source and drain are **automatically aligned to the gate edges** with sub-micron precision, eliminating the mask-alignment tolerance that older non-self-aligned processes had. This dramatically reduces **gate-to-source/drain overlap capacitance**, improving speed and reducing leakage.

**(iii) LDD – Lightly Doped Drain (1.5 Marks):**
LDD is a technique used in **sub-micron processes** to mitigate the **hot-carrier effect (HCE)**. As channel lengths shrink, the electric field near the drain becomes intense, causing carriers to gain high kinetic energy and "inject" into the gate oxide, degrading the device over time. LDD introduces a **lightly doped $n^-$ region** between the channel and the $n^+$ drain (created by a low-dose implant before a side-wall spacer is formed, followed by the heavy $n^+$ implant). The graded $n^-$ region **softens the electric field** at the drain junction, dramatically improving long-term device reliability.

**(iv) Latch-up and Its Prevention (1.5 Marks):**
Latch-up is a **destructive short-circuit phenomenon** in CMOS caused by the inherent **parasitic p-n-p-n thyristor (SCR) structure** formed by the nMOS (n+ in p-sub) and pMOS (p+ in n-well) sharing the same substrate. Under certain transients (ionizing radiation, supply over-voltage, I/O pin ESD), the parasitic bipolar transistors turn on, creating a low-impedance path from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$ that **crowbars the supply** and can cause permanent damage.

**Prevention methods:**
1. **Well/Substrate Taps** placed close to every transistor, tied to the appropriate rail, to keep the body at a fixed potential.
2. **Retrograde wells** — heavy doping at the bottom of the well to reduce lateral resistance.
3. **Epitaxial wafers** — using a lightly doped epitaxial layer on a heavily doped substrate.
4. **SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator)** — eliminates the parasitic thyristor entirely by isolating devices with buried oxide.
5. **Guard rings** — $n^+$ and $p^+$ rings around sensitive nodes to collect minority carriers before they trigger the SCR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common 2-Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing LOCOS with STI:** In modern sub-100 nm processes, LOCOS has been replaced by **Shallow Trench Isolation (STI)** because LOCOS creates the "bird's beak" encroachment problem. KTU allows either term, but the student must know the difference.
> 2. **Misdefining LDD:** Students often describe LDD as a heavy doping, but it is *light* doping. Write "lightly doped" explicitly.
> 3. **Vague latch-up prevention:** Saying "use guard rings" alone is not enough. Mention **well taps close to source** for full credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Four CMOS process variants:** n-well (industry standard), p-well (older), twin-tub (high performance), SOI (low power).
- **Lambda rule definition:** $\lambda = L_{min} / 2$. For a $0.5\ \mu m$ process, $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$.
- **8 masks in standard n-well CMOS:** n-well → active area → channel stop → poly → $n^+$ S/D → $p^+$ S/D → contact → metal 1.
- **Photolithography is a 7-8 step sequence:** oxidation → PR coat → soft bake → align+expose → develop → hard bake → etch → strip.
- **LOCOS = Local Oxidation of Silicon** — uses a patterned nitride mask to grow thick field oxide for isolation.
- **Self-aligned gate:** poly mask pattern serves as the implant mask — eliminates S/D-to-gate alignment error.
- **Stick diagram color code:** green = n-diff, yellow = p-diff, red = poly, blue = metal-1, black X = contact.
- **Well tap and substrate tap are mandatory** — they prevent latch-up and body effect.
- **pMOS-to-nMOS width ratio in an inverter:** $W_p / W_n \approx 2{-}3$ to balance rise and fall delays.
- **Latch-up cause:** parasitic SCR structure; prevention — well taps, retrograde wells, SOI, guard rings.
- **Modern node design rules are absolute micron rules** (e.g., 28 nm, 14 nm) — lambda is now a pedagogical tool.
- **For any KTU 14-mark process question**, the model answer template is: (1) Substrate, (2) Well formation, (3) Active area, (4) LOCOS, (5) Channel stop, (6) Gate oxide, (7) Poly, (8) S/D implants, (9) Contacts, (10) Metallization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Partitioning** is the systematic process of decomposing a complex VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) system into a hierarchy of smaller, manageable, and well-defined functional sub-modules or blocks, such that each sub-module can be designed, verified, optimized, and tested independently before being integrated into the final system-on-chip (SoC).

In the context of CMOS VLSI design, partitioning is the **first and most critical step** in the top-down design methodology. It determines how the overall functionality is distributed across:
- **Transistor-level cells** (basic gates: NAND, NOR, Inverter)
- **Gate-level modules** (Flip-flops, Multiplexers, Adders)
- **Register-Transfer Level (RTL) blocks** (ALU, Register File, Control Unit)
- **System-level blocks** (Processor Core, Memory, I/O Controllers)

The goal of partitioning is to ensure **functional correctness, design reusability, timing closure, and testability** while minimizing interconnect complexity, area, and power dissipation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a Modern Hospital**
>
> Imagine you are tasked with building a massive 50-story hospital. You would **never** start by laying bricks randomly across the entire site. Instead, you would:
>
> 1. **Divide the building into functional zones** — Emergency Wing, Cardiology, Pediatrics, Administration, Parking, Power Systems.
> 2. **Design each zone independently** with its own architects, structural plans, and electrical layouts.
> 3. **Define clear interfaces** — the Emergency Wing connects to the Reception via a specific corridor width and signage standard.
> 4. **Integrate the zones** into the final hospital blueprint.
>
> This is **exactly what design partitioning does for a chip**. The "hospital" is your chip, the "zones" are your sub-modules, and the "corridors" are the interconnect wires and buses.

## 1.3 Why Partitioning is Essential in CMOS Design

Modern chips contain **billions of transistors** (e.g., Apple M-series, Snapdragon SoCs). Without partitioning:

| Problem | Consequence |
|---------|-------------|
| **Design Complexity** | A single engineer (or even a team) cannot hold the entire design in mind simultaneously. |
| **Verification Bottleneck** | Simulating billions of transistors at once is computationally impossible. |
| **Timing Closure Failure** | Long global interconnects cause signal delay, race conditions, and clock skew. |
| **Testability Issues** | Internal nodes become inaccessible, making fault detection nearly impossible. |
| **Team Coordination** | Multiple teams cannot work in parallel on an unpartitioned design. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Partitioning directly supports the Course Outcome **CO1: "Design and analyze CMOS digital circuits using the VLSI design flow."** It is the conceptual foundation of the entire RTL-to-GDSII flow.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constraints

The following are the **primary optimization parameters** evaluated during partitioning:

- **Area (A):** Total silicon area in $\mu m^2$ or $mm^2$ — must be minimized.
- **Interconnect Length / Wire Count:** Number of signals crossing partition boundaries — must be minimized.
- **Timing Delay (T):** Propagation delay across blocks — must meet clock constraints.
- **Power Dissipation (P):** Static + dynamic power in Watts.
- **Pins / I/O per Block:** Boundary interface complexity.
- **Test Coverage:** Must remain high post-partitioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The VLSI Design Abstraction Hierarchy

CMOS design follows a strict **abstraction pyramid**. Partitioning operates at every level of this pyramid:

1. **System Level (Highest Abstraction):** Defines overall chip behavior (specification, algorithms).
2. **Block / Architectural Level:** Partitions the system into functional units (CPU, Memory, Bus).
3. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL):** Each block is described by data flow between registers.
4. **Logic / Gate Level:** RTL is synthesized into Boolean gates (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, XOR).
5. **Circuit / Transistor Level:** Gates are implemented using CMOS transistor networks.
6. **Layout / Physical Level (Lowest Abstraction):** Transistors are drawn as polygons in silicon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Partitioning is performed at the architectural and RTL levels**, but its decisions ripple down to every lower level.

## 2.2 Approaches to Design Partitioning

### 2.2.1 Top-Down Design (Most Common in Industry)

The designer starts from the **system specification** and recursively decomposes it into smaller sub-modules until each sub-module is simple enough to be implemented directly.

**Steps:**
1. Define the chip's overall function (e.g., "a 32-bit RISC processor").
2. Identify major functional blocks (ALU, Register File, Instruction Decoder, Cache).
3. Further divide each block into sub-blocks (ALU → Adder, Multiplier, Shifter, Comparator).
4. Continue until reaching primitive gates and standard cells.

**Advantages:**
- Clean architectural overview.
- Easy to assign work to multiple teams.
- Aligns with hierarchical verification.

### 2.2.2 Bottom-Up Design

The designer starts from **pre-designed primitive cells** (inverters, NAND gates) and builds larger modules by combining them.

**Advantages:**
- Reuses verified cells.
- Faster for designs with strong IP libraries.
- Used heavily in **FPGA-based prototyping**.

### 2.2.3 Mixed Approach (Industry Standard)

Modern VLSI design uses a **Y-chart (Gajski-Kuhn) methodology** combining top-down and bottom-up simultaneously across three domains: behavioral, structural, and geometric.

## 2.3 Types / Levels of Partitioning

| Partitioning Type | Granularity | Example |
|-------------------|-------------|---------|
| **Transistor-level Partitioning** | Individual NMOS/PMOS | CMOS inverter, transmission gate |
| **Gate-level Partitioning** | Standard cells | NAND3, DFF, MUX2 |
| **Block-level Partitioning** | Functional units | ALU, Memory array, PLL |
| **System-level Partitioning** | SoC sub-systems | CPU core, GPU, DSP, I/O ring |

## 2.4 Structured Design Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The three pillars of structured CMOS design (as per classic VLSI pedagogy by Weste & Harris, and Ming-Bo Lin) are:
>
> 1. **Hierarchy** — Divide and conquer recursively.
> 2. **Regularity** — Reuse identical sub-modules (e.g., 32-bit adder = 32 copies of 1-bit full adder).
> 3. **Modularity** — Each sub-module has well-defined inputs, outputs, and functionality.

## 2.5 Partitioning Objectives (The Optimization Problem)

Mathematically, design partitioning is formulated as:

> **Minimize:** Inter-block communication cost
> **Subject to:** Block area constraint, timing constraint, power budget, pin limit

Let:
- $n$ = total number of cells in the design.
- $p$ = number of partitions.
- $c_{ij}$ = connection cost between cell $i$ and cell $j$.
- $A_k$ = area of partition $k$.
- $A_{max}$ = maximum allowed area per partition.

The objective function is:

$$\text{Minimize } C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$

Subject to:
$$\sum_{i \in P_k} A_i \leq A_{max}, \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, \dots, p\}$$

where $x_{ij} = 1$ if cells $i$ and $j$ are in different partitions, else $0$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Purpose |
|---------------------|------------|------|---------|
| **Partition Count** | $p$ | dimensionless | Number of sub-blocks |
| **Cut Size (Inter-block Nets)** | $\text{Cut} = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} 1$ | count of nets | Measures partitioning quality |
| **Inter-block Cost** | $C = \sum c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$ | cost units | Total interface complexity |
| **Area Constraint** | $\sum_{i \in P_k} A_i \leq A_{max}$ | $\mu m^2$ | Per-block silicon limit |
| **Pin Constraint** | $\text{Pins}(P_k) \leq P_{max}$ | pins | I/O limitation per block |
| **Critical Path Delay** | $T_{cp} = \sum t_{logic} + t_{interconnect}$ | seconds (or ps/ns) | Timing closure target |
| **Rent's Rule (Estimation)** | $P = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Relates pins to block size |
| **Fan-out (Static)** | $FO = C_{load} / C_{in}$ | ratio | Drive strength requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rent's Rule** is a key empirical formula: a block with $N$ internal gates has approximately $P = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ external pins, where $K \approx 3$ and $\beta \approx 0.5$ for typical CMOS designs. This guides partitioning decisions to avoid pin-limited blocks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SoC Design:** Apple A-series chips partition CPU, GPU, NPU, ISP into separate physical blocks (chiplets) connected via interconnect fabric.
- **FPGA Design:** Xilinx Vivado partitions user RTL into Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs) and routing resources.
- **ASIC Design:** Synopsys Design Compiler and Cadence Genus perform automatic partitioning during RTL synthesis.
- **Memory Design:** SRAM arrays are partitioned into banks, sub-banks, and mat tiles to manage access time and power.
- **3D ICs / Chiplets:** Modern AMD, Intel, and Nvidia designs partition dies physically and stack them using through-silicon vias (TSVs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rent's Rule Applied to Design Partitioning

Rent's Rule is **empirically derived** from observations of existing chip layouts and is used to predict the number of external pins a partitioned block should have.

### 3.1.1 Starting Premises

Let:
- $N$ = number of internal logic gates (or cells) within a block.
- $B$ = number of external connection points (pins/terminals).
- $K$ = Rent's constant (empirically determined).
- $\beta$ = Rent's exponent (0 < $\beta$ < 1; typically $\beta \approx 0.5$ for standard logic).

### 3.1.2 Functional Form

The relationship is expressed as a power law:

$$B = K \cdot N^{\beta}$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Interpretation

**Step 1 — Define the relationship between internal complexity and external connectivity.**

When a block is partitioned, every internal signal that exits the block becomes an external pin. A larger block inherently contains more internal signals, hence more pins.

**Step 2 — Apply logarithmic transformation to linearize the relationship.**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(B) = \ln(K) + \beta \cdot \ln(N)$$

This is in the form $y = m \cdot x + c$, where:
- $y = \ln(B)$
- $m = \beta$ (slope)
- $x = \ln(N)$
- $c = \ln(K)$ (intercept)

**Step 3 — Solve for $\beta$ from two data points.**

Given two observed blocks with $(N_1, B_1)$ and $(N_2, B_2)$:

$$\beta = \frac{\ln(B_2) - \ln(B_1)}{\ln(N_2) - \ln(N_1)} = \frac{\ln(B_2 / B_1)}{\ln(N_2 / N_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Use $K$ for absolute pin estimation.**

Rearranging the original equation:

$$K = \frac{B}{N^{\beta}}$$

**Step 5 — Partitioning Constraint.**

For a design partitioned into $p$ blocks, the total pin count must be consistent with the global Rent's Rule. If a single block has $N_i$ cells, its required pins are:

$$P_i = K \cdot N_i^{\beta}$$

The total required interface pins (assuming no pin reuse) is:

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} P_i = K \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{p} N_i^{\beta}$$

Since $\sum N_i = N_{total}$, by the power-mean inequality:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{p} N_i^{\beta} \leq p^{1-\beta} \cdot N_{total}^{\beta}$$

This means **partitioning increases the total pin requirement** when $p > 1$, which is why over-partitioning is penalized.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A CMOS design has $N = 10{,}000$ gates. It is partitioned into 4 equal blocks of $N_i = 2{,}500$ each. Given $K = 3$ and $\beta = 0.5$, find the total pin requirement before and after partitioning.

**Solution:**

**Before Partitioning (Single Block):**

$$P_{single} = 3 \cdot (10{,}000)^{0.5}$$

$$P_{single} = 3 \cdot 100 = 300 \text{ pins}$$

**After Partitioning (4 Equal Blocks):**

Per-block pin requirement:

$$P_{block} = 3 \cdot (2{,}500)^{0.5} = 3 \cdot 50 = 150 \text{ pins}$$

Total pin requirement (4 blocks):

$$P_{total} = 4 \cdot 150 = 600 \text{ pins}$$

**Pin Overhead:**

$$\Delta P = 600 - 300 = 300 \text{ pins (100% overhead)}$$

This demonstrates why **partitioning should be done judiciously** — over-partitioning inflates interface complexity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Inter-Block Cut Size Minimization

### 3.2.1 Setup

Given a netlist with $n$ cells and $m$ nets, we want to partition cells into two sets $A$ and $B$ such that the number of nets crossing the boundary is minimized.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let:
- $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ = assignment variable for cell $i$. (0 = in $A$, 1 = in $B$)
- $e_k$ = net $k$ has at least one cell in $A$ and at least one cell in $B$.

Define $e_k = 1$ if net $k$ is "cut", else $e_k = 0$:

$$e_k = \left( \bigvee_{i \in \text{net}_k} \bar{x_i} \right) \cdot \left( \bigvee_{i \in \text{net}_k} x_i \right)$$

Total cut size:

$$\text{CutSize} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} e_k$$

This is an **NP-hard** problem (related to the graph bisection problem), so heuristic algorithms like **Kernighan-Lin**, **Fiduccia-Mattheyses**, and **simulated annealing** are used in practice.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Partitioning Trade-offs

| Partitioning Choice | Effect on Area | Effect on Speed | Effect on Power | Effect on Testability |
|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Few large blocks** | Lower area (less overhead) | Faster (shorter interconnects) | Lower leakage | Harder to test internally |
| **Many small blocks** | Higher area (more buffers, padding) | Slower (longer wires) | Higher dynamic power | Easier scan/test access |
| **Group by function** | Moderate | Moderate | Lower switching activity | Easier verification |
| **Group by timing criticality** | Moderate | Optimized | Variable | Moderate |
| **Group by clock domain** | Lower | Better skew control | Lower | Easier CDC handling |

## 3.4 Design Partitioning Decision Algorithm (Logical Flow)

1. **Analyze netlist connectivity graph** $G = (V, E)$.
2. **Compute Rent's exponent $\beta$** from historical data of similar designs.
3. **Apply area budget** to determine maximum number of partitions $p$.
4. **Run Fiduccia-Mattheyses heuristic** to minimize cut size iteratively.
5. **Validate timing closure** using static timing analysis (STA).
6. **Verify power budget** using dynamic power estimation.
7. **Re-iterate** if any constraint is violated.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down Design Partitioning Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysSpec["SYSTEM SPECIFICATION"] --> archDes["ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN"]
    archDes --> idBlocks["IDENTIFY MAJOR BLOCKS"]
    idBlocks --> subDivide1["SUB DIVIDE ALU"]
    idBlocks --> subDivide2["SUB DIVIDE REGFILE"]
    idBlocks --> subDivide3["SUB DIVIDE CONTROL UNIT"]
    subDivide1 --> add1["1 BIT ADDER"]
    subDivide1 --> mul1["MULTIPLIER UNIT"]
    subDivide1 --> sh1["BARREL SHIFTER"]
    subDivide2 --> dec1["DECODER STAGE"]
    subDivide2 --> ff1["FLIP FLOP ARRAY"]
    subDivide3 --> fsm1["FSM MACHINE"]
    add1 --> cmosInv["CMOS TRANSISTOR NETWORK"]
    mul1 --> cmosInv
    sh1 --> cmosInv
    dec1 --> cmosInv
    ff1 --> cmosInv
    fsm1 --> cmosInv
    cmosInv --> layout["PHYSICAL LAYOUT"]
    layout --> fab["FABRICATION"]
```

> **Caption:** This diagram illustrates the recursive top-down decomposition from a system specification all the way down to CMOS transistor networks. Each level of decomposition represents a partitioning decision.

## 4.2 Partitioning Cost vs. Block Size Trade-off (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["FEW LARGE BLOCKS"] --> B["LOW PIN COUNT"]
    A --> C["LOW INTERFACE OVERHEAD"]
    A --> D["HARDER INTERNAL TESTING"]
    E["MANY SMALL BLOCKS"] --> F["HIGH PIN COUNT"]
    E --> G["HIGHER INTERFACE OVERHEAD"]
    E --> H["EASIER MODULAR TESTING"]
    B --> I["OPTIMAL PARTITION"]
    F --> I
    C --> I
    H --> I
    D --> J["NOT OPTIMAL"]
    G --> J
```

> **Caption:** This block-level functional architecture flow visualizes the trade-off curve in partitioning decisions. The optimal partition lies between the two extremes.

## 4.3 Hierarchical Partitioning of a Simple Processor (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    socBlock["SoC TOP LEVEL"] --> cpuCore["CPU CORE"]
    socBlock --> memCtrl["MEMORY CONTROLLER"]
    socBlock --> ioRing["I O RING"]
    socBlock --> pllBlock["PLL CLOCK BLOCK"]
    cpuCore --> intAlu["INTEGER ALU"]
    cpuCore --> fpuBlock["FPU UNIT"]
    cpuCore --> regFile["REGISTER FILE 32X32"]
    cpuCore --> cacheL1["L1 CACHE 32KB"]
    intAlu --> adder32["32 BIT RIPPLE ADDER"]
    intAlu --> boothMul["BOOTH MULTIPLIER"]
    intAlu --> logicUnit["LOGIC UNIT"]
    adder32 --> fa1["FULL ADDER CELL 1"]
    adder32 --> fa2["FULL ADDER CELL 2"]
    adder32 --> fan["FULL ADDER CELL N"]
    cacheL1 --> sramCell["6T SRAM CELL"]
```

> **Caption:** This diagram represents the standard partitioning of a RISC-V / ARM-class processor. Notice the recursive decomposition: SoC → CPU Core → ALU → 32-bit Adder → 1-bit Full Adder → 6T SRAM Cell.

## 4.4 Partitioning Algorithm Workflow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["INPUT NETLIST G V E"] --> initPart["INITIAL PARTITION A B"]
    initPart --> gainCalc["COMPUTE GAIN FOR EACH CELL"]
    gainCalc --> lockMax["LOCK MAX GAIN CELL"]
    lockMax --> moveCell["MOVE CELL TO OTHER PARTITION"]
    moveCell --> updateGain["UPDATE GAINS OF NEIGHBORS"]
    updateGain --> convCheck{"CONVERGED?"}
    convCheck --> noCheck["NO"] --> gainCalc
    convCheck --> yesCheck["YES"] --> finalPart["FINAL PARTITION A B"]
    finalPart --> report["GENERATE CUT SIZE REPORT"]
```

> **Caption:** This flow illustrates the Fiduccia-Mattheyses heuristic algorithm used widely in industry for bipartitioning. The "gain" is the reduction in cut size achieved by moving a cell.
