<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Yield** in the context of IC fabrication. How is it related to the **Defect Level (DL)**?

**Model Answer:**

Yield ($Y$) is the ratio of the number of good dies on a wafer to the total number of dies fabricated. Mathematically,

$$
Y = \frac{\text{Number of good dies}}{\text{Total number of dies}}
$$

The Defect Level ($DL$) is the fraction of faulty chips that escape testing and reach the customer. It depends on yield and the test coverage $T$ through the Williams-Brown formula:

$$
DL = 1 - Y^{(1-T)}
$$

Higher yield and higher test coverage both reduce the defect level, which is critical for reliability-sensitive applications.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **wire bonding** and **flip-chip** interconnection techniques in IC packaging.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wire Bonding | Flip-Chip (C4) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Fine Au/Cu wires connect die pads to leadframe. | Solder bumps on die pads reflowed directly onto substrate. |
| Lead Inductance | High (10–20 nH) | Very low (< 1 nH) |
| I/O Density | Low–Medium | Very high |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Repairability | Easy | Hard |

*(3 marks: 1 for tabulation, 1 for application, 1 for trade-off.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), you must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** from any chosen module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the construction and features of the following IC packages: **(i) DIP, (ii) QFP, (iii) BGA.** Compare them in terms of pin count, pitch, and typical applications.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dual In-line Package (DIP):**
The DIP has two parallel rows of through-hole pins, with a standard pitch of **2.54 mm**. The silicon die is mounted on a leadframe, wire-bonded to the inner leads, and encapsulated in black epoxy. Pin counts typically range from 8 to 40. DIP is used in legacy logic ICs and through-hole educational kits. Its main drawback is large footprint and parasitic inductance.

**(ii) Quad Flat Package (QFP):**
The QFP has gull-wing leads protruding on all four sides. The pin pitch is **0.4 mm – 0.8 mm**, allowing 44 to 304 pins. The die is mounted on a metal pad for heat spreading, bonded to the leadframe, and overmolded. QFP is common in microcontrollers, ASICs, and DSPs where moderate pin counts and surface-mount assembly are required.

**(iii) Ball Grid Array (BGA):**
The BGA replaces peripheral leads with an array of solder balls on the package bottom. Pitch is **1.0 mm or 1.27 mm**, and pin counts can exceed 1000. Short electrical path gives superior high-frequency performance. The die often uses **flip-chip** connection. BGA is the standard for microprocessors, FPGAs, and GPUs.

**Comparison Table (mandatory for full marks):**

| Feature | DIP | QFP | BGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pin count | 8–40 | 44–304 | 100–2000+ |
| Pitch | 2.54 mm | 0.4–0.8 mm | 1.0–1.27 mm |
| Lead inductance | High | Medium | Low |
| Application | Hobby, legacy | MCUs, ASICs | CPUs, FPGAs |

*(7 marks: 2 per package + 1 for table.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Stuck-At Fault Model** in CMOS testing. A combinational circuit has **15 signal lines**. Calculate the total number of single stuck-at faults. If the test coverage is **90%** and yield is **0.9**, determine the Defect Level using the Williams-Brown formula.

**Model Solution:**

The **Stuck-At Fault Model** is a logical abstraction used to represent physical defects in CMOS circuits. It assumes that any signal line may be permanently tied to logic 0 (s-a-0) or logic 1 (s-a-1), regardless of the circuit's intended behavior. This model is widely used because it accurately represents many real defects such as shorts to $V_{DD}$/GND, broken interconnects, and certain bridging faults.

**Step 1 — Compute total single stuck-at faults.**

For a circuit with $n$ signal lines, the total number of single stuck-at faults is:

$$
F = 2n
$$

Substituting $n = 15$:

$$
F = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ faults}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify yield and test coverage values.**

$$
Y = 0.9, \quad T = 0.9
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1 - T$.**

$$
1 - T = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $Y^{(1-T)}$.**

$$
Y^{(1-T)} = (0.9)^{0.1}
$$

Using logarithms:

$$
\ln(0.9) \approx -0.10536
$$

$$
0.1 \times (-0.10536) = -0.010536
$$

$$
(0.9)^{0.1} = e^{-0.010536} \approx 0.98952
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Defect Level.**

$$
DL = 1 - 0.98952 = 0.01048
$$

Converting to DPPM:

$$
DL_{\text{ppm}} = 0.01048 \times 10^6 \approx 10{,}480 \text{ DPPM}
$$

**Conclusion:** With 30 single stuck-at faults, 90% test coverage, and 90% yield, the shipment contains approximately **10,480 defective parts per million** — far exceeding automotive-grade limits.

*(7 marks: 2 for theory, 1 for fault count, 4 for numerical calculation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **levels of packaging** in VLSI. With a neat block diagram, explain the **wafer-level packaging (WLP)** flow.

**Model Solution:**

VLSI packaging is structured in four hierarchical levels:

1. **Level 0 — Die on Wafer:** The bare silicon die is the lowest level. It is not yet a usable component.

2. **Level 1 — Single-Chip Package:** The die is mounted on a leadframe or substrate, wire-bonded or flip-chipped, and encapsulated. Examples: DIP, SOIC, QFP, BGA.

3. **Level 2 — Multi-Chip Module (MCM):** Multiple dies (e.g., CPU + memory) are mounted on a common substrate within one package, enabling heterogeneous integration. Variants include MCM-L (laminate), MCM-C (ceramic), and MCM-D (deposited).

4. **Level 3 — PCB / System Level:** The packaged ICs are soldered onto a printed circuit board along with passive components, connectors, and other ICs.

**Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP) Flow:**

In WLP, the package is fabricated **directly on the wafer** before dicing. The steps are:

1. **Wafer Preparation** — completed CMOS wafer with passivation.
2. **Redistribution Layer (RDL) Fabrication** — a copper layer reroutes peripheral pads to a new area-array footprint.
3. **Under-Bump Metallization (UBM)** — a thin metal stack (e.g., Ti/Ni/Au) is deposited at the new pad locations.
4. **Solder Bump Deposition** — solder balls are placed and reflowed.
5. **Wafer Dicing** — the wafer is cut into individual packaged chips.
6. **Test and Ship** — known-good-die-level testing is performed.

The result is a "package" that is **essentially the same size as the die** itself, hence the alternative name **Chip-Scale Package (CSP)**. WLP is dominant in mobile and IoT SoCs.

*(7 marks: 2 for levels, 3 for WLP explanation, 2 for diagram or chip-scale remark.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Boundary Scan (JTAG – IEEE 1149.1)** technique in VLSI testing. List its four mandatory pins and state two key advantages.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Boundary Scan:**

Boundary scan is a DFT technique standardized as **IEEE 1149.1** (also known as **JTAG — Joint Test Action Group**) in 1990. The standard defines a serial test architecture embedded around the periphery of a digital IC, allowing comprehensive testing of:

- Internal logic (using scan chains)
- Interconnections between ICs on a PCB
- Cluster logic that has no scan chains

**Architecture:**

1. **TAP Controller** — A 16-state finite state machine that decodes TMS to navigate test modes.
2. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Stores the current test instruction (e.g., BYPASS, EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, INTEST).
3. **Data Registers (DR)** — Includes the **Bypass Register** and the **Boundary Scan Chain** consisting of **boundary scan cells** at every I/O pad.
4. **Four Mandatory Pins:**
   - **TDI** — Test Data In (serial input)
   - **TDO** — Test Data Out (serial output)
   - **TMS** — Test Mode Select (state control)
   - **TCK** — Test Clock (synchronizes TAP)

**Key Advantages:**

1. **Board-level interconnect testability** without physical "bed-of-nails" probing — solder joints and traces can be checked using only four pins.
2. **Built-in self-test capability** — multiple ICs on a board can be daisy-chained into a single JTAG chain, enabling factory and field diagnostics.

**Common Instructions:**
- **BYPASS** — routes TDI to TDO through a 1-bit register (used to skip a device in a chain).
- **EXTEST** — drives boundary scan cells to test board interconnects.
- **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** — snapshots normal operation data.
- **INTEST** — tests internal logic of the IC.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept, 2 for architecture, 2 for pin list, 1 for advantages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Yield with Defect Level.** Yield is a *fabrication* metric; DL is a *testing* metric. Many students use them interchangeably and lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **For Williams-Brown formula, always show the logarithm step** when the exponent is fractional. Direct calculator use without showing work attracts partial marking only.
> 3. **Package pin counts must be quoted with the standard pitch** (e.g., "QFP with 0.5 mm pitch, 100 pins"). A bare number without pitch is considered incomplete.
> 4. **JTAG pin order** is often asked: remember **TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK** — TCK is the clock, TMS controls state, and TDI/TDO are the data shift path.
> 5. **Scan chain length is always equal to the number of flip-flops** in the design — do not confuse with the gate count or the number of inputs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Packaging** is the final stage of IC manufacturing; its five purposes are **mechanical, environmental, electrical, thermal, and standardization**.
- **Standard package pitch values** to memorize: **DIP = 2.54 mm**, **SOIC = 1.27 mm**, **QFP = 0.4–0.8 mm**, **BGA = 1.0–1.27 mm**.
- **BGA** is the modern choice for high-I/O ICs because it uses an **area array** of solder balls, removing the perimeter-pin bottleneck.
- **Wire bonding** is cheap but inductive; **flip-chip (C4)** is the industry standard for high-performance and high-frequency designs.
- **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP)** integrates the package directly on the wafer — die and package are essentially the same size (**Chip-Scale Package**).
- **Yield (Y)** = good dies / total dies. **Defect Level (DL)** is governed by the **Williams-Brown formula**: $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-T)}$.
- **Single stuck-at faults** in an $n$-line circuit = $2n$.
- **Three pillars of DFT**: **Scan Chains**, **BIST (LFSR-based)**, and **Boundary Scan (JTAG – IEEE 1149.1)**.
- **JTAG** uses four mandatory pins: **TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK**.
- **LFSR maximal length** = $2^n - 1$ (used in PRPG for BIST).
- **Thermal resistance $\theta_{JA}$** is a critical package parameter; lower is better. For high-power ICs, a heatsink is mandatory.
- **Industry-grade defect limits**: Consumer < 1000 DPPM, **Automotive < 100 DPPM**, Medical < 10 DPPM.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOS Transistor Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOS Transistor Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is a four-terminal voltage-controlled semiconductor device in which the current flowing between two terminals (Source and Drain) is modulated by an electric field generated by the voltage applied to a third terminal (Gate), which is electrically isolated from the conducting channel by a thin layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂). In the context of **Digital CMOS Design (PCEVT501)**, the MOSFET is the fundamental building block of all modern digital integrated circuits (ICs).

A MOSFET is intrinsically a **symmetric device** — the Source (S) and Drain (D) terminals are physically and electrically identical in the absence of applied bias. Their naming convention is determined *dynamically* by the applied voltages: the terminal at the *lower* potential (for NMOS) is designated as the Source, while the terminal at the *higher* potential is the Drain.

### 1.2 The Four Terminals

| Terminal | Symbol | Function |
|----------|--------|----------|
| Gate | G | Controls channel formation via vertical electric field |
| Source | S | Origin of charge carriers entering the channel |
| Drain | D | Terminal where charge carriers exit the channel |
| Body (Bulk / Substrate) | B | The silicon wafer on which the device is fabricated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In digital CMOS design, the Body terminal of NMOS is tied to **GND (0 V)** and PMOS to **VDD**, so the **Body Effect is eliminated** in standard digital circuits. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

### 1.3 Two Primary Device Types

- **NMOS (n-channel MOSFET):** Built on a **p-type substrate**, uses electrons (high mobility $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ for silicon) as charge carriers.
- **PMOS (p-channel MOSFET):** Built on an **n-type substrate** (or n-well), uses holes (lower mobility $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ for silicon) as charge carriers.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Water Tap

Imagine a water pipeline carrying current. Without any control, water (current) would either flow freely or be completely blocked. A traditional tap uses a *mechanical handle* to throttle water — a **voltage-controlled** current-source uses a similar idea, but without moving parts:

- **Source ↔ Water Inlet**
- **Drain ↔ Water Outlet**
- **Gate Voltage (V_GS) ↔ Tap Handle / Wheel**
- **Channel ↔ Pipe Diameter**
- **Substrate (Body) ↔ The Building Foundation / Outer Pipe**

When you turn the handle gently (small $V_{GS}$), only a trickle flows (subthreshold / weak inversion). When you turn it past a certain point (the **threshold voltage $V_{TH}$**), a full pipe forms and water gushes through (strong inversion). The harder you turn (higher $V_{GS}$), the wider the pipe becomes, and the more current flows. This is precisely how a MOSFET works — **voltage at the gate creates an electric "field" that opens or closes a conducting channel between source and drain**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **threshold voltage $V_{TH}$** is the most critical parameter in MOSFET operation. It is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to *invert* the surface of the semiconductor beneath the gate, creating a conducting channel. For a typical **180 nm CMOS process**, $V_{TH,n} \approx 0.4 \text{ V}$ and $V_{TH,p} \approx -0.4 \text{ V}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Structure of an NMOS Transistor

The MOSFET is fabricated on a silicon substrate through a sequence of oxidation, photolithography, ion implantation, and etching steps. The layered structure from bottom to top is:

1. **p-type Silicon Substrate (Body)** — lightly doped, e.g., $\sim 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$.
2. **Two heavily doped n⁺ regions** — Source and Drain (typically $\sim 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ Arsenic or Phosphorus).
3. **Thin Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) layer** — the gate dielectric, thickness $t_{ox} \approx 1.2 \text{ nm}$ in modern 28 nm nodes.
4. **Polysilicon Gate** (or metal gate in advanced nodes) — heavily doped to act as a conductor.

The MOS acronym itself describes the sandwich: **Metal (gate) – Oxide (SiO₂) – Semiconductor (silicon channel)**.

### 2.2 Operating Regimes — The Three Modes of MOSFET Operation

The MOSFET exhibits three primary modes of operation based on the biasing conditions:

#### (A) Cut-off Region
- Condition: $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ (NMOS)
- Physical meaning: The applied gate voltage is insufficient to invert the surface. The channel has **not formed**.
- Conduction: Ideally **zero** drain current ($I_D = 0$).
- **Digital interpretation: Logic '0' state for NMOS (OFF switch).**

#### (B) Triode / Linear Region
- Condition: $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **AND** $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$
- Physical meaning: A continuous channel exists between source and drain. The channel behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** whose resistance decreases as $V_{GS}$ increases.
- **Digital interpretation: NMOS is ON, acting as a closed switch between output and GND.**

#### (C) Saturation Region
- Condition: $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **AND** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$
- Physical meaning: The channel is "pinched off" near the drain end. Current is limited by **channel pinch-off** and ideally becomes **independent of $V_{DS}$** (constant current source behavior).
- **Digital interpretation: NMOS in saturation — still ON, but acts as a current source.**

### 2.3 The Threshold Voltage Equation

The threshold voltage is a function of multiple physical parameters:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$$

Where:
- $V_{TH0}$ = Zero-bias threshold voltage (with $V_{SB} = 0$)
- $\gamma$ = **Body-effect coefficient** (typically $0.3$ to $0.7 \text{ V}^{1/2}$)
- $\phi_F$ = **Fermi potential** of the substrate, $\phi_F = \pm \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_{sub}}{n_i}\right)$
- $V_{SB}$ = Source-to-body voltage (reverse body bias)
- $N_{sub}$ = Substrate doping concentration
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ (silicon intrinsic carrier concentration at 300 K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The second term in the $V_{TH}$ equation is the **Body Effect**. In standard digital CMOS, the source and body are tied together, so $V_{SB} = 0$ and the body effect vanishes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for ESE)

| # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Condition / Region |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Cut-off current | $I_D = 0$ | $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ |
| 2 | Triode / Linear current | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ |
| 3 | Saturation current (ideal) | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ |
| 4 | With Channel Length Modulation | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation, real device |
| 5 | On-resistance in triode | $R_{on} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})}$ | Deep triode ($V_{DS} \ll V_{GS} - V_{TH}$) |
| 6 | Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Per unit area |
| 7 | Body-effect threshold | $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$ | With $V_{SB} > 0$ |
| 8 | Transconductance (saturation) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ | Derivative of $I_D$ w.r.t. $V_{GS}$ |
| 9 | Fermi potential | $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_{sub}}{n_i}\right)$ | At 300 K, $kT/q = 25.85 \text{ mV}$ |
| 10 | Aspect ratio notation | $\frac{W}{L}$ | $W$ = width, $L$ = channel length |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $\mu_n$ (or $\mu_p$) is the **surface mobility**, $C_{ox}$ is the **gate oxide capacitance per unit area** ($\text{F/cm}^2$), $W$ is the effective channel width, and $L$ is the effective channel length. Always include units in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MOSFET is the workhorse of the modern electronics industry. Its primary applications in digital CMOS design include:

- **Logic Gates:** CMOS inverters, NAND, NOR, XOR gates form the basis of every micro-processor.
- **Memory Cells:** Static RAM (SRAM) uses 6 MOSFETs per bit; DRAM uses 1 MOSFET + 1 capacitor per bit.
- **Analog Building Blocks:** Operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), mixers, voltage-controlled oscillators.
- **Power Management:** Power MOSFETs handle kiloampere currents in EV battery management, SMPS circuits, and motor drivers.
- **RF Design:** Short-channel MOSFETs act as low-noise amplifiers up to millimeter-wave frequencies in 5G transceivers.

The MOSFET is the **single most mass-produced human artifact in history** — over **$1.2 \times 10^{22}$** transistors are manufactured annually (over 100 billion per second).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Square-Law I-V Equation in Saturation

We start from the fundamental drift-current equation for an n-channel inversion layer.

**Step 1 — Inversion Layer Charge Density**

When $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, an inversion layer of electrons forms at the Si-SiO₂ interface. The sheet charge density (charge per unit area) in the channel is given by:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \right]$$

Here, $V(y)$ is the channel potential at position $y$ along the channel, measured with respect to the source. The negative sign indicates electron charge. The gate capacitance is:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

**Step 2 — Drift Current Expression**

The drain current at any point $y$ in the channel is the product of the charge per unit length, the drift velocity, and is integrated across the width $W$:

$$I_D = -W Q_n(y) v_{drift}(y)$$

The drift velocity is related to the electric field $E(y) = -dV(y)/dy$ and the electron mobility $\mu_n$:

$$v_{drift} = \mu_n E(y) = -\mu_n \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and Separating Variables**

Substituting the charge and velocity expressions into the current equation:

$$I_D = -W \cdot \left[ -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)) \right] \cdot \left[ -\mu_n \frac{dV(y)}{dy} \right]$$

Simplifying the three negatives:

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \right] \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

Rearranging so that all position-dependent terms are on the right-hand side:

$$I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

**Step 4 — Integrating Across the Channel**

Integrate the left-hand side from $y = 0$ (source) to $y = L$ (drain) and the right-hand side from $V(y) = 0$ (at source) to $V(y) = V_{DS}$ (at drain):

$$\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_{0}^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel (charge conservation):

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $I_D$ in the Triode Region**

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]}$$

This is the **triode-region current equation**, valid for $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$.

**Step 6 — Finding the Boundary Between Triode and Saturation**

Set $\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS} = 0$ to find the $V_{DS}$ at which current is maximum:

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) - V_{DS} \right] = 0$$

Solving for $V_{DS}$:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$$

**Step 7 — Deriving the Saturation Equation**

Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ back into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}$$

This is the **square-law I-V equation**, the most-used formula in digital CMOS design. The transconductance parameter is $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$, and the device transconductance parameter is $k_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Identifying the Region of Operation

Given any bias condition, the following table shows how to determine the region and the correct current equation:

| Given Bias | Inequality to Test | Region | $I_D$ Equation |
|------------|---------------------|--------|----------------|
| $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ | — | Cut-off | $I_D = 0$ |
| $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $V_{DS} - V_{GS} + V_{TH} < 0$ | Triode | Use full triode expression |
| $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Edge of saturation | Either equation valid |
| $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $V_{DS} - V_{GS} + V_{TH} > 0$ | Saturation | Use square-law |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor in a 65 nm process has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \text{ μA/V}^2$, $W = 2 \text{ μm}$, $L = 0.1 \text{ μm}$, $V_{TH} = 0.35 \text{ V}$. Determine the region of operation and the drain current for:
- (i) $V_{GS} = 0.2 \text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 1.0 \text{ V}$
- (ii) $V_{GS} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 0.2 \text{ V}$
- (iii) $V_{GS} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 1.0 \text{ V}$

**Solution (i):** Since $V_{GS} = 0.2 \text{ V} < V_{TH} = 0.35 \text{ V}$, the transistor is in **cut-off**. Therefore:

$$I_D = 0 \text{ μA}$$

**Solution (ii):** Check the overdrive voltage: $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.0 - 0.35 = 0.65 \text{ V}$. Since $V_{DS} = 0.2 \text{ V} < 0.65 \text{ V}$, the device is in the **triode region**.

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \left[ (0.65)(0.2) - \frac{(0.2)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times \left[ 0.13 - 0.02 \right]$$

$$I_D = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.11 = 0.55 \text{ mA} = 550 \text{ μA}$$

**Solution (iii):** Overdrive = $0.65 \text{ V}$. Since $V_{DS} = 1.0 \text{ V} > 0.65 \text{ V}$, the device is in **saturation**.

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times (0.65)^2$$

$$I_{D,sat} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4225 = 1.056 \text{ mA} \approx 1.06 \text{ mA}$$
