> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: The block diagrams above map the causal flow. The *Fabrication Process* injects variation in raw geometric/physical parameters ($L$, $t_{ox}$, $N_a$, $W$). These propagate into the **electrical** parameters of the transistor ($V_{th}$, $I_{on}$, $I_{off}$, $C_{ox}$). The electrical parameters then determine the **circuit-level** performance metrics (gate delay, total power, SRAM noise margin), which finally determine **die yield**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term "Process Variation" in VLSI design. Distinguish between die-to-die (D2D) and within-die (WID) variations.*

**Model Answer:**
Process variation refers to the statistical deviation in physical and electrical parameters of CMOS transistors (such as channel length $L$, oxide thickness $t_{ox}$, threshold voltage $V_{th}$, and channel doping $N_a$) from their nominal design values, caused by imperfections in the semiconductor fabrication process. These variations directly impact circuit timing, power, and yield.

**Die-to-Die (D2D) Variation**: Refers to parameter differences *between* different chips on a wafer. All transistors on a single die are assumed to shift *uniformly*. Example: one wafer is processed with slightly more implant dose, making every NMOS on every die on that wafer faster.

**Within-Die (WID) Variation**: Refers to parameter differences *within* a single chip. Transistors at different physical locations on the same die may have different parameters. Example: due to lithography or stress, the NMOS on the top-left of the die is slower than the NMOS on the bottom-right.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]**
*What is Random Dopant Fluctuation (RDF)? Why is it more critical in advanced (smaller) technology nodes?*

**Model Answer:**
Random Dopant Fluctuation (RDF) is a **stochastic, random** variation in the threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) of a MOSFET caused by the discrete, atomic nature of the dopant atoms implanted in the silicon channel. The number of dopant atoms in the channel follows Poisson statistics, so the standard deviation is $\sigma_N = \sqrt{N}$. 

It is more critical in advanced nodes because:
1. As $L$ and $W$ shrink, the total number of dopant atoms ($N$) in the channel decreases. With fewer atoms, the *relative* fluctuation ($\sigma_N / N = 1/\sqrt{N}$) becomes very large.
2. The thinner oxide ($t_{ox}$) increases $C_{ox}$, which amplifies the propagation of dopant fluctuations into $V_{th}$ shifts (as seen in the derived formula).
3. This causes severe $V_{th}$ mismatch, leading to SRAM bitcell instability and timing failures.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Equivalent Pattern]**
*Q. A (a)* Discuss in detail the different sources of process variations in deep sub-micron CMOS technology. Categorize them into systematic and random variations with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

*Q. A (b)* Derive the expression for the standard deviation of threshold voltage mismatch ($\sigma_{\Delta V_{th}}$) using Pelgrom's law. Explain its significance in analog and SRAM design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q. A (a):**

The various sources of process variations in deep sub-micron CMOS technology are categorized as follows:

**A. Systematic (Deterministic) Variations:**
These are predictable and layout-dependent.
1. **Lithography / Optical Proximity Effects (OPE)**: Light diffraction during photolithography causes printed features to differ from drawn mask patterns. Corners get rounded, line ends get shortened. Corrected using Optical Proximity Correction (OPC) and Inverse Lithography Technology (ILT).
2. **Shallow Trench Isolation (STI) Stress**: The STI oxide exerts mechanical compressive/tensile stress on the active silicon, altering carrier mobility ($\mu$) and thus $I_{on}$. This is highly layout-dependent.
3. **Well Proximity Effect**: Ions scattered during well implantation land near the mask edge, causing non-uniform channel doping near the well boundaries.
4. **Etch Micro-loading**: The plasma etch rate depends on local pattern density, causing lines in dense regions to etch differently than isolated lines.
5. **Polysilicon Gate Granularity**: The grain orientation of polysilicon affects the local work function, causing local $V_{th}$ shifts.

**B. Random (Stochastic) Variations:**
These are unpredictable and statistically distributed.
1. **Random Dopant Fluctuation (RDF)**: The dominant random effect. The number of dopant atoms in the channel is finite; the statistical variation in this number translates to $V_{th}$ variation.
2. **Line Edge Roughness (LER)**: Photoresist and etch processes leave rough gate edges. LER is quantified as the $3\sigma$ deviation of the edge from a straight line. Typical LER $\approx$ 1-2 nm.
3. **Gate Oxide Thickness Variation**: A few Angstroms of fluctuation in $t_{ox}$ exponentially affects gate leakage and $C_{ox}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q. A (b):**

The derivation steps (as in Section 3.1) yield:

$$\sigma_{\Delta V_{th}} = \frac{A_V}{\sqrt{W \cdot L}}$$

**Significance:**
- **Analog Design**: In current mirrors, differential pairs, and op-amp input stages, mismatch in $V_{th}$ directly causes input-referred offset voltage, CMRR degradation, and gain error. Designers *enlarge* the transistor area to reduce mismatch.
- **SRAM Design**: The 6T SRAM bitcell stability is highly sensitive to $V_{th}$ mismatch between the cross-coupled inverters and access transistors. A mismatch causes read-disturb failures or write failures. Pelgrom's law dictates the **minimum bitcell area** to achieve a target yield.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Equivalent Pattern]**
*Q. B (a)* What are the five standard process corners in CMOS design? Explain how they are used in Static Timing Analysis (STA) for setup and hold checks. **(7 Marks)**

*Q. B (b)* Explain the impact of process variations on threshold voltage and drive current in nanoscale MOSFETs. Discuss the role of FinFET technology in mitigating these variations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q. B (a):**

The five standard process corners model the die-to-die extremes of NMOS and PMOS transistor speeds:

| Corner | NMOS | PMOS | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TT** (Typical-Typical) | Typical | Typical | Nominal performance estimation |
| **FF** (Fast-Fast) | Fast | Fast | Worst-case dynamic power, race conditions |
| **SS** (Slow-Slow) | Slow | Slow | Worst-case setup time (longest delay) |
| **SF** (Slow NMOS, Fast PMOS) | Slow | Fast | Latch race, clock skew analysis |
| **FS** (Fast NMOS, Slow PMOS) | Fast | Slow | Latch race, clock skew analysis |

**STA Usage**:
- For **Setup Time** (max delay): Analyze at the **SS** corner (slowest transistors = longest path delay, smallest clock frequency).
- For **Hold Time** (min delay): Analyze at the **FF** corner (fastest transistors = shortest path delay, potential hold violation).
- SF and FS corners are used for **advanced node race analysis** in latches and clock paths.

---

**Model Solution for Q. B (b):**

**Impact on Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)**:
As derived in Section 3.2, the standard deviation of $V_{th}$ due to random dopant fluctuations is:

$$\sigma_{V_{th}} = \frac{q}{2 C_{ox}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s N_a}{(2\phi_F) \cdot L \cdot W}}$$

At nanoscale nodes, $L$ and $W$ decrease (denominator shrinks, so $\sigma_{V_{th}}$ grows), and $C_{ox}$ increases (numerator denominator trade-off, but the net effect is a worsening $V_{th}$ distribution). This means the $V_{th}$ of an individual transistor can no longer be treated as a deterministic value — it must be treated as a *probability distribution*.

**Impact on Drive Current ($I_{on}$)**:
Since $I_{on} \propto (V_{GS} - V_{th})^\alpha$, a shift in $V_{th}$ of just a few tens of millivolts (which is $\sim 3\sigma$ in advanced nodes) causes a significant change in $I_{on}$. The on-current can vary by $\pm 20\text{--}30\%$ across the same chip, breaking traditional corner-based STA.

**Role of FinFET / GAA**:
The FinFET (and subsequent Gate-All-Around / GAA) architecture mitigates these issues because:
1. The fin is **undoped or lightly doped** (channel is intrinsic). This virtually *eliminates* random dopant fluctuations.
2. The gate wraps around the fin, providing superior electrostatic control. The threshold voltage is set by the gate work function (metal gate) rather than by channel doping, dramatically reducing $V_{th}$ variation.
3. The fully depleted channel makes the device far more immune to short-channel effects (DIBL).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing D2D with WID**: Students often write "D2D variations occur within a single die" — this is a guaranteed zero. Remember: D2D = *between* dies, WID = *within* one die.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Pelgrom's Law**: $A_V$ has units of V·μm (or mV·μm). Writing it as just "V" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing RDF with systematic variations**: RDF is **random**, not systematic. Examiners specifically test this classification.
> 4. **Not drawing Mermaid/block diagrams in the exam**: A neat block diagram (Sources → Effects → Mitigation) guarantees full marks in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Forgetting the "FF corner = hold violation" rule**: Setup uses SS, Hold uses FF — confusing these loses the entire STA question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Process Variation = statistical deviation** in transistor parameters from nominal due to fab imperfections.
- [x] **D2D (Inter-Die)**: variation *between* chips. **WID (Intra-Die)**: variation *within* a chip. **OCV**: STA abstraction of WID.
- [x] **Systematic variations** (predictable, layout-dependent): Lithography (OPE), STI stress, well proximity, etch micro-loading, poly granularity.
- [x] **Random variations** (stochastic, unpredictable): **RDF** (dominant), LER/LWR, oxide thickness variation.
- [x] **Pelgrom's Law**: $\sigma_{\Delta V_{th}} = A_V / \sqrt{W \cdot L}$ — mismatch reduces with the **square root of area**.
- [x] **RDF formula**: $\sigma_{V_{th}} \propto \frac{q}{C_{ox}} \sqrt{\frac{N_a}{L \cdot W}}$ — dominant in bulk nanoscale CMOS.
- [x] **Process Corners (5)**: TT, FF, SS, SF, FS. **SS = Setup**, **FF = Hold**.
- [x] **LER (Line Edge Roughness)** $\approx$ 1-2 nm; its *relative* impact grows as $L$ shrinks.
- [x] **FinFET/GAA** eliminates RDF by using undoped fins and metal gate work-function engineering.
- [x] **Yield** is the integral of the performance distribution up to the specification limit; variations shrink the yield.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway**: At sub-7nm, *every* design is a statistical design — deterministic corner analysis is no longer sufficient; **SSTA (Statistical Static Timing Analysis)** and **Monte Carlo** are mandatory.
- [x] **Real-world impact**: SRAM bitcell stability, analog offset voltage, clock skew, IR-drop variability — all are manifestations of process variation.
- [x] **Mitigation techniques**: Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB), Adaptive Voltage Scaling (AVS), redundancy, error-correcting codes, larger device sizing, common-centroid layout.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Technology Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Technology Scaling** in CMOS VLSI design refers to the systematic reduction of the physical dimensions of a MOSFET (primarily channel length $L$, channel width $W$, gate oxide thickness $t_{ox}$, and junction depths) along with the corresponding adjustment of doping concentrations and supply voltages, in order to achieve higher transistor density, improved switching speed, and lower power dissipation per function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Moore's Law (1965):** The observation by Gordon Moore that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every **18 to 24 months**, driving the semiconductor industry to continuously scale down device dimensions.

The two classical scaling paradigms studied in KTU 2024 Module 1 are:

1. **Constant Electric Field Scaling (Full Scaling / Dennard Scaling, 1974)** — devised by Robert Dennard, where both dimensions *and* voltages are scaled by the same factor $S > 1$ to keep the internal electric field unchanged.
2. **Constant Voltage Scaling** — only the physical dimensions are reduced while the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ is held constant (mainly for compatibility with existing system infrastructure).

A modern performance figure of merit associated with scaling is the **CV/I metric** (also called the *intrinsic gate delay* or *switching energy per unit current*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shrinking City"

Imagine a busy city where each building (transistor) is shrunk uniformly, the roads between them are narrowed, and the traffic-signal voltages (supply voltage) are also reduced in proportion so that "crowding" inside the building remains the same. This is **Constant Field Scaling** — uniform and elegant.

Now imagine shrinking the buildings but keeping the *electrical pressure* (supply voltage) unchanged. Cramming a huge voltage into a tiny device causes internal electric fields to *spike* — wires overheat, traffic accidents (hot carriers) become frequent, and eventually the system fails. This is **Constant Voltage Scaling** — it gave us faster chips but introduced severe reliability problems (short-channel effects).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Highlight:** Real-world technology nodes since the late 1990s have followed a *hybrid* approach: dimensions scale aggressively, but $V_{DD}$ has been reduced only modestly (e.g., from **5 V → 3.3 V → 2.5 V → 1.8 V → 1.2 V → 0.9 V → 0.7 V**) because of practical circuit-design and noise-margin constraints.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Scaling Studies

- **Scaling factor $S > 1$** (typically $S = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ per technology generation).
- **Drain current** $I_D$ in **amperes (A)**.
- **Gate capacitance** $C_g$ in **farads (F)**.
- **Intrinsic delay** $t_d$ in **seconds (s)**.
- **Power dissipation** $P$ in **watts (W)**.
- **Power density** $P/A$ in **W/cm²** — Dennard's central invariant.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** in **joules (J)** — the figure of merit for switching energy.
- **Threshold voltage** $V_{th}$ in **volts (V)**.
- **Oxide capacitance per unit area** $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ in **F/m²**.
- **Subthreshold slope** $S$ in **mV/decade** (ideally ≈ **60 mV/decade** at 300 K).
- **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at 300 K (used in subthreshold analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase "**constant field**" means the longitudinal electric field $E = V_{DS}/L$ stays the same before and after scaling. This preserves the *physics* of carrier transport (velocity saturation, mobility, impact ionization), avoiding catastrophic reliability problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Scale? — The Three Engineering Drivers

- **Higher Integration Density (Cost):** Halving the linear dimension quadruples the number of devices per unit silicon area, lowering cost-per-function.
- **Higher Speed (Performance):** Intrinsic gate delay $t_d \propto C_g V_{DD}/I_D$ shrinks with scaling, enabling faster clock frequencies.
- **Lower Power per Function (Energy Efficiency):** Switching energy per gate decreases, which is critical for mobile and IoT applications.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Scaling Rules

Let $S > 1$ be the dimensionless scaling factor (e.g., $S = 1.4$ per "node").

#### 2.2.1 Constant Electric Field Scaling (Dennard, 1974)

- All linear dimensions (channel length $L$, width $W$, oxide thickness $t_{ox}$, junction depth $x_j$) are divided by $S$.
- All voltages ($V_{DD}$, $V_{th}$, all internal node voltages) are divided by $S$.
- Doping concentrations ($N_A$, $N_D$) are multiplied by $S$ — to preserve depletion widths scaled by $L/S$ and to keep threshold voltage scaled.

#### 2.2.2 Constant Voltage Scaling

- Linear dimensions divided by $S$.
- **$V_{DD}$ and $V_{th}$ remain unchanged** for system-level compatibility.
- Doping concentrations multiplied by $S^2$ — needed to maintain proper subthreshold behavior and to suppress punch-through.

### 2.3 The CV/I Performance Figure of Merit

The **CV/I** metric expresses the intrinsic switching delay of a logic gate normalized to the available current drive:

$$
\tau_{d,\text{intrinsic}} \;=\; \frac{C_g \, V_{DD}}{I_{D,\text{sat}}}
$$

For a long-channel device, $C_g = C_{ox} W L$ and $I_{D,\text{sat}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$, so:

$$
\frac{C V}{I} \;=\; \frac{2 L^2}{\mu_n (V_{GS}-V_{th})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CV/I matters:** It is *technology-independent of device width*, making it a clean way to compare different CMOS technology nodes. As $L$ shrinks, CV/I drops roughly as $L^2$, which is the fundamental speed advantage of scaling.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All cell-boundary characters are vertical bars placed using markdown-safe table syntax. No `|` is used inside mathematical expressions; the absolute-value sign is rendered as `\vert`.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Constant Field Scaling | Constant Voltage Scaling | Unit |
|---|----------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Channel length $L$ | $L / S$ | $L / S$ | m |
| 2 | Channel width $W$ | $W / S$ | $W / S$ | m |
| 3 | Gate oxide thickness $t_{ox}$ | $t_{ox} / S$ | $t_{ox} / S$ | m |
| 4 | Junction depth $x_j$ | $x_j / S$ | $x_j / S$ | m |
| 5 | Supply voltage $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD} / S$ | $V_{DD}$ (constant) | V |
| 6 | Threshold voltage $V_{th}$ | $V_{th} / S$ | $V_{th}$ (constant) | V |
| 7 | Substrate doping $N_A$ | $N_A \cdot S$ | $N_A \cdot S^2$ | cm$^{-3}$ |
| 8 | Oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox} \cdot S$ | $C_{ox} \cdot S$ | F/m² |
| 9 | Drain current $I_{D,\text{sat}}$ | $I_D / S$ | $I_D \cdot S$ | A |
| 10 | Gate capacitance $C_g = C_{ox}WL$ | $C_g / S$ | $C_g / S$ | F |
| 11 | Intrinsic delay $t_d = C_g V_{DD} / I_D$ | $t_d / S$ | $t_d / S^2$ | s |
| 12 | Power dissipation $P = C_g V_{DD}^2 f$ | $P / S^2$ | $P \cdot S$ | W |
| 13 | Power density $P / A$ | **Constant** (the Dennard invariant) | $S^3 \cdot P / A$ | W/cm² |
| 14 | Power-delay product $PDP = P \cdot t_d$ | $PDP / S^3$ | $PDP / S$ | J |
| 15 | Electric field $E = V/L$ | **Constant** (by construction) | Increases by factor $S$ | V/cm |
| 16 | Device area $A = W L$ | $A / S^2$ | $A / S^2$ | cm² |
| 17 | Current density $J = I/A$ | $I_D / S \div A/S^2 = J \cdot S$ | $I_D S \div A/S^2 = J \cdot S^3$ | A/cm² |
| 18 | Interconnect resistance per unit length | $R / S$ | $R / S$ | $\Omega$/m |
| 19 | Line capacitance per unit length | $C / S$ | $C / S$ | F/m |
| 20 | CV/I figure of merit | $CV/I \cdot 1/S$ | $CV/I \cdot 1/S^2$ | s |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundry Process Engineers** use these rules to develop new technology nodes (e.g., TSMC N3, N2).
- **Digital VLSI Designers** use the delay scaling rule to predict how a design ported from a 90 nm node to a 28 nm node will perform in terms of clock frequency and energy.
- **System Architects** use power-density invariance to plan cooling (or note its breakdown, which ended Dennard scaling around 2006).
- **Memory designers** (SRAM, DRAM) use the area scaling rule to project bit-cell size and therefore memory density.
- **Reliability Engineers** use the electric-field column to predict hot-carrier injection (HCI) and time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Field Scaling Equations

We start with the standard long-channel MOSFET saturation equations.

#### Step 1 — Oxide Capacitance Per Unit Area

The gate-oxide capacitance per unit area is the permittivity of SiO₂ divided by the oxide thickness:

$$
C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}
$$

Under full scaling, $t_{ox}$ is divided by $S$ while $\varepsilon_{ox}$ is unchanged (it is a material constant of SiO₂):

$$
C_{ox}' \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} \;=\; S \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \;=\; S \cdot C_{ox}
$$

**Interpretation:** The oxide becomes thinner by $S$, so the capacitance per unit area *increases* by a factor $S$.

#### Step 2 — Drain Current in Saturation

For a long-channel MOSFET in saturation:

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n \, C_{ox} \,\frac{W}{L} \bigl(V_{GS} - V_{th}\bigr)^2
$$

Substitute the scaled quantities (Cox × S, W/S, L/S, (VGS − Vth)/S):

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}}' \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n \,\bigl(S \, C_{ox}\bigr)\, \frac{W/S}{L/S} \,\bigl(\tfrac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{S}\bigr)^2
$$

The aspect ratio $W/L$ is unchanged, and the squared voltage term produces a $1/S^2$ factor:

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}}' \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n \,C_{ox} \,\frac{W}{L} \,\bigl(V_{GS}-V_{th}\bigr)^2 \,\cdot \frac{S}{S^2} \;=\; \frac{I_{D,\text{sat}}}{S}
$$

**Result:** Drain current scales as $1/S$ under constant-field scaling.

#### Step 3 — Gate Capacitance

$$
C_g \;=\; C_{ox}\, W\, L
$$

Substituting scaled parameters:

$$
C_g' \;=\; \bigl(S\,C_{ox}\bigr)\,\bigl(W/S\bigr)\,\bigl(L/S\bigr) \;=\; \frac{C_{ox}\,W\,L}{S} \;=\; \frac{C_g}{S}
$$

**Result:** Each gate becomes $S$ times *less* capacitive — a key contributor to faster switching.

#### Step 4 — Intrinsic Gate Delay

The first-order propagation delay through a digital gate is the time to charge/discharge the load capacitance through the device current:

$$
t_d \;=\; \frac{C_g \, V_{DD}}{I_{D,\text{sat}}}
$$

Substituting scaled values:

$$
t_d' \;=\; \frac{(C_g/S)\,(V_{DD}/S)}{I_{D,\text{sat}}/S} \;=\; \frac{C_g V_{DD}}{S \, I_{D,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{t_d}{S}
$$

**Result:** The intrinsic delay drops by $S$ — chips become $S$ times faster per generation (historically the basis of the **clock-frequency doubling** observed from 1980 to 2005).

#### Step 5 — Dynamic Power Dissipation

The switching power of a CMOS gate driving a load $C_L$ at clock frequency $f$ is:

$$
P \;=\; \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f
$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor (assume constant). Substituting the scaled parameters and noting that $f = 1/t_d$ therefore $f' = S\,f$:

$$
P' \;=\; \alpha\,(C_L/S)\,(V_{DD}/S)^2\,(S\,f) \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,C_L\,V_{DD}^{\,2}\,f}{S^2} \;=\; \frac{P}{S^2}
$$

**Result:** Dynamic power decreases as $1/S^2$ per node.

#### Step 6 — Power Density Invariance (Dennard's Key Result)

The silicon area occupied by one gate is $A = W\,L$. The scaled area is $A' = (W/S)(L/S) = A/S^2$.

$$
\frac{P'}{A'} \;=\; \frac{P/S^2}{A/S^2} \;=\; \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Result:** Power density is *exactly constant* under constant-field scaling — this is Dennard's great discovery and is why air cooling and even passive heat sinks worked for decades.

#### Step 7 — Power-Delay Product (Switching Energy)

The PDP is the energy consumed per switching event:

$$
PDP' \;=\; P' \cdot t_d' \;=\; \left(\frac{P}{S^2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{t_d}{S}\right) \;=\; \frac{PDP}{S^3}
$$

**Result:** Switching energy falls as $1/S^3$ — making each logic operation progressively cheaper.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Constant-Voltage Scaling Equations

Here $V_{DD}$ and $V_{th}$ are unchanged. To control short-channel effects, the depletion regions must remain narrow relative to the shrunken $L$, which requires *doping × $S^2$*.

#### Step 1 — Drain Current

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}}' \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n \,(S\,C_{ox})\,\frac{W/S}{L/S}\,(V_{DD}-V_{th})^2 \;=\; S \cdot I_{D,\text{sat}}
$$

The current *increases* by a factor of $S$.

#### Step 2 — Gate Delay

$$
t_d' \;=\; \frac{(C_g/S)\,V_{DD}}{S\,I_{D,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{t_d}{S^2}
$$

Delay improves by $S^2$ — even faster than constant-field scaling.

#### Step 3 — Dynamic Power

$$
P' \;=\; \alpha\,\frac{C_g}{S}\,V_{DD}^{\,2}\,\frac{S^2}{t_d} \;=\; S \cdot P
$$

Power *increases* by $S$.

#### Step 4 — Power Density

$$
\frac{P'}{A'} \;=\; \frac{S \cdot P}{A/S^2} \;=\; S^3 \cdot \frac{P}{A}
$$

Power density *explodes* as $S^3$ — the principal reason this scaling mode could not be sustained.

#### Step 5 — Power-Delay Product

$$
PDP' \;=\; P'\,t_d' \;=\; (S\,P)\,(t_d/S^2) \;=\; \frac{PDP}{S}
$$

Switching energy still drops, but only as $1/S$.

### 3.3 Summary Comparison Matrix

| Quantity | Constant Field (CF) | Constant Voltage (CV) | Verdict |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|---------|
| Delay | $t_d/S$ | $t_d/S^2$ | CV is *faster* |
| Power | $P/S^2$ | $S \cdot P$ | CF uses *less* power |
| Power density | **constant** | $\propto S^3$ | CF is thermally safe |
| Internal field | **constant** | increases $\propto S$ | CF avoids breakdown |
| Practicality | $V_{DD}$ must change | $V_{DD}$ stays put | CV is system-friendly |
| Modern use | The *intent* | Implemented with stepped $V_{DD}$ reductions | Real chips use a hybrid |

### 3.4 Subthreshold Conduction — A Hard Scaling Limit

As $L$ shrinks, the subthreshold leakage current grows dramatically. The subthreshold current is:

$$
I_{D,\text{sub}} \;=\; I_0 \, \exp\!\Bigl(\tfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n \, V_T}\Bigr) \Bigl(1 - \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

with $V_T = kT/q$. The **subthreshold slope** $S_s$ is ideally:

$$
S_s \;=\; \ln(10)\,\frac{kT}{q}\,\Bigl(1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}\Bigr) \;\approx\; 60\;\text{mV/decade} \quad \text{(at 300 K)}
$$

As $t_{ox}$ shrinks, $C_{ox}$ rises, so $S_s$ approaches 60 mV/dec — but never below. This 60 mV/decade floor is a *fundamental thermodynamic limit* and forces a minimum $V_{th} \approx 0.3$ V, in turn limiting how low $V_{DD}$ can be pushed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 60 mV/decade "Boltzmann Tyranny":** This is the *deepest physical reason* why classical Dennard scaling could not continue indefinitely, and is the source of much of the leakage power that has dominated chip design since the 90 nm node.

### 3.5 Short-Channel Effects That Arise When $L$ Becomes Small

When $L$ approaches the source/drain depletion widths, the gradual-channel approximation breaks down:

1. **Threshold Voltage Roll-off** — $V_{th}$ decreases as $L$ shrinks, due to charge-sharing between gate and S/D depletion regions.
2. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** — High $V_{DS}$ lowers the source-side barrier, further reducing $V_{th}$ and increasing leakage.
3. **Velocity Saturation** — Carriers reach $v_{sat}$ before the channel is fully depleted; current becomes less responsive to $V_{GS}$.
4. **Hot-Carrier Injection (HCI)** — High fields near the drain inject carriers into the gate oxide, causing permanent device degradation.
5. **Subthreshold Conduction & Punch-through** — Source and drain depletion regions merge, the barrier disappears, and $I_{D}$ flows even when $V_{GS}=0$.
6. **Gate-Oxide Tunneling** — When $t_{ox} < 2$ nm, electrons *tunnel* through the oxide, producing a direct gate-leakage current.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define technology scaling. State Moore's Law.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Technology scaling in CMOS VLSI design is the systematic reduction of MOSFET physical dimensions (channel length $L$, width $W$, gate-oxide thickness $t_{ox}$, junction depth $x_j$) along with proportional adjustment of doping concentrations and supply voltages, in order to achieve higher integration density, faster switching speed, and lower power dissipation per logic function.

**Moore's Law (1965):** The number of transistors per integrated circuit approximately doubles every **18 to 24 months**, while the per-transistor cost decreases. This empirical observation, articulated by Gordon Moore, has driven the semiconductor industry to scale down feature sizes at a remarkably regular cadence for nearly six decades.

---

#### **Q2. What is constant electric field scaling? Name its key features.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Constant electric field scaling (also called *full scaling* or *Dennard scaling*, 1974) is a scaling methodology in which:

- All linear dimensions of the MOSFET (channel length $L$, width $W$, gate-oxide thickness $t_{ox}$, junction depth $x_j$) are scaled by a factor $1/S$.
- All voltages (supply $V_{DD}$, threshold $V_{th}$, and internal node voltages) are also scaled by $1/S$.
- Substrate and well doping concentrations are scaled *up* by a factor $S$.

Because both the dimension *and* the voltage are reduced in the same proportion $1/S$, the internal longitudinal electric field ($E = V_{DS}/L$) remains **constant** — hence the name. The chief benefit is that **power density is preserved** ($P/A$ = constant), enabling higher integration without thermal runaway, while intrinsic delay falls as $1/S$, giving a faster circuit.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Derive the scaling relationships for the key electrical parameters of a MOSFET under **constant electric field (Dennard) scaling**. Assume the long-channel saturation-current expression and the standard gate-capacitance model. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare and contrast constant field scaling with constant voltage scaling in a tabular form. Discuss why constant field scaling eventually failed in industry. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]**

Let the scaling factor be $S > 1$. The starting equations are:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}, \quad
I_{D,\text{sat}} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2, \quad
C_g = C_{ox} W L, \quad
t_d = \frac{C_g V_{DD}}{I_{D,\text{sat}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Oxide capacitance per unit area.** Replacing $t_{ox} \to t_{ox}/S$:

$$
C_{ox}' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} = S\,C_{ox}
$$

**Step 2 — Drain current.** With $L \to L/S$, $W \to W/S$, $V_{GS}-V_{th} \to (V_{GS}-V_{th})/S$, and $C_{ox} \to S\,C_{ox}$:

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}}' = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n (S C_{ox}) \cdot \frac{W/S}{L/S} \cdot \Bigl(\frac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{S}\Bigr)^2
$$

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}}' = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 \cdot \frac{S}{S^2} = \frac{I_{D,\text{sat}}}{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Gate capacitance.**

$$
C_g' = (S C_{ox})\cdot\frac{W}{S}\cdot\frac{L}{S} = \frac{C_g}{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Intrinsic delay.**

$$
t_d' = \frac{(C_g/S)(V_{DD}/S)}{I_{D,\text{sat}}/S} = \frac{C_g V_{DD}}{S\,I_{D,\text{sat}}} = \frac{t_d}{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic power.** With switching activity $\alpha$ and frequency $f$:

$$
P' = \alpha \frac{C_g}{S}\Bigl(\frac{V_{DD}}{S}\Bigr)^2 (S f) = \frac{\alpha C_g V_{DD}^2 f}{S^2} = \frac{P}{S^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Power density invariance.** Because area scales as $1/S^2$:

$$
\frac{P'}{A'} = \frac{P/S^2}{A/S^2} = \frac{P}{A} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 7 — Power-delay product.**

$$
PDP' = P' \cdot t_d' = \frac{P}{S^2} \cdot \frac{t_d}{S} = \frac{PDP}{S^3}
$$

Hence, under constant field scaling, intrinsic delay drops by $S$, dynamic power by $S^2$, and switching energy by $S^3$, while power density is preserved.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | Constant Field | Constant Voltage |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| $L, W, t_{ox}$ | $\div S$ | $\div S$ |
| $V_{DD}, V_{th}$ | $\div S$ | unchanged |
| Doping $N_A$ | $\times S$ | $\times S^2$ |
| Delay $t_d$ | $t_d/S$ | $t_d/S^2$ |
| Dynamic power $P$ | $P/S^2$ | $S \cdot P$ |
| Power density $P/A$ | **constant** | $\propto S^3$ |
| Internal field $E$ | **constant** | $\propto S$ |
| PDP | $PDP/S^3$ | $PDP/S$ |

**Why Dennard scaling eventually failed:**

1. **Threshold-voltage scaling stalled.** Lowering $V_{th}$ exponentially increases the subthreshold leakage current ($I_{leak} \propto 10^{-V_{th}/S_s}$). The 60 mV/dec Boltzmann limit on $S_s$ forced designers to halt $V_{th}$ reduction near **0.3 V**, breaking the proportional $V_{DD}$/$V_{th}$ scaling assumption.
2. **Power density was no longer constant.** Once $V_{DD}$ could not keep falling, power density began rising per node, ending the "free lunch" of frequency scaling around 2006 — the so-called **end of Dennard scaling**.
3. **Leakage power dominated.** As $t_{ox}$ shrank below 2 nm, direct gate tunneling current rose rapidly, and subthreshold leakage increased because of DIBL and $V_{th}$ roll-off.
4. **Process variability.** Atomic-scale fluctuations in dopant placement, line-edge roughness, and oxide thickness became a significant fraction of the device dimensions, making the *continuous* "scaling factor $S$" assumption untenable.
5. **Interconnect-limited delay.** Local wires shrank in cross-section, increasing resistance; global wires did not shrink, so delay became wire-dominated rather than transistor-dominated.

Industry responded with **multi-core architectures**, **3D FinFET/GAA transistors**, and **3D stacking** — collectively referred to as the "**More than Moore**" era.

---

#### **Question B**

**(a)** With neat illustrations, explain the major **short-channel effects** that emerge as channel length $L$ is aggressively scaled. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define the **CV/I performance metric**. Show analytically how CV/I scales under (i) constant field scaling, and (ii) constant voltage scaling. Discuss its significance in modern CMOS technology. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 Marks]**

As $L$ approaches the source/drain depletion widths, the *gradual channel approximation* of long-channel MOSFET theory breaks down. The principal short-channel effects are:

1. **Threshold Voltage Roll-off.** As $L$ shrinks, the gate-induced depletion charge is no longer much larger than the source/drain depletion charge. The fraction of channel charge controlled by the S/D junctions grows, effectively *lowering $V_{th}$*. The empirical expression is:

   $$
   V_{th}(L) = V_{th,\infty} - \frac{\Delta V_{th}}{1 - e^{-L/\ell}}
   $$

   where $V_{th,\infty}$ is the long-channel value and $\ell$ is a characteristic length.

2. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL).** A high $V_{DS}$ widens the drain depletion region, which couples electrostatically to the source barrier, lowering the source-side potential and reducing $V_{th}$. DIBL is quantified in **mV/V** and grows rapidly when $L < L_{min}$.

3. **Velocity Saturation.** At high lateral fields, carrier drift velocity saturates at $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s. The current becomes $I_D \approx W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS}-V_{th})$, a *linear* (not quadratic) dependence on overdrive — reducing transconductance efficiency.

4. **Hot-Carrier Injection (HCI).** Carriers accelerated by the high field near the drain gain enough energy to be injected *into* the gate oxide or the spacer, producing a permanent shift in $V_{th}$ and device degradation over time. The "lucky-electron" model captures the rate:

   $$
   I_{inj} \propto I_D \exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{\phi_B}{q \lambda E_m}\Bigr)
   $$

   where $E_m$ is the peak channel field and $\phi_B$ is the Si–SiO₂ barrier height.

5. **Punch-through.** When the source and drain depletion regions *merge*, the potential barrier vanishes and $I_D$ flows uncontrolled even at $V_{GS}=0$. This is prevented by increasing the channel doping.

6. **Subthreshold Conduction.** Even with $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, an exponential leakage current flows. With a 60 mV/dec limit, leakage rises tenfold for every 60 mV decrease in $V_{th}$ — a critical limit on $V_{th}$ scaling.

7. **Gate-Oxide Tunneling.** When $t_{ox} < 2$ nm, electrons tunnel directly through the gate dielectric, producing a gate-leakage current that scales as $\exp(-t_{ox})$. Modern nodes use *high-κ dielectrics* (e.g., HfO₂) to thicken the physical oxide while keeping the **equivalent oxide thickness (EOT)** small.

**Mitigation strategies** include lightly-doped drains (LDD), retrograde wells, halo implants, multi-gate/MOSFET structures (FinFET, GAA), high-κ metal gates, and strained silicon channels.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]**

**Definition.** The CV/I figure of merit is the *intrinsic propagation delay* of a logic gate, obtained by dividing the total charge ($C V$) that the gate must deliver by the current ($I$) it can source. It is independent of the device width $W$ and is widely used to compare the speed potential of different CMOS technologies.

For a long-channel device:

$$
\tau_{CV/I} \;=\; \frac{C_g V_{DD}}{I_{D,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{C_{ox} W L \cdot V_{DD}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2} \;=\; \frac{2 L^2}{\mu_n (V_{GS}-V_{th})}
$$

**Result (long channel):** $\tau_{CV/I} = \dfrac{2 L^2}{\mu_n (V_{GS}-V_{th})}$

For a velocity-saturated short-channel device, $I_{D,\text{sat}} = W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS}-V_{th})$, so:

$$
\tau_{CV/I} \;=\; \frac{C_{ox} W L \cdot V_{DD}}{W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS}-V_{th})} \;=\; \frac{L\, V_{DD}}{v_{sat}(V_{GS}-V_{th})}
$$

**(i) Under Constant Field Scaling.** $L \to L/S$, $V_{DD} \to V_{DD}/S$, $V_{th} \to V_{th}/S$:

$$
\tau_{CV/I}' \;=\; \frac{(L/S)(V_{DD}/S)}{v_{sat}\bigl((V_{GS}-V_{th})/S\bigr)} \;=\; \frac{L V_{DD}}{S \, v_{sat}(V_{GS}-V_{th})} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{CV/I}}{S}
$$

So CV/I improves by a factor of $S$.

**(ii) Under Constant Voltage Scaling.** $L \to L/S$, $V_{DD}$ and $V_{th}$ unchanged:

$$
\tau_{CV/I}' \;=\; \frac{(L/S)\,V_{DD}}{v_{sat}(V_{GS}-V_{th})} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{CV/I}}{S}
$$

So CV/I *also* improves by a factor of $S$ — but note that with $V_{th}$ unchanged, the overdrive $V_{GS}-V_{th}$ is reduced by leakage constraints, and the *short-channel* model itself becomes more appropriate, slightly altering the dependence.

**Significance in modern CMOS:**

- CV/I is a *device-level* benchmark that lets foundry customers compare 28 nm vs 16 nm vs 7 nm processes without revealing proprietary details.
- The ITRS (now IRDS) used CV/I as a *technology-target* metric: each node should reduce CV/I by ~30 %, which is $\approx 1/S$ for $S = \sqrt{2}$.
- As $L$ approaches 10 nm, velocity saturation dominates, and CV/I asymptotically approaches $L/v_{sat}$ — providing a *physical floor* on the speed of any planar single-gate device. This is one reason the industry adopted **non-planar multi-gate** structures (FinFET, nanosheet/GAA) at the 22 nm node and below.
- CV/I is independent of clock frequency and load, making it a clean figure of merit for *process development*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Many students write $V_{DD}$ is scaled in *constant voltage* scaling — this is wrong. By definition, $V_{DD}$ is *constant*. The name refers to the *terminal voltage*, not the internal field.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **power-density invariance** under constant-field scaling. This single result is worth **2–3 marks** in most KTU answers and is often skipped.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up $S > 1$ (the linear scaling factor) with the *areal* density factor $S^2$. Stating that "power density falls by $S$" instead of "remains constant" is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Ignoring the **60 mV/decade Boltzmann limit** when discussing why $V_{th}$ cannot scale indefinitely. Examiners specifically look for this physical reasoning in 7-mark sub-parts.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing *intrinsic* delay $t_d$ (transistor-only) with the *loaded* gate delay (with $C_L$). The formulas in Section 2.2 use $C_g$ as the device's own gate capacitance; loaded delay would use an external $C_L$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Technology Scaling** = systematic reduction of MOSFET dimensions + adjustment of $V_{DD}$ and doping.
- **Moore's Law** ≈ transistor count doubles every 18–24 months.
- **Dennard (1974) Constant-Field Scaling:** every linear dimension $\div S$, every voltage $\div S$, doping $\times S$.
- **Constant-Voltage Scaling:** dimensions $\div S$, voltages unchanged, doping $\times S^2$.
- **Master Invariant of Dennard Scaling:** **Power density stays constant** — the cornerstone of multi-decade frequency scaling.
- **Drain current** scales as $1/S$ (CF) and $\times S$ (CV).
- **Gate capacitance** scales as $1/S$ in *both* schemes.
- **Intrinsic delay** scales as $1/S$ (CF) and $1/S^2$ (CV).
- **Dynamic power** scales as $1/S^2$ (CF) and $\times S$ (CV).
- **Power-delay product** scales as $1/S^3$ (CF) and $1/S$ (CV).
- **CV/I** is the technology-independent figure of merit; scales as $1/S$ under both methods.
- **Subthreshold slope** has a hard floor of $\approx$ **60 mV/decade** at 300 K (the "Boltzmann Tyranny") — limits $V_{th}$ scaling.
- **Short-channel effects** — $V_{th}$ roll-off, DIBL, velocity saturation, hot-carrier injection, punch-through, subthreshold leakage, gate-oxide tunneling.
- **Dennard scaling broke down around 2006** because $V_{th}$ could not keep falling, leakage power rose, and interconnects became the bottleneck.
- **Modern responses:** FinFET (22 nm and below), Gate-All-Around / nanosheet (3 nm and below), high-κ metal gates, strained silicon, multi-core architectures, 3D stacking.
- **Remember the constant $S > 1$** — never write $S < 1$ in scaling derivations; it represents the *ratio of the old to the new* dimension.
- **Be exam-careful with signs:** "scales by $S$" in scaling context usually means "multiplied by $1/S$" — the wording in KTU papers is sometimes ambiguous, so always state your convention explicitly at the top of the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static CMOS Inverter: DC Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Static CMOS Inverter: DC Characteristics # Module 2: Static CMOS Inverter — DC Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Static CMOS Inverter** is the fundamental logic gate of Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS) technology, constructed by cascading a **PMOS pull-up transistor** (source tied to V_DD) and an **NMOS pull-down transistor** (source tied to GND) such that their gates are tied together to form the input node (V_in) and their drains are tied together to form the output node (V_out). The **DC Characteristics** refer to the static, time-independent behavior of the inverter's output voltage as a function of the input voltage under DC bias conditions. This relationship is plotted as the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** curve, V_out = f(V_in). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DC characteristics establish four critical design parameters: (1) Logic Levels (V_OH, V_OL), (2) Switching Threshold (V_M), (3) Noise Margins (NM_H, NM_L), and (4) Region-wise MOSFET operating states. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the CMOS inverter as a **two-person seesaw gate**: - A heavy friend (PMOS) sits on the high side (connected to V_DD = **+V**). - A heavy friend (NMOS) sits on the low side (connected to GND = **0V**). - Both friends are tied at the middle by a single rope (the **gate input**). - When you pull the rope to **0V (logic 0)**: the NMOS friend lets go (turns OFF), the PMOS friend pulls down hard (turns ON), and the output rockers to **V_DD (logic 1)**. - When you pull the rope to **V_DD (logic 1)**: the PMOS friend lets go (turns OFF), the NMOS friend pulls down (turns ON), and the output rockers to **0V (logic 0)**. - In the middle range, both are pulling, and the output smoothly swings — this is the **transition region**. This elegant "complementary" action ensures that in steady state (logic 0 or 1), **no DC current flows** from V_DD to GND, making CMOS the most energy-efficient logic family in history. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **V_DD** | 1.2 V – 5 V | Supply voltage (modern KTU syllabus: 1.8 V / 3.3 V typical) | | **V_Tn** | 0.4 V – 0.7 V | NMOS threshold voltage | | **V_Tp** | −0.4 V to −0.7 V | PMOS threshold voltage | | **k'_n, k'_p** | Process transconductance | μ·C_ox (NMOS ≈ 2–3× PMOS due to mobility) | | **r = k_p / k_n** | 0.5 – 2 | Beta ratio of the inverter | | **T_ox, ε_ox** | Physical constants | Gate oxide thickness & permittivity | > [!NOTE] > **Static Power = 0 (ideal case):** Because one transistor is always OFF in steady state, the static power dissipation of an ideal CMOS inverter is **zero**, which is the foundational reason CMOS dominates modern VLSI design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Operating Regions of the VTC The CMOS inverter VTC is divided into **five distinct regions**, each defined by the operating state (cutoff, triode, saturation) of the NMOS and PMOS transistors: ### Region A: V_in < V_Tn (Logic LOW input) - **NMOS:** Cutoff (OFF) — I_DN = 0 - **PMOS:** Triode (linear, ON) — I_DP = maximum - **V_out:** Pulled up to V_DD → **V_OH = V_DD** - **Power:** Zero static current; node charges to V_DD through PMOS. ### Region B: V_Tn ≤ V_in < V_M (Transition starts) - **NMOS:** Saturation - **PMOS:** Triode - **V_out:** Begins to drop from V_DD; V_out > V_in. ### Region C: V_in = V_M (Switching Threshold / High-Gain Region) - **NMOS:** Saturation (just at the edge) - **PMOS:** Saturation (just at the edge) - **Both devices carry the same current:** I_DN = I_DP - **V_out = V_in = V_M** (point of unity gain crossover) - This is the **steepest** part of the VTC (high small-signal gain ≈ −(g_mn + g_mp)·(r_on ∥ r_op)). ### Region D: V_M < V_in ≤ V_DD − |V_Tp| (Transition completes) - **NMOS:** Triode - **PMOS:** Saturation - **V_out:** Continues to drop sharply; V_out < V_in. ### Region E: V_in > V_DD − |V_Tp| (Logic HIGH input) - **NMOS:** Triode (strongly ON) - **PMOS:** Cutoff (OFF) — I_DP = 0 - **V_out:** Pulled down to 0 → **V_OL = 0** > [!IMPORTANT] > The "WHY" behind the region splits: A MOSFET leaves cutoff when V_GS crosses V_T, and leaves saturation for triode when V_DS drops below V_GS − V_T. The boundary between Region B–C and Region C–D is precisely where the *saturation condition* flips between the two transistors. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Condition | |---|---|---| | 1 | $k_n = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS conduction parameter | | 2 | $k_p = \dfrac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS conduction parameter | | 3 | $r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n}$ | Beta ratio (mobility + sizing combined) | | 4 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}$ | **Switching threshold** (general case) | | 5 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | Switching threshold for **symmetric inverter** (V_Tn = \|V_Tp\|, r = 1) | | 6 | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | Sizing for symmetric VTC | | 7 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin | | 8 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin | | 9 | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{2\,V_{out} + V_{Tp} - V_{DD} + k_r V_{Tn}}{1 + k_r}$ with $k_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{r}$ | Lower transition point | | 10 | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{V_{DD} + V_{Tp} + k_r\,(2V_{out} + V_{Tn})}{1 + k_r}$ | Upper transition point | | 11 | $P_{static} = 0$ (ideal) | Static power dissipation | | 12 | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69\,C_L}{k_n (V_{DD}-V_{Tn})}$ | Propagation delay (first-order) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitution Rule:** When solving KTU problems, if μ_n ≈ 2.5 μ_p, the **default PMOS/NMOS width ratio is 2.5:1** for a symmetric inverter, so always remember $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.5$ unless otherwise stated. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The DC characteristics of the static CMOS inverter form the **cornerstone of every digital IC designed today**: - **Microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM cores):** Billions of inverters stitched into flip-flops, SRAM cells, and clock trees. - **Noise immunity** of entire SoCs is dictated by NM_H and NM_L computed from this VTC. - **Power-performance trade-off** curves used by industry tools (Cadence, Synopsys) are direct extensions of this VTC analysis. - **Mixed-signal design:** The high DC gain in Region C is exploited to build analog amplifiers (CMOS op-amps) from the same inverter topology. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold V_M The **switching threshold V_M** is the point on the VTC where **V_in = V_out**. At this point, both transistors are at the **edge of saturation**, and their drain currents must be equal: **I_DN = I_DP**. ### Step 1: Write the saturation-region drain current for each transistor For the **NMOS** in saturation, with V_GS = V_in, V_DS = V_out = V_in: $$ I_{DN} = k_n \left( V_{in} - V_{Tn} \right)^2 $$ For the **PMOS** in saturation, with V_GS = V_in − V_DD, V_DS = V_out − V_DD = V_in − V_DD. Note that V_GS − V_Tp (PMOS) becomes (V_in − V_DD − V_Tp). Since V_Tp is negative: $$ I_{DP} = k_p \left( V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp} \right)^2 $$ ### Step 2: Set the currents equal (KCL at output node) $$ k_n \left( V_{M} - V_{Tn} \right)^2 = k_p \left( V_{M} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp} \right)^2 $$ ### Step 3: Take the square root of both sides (positive root) $$ \sqrt{k_n}\, \left( V_{M} - V_{Tn} \right) = \sqrt{k_p}\, \left( V_{M} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp} \right) $$ ### Step 4: Isolate V_M $$ V_{M} \left( \sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p} \right) = \sqrt{k_n}\, V_{Tn} - \sqrt{k_p}\, \left( V_{DD} + V_{Tp} \right) $$ ### Step 5: Divide numerator and denominator by √k_n and substitute r = k_p / k_n $$ V_{M} = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}\,\left( -V_{DD} - V_{Tp} \right)}{1 + \sqrt{r}} $$ Re-arranging (using |V_Tp| = −V_Tp, so −V_DD − V_Tp = V_DD − |V_Tp|): $$ \boxed{\,V_{M} = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}\,} $$ where $r = k_p / k_n = (\mu_p W_p L_n)/(\mu_n W_n L_p)$. > [!NOTE] > **Special case:** If V_Tn = |V_Tp| and r = 1 (symmetric inverter), the equation collapses neatly to V_M = V_DD / 2. This is the KTU-favorite result. ## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Margins (NM_H and NM_L) The transition points **V_IL** and **V_IH** are defined where the **small-signal gain dV_out / dV_in = −1**. ### Step 3.2.1: Derive V_IL (lower transition point) In Region B (V_Tn ≤ V_in ≤ V_M), the NMOS is in saturation and PMOS is in triode. The PMOS drain current in triode: $$ I_{DP} = k_p \left[ 2(V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})(V_{out} - V_{DD}) - (V_{out} - V_{DD})^2 \right] $$ Setting I_DN = I_DP and differentiating dV_out/dV_in = −1 yields, after standard algebra: $$ \boxed{\,V_{IL} = \dfrac{2 V_{out} + V_{Tp} - V_{DD} + k_r V_{Tn}}{1 + k_r}\,} $$ where $k_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$. ### Step 3.2.2: Derive V_IH (upper transition point) By symmetry, swapping NMOS ↔ PMOS roles, V_DD ↔ GND, V_Tn ↔ V_Tp, V_out ↔ (V_DD − V_out): $$ \boxed{\,V_{IH} = \dfrac{V_{DD} + V_{Tp} + k_r (2 V_{out} + V_{Tn})}{1 + k_r}\,} $$ ### Step 3.2.3: Compute the noise margins $$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IH} $$ $$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{IL} - 0 = V_{IL} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Problem (KTU Practice):** A static CMOS inverter is designed with V_DD = 3.3 V, V_Tn = 0.6 V, V_Tp = −0.7 V, k_n = 50 μA/V², k_p = 20 μA/V². Compute V_M, V_IL, V_IH, and the noise margins. ### Step 1: Compute the beta ratio r $$ r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n} = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.4 $$ ### Step 2: Compute V_M $$ V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r} = \dfrac{0.6 + 0.4\,(3.3 - 0.7)}{1 + 0.4} $$ $$ V_M = \dfrac{0.6 + 0.4 \times 2.6}{1.4} = \dfrac{0.6 + 1.04}{1.4} = \dfrac{1.64}{1.4} $$ $$ \boxed{V_M \approx 1.171 \text{ V}} $$ > [!NOTE] > Since V_M < V_DD/2 = 1.65 V, the **PMOS is "weaker"** than the NMOS, which shifts the VTC curve to the right. The inverter is "NMOS-strong" — a typical scenario before sizing adjustment. ### Step 3: Compute k_r $$ k_r = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.6325} \approx 1.581 $$ ### Step 4: Compute V_IL Using V_out = V_M (approximation at unity-gain boundary) and ignoring the iterative correction: $$ V_{IL} \approx \dfrac{2(1.171) + (-0.7) - 3.3 + 1.581(0.6)}{1 + 1.581} = \dfrac{2.342 - 0.7 - 3.3 + 0.949}{2.581} $$ $$ V_{IL} = \dfrac{-0.709}{2.581} \approx -0.275 \text{ V (clamped to 0)} $$ The physical boundary is V_IL = V_Tn = 0.6 V (a more refined KTU calculation takes iterations; the conservative answer is **V_IL ≈ 0.6 V**). ### Step 5: Compute V_IH By similar procedure, **V_IH ≈ V_DD − |V_Tp| = 2.6 V** as a first-order bound. ### Step 6: Noise Margins $$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \approx 0.6 - 0 = 0.6 \text{ V} $$ $$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx 3.3 - 2.6 = 0.7 \text{ V} $$ $$ \boxed{NM_L \approx 0.6 \text{ V}, \quad NM_H \approx 0.7 \text{ V}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Both noise margins are well-defined and positive, confirming a **robust inverter design**. KTU problems typically test whether the student correctly identifies that the **summation NM_H + NM_L ≤ V_DD**, with equality only for ideal symmetric inverters. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the switching threshold V_M of a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for V_M in terms of the device parameters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The switching threshold **V_M** is the point on the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) where V_in = V_out. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS are at the **edge of saturation** and carry equal drain currents. Setting I_DN(sat) = I_DP(sat): $$ k_n (V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2 $$ Taking the positive square root and solving: $$ V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}, \quad r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n} $$ For a symmetric inverter (V_Tn = |V_Tp|, r = 1): **V_M = V_DD / 2**. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark simplification — awarding scheme as per KTU 2024 key.)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What are noise margins? Define NM_H and NM_L with the help of the VTC curve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Noise margins quantify the **immunity of a logic gate to noise** at its input. They are defined using the unity-gain points V_IL and V_IH on the VTC. $$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \quad \text{(high noise margin)} $$ $$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \quad \text{(low noise margin)} $$ A positive NM_H and NM_L ensures the gate does not misinterpret a noisy input as the opposite logic level. The summation NM_H + NM_L = V_DD only for the **ideal symmetric inverter**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a static CMOS inverter. Identify the **five regions of operation** of the VTC and tabulate the state of NMOS and PMOS in each region. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a CMOS inverter with V_DD = 2.5 V, V_Tn = 0.5 V, V_Tp = −0.6 V, k_n = 60 μA/V², k_p = 30 μA/V², compute the switching threshold V_M and the noise margins. Comment on the robustness of the design. --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** **Circuit diagram (3 marks):** - Draw V_DD at top, GND at bottom. - PMOS: source to V_DD, drain to V_out, gate to V_in (arrow inward on source). - NMOS: source to GND, drain to V_out, gate to V_in (arrow outward on source). - Label V_in at gates, V_out at common drain. **Operation (1 mark):** When V_in = 0, PMOS ON and NMOS OFF, V_out = V_DD. When V_in = V_DD, NMOS ON and PMOS OFF, V_out = 0. **Five regions table (3 marks):** | Region | V_in Range | NMOS | PMOS | V_out | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 ≤ V_in < V_Tn | Cutoff | Triode | V_DD | | 2 | V_Tn ≤ V_in < V_M | Saturation | Triode | High (decreasing) | | 3 | V_in ≈ V_M | Saturation | Saturation | V_M | | 4 | V_M < V_in ≤ V_DD − \|V_Tp\| | Triode | Saturation | Low (decreasing) | | 5 | V_in > V_DD − \|V_Tp\| | Triode | Cutoff | 0 | --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute beta ratio. $$ r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n} = \dfrac{30}{60} = 0.5 $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute V_M. $$ V_M = \dfrac{0.5 + 0.5(2.5 - 0.6)}{1 + 0.5} = \dfrac{0.5 + 0.95}{1.5} = \dfrac{1.45}{1.5} $$ $$ \boxed{V_M \approx 0.967 \text{ V}} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute V_IL and V_IH (approximate, conservative bounds). $$ V_{IL} \approx V_{Tn} = 0.5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{IH} \approx V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert = 1.9 \text{ V} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute noise margins. $$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.5 - 0 = 0.5 \text{ V} $$ $$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.9 = 0.6 \text{ V} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment. Since V_M < V_DD/2 = 1.25 V, the PMOS is **weaker** than the NMOS. Both noise margins are positive and reasonable (>20% of V_DD), so the design is **operationally robust** but can be improved by increasing the PMOS W/L ratio to push V_M toward 1.25 V (symmetric design). --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **switching threshold V_M** of a CMOS inverter. Show that for V_Tn = |V_Tp| and k_n = k_p, V_M = V_DD/2. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **noise margins** with a neat sketch of the VTC. Compute NM_H and NM_L for an inverter with V_OH = 3.3 V, V_OL = 0.1 V, V_IH = 2.4 V, V_IL = 0.9 V. State whether the design is acceptable for cascading. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** At V_M, both NMOS and PMOS are at the **saturation edge** and I_DN = I_DP. **Step 1 (2 marks):** Write saturation drain current equations. $$ I_{DN} = k_n (V_M - V_{Tn})^2, \quad I_{DP} = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2 $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Equate and take square root. $$ \sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp}) $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve for V_M. $$ V_M \left( \sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p} \right) = \sqrt{k_n} V_{Tn} - \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} + V_{Tp}) $$ $$ V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Special case V_Tn = |V_Tp| and r = k_p/k_n = 1. $$ V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} $$ Hence **V_M = V_DD/2** for the symmetric CMOS inverter. QED. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:** **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a VTC showing V_OL, V_OH, V_IL, V_IH, and the unity-gain slope points; mark NM_H and NM_L as horizontal distances on the V_in axis. **Definitions (2 marks):** - V_IL: max V_in treated as logic 0; V_IH: min V_in treated as logic 1. - NM_H: amount of noise tolerable when output is HIGH. - NM_L: amount of noise tolerable when output is LOW. **Computation (2 marks):** $$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 3.3 - 2.4 = 0.9 \text{ V} $$ $$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.9 - 0.1 = 0.8 \text{ V} $$ **Cascadability check (1 mark):** A logic gate is cascadable only if V_OH ≥ V_IH and V_OL ≤ V_IL of the next stage. - V_OH = 3.3 V ≥ V_IH = 2.4 V ✓ - V_OL = 0.1 V ≤ V_IL = 0.9 V ✓ **Both conditions hold; the design is acceptable for cascading.** Also, the total noise margin NM_H + NM_L = 1.7 V is a healthy fraction of V_DD (51.5%), indicating a robust design. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error on V_Tp:** Students frequently forget that V_Tp is **negative**, and write V_DD + V_Tp instead of V_DD − |V_Tp|. This single mistake cascades through V_M and noise margin calculations, costing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing r and 1/r:** The beta ratio r = k_p/k_n is used in V_M, but its **square root** 1/√r = k_r is used in V_IL / V_IH. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > 3. **Skipping the VTC sketch:** Even when not explicitly asked, KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** for a labelled VTC. Never describe the VTC without drawing it. > 4. **Forgetting units:** Always quote noise margins in **Volts**, and switching threshold in **Volts**. Unit-less answers are penalized. > 5. **Symmetric inverter misconception:** Symmetric VTC (V_M = V_DD/2) requires *both* V_Tn = |V_Tp| *and* r = 1. Many students only enforce one condition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static CMOS Inverter DC Characteristics** - ⭐ **Topology:** PMOS (pull-up) + NMOS (pull-down), complementary, gates tied together, drains tied together. The output node is the common drain. - ⭐ **VTC shape:** A smooth, monotonically decreasing curve from V_OH = V_DD to V_OL = 0, with a high-slope transition region centered at V_M. - ⭐ **Five regions:** - R1 — NMOS OFF, PMOS Triode → V_out = V_DD - R2 — NMOS Sat, PMOS Triode - R3 — Both Sat (the **switching point**) - R4 — NMOS Triode, PMOS Sat - R5 — NMOS Triode, PMOS OFF → V_out = 0 - ⭐ **Switching threshold formula:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}$, with r = k_p / k_n. - ⭐ **Symmetric design condition:** V_Tn = |V_Tp| AND r = 1 → V_M = V_DD/2 → requires μ_p W_p / (μ_n W_n) = 1 → since μ_n ≈ 2.5 μ_p, the W_p : W_n ratio must be ≈ **2.5 : 1**. - ⭐ **Noise margins:** NM_H = V_OH − V_IH, NM_L = V_IL − V_OL; both must be positive and ideally ≥ 10–15% of V_DD. - ⭐ **Cascadability requirement:** V_OH ≥ V_IH and V_OL ≤ V_IL of the next stage — a fundamental design rule. - ⭐ **Static power:** Zero for ideal CMOS (one transistor always OFF in steady state) — the **defining advantage** of CMOS over NMOS/PMOS-only logic. - ⭐ **V_IL and V_IH** are defined where |dV_out/dV_in| = 1 (the unity-gain points on the VTC). - ⭐ **KTU's favorite trick:** Compare the VTC of a *symmetric* inverter (V_M at centre) with a *skewed* inverter (V_M off-centre) — high-V_M designs are **NMOS-strong** (good for noise on the LOW side), low-V_M designs are **PMOS-strong** (good for noise on the HIGH side). - ⭐ **Process constants to memorize:** μ_n ≈ 2.5 μ_p (a default assumption in most KTU problems unless specified).

topics.title: Beta Ratio Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beta Ratio Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Beta Ratio (βR)** in a CMOS inverter is defined as the ratio of the transconductance gain factor of the NMOS pull-down transistor to that of the PMOS pull-up transistor:

$$\beta_R = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_n}{\mu_p C_{ox}(W/L)_p} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Beta ratio effects refer to the influence of the relative sizing (W/L) and carrier mobility (μn, μp) of NMOS and PMOS transistors on the **switching threshold (VM)**, **noise margins**, **propagation delays (τPLH, τPHL)**, and the **symmetry of the DC transfer characteristics** of a static CMOS inverter.

Where:
- **βn** = Transconductance parameter of NMOS (μn ≈ **1350 cm²/V·s** for bulk silicon)
- **βp** = Transconductance parameter of PMOS (μp ≈ **480 cm²/V·s** for bulk silicon)
- The intrinsic mobility ratio **μn/μp ≈ 2 to 3** (typically taken as 2.5 for 0.25μm technology)

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person See-Saw" Model

Imagine a see-saw (DC transfer curve) in a playground:
- The **left side of the pivot** is the **NMOS** pulling the output DOWN to ground.
- The **right side of the pivot** is the **PMOS** pulling the output UP to VDD.
- The **pivot point's height** = the **switching threshold VM**.

If the NMOS boy is **much stronger** (large βn), he can lift the PMOS easily, the pivot slides toward the VDD side, and **VM shifts UP**.
If the PMOS boy is **much stronger** (large βp), the pivot slides toward the GND side, and **VM shifts DOWN**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric Inverter:** When the two sides are perfectly balanced (βn = βp), the pivot is exactly in the middle, giving **VM = VDD/2**. This is the designer's goal for maximum noise immunity and equal rise/fall delays.

## Standard Metrics in Bold

- **βn = μn · Cox · (W/L)n** — units: A/V²
- **βp = μp · Cox · (W/L)p** — units: A/V²
- **Mobility Ratio (r) = μn/μp ≈ 2.5** (technology dependent)
- **Switching Threshold VM** — typically designed at **0.5 VDD**
- **Noise Margin Low (NML)** and **Noise Margin High (NMH)**
- **Sizing Ratio (W/L)p / (W/L)n = μn/μp ≈ 2 to 3** for symmetric inverter

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Effects of Beta Ratio

### Effect 1: Shifting the Switching Threshold (VM)

The DC switching threshold is the single most important parameter affected by βR. At VM:
- Both transistors are in **saturation** (this is the rigorous assumption used in KTU derivations).
- The drain currents are equal in magnitude: **IDN = |IDP|**.

### Effect 2: Asymmetric Propagation Delays

| Transition | Driven by | Time Constant |
|------------|-----------|---------------|
| High-to-Low (Fall) | NMOS strength (βn) | τPHL ∝ Cload / βn |
| Low-to-High (Rise) | PMOS strength (βp) | τPLH ∝ Cload / βp |

A **small βR (βn << βp)** means a very strong PMOS → fast rise, slow fall.
A **large βR (βn >> βp)** means a very strong NMOS → fast fall, slow rise.

### Effect 3: Noise Margin Asymmetry

- **Increasing βR** → VM shifts UP → NML decreases, NMH increases.
- **Decreasing βR** → VM shifts DOWN → NML increases, NMH decreases.
- The design point **VM = VDD/2** is optimal because it **maximizes the minimum of (NML, NMH)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In standard cell libraries, the beta ratio is fixed by the cell's W/L ratios. Inverter cells are often characterized at βR = 1 (matched drive), but buffer chains use progressively increasing βR to drive large capacitive loads (clock trees, output pads) while balancing rise/fall times.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\beta_n = \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance (A/V²) |
| 2 | $\beta_p = \mu_p C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance (A/V²) |
| 3 | $\beta_R = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}\cdot\frac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p}$ | Definition of Beta Ratio |
| 4 | $V_M = \frac{V_{TN}\sqrt{\beta_R} + (V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1+\sqrt{\beta_R}}$ | Switching threshold (general form) |
| 5 | $V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | When $\beta_R = 1$ and $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$ (symmetric case) |
| 6 | $\tau_{PHL} = \frac{C_{load}}{\beta_n(V_{DD}-V_{TN})}$ | Fall delay (NMOS drives) |
| 7 | $\tau_{PLH} = \frac{C_{load}}{\beta_p(V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)}$ | Rise delay (PMOS drives) |
| 8 | $\frac{\tau_{PLH}}{\tau_{PHL}} = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \beta_R$ | Delay ratio (assuming $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$) |
| 9 | $\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | Sizing rule for symmetric inverter (βR = 1) |
| 10 | $r = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2.5$ | Intrinsic mobility ratio (bulk Si) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** All absolute value notations use `\vert` syntax to preserve markdown table integrity. The square-root terms in equation 4 must be evaluated carefully — the KTU board expects **positive real roots only** since VM is a physical voltage between 0 and VDD.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold VM in Terms of βR

### Setup

Consider a static CMOS inverter with:
- VDD supply, VSS = 0
- NMOS threshold = VTN
- PMOS threshold = VTP (negative value, magnitude $\vert V_{TP}\vert$)
- Output voltage = Vin = VM (definition of switching threshold)

At VM, both transistors are assumed to be in **saturation** (this is the KTU-accepted simplification).

### Step 1: Write the drain current equations

For NMOS in saturation:

$$I_{DN} = \frac{\beta_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2$$

For PMOS in saturation (VGS,p = Vin - VDD = VM - VDD; VSG,p = VDD - VM):

$$I_{DP} = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

### Step 2: Apply the current continuity condition

At the switching threshold, the current flowing into the NMOS equals the current flowing out of the PMOS (no DC path to ground in steady state). Thus:

$$I_{DN} = I_{DP}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\beta_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

The factor of 1/2 cancels on both sides:

$$\beta_n(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \beta_p(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

### Step 3: Take the square root of both sides

Since both bracketed terms are positive at VM (between VTN and VDD - $\vert V_{TP}\vert$):

$$\sqrt{\beta_n}(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{\beta_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

### Step 4: Expand and group terms containing VM

Left side expansion:

$$\sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_M - \sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_{TN}$$

Right side expansion:

$$\sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot V_M - \sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot \vert V_{TP}\vert$$

### Step 5: Collect VM terms on the left side

$$\sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_M + \sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot V_M = \sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{\beta_p}\cdot \vert V_{TP}\vert + \sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_{TN}$$

Factor out VM:

$$V_M(\sqrt{\beta_n} + \sqrt{\beta_p}) = \sqrt{\beta_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_{TN}$$

### Step 6: Solve for VM

$$V_M = \frac{\sqrt{\beta_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{\beta_n}\cdot V_{TN}}{\sqrt{\beta_n} + \sqrt{\beta_p}}$$

### Step 7: Introduce the Beta Ratio βR

Divide numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{\beta_p}$:

$$V_M = \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{\beta_n/\beta_p}\cdot V_{TN}}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_n/\beta_p}}$$

Recognize $\sqrt{\beta_n/\beta_p} = \sqrt{\beta_R}$:

$$\boxed{\,V_M = \frac{\sqrt{\beta_R}\cdot V_{TN} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}}\,}$$

### Step 8: Special case — Symmetric Inverter (βR = 1, VTN = $\vert V_{TP}\vert$)

When $\beta_R = 1$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert}{2}$$

If additionally $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + V_{DD} - V_{TN}}{2} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU result**: a symmetric inverter (matched strengths and thresholds) has its switching threshold exactly at the **mid-rail point**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Delay Ratio

Using the standard first-order RC delay model for CMOS:

$$\tau_{PHL} = \frac{C_{load}\cdot \ln 2}{\beta_n(V_{DD}-V_{TN})} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_{PLH} = \frac{C_{load}\cdot \ln 2}{\beta_p(V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)}$$

Assuming $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$ for simplicity (KTU frequent assumption):

$$\frac{\tau_{PLH}}{\tau_{PHL}} = \frac{\beta_n(V_{DD}-V_{TN})}{\beta_p(V_{DD}-V_{TN})} = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \beta_R$$

This proves that **the ratio of rise-to-fall delay is directly equal to the beta ratio** — a high-yield KTU exam point.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: βR Effect Summary

| βR Value | NMOS vs PMOS | VM Position | τPLH vs τPHL | Best Use Case |
|----------|---------------|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| βR < 1 (e.g., 0.25) | PMOS stronger | VM < VDD/2 | Slow fall, fast rise | Rare; pull-up dominant |
| **βR = 1 (matched)** | Balanced | **VM = VDD/2** | **Equal rise/fall** | **Standard logic gates** |
| βR = 2 to 3 (typical) | NMOS slightly stronger | VM slightly > VDD/2 | τPLH ≈ τPHL | Practical symmetric design |
| βR >> 1 (e.g., 10) | NMOS dominant | VM ≈ VDD - $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ | Fast fall, slow rise | Clock buffers, large fan-out |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The CMOS Inverter Under Study

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Voltage Vin] --> B[NMOS Pull-Down Network]
    A --> C[PMOS Pull-Up Network]
    B --> D[Output Node Vout]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Load Capacitance Cload]
    B -- beta_n --> F[Beta Ratio Controller]
    C -- beta_p --> F
    F --> G[Switching Threshold VM]
    F --> H[Noise Margins NML NMH]
    F --> I[Propagation Delays tauPLH tauPHL]
    G --> J[DC Transfer Characteristic]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: How Beta Ratio Determines Inverter Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Define Sizing Ratios W over L of N and P] --> B[Compute Beta Ratio betaR = beta_n over beta_p]
    B --> C{Is betaR equal to 1?}
    C -- Yes --> D[VM equals VDD divided by 2]
    C -- No --> E[VM shifts above or below VDD by 2]
    D --> F[Symmetric Noise Margins]
    D --> G[Equal Rise and Fall Delays]
    E --> H[Asymmetric Noise Margins]
    E --> I[Skewed Delays tauPLH or tauPHL dominant]
    F --> J[Optimal Static CMOS Inverter]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Application Specific Inverter]
    I --> K
```

## 4.3 Effect Mapping Diagram: Cause-Effect Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Increase PMOS Width Wp] --> P2[Increase beta_p]
    P2 --> P3[Decrease betaR]
    P3 --> P4[VM shifts downward toward GND]
    P3 --> P5[tauPLH decreases faster rise time]
    P3 --> P6[NMH decreases, NML increases]
    
    N1[Increase NMOS Width Wn] --> N2[Increase beta_n]
    N2 --> N3[Increase betaR]
    N3 --> N4[VM shifts upward toward VDD]
    N3 --> N5[tauPHL decreases faster fall time]
    N3 --> N6[NMH increases, NML decreases]
```

## 4.4 Symbolic Schematic of the CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD1[VDD Supply] --> PU[PMOS Transistor Wp over Lp]
    PU --> OUT[Output Node Vout]
    IN[Input Vin] --> PU
    IN --> PD[NMOS Transistor Wn over Ln]
    PD --> VSS1[VSS Ground]
    OUT --> CAP[Load Capacitor Cload]
    OUT --> NEXT[Next Stage Input]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the term 'Beta Ratio' in a CMOS inverter. State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Beta Ratio (βR)** of a CMOS inverter is defined as the ratio of the transconductance parameter of the NMOS pull-down transistor to that of the PMOS pull-up transistor:

$$\beta_R = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{\mu_n(W/L)_n}{\mu_p(W/L)_p}$$

**Significance (any two for full marks):**
1. It determines the **switching threshold VM** of the inverter (shifts VM up if βR > 1, down if βR < 1).
2. It governs the **relative propagation delays** τPLH and τPHL — equal delays are obtained when βR = 1.
3. It controls the **symmetry of the noise margins** (NML and NMH).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is the condition for a symmetric CMOS inverter? Why is the switching threshold set to VDD/2?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **symmetric CMOS inverter** satisfies two conditions:
1. **Equal drive strengths:** βn = βp, which gives $\beta_R = 1$, and therefore $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p)(W/L)_n \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \times (W/L)_n$.
2. **Equal threshold magnitudes:** $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$.

**Why VM = VDD/2 is desired:**
Setting the switching threshold at **VDD/2** maximizes the **smallest of the two noise margins** (NML and NMH), thereby providing **maximum noise immunity** against both low-going and high-going noise disturbances on the input. It also gives **equal rise and fall propagation delays**, which is critical for high-speed digital operation.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for the switching threshold VM of a CMOS inverter in terms of the beta ratio βR. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a CMOS inverter with VDD = 3.3 V, VTN = 0.5 V, $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ = 0.6 V, μn/μp = 2.5, (W/L)n = 2, and (W/L)p = 5, calculate: (i) the beta ratio, (ii) the switching threshold VM, and (iii) comment on the noise margin symmetry. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The body effect is neglected — VSB = 0 for both transistors.
2. Both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation** at the switching point.
3. Channel length modulation (λ = 0) is neglected.
4. The inverter is driving no DC current in steady state, so IDN = IDP at VM.

**Derivation** (refer to detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above):

Starting from the saturation current equality:

$$\frac{\beta_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

Taking square roots and rearranging gives the final boxed result:

$$V_M = \frac{\sqrt{\beta_R}\cdot V_{TN} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}}$$

where $\beta_R = \beta_n/\beta_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p)\cdot[(W/L)_n/(W/L)_p]$.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** VDD = 3.3 V, VTN = 0.5 V, $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ = 0.6 V, μn/μp = 2.5, (W/L)n = 2, (W/L)p = 5.

**(i) Beta Ratio Calculation:**

$$\beta_R = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p} = 2.5 \times \frac{2}{5} = 2.5 \times 0.4 = 1.0$$

So **βR = 1.0**.

**(ii) Switching Threshold Calculation:**

Since βR = 1, $\sqrt{\beta_R} = 1$:

$$V_M = \frac{(1.0)(0.5) + (3.3 - 0.6)}{1 + 1.0} = \frac{0.5 + 2.7}{2.0} = \frac{3.2}{2.0}$$

$$\boxed{V_M = 1.6 \text{ V}}$$

As a sanity check, VDD/2 = 1.65 V. Since VTN ≠ $\vert V_{TP}\vert$, VM is slightly below VDD/2 (the PMOS appears slightly stronger because $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ is larger than VTN).

**(iii) Noise Margin Comment:**

With VM = 1.6 V (slightly below the ideal 1.65 V), the transfer curve is **nearly symmetric** because βR = 1 ensures equal drive strengths. The **NML will be slightly larger than NMH** because VM is below the mid-rail. The inverter is well-suited for general-purpose logic with **balanced noise immunity** on both sides.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the effect of beta ratio on the propagation delays τPLH and τPHL of a CMOS inverter. (7 marks)**

**(b) A CMOS inverter is to be designed with τPLH = τPHL = 50 ps when driving a load of 100 fF at VDD = 2.5 V. Given μn/μp = 2.5, VTN = $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ = 0.4 V, and (W/L)n = 1, find the required (W/L)p. (Assume the simplified delay formula τ = Cload·0.69 / [β·(VDD - VT)].) (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Effect of Beta Ratio on Delays:**

The propagation delay through a CMOS inverter is **strongly asymmetric** because the NMOS and PMOS transistors have **different carrier mobilities** (μn > μp). Using the simplified delay model:

$$\tau_{PHL} = \frac{0.69 \cdot C_{load}}{\beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})} \quad \text{(fall time, NMOS drives)}$$

$$\tau_{PLH} = \frac{0.69 \cdot C_{load}}{\beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)} \quad \text{(rise time, PMOS drives)}$$

**Three key observations:**

1. **τPHL is inversely proportional to βn** (NMOS strength). A larger (W/L)n reduces the fall time.
2. **τPLH is inversely proportional to βp** (PMOS strength). A larger (W/L)p reduces the rise time.
3. The **delay ratio** is:

$$\frac{\tau_{PLH}}{\tau_{PHL}} = \frac{\beta_n(V_{DD}-V_{TN})}{\beta_p(V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)} = \beta_R \quad \text{(when } V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert \text{)}$$

**Design implications:**
