- For **equal rise/fall times** (τPLH = τPHL), we need **βR = 1**, which requires $(W/L)_p/ (W/L)_n = \mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.5$.
- If βR > 1 (NMOS stronger), the **fall is fast** but the **rise is slow** — this is the typical case in poorly designed inverters.
- If βR < 1 (PMOS stronger), the **rise is fast** but the **fall is slow**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.69** in the delay formula. The exact discharge equation gives $\tau = C \cdot V / I$, and the 50% point of an exponential corresponds to **ln(2) ≈ 0.69**, not 1.0. Marks are deducted for using τ = C·VDD/[β(VDD-VT)²] incorrectly.
> 2. **Confusing τPHL and τPLH** — the subscripts H and L refer to the **output**, not the input. τPHL = output going from **H**igh to **L**ow.
> 3. **Mixing up the mobility ratio** — students often write (W/L)p = (W/L)n instead of $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p)(W/L)_n$.
> 4. **Ignoring the body effect** for the PMOS source — in a real inverter, the PMOS source is tied to VDD, so VSB = 0, but the NMOS source is at ground, so this assumption is valid. State this clearly.
> 5. **Algebraic sign errors** in the VM derivation — remember that for PMOS, $V_{GS} = V_{in} - V_{DD}$ is negative, and $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ is positive.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** τPLH = τPHL = 50 ps, Cload = 100 fF, VDD = 2.5 V, μn/μp = 2.5, VTN = $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ = 0.4 V, (W/L)n = 1.

**Step 1: Write the delay equation for τPHL (NMOS drives):**

$$50 \times 10^{-12} = \frac{0.69 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}}{\beta_n (2.5 - 0.4)}$$

Solve for βn:

$$\beta_n = \frac{0.69 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}}{50 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.1} = \frac{69 \times 10^{-15}}{105 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.657 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/V}^2$$

$$\beta_n \approx 657 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the overdrive and effective mobility:**

$$\beta_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n \implies \mu_n C_{ox} = \frac{\beta_n}{(W/L)_n} = \frac{657 \text{ μA/V}^2}{1} = 657 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

**Step 3: Use the mobility ratio to find μpCox:**

$$\mu_p C_{ox} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2.5} = \frac{657}{2.5} \approx 263 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply the equal-delay condition (βn = βp, since VTN = $\vert V_{TP}\vert$):**

For equal rise and fall delays, we need:

$$\beta_p = \beta_n = 657 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

**Step 5: Solve for (W/L)p:**

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\beta_p}{\mu_p C_{ox}} = \frac{657}{263} = 2.5$$

$$\boxed{\,(W/L)_p = 2.5\,}$$

This confirms the fundamental design rule: **the PMOS must be sized 2.5× larger than the NMOS** to achieve equal rise and fall times in a 2.5 V symmetric CMOS inverter.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Beta Ratio Definition:** $\beta_R = \beta_n/\beta_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p) \cdot [(W/L)_n/(W/L)_p]$ — always write the full expression in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Switching Threshold Formula:** $V_M = [\sqrt{\beta_R} \cdot V_{TN} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)] / [1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}]$ — must be derived from $I_{DN} = I_{DP}$ with both transistors in saturation.
- [ ] **Symmetric Inverter Condition:** βR = 1 AND $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$ gives $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- [ ] **Standard Mobility Ratio:** μn/μp ≈ 2 to 3 (use **2.5** unless otherwise specified).
- [ ] **Sizing Rule for Symmetry:** $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p)(W/L)_n \approx 2.5 \cdot (W/L)_n$.
- [ ] **Delay Asymmetry:** τPLH/τPHL = βR (when thresholds are equal).
- [ ] **High βR** → VM shifts UP → fast fall, slow rise, large NMH, small NML.
- [ ] **Low βR** → VM shifts DOWN → slow fall, fast rise, large NML, small NMH.
- [ ] **Optimal Design Point:** βR = 1, VM = VDD/2 → maximum balanced noise immunity.
- [ ] **Assumptions to Always State:** saturation at VM, no body effect, no channel length modulation, steady-state (no DC current).
- [ ] **Frequent Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the mobility ratio μn/μp with the sizing ratio (W/L)p/(W/L)n — they are inversely related.
- [ ] **Practical Use:** Clock buffers, I/O pads, and tapered buffers use **progressively increasing** βR to drive large loads efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Margin
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noise Margin

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In CMOS digital VLSI design, **Noise Margin (NM)** is defined as the maximum amount of extraneous (unwanted) voltage that can be superimposed on the worst-case input signal of a logic gate without causing the output to deviate from its specified logic level or flip to the wrong state. It is the quantitative figure-of-merit that measures the **noise immunity** of a digital logic family.

A CMOS inverter's Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) is partitioned into four critical DC operating points:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output High Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | The minimum high output voltage the gate guarantees in steady state. |
| Output Low Voltage | $V_{OL}$ | The maximum low output voltage the gate guarantees in steady state. |
| Input High Voltage | $V_{IH}$ | The smallest input voltage that is still recognized as a valid logic `1`. |
| Input Low Voltage | $V_{IL}$ | The largest input voltage that is still recognized as a valid logic `0`. |

The two noise margins are mathematically defined as:

$$NM_{H} \;=\; V_{OH} \;-\; V_{IH}$$

$$NM_{L} \;=\; V_{IL} \;-\; V_{OL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *Noise margin is the difference between the guaranteed output voltage level of a gate (driver) and the minimum input voltage level required by the next stage (receiver) for proper logic recognition. It represents the safe DC noise budget allowed in cascade.*

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine two friends standing far apart in a noisy marketplace trying to communicate using hand signals:

- The **sender (driver)** raises a red flag to mean **"STOP"** and a green flag to mean **"GO"**.
- The **receiver** has slightly imperfect eyesight; he can confidently recognize the red flag only if it is raised **at least 90° high**, and the green flag **only if it is raised at least 30° high**.
- Any wind gust (noise) below these thresholds will not fool the receiver into a wrong interpretation.

In this analogy:
- $V_{OH}$ = The height at which the sender actually raises the green flag (e.g., 100°).
- $V_{IH}$ = The minimum height required by the receiver to confirm "green" (e.g., 30°).
- $NM_H = 100° - 30° = 70°$ → the **extra tolerance** before a wrong decision is made.

The larger the noise margin, the more robust the communication chain against random disturbances.

> [!NOTE]
> The four voltages ($V_{OH}, V_{IH}, V_{IL}, V_{OL}$) are **not arbitrary** — $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$ are measured on the **output axis** of the VTC, while $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ are measured on the **input axis** at the points where the slope of the VTC equals **−1** (the unity-gain points).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Regions Behind the Four Key Voltages

The CMOS inverter VTC traverses five distinct regions as $V_{in}$ sweeps from 0 to $V_{DD}$:

| Region | NMOS State | PMOS State | $V_{out}$ Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I (0 → $V_{IL}$) | Cutoff | Linear/Triode | $V_{out} \approx V_{DD}$ |
| II ($V_{IL}$ → $V_M$) | Saturation | Triode | Falling steeply |
| III (around $V_M$) | Saturation | Saturation | Sharp transition |
| IV ($V_M$ → $V_{IH}$) | Triode | Saturation | Falling steeply |
| V ($V_{IH}$ → $V_{DD}$) | Linear | Cutoff | $V_{out} \approx 0$ |

$V_M$ is the **switching threshold** (logic threshold), found by equating the NMOS and PMOS saturation currents:

$$V_{M} \;=\; \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

### 2.2 Why $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ Are Defined at Slope = −1

The boundary between reliable logic detection and the high-gain transition zone is set by the **unity-gain criterion**. Beyond these points (closer to $V_M$), the gain $\vert A_v \vert > 1$ amplifies small noise spikes dangerously; inside them (near 0 or $V_{DD}$), the gain is much less than 1 and small noise is rejected. The two noise margins together define the largest **"forbidden" rectangular region** in the VTC where the gate behaviour is ambiguous.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume a **symmetric CMOS inverter** unless stated otherwise: $\beta_n = \beta_p$ and $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP} \vert = V_T$.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Switching threshold | $V_{M} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ (symmetric) | Point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ |
| 2 | Ideal $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Output high (NMOS off, PMOS on) |
| 3 | Ideal $V_{OL}$ | $V_{OL} = 0$ | Output low (NMOS on, PMOS off) |
| 4 | Low-noise margin | $NM_{L} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Tolerable noise on a `0` level |
| 5 | High-noise margin | $NM_{H} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Tolerable noise on a `1` level |
| 6 | Input low voltage | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$ | Unity-gain point on falling edge |
| 7 | Input high voltage | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}$ | Unity-gain point on falling edge (mirror) |
| 8 | Symmetric NM | $NM_{H} = NM_{L} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$ | When $r = \beta_n/\beta_p = 1$ |
| 9 | Asymmetric NM (general) | $NM_{L} = \dfrac{(3V_{DD} + 2V_T)}{(1+r)(\text{...})}$ — see derivation | When $r \neq 1$ |
| 10 | DC noise immunity | $N.I. = \dfrac{NM_{L} + NM_{H}}{V_{DD}}$ (unitless) | Normalized noise budget |

> [!NOTE]
> For asymmetric designs where $\beta_n / \beta_p = r \neq 1$, the full expressions derived from cross-coupled analysis become more involved. KTU examiners typically expect the **symmetric-case formulas (rows 6–8)** unless explicitly specified.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chip-to-chip communication**: Buses that run long traces on PCBs are exposed to crosstalk, ground bounce, and EMI. Higher noise margins allow the system to tolerate these disturbances without re-driving signals.
- **Sub-threshold / low-$V_{DD}$ operation**: As $V_{DD}$ scales below 1 V for IoT and energy-harvesting devices, the noise margin shrinks dramatically, forcing designers to use **multi-threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)** or **schmitt-trigger** buffers to recover it.
- **Radiation-hardened design**: Aerospace circuits (satellites, nuclear instrumentation) must survive single-event transients. Larger noise margins translate directly to lower soft-error rates.
- **PVT corner testing**: Noise margin is a corner-dependent specification — worst-case (slow NMOS, fast PMOS, high $V_T$) is what guarantees correct operation across all silicon dies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $V_{IL}$ (Symmetric CMOS Inverter)

In the region just **below** the switching threshold, the NMOS operates in **saturation** while the PMOS operates in the **triode (linear)** region. Setting the current through both transistors equal ($I_{DN} = I_{DP}$):

$$I_{DN} \;=\; \frac{\beta_n}{2}\,(V_{in} - V_T)^{2}$$

$$I_{DP} \;=\; \beta_p\!\left[(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_T)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Equating and setting $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$, then solving for $V_{out}$ as a function of $V_{in}$ yields the VTC equation valid in this region:

$$V_{out} \;=\; (V_{in} - V_T)\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\beta_n}{\beta_p}\!\left[\frac{V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_T}{(V_{in} - V_T)} - \frac{1}{2}\right]}$$

For the symmetric case, this simplifies considerably. To find $V_{IL}$, we differentiate $V_{out}$ with respect to $V_{in}$ and impose the **unity-gain condition**:

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \;=\; -1$$

After substituting and simplifying (a long but standard textbook derivation), we arrive at the closed-form result for the symmetric inverter:

$$\boxed{\,V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{3\,V_{DD} + 2\,V_T}{8}\,}$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Expansion of the $V_{IL}$ Result

Starting from the differential equation obtained by applying $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ to the VTC expression and after clearing all fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } r = 1, \quad V_T = V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert. \\
&\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{quadratic in } V_{in} \\
&8\,V_{in}^{2} \;+\; \bigl(4V_T - 6V_{DD}\bigr)\,V_{in} \;+\; \bigl(2V_{DD} - 2V_T\bigr)^{2}/\,\bigl(\text{...}\bigr) \;=\; 0 \\
&\text{Select the smaller root (valid in NMOS-saturation region):} \\
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{(6V_{DD} - 4V_T) - \sqrt{(6V_{DD} - 4V_T)^{2} - 32(V_{DD} - V_T)^{2}/2}}{16} \\
&\phantom{V_{IL}} \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} \quad \text{(simplified, symmetric case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of $V_{IH}$

By exploiting the **mirror symmetry** of the symmetric VTC about the point $(V_M, V_M)$, $V_{IH}$ can be obtained directly from $V_{IL}$ by substituting $V_{in}' = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ and $V_{out}' = V_{DD} - V_{out}$. This symmetry gives:

$$\boxed{\,V_{IH} \;=\; V_{DD} - V_{IL} \;=\; V_{DD} - \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} \;=\; \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}\,}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of $NM_H$ and $NM_L$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_{H} \;=\; V_{OH} - V_{IH} \;=\; V_{DD} - \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8} \;=\; \frac{8V_{DD} - 5V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} \\
\\
NM_{L} \;=\; V_{IL} - V_{OL} \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} - 0 \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the **beautiful symmetry result**:

$$NM_{H} \;=\; NM_{L} \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $V_T = 0.5\,\text{V}$, symmetric CMOS inverter.
> **Find:** $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$, $NM_L$, noise immunity percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{3(3.3) + 2(0.5)}{8} \;=\; \frac{9.9 + 1.0}{8} \;=\; \frac{10.9}{8} \;=\; 1.3625\,\text{V} \\
&V_{IH} \;=\; \frac{5(3.3) - 2(0.5)}{8} \;=\; \frac{16.5 - 1.0}{8} \;=\; \frac{15.5}{8} \;=\; 1.9375\,\text{V} \\
&NM_{H} \;=\; 3.3 - 1.9375 \;=\; 1.3625\,\text{V} \\
&NM_{L} \;=\; 1.3625 - 0 \;=\; 1.3625\,\text{V} \\
&N.I.\% \;=\; \frac{NM_H + NM_L}{2 V_{DD}}\times 100 \;=\; \frac{1.3625 + 1.3625}{2(3.3)}\times 100 \;\approx\; 41.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently uses $V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_T = 0.8\,\text{V}$ for hand-calculations to keep the numbers clean. Memorize the substitution: $V_{IL} = \dfrac{15 + 1.6}{8} = 2.075\,\text{V}$ and $V_{IH} = \dfrac{25 - 1.6}{8} = 2.925\,\text{V}$.

### 3.6 Cross-Coupled Inverter Method (Alternative — KTU Optional)

Some KTU questions ask you to find the noise margins by **graphically plotting the VTC of two inverters head-to-tail** (one normal, one inverted) on the same axes. The intersections with the line $V_{in} = V_{out}$ give the equilibrium points, and the unity-gain intersections define the four voltages. This is the **"butterfly curve"** method.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Noise Margin Identification

Because Mermaid cannot render continuous mathematical plots, the diagram below maps the **logical regions and parameters** that define noise margin on the CMOS VTC. Treat it as a labeled schematic of the VTC curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Vin Sweep from 0 to VDD]) --> regionA
    regionA["REGION I  Cutoff NMOS  Triode PMOS  Vout near VDD  VOH region"]
    regionA --> p1{{"VOH   output high guarantee  point on Y axis"}}
    regionA --> regionB["REGION II  NMOS Saturation  PMOS Triode  Falling steeply"]
    regionB --> p2{{"VIL   unity gain point  slope equals minus one"}}
    regionB --> regionC["REGION III  Both in Saturation  Sharp transition  VM at centre"]
    regionC --> p3{{"VM   switching threshold  Vin equals Vout"}}
    regionC --> regionD["REGION IV  NMOS Triode  PMOS Saturation  Falling steeply"]
    regionD --> p4{{"VIH   unity gain point  slope equals minus one"}}
    regionD --> regionE["REGION V  NMOS Triode  PMOS Cutoff  Vout near zero  VOL region"]
    regionE --> p5{{"VOL   output low guarantee  point on Y axis"}}

    p1 -. "NMH equals VOH minus VIH  HIGH NOISE MARGIN" .-> p4
    p2 -. "NML equals VIL minus VOL  LOW NOISE MARGIN" .-> p5

    subgraph SAFE["SAFE OPERATING RECTANGLE"]
        p1
        p2
        p4
        p5
    end

    subgraph FORBIDDEN["FORBIDDEN AMBIGUITY REGION  between VIL and VIH"]
        p3
    end

    style SAFE fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style FORBIDDEN fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    style p3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Functional Relationship Between VTC Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VOH at top of VTC] -->|"subtract VIH"| B[NMH value]
    C[VIH on steep edge] --> B
    D[VIL on steep edge] -->|"subtract VOL"| E[NML value]
    F[VOL at bottom of VTC] --> E
    B --> G["Noise Immunity  NMH plus NML divided by VDD"]
    E --> G
    G --> H{"Both NMH and NML greater than VDD divided by 6"}
    H -->|"Yes"| I["Logic family is ROBUST"]
    H -->|"No"| J["Logic family is FRAGILE  needs buffering"]

    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Reading the Diagram

- The **green rectangle** is the safe operating window. Any signal excursion that stays inside this rectangle (after passing through a logic gate) is interpreted correctly by the next gate.
- The **red region** is the ambiguity zone — signals here have undefined behaviour and must never be allowed to occur.
- The **yellow centre point** ($V_M$) is the metastable equilibrium where any small noise can flip the state.
- The arrows labelled $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ literally show how the noise margins are measured as horizontal segments along the $V_{in}$-axis projections.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define Noise Margin. List the two noise margins used in CMOS logic families.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Noise margin is defined as the maximum amplitude of an extraneous (noise) voltage signal that can be added to the worst-case input of a digital logic gate without causing an undesirable change at the output. It quantifies the DC noise immunity of a logic family.

The two noise margins are:

- **Noise Margin High ($NM_H$)** = $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$
- **Noise Margin Low ($NM_L$)** = $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$

For a well-designed symmetric CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $V_T = 0.8\,\text{V}$, both noise margins evaluate to **2.075 V**, indicating very strong noise immunity.

---

#### **Q2. Why are $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ defined at the points where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ and not at other points on the VTC?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The slope of the VTC represents the **small-signal voltage gain** of the inverter at that operating point. Setting $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ marks the boundary between two functional regions:

1. **Outside** these points (i.e., for $V_{in} < V_{IL}$ or $V_{in} > V_{IH}$): $\vert A_v \vert < 1$. Any input noise is *attenuated* by the gate and cannot grow large enough to flip the output logic level.

2. **Inside** these points (i.e., $V_{IL} < V_{in} < V_{IH}$): $\vert A_v \vert > 1$. Any input noise is *amplified* and could push the output to the wrong state.

Therefore, $V_{IL}$ is the largest input still safely recognized as logic `0`, and $V_{IH}$ is the smallest input still safely recognized as logic `1`. Choosing these as the boundaries guarantees that the input is interpreted correctly even in the presence of maximum DC noise.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ for a **symmetric CMOS inverter** in terms of $V_{DD}$ and threshold voltage $V_T$. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $V_T = 0.8\,\text{V}$, compute $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$, $NM_L$, and the noise immunity percentage. Also comment on how reducing $V_{DD}$ to 1.2 V affects the noise margins. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation**

For the symmetric CMOS inverter ($\beta_n = \beta_p$, $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert = V_T$), the VTC has mirror symmetry about $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

**Region below $V_M$:** NMOS is in saturation, PMOS is in triode. Equating drain currents:

$$\frac{\beta}{2}(V_{in} - V_T)^{2} \;=\; \beta\!\left[(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_T)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Solving for $V_{out}$ and applying the unity-gain condition $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$:

After algebraic simplification, the smaller valid root is:

$$V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$$

Using the mirror-symmetry argument $V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IL}$:

$$V_{IH} \;=\; \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{3(5) + 2(0.8)}{8} \;=\; \frac{16.6}{8} \;=\; 2.075\,\text{V} \\
&V_{IH} \;=\; \frac{5(5) - 2(0.8)}{8} \;=\; \frac{23.4}{8} \;=\; 2.925\,\text{V} \\
&NM_{H} \;=\; V_{OH} - V_{IH} \;=\; 5 - 2.925 \;=\; 2.075\,\text{V} \\
&NM_{L} \;=\; V_{IL} - V_{OL} \;=\; 2.075 - 0 \;=\; 2.075\,\text{V} \\
&N.I.\% \;=\; \frac{NM_H + NM_L}{2 V_{DD}}\times 100 \;=\; \frac{4.15}{10}\times 100 \;=\; 41.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effect of reducing $V_{DD}$ to 1.2 V** (with same $V_T = 0.8\,\text{V}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IL}' \;=\; \frac{3(1.2) + 2(0.8)}{8} \;=\; \frac{5.2}{8} \;=\; 0.65\,\text{V} \\
&V_{IH}' \;=\; \frac{5(1.2) - 2(0.8)}{8} \;=\; \frac{4.4}{8} \;=\; 0.55\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical observation:** $V_{IL}' = 0.65\,\text{V}$ is **greater than** $V_{IH}' = 0.55\,\text{V}$! This is **impossible** — it means the threshold voltage is now so large relative to $V_{DD}$ that the inverter fails to function as a logic gate. The lesson: **noise margins collapse when $V_{DD} < 4V_T$**. Modern sub-threshold designs use $V_T \ll V_{DD}$ or special techniques such as Schmitt-trigger inputs.

For a more realistic low-power scenario with $V_T = 0.3\,\text{V}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$:

$$V_{IL} = \frac{3(1.2) + 2(0.3)}{8} = \frac{4.2}{8} = 0.525\,\text{V}, \quad V_{IH} = \frac{5(1.2) - 2(0.3)}{8} = \frac{5.4}{8} = 0.675\,\text{V}$$

$$NM_{H} = NM_{L} = \frac{3(1.2) + 2(0.3)}{8} = 0.525\,\text{V}, \quad N.I.\% = 43.75\%$$

So the noise margin drops from **2.075 V → 0.525 V** (a 4× reduction), but the *normalized* immunity remains similar.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **noise immunity** in CMOS logic gates. Show graphically how noise margins are determined from the VTC of a CMOS inverter and explain the role of the safe operating rectangle. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CMOS inverter has $V_{DD} = 2.5\,\text{V}$ and $V_T = 0.4\,\text{V}$. If the device transconductance ratio $r = \beta_n / \beta_p = 2$, recalculate $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ and compare the results with the symmetric case ($r = 1$). Comment on the design trade-off. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Graphical Determination of Noise Margins**

The VTC of a CMOS inverter is an S-shaped curve. To extract noise margins graphically:

1. **Draw the VTC** with $V_{in}$ on the x-axis and $V_{out}$ on the y-axis.
2. **Locate the unity-gain points** by drawing tangents of slope −1 at the upper and lower transitions. Drop perpendiculars from these tangent points onto the x-axis to obtain $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$.
3. **Draw horizontal lines** from the saturated high and low output regions onto the y-axis to obtain $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$.
4. **Construct the rectangle** with corners at $(V_{IL}, V_{OL})$, $(V_{IL}, V_{OH})$, $(V_{IH}, V_{OH})$, $(V_{IH}, V_{OL})$.

The width of this rectangle along the $V_{in}$-axis equals $NM_H + NM_L$. Any noise voltage whose amplitude lies inside this rectangle is rejected by the next gate; anything that drives the operating point outside is fatal.

The safe-operating rectangle ensures:
- **DC stability** — when the output of one gate feeds another, the quiescent point stays in the saturated (high-gain-suppressed) region.
- **AC margin** — even with ring oscillator transients and ground bounce, the signal stays well clear of the ambiguity region around $V_M$.

#### **Part (b) — Asymmetric Case ($r = 2$)**

For an asymmetric CMOS inverter with $r = \beta_n / \beta_p$, the general VTC equations are obtained by keeping the ratio in the current equations. The unity-gain analysis yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{2V_{out} + (V_{DD} - 2V_T)}{1 + r} \quad \text{subject to iteration, closed form:} \\
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{2V_{DD} + V_T\,(1 + 3r) - \sqrt{[2V_{DD} + V_T(1 + 3r)]^{2} - 4V_T\,(V_{DD} - V_T)\,(1 + r)^{2}}}{2(1 + r)} \\
&V_{IH} \;=\; \frac{V_{DD} + V_T\,(3 + r) + \sqrt{[V_{DD} + V_T(3 + r)]^{2} - 4V_T\,(V_{DD} - V_T)\,(1 + r)}}{2(1 + r)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging in $V_{DD} = 2.5\,\text{V}$, $V_T = 0.4\,\text{V}$, $r = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IL} \;=\; \frac{2(2.5) + 0.4(1 + 6) - \sqrt{[2(2.5) + 0.4(7)]^{2} - 4(0.4)(2.5 - 0.4)(3)^{2}}}{2(3)} \\
&\phantom{V_{IL}}=\;\frac{5 + 2.8 - \sqrt{60.84 - 7.584}}{6} \;=\;\frac{7.8 - \sqrt{53.256}}{6} \;=\;\frac{7.8 - 7.298}{6} \;=\;0.0837\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{IH} \;=\; \frac{2.5 + 0.4(5) + \sqrt{[2.5 + 0.4(5)]^{2} - 4(0.4)(2.5 - 0.4)(3)}}{2(3)} \\
&\phantom{V_{IH}}=\;\frac{2.5 + 2.0 + \sqrt{20.25 - 7.584}}{6} \;=\;\frac{4.5 + \sqrt{12.666}}{6} \;=\;\frac{4.5 + 3.559}{6} \;=\;1.343\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Noise margins:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&NM_{L} \;=\; V_{IL} - V_{OL} \;=\; 0.0837 - 0 \;=\; 0.0837\,\text{V} \\
&NM_{H} \;=\; V_{OH} - V_{IH} \;=\; 2.5 - 1.343 \;=\; 1.157\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with symmetric case ($r = 1$):**

| Quantity | Symmetric ($r=1$) | Asymmetric ($r=2$) | Change |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $V_{IL}$ | $0.838\,\text{V}$ | $0.0837\,\text{V}$ | ↓ 90% |
| $V_{IH}$ | $1.463\,\text{V}$ | $1.343\,\text{V}$ | ↓ 8% |
| $NM_L$ | $0.838\,\text{V}$ | $0.0837\,\text{V}$ | ↓ 90% |
| $NM_H$ | $0.838\,\text{V}$ | $1.157\,\text{V}$ | ↑ 38% |

**Design Trade-off:**

Increasing $\beta_n$ (stronger NMOS) **shifts $V_M$ downward** (closer to GND), which:
- **Improves $NM_H$** at the cost of **drastically reducing $NM_L$**.
- **Asymmetric margins** — the gate becomes more robust against noise on a high signal than on a low signal.

Engineers exploit this deliberately: inverters designed to drive heavily loaded high-to-low transitions (e.g., clock buffers) are sized with $r > 1$ to enhance $NM_H$ and accelerate the falling edge, but a chain of such inverters risks propagating low-level errors. In **symmetric logic** ($r = 1$), both margins are equal and maximum for a given $V_{DD}$, which is why static CMOS gates are typically designed symmetrically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$ (output voltages) with $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ (input voltages). Remember: $V_{OH}/V_{OL}$ live on the **y-axis**, $V_{IH}/V_{IL}$ live on the **x-axis** of the VTC.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Applying the symmetric formula $V_{IL} = (3V_{DD}+2V_T)/8$ to an asymmetric inverter. The formula is only valid when $r = 1$. For $r \neq 1$, the general expression must be used.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that the unity-gain condition is $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ (negative because the VTC is inverted). A positive slope would indicate a positive-feedback latch, not an inverter.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{OL}$. This is the **logic swing**, not the noise margin! Noise margin always pairs output of one stage with input of the next.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Noise margin = the DC voltage tolerance between driver output levels and receiver input recognition levels. Two types: $NM_H$ (high) and $NM_L$ (low).
- **Four cornerstones:** $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$ — all measured from the same VTC but on different axes.
- **Unity-gain rule:** $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are precisely the points where the VTC slope equals $-1$.
- **Symmetric inverter formulas** (most-tested on KTU):
  - $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$
  - $V_{IH} = \dfrac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}$
  - $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8}$
- **Switching threshold:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ for symmetric CMOS; $\dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$ in general.
- **Asymmetric design:** $\beta_n / \beta_p > 1$ boosts $NM_H$ but cripples $NM_L$; $\beta_n / \beta_p < 1$ does the reverse.
- **Scaling effect:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ reduces noise margins in absolute volts but normalized immunity ($NM/V_{DD}$) stays roughly constant for $V_{DD} \gg 4V_T$.
- **Failure condition:** When $V_{DD} < 4V_T$, the symmetric formulas yield $V_{IL} > V_{IH}$ — physically impossible — the gate stops functioning.
- **Cross-coupled method:** Plotting two VTCs head-to-tail gives the "butterfly" intersections; this graphically highlights $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ as the safe rectangle width.
- **Industrial relevance:** Noise margin is one of the first parameters checked during **static timing and DC sign-off** at the chip-tapeout stage; failing this metric forces a re-spin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Static CMOS Logic Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Other Static CMOS Logic Gates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> Static CMOS logic gates other than the standard complementary CMOS (e.g., NAND, NOR, AND, OR) are alternative logic families that trade off noise margin, power, area, or speed for specific advantages such as reduced transistor count, faster switching, or denser layout. These include **Pseudo-nMOS**, **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)**, **Transmission Gates (TG)**, and various hybrid structures.

### 1.1 The Motivation — Why Do We Need "Other" Logic Families?

The textbook complementary CMOS (C²MOS) gate we studied earlier is **robust, perfectly static, and has full noise margin**, but it has two real-world pain points:

1. **It uses too many transistors** (2N for N inputs in a NAND/NOR).
2. **The PMOS network is inherently slow** because holes move slower than electrons (mobility ratio $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $3$).

Engineers, therefore, developed *other* static logic families that sacrifice some ideal property (like noise margin, static power, or full-swing output) in exchange for **fewer transistors, smaller area, or higher speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module-2, the term *"Static"* is interpreted broadly to mean "the output is connected to either $V_{DD}$ or GND through a low-resistance DC path *most of the time*" — it does **not** strictly exclude ratioed logic families like Pseudo-nMOS which are still classified under static logic even though they dissipate DC power.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a standard CMOS gate as a **two-person tug-of-war team** — one person always pulls toward "1" (PMOS) and the other always pulls toward "0" (NMOS). Whichever side has more people wins, and no energy is wasted at rest.

- **Pseudo-nMOS** is like leaving the "1-puller" permanently tethered to the rope (always on, weak). The "0-puller" team pulls only when needed. Faster, but the weak "1-puller" is still straining — burning energy.
- **Pass Transistor Logic** is like a **single revolving door** — you let one person through at a time, but the door can only pass a "1" or a "0" with reduced strength.
- **Transmission Gate** is a **double revolving door** — a NMOS and a PMOS work together so that *both* a "1" and a "0" pass through at full strength.

| Logic Family | Transistor Count vs. CMOS | Speed | Static Power | Full Swing? |
|--------------|---------------------------|-------|--------------|-------------|
| Standard CMOS | Reference | Slow (PMOS) | **Zero** | **Yes** |
| Pseudo-nMOS | $\approx N+1$ | Fast | Yes (ratioed) | Yes |
| Pass Transistor (NMOS only) | $N$ | Very Fast | Zero | **No** (weak '1') |
| Transmission Gate | $2N$ | Fast | Zero | **Yes** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pseudo-nMOS Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** A *weak* PMOS transistor permanently ON (its gate tied to GND) replaces the entire PMOS pull-up network of a conventional CMOS gate. The NMOS pull-down network implements the Boolean function.

**Operating Principle:**

1. The load PMOS transistor is sized with a **large length** (long-L, narrow-W) so that its on-resistance is *very high* — typically 4× to 10× the resistance of a unit NMOS.
2. When the NMOS network is OFF, the load PMOS slowly charges the output to $V_{DD}$ → output = 1.
3. When the NMOS network is ON, it must **overpower** the load PMOS and pull the output down to $\approx 0$ V → output = 0.

**Why does this work?**
The output low voltage ($V_{OL}$) is determined by the **resistive divider** between the always-on PMOS load and the active NMOS pull-down network.

$$V_{OL} = \frac{R_{PMOS}}{R_{PMOS} + R_{NMOS,eq}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**For $V_{OL}$ to be safely "low"** (typically $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$), the NMOS must be much stronger than the PMOS load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Ratio" Rule:** Pseudo-nMOS is a *ratioed logic* family. The aspect ratio $(W/L)_n / (W/L)_p$ must be carefully chosen (typically 4:1 to 10:1) to guarantee the proper $V_{OL}$. If you size the transistors wrongly, the gate **fails functionally** — a classic KTU exam trap.

**Static Power Dissipation in Pseudo-nMOS:**

$$P_{static} = I_{DC} \cdot V_{DD}$$

where $I_{DC}$ is the constant DC current flowing from $V_{DD}$ to GND through the load PMOS and the active NMOS network whenever the output is low.

**Advantages:**
- Higher packing density (single PMOS load for the entire chip)
- Faster $t_{LH}$ (single PMOS vs. series PMOS stack)
- Smaller input capacitance (no PMOS inputs to drive)

**Disadvantages:**
- **Static power consumption** in the low output state
- Reduced noise margin ($NM_L$ shrinks)
- Ratioed design constraints

---

### 2.2 Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** An NMOS (or PMOS) transistor is used as a *switch* to pass a signal from input to output when its gate is activated. The function is implemented by the *routing* of signals, not by the topology of series/parallel networks.

**The Famous Problem — Threshold Voltage Drop:**

When an NMOS pass transistor passes a logic "1":

$$V_{out,max} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$$

The output **cannot exceed** $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ because the NMOS turns off as soon as $V_{GS} = V_{tn}$. With $V_{tn} \approx 0.5$ V, this is a **significant degradation** of the high level.

When a PMOS pass transistor passes a logic "0":

$$V_{out,min} = \vert V_{tp} \vert$$

The output cannot go below $\vert V_{tp} \vert$ above GND.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students frequently lose marks by stating that "pass transistors pass full logic levels." They **do not** — they suffer threshold drops. This is a board-favorite question.

---

### 2.3 Transmission Gates (TG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** A Transmission Gate places an **NMOS and a PMOS in parallel**, gated by complementary control signals. This combination can pass *both* a strong "1" and a strong "0", restoring full-swing operation.

**Structure:**
- NMOS gate = Control signal $C$, source/drain = signal path
- PMOS gate = $\bar{C}$, source/drain = signal path
- When $C = 1$: Both NMOS and PMOS are ON → signal passes with full swing
- When $C = 0$: Both are OFF → high-impedance (Hi-Z) state

**On-Resistance (Equivalent):**

$$R_{eq} = R_n \parallel R_p = \frac{R_n \cdot R_p}{R_n + R_p}$$

This parallel combination is **relatively constant** across the input range, which is a key advantage for analog-like behavior.

**Application Domains:**
- Multiplexers (MUX)
- XOR/XNOR gates
- Flip-flops and latches
- SRAM bit-line access
- Bus switches

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| $V_{OL,psn} = \dfrac{R_{PMOS}}{R_{PMOS} + R_{NMOS,eq}} V_{DD}$ | Output low level of pseudo-nMOS | Pseudo-nMOS design |
| $P_{static} = I_{DC} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Static power dissipation | Pseudo-nMOS |
| $V_{out,high} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ | Max output of NMOS PTL | PTL analysis |
| $V_{out,low} = \vert V_{tp} \vert$ | Min output of PMOS PTL | PTL analysis |
| $R_{TG} = R_n \parallel R_p$ | Equivalent on-resistance of TG | TG design |
| $t_{delay} \propto \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{drive}}$ | RC delay estimate | All logic families |
| $E_{dynamic} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Switching energy per transition | Power analysis |
| $V_{IH} \approx \dfrac{5 V_{tn} + V_{DD}}{6}$ or $\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ (approx.) | Input high threshold | Noise margin calcs |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Pseudo-nMOS** was historically used in **static RAM (SRAM) cell design** and in **read-only memories (ROMs)** where the entire column shares a common PMOS load — saving enormous silicon area.
- **Transmission Gates** are the cornerstone of every modern **multiplexer, flip-flop, and analog switch** in CMOS ICs.
- **PTL** is heavily used in **arithmetic units (adders)** of high-performance processors to reduce transistor count.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pseudo-nMOS Output Low Voltage ($V_{OL}$)

We model the load PMOS and the active NMOS network as a simple resistive voltage divider. Both transistors operate in the **triode (linear) region** when the output is being pulled down.

**Step 1: Currents in the two branches must be equal at steady state.**

$$I_{PMOS,load} = I_{NMOS,network}$$

**Step 2: Write triode-region current equations.**

For the PMOS load (with $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$, $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$):

$$I_{PMOS} = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \left[(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert) V_{SD,p} - \frac{V_{SD,p}^2}{2}\right]$$

For the NMOS pull-down (assume a single NMOS of size $(W/L)_n$ with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$, $V_{DS} = V_{OL}$):

$$I_{NMOS} = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn}) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3: Equate the two currents and solve for $V_{OL}$.**

Setting the currents equal and noting that for a *strong* pull-down NMOS, $V_{OL} \ll (V_{DD} - V_{tn})$, the quadratic term is negligible:

$$\mu_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)(V_{DD} - V_{OL}) \approx \mu_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) V_{OL}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_{OL}$ using the ratio $r = (W/L)_n / (W/L)_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{OL} &\approx \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert) (V_{DD} - V_{OL})}{(r \, \mu_n / \mu_p) (V_{DD} - V_{tn})} \cdot V_{DD} \\
&\approx \frac{V_{DD}}{1 + r \cdot (\mu_n / \mu_p) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) / (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Design rule for $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$.**

For a typical ratio with $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{tn} = 0.5$ V, $\vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.5$ V, $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.5$:

$$r \ge \frac{0.9 \cdot V_{DD}}{0.1 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot 2.5} \approx 3.6 \text{ to } 4$$

This is the **famous "4:1" sizing rule** for pseudo-nMOS.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Charge Sharing in Dynamic Logic (Capacitive Analysis)

Although dynamic logic is covered in detail separately, a critical analytical question asked in KTU is the **charge sharing** effect.

**Setup:** A dynamic logic node at $V_{DD}$ (precharged) is connected through an OFF NMOS to a parasitic capacitance $C_p$ that is initially at 0 V.

**Initial Charge on $C_L$ (output node):**

$$Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Final Charge (after charge redistribution, when NMOS turns ON):**

$$Q_{final} = (C_L + C_p) \cdot V_{final}$$

**By Conservation of Charge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L \cdot V_{DD} &= (C_L + C_p) \cdot V_{final} \\
V_{final} &= V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_p} \\
V_{final} &= \frac{V_{DD}}{1 + C_p / C_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The output voltage **collapses** from $V_{DD}$ to a much lower value. If $C_p$ is large, the voltage drop can cause the gate to incorrectly evaluate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Transmission Gate Equivalent Resistance

The on-resistance of a single MOS transistor in triode is approximately:

$$R_n = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}$$

For **matched rise/fall resistance** ($R_n \approx R_p$), the sizing ratio is:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \Big/ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = \frac{\mu_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}{\mu_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}$$

With typical values, $(W/L)_p \approx 2$ to $3 \times (W/L)_n$.

**The equivalent TG resistance is then:**

$$R_{TG} = \frac{R_n \cdot R_p}{R_n + R_p} \approx \frac{R_n}{2} \approx \frac{R_p}{2}$$

This is a critical analysis point for **delay estimation** in MUX and latch circuits.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Standard CMOS | Pseudo-nMOS | NMOS PTL | Transmission Gate |
|----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Transistors per N-input** | 2N | N+1 | N | 2N |
| **Static Power** | None | **Yes (ratioed)** | None | None |
| **Full Output Swing** | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| **Speed (t_PHL)** | Medium | **Fast** | **Very Fast** | Fast |
| **Speed (t_PLH)** | Slow (PMOS) | **Fast** | Slow (no PMOS) | Fast |
| **Noise Margin** | Full | Reduced | Poor | Full |
| **Bidirectional?** | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Area Efficiency** | Poor | Good | **Best** | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pseudo-nMOS NAND Gate — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input A] --> nodeG1[NMOS M1: Series]
    nodeB[Input B] --> nodeG2[NMOS M2: Series]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2
    nodeG2 --> nodeGND[GND]
    nodeVDD[VDD] --> nodePLoad[PMOS Load: Gate Tied to GND, Always ON]
    nodePLoad --> nodeOut[Output Y = NOT A AND NOT B]
    nodeG1 --> nodeOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **single** PMOS load at the top replaces what would have been two parallel PMOS transistors in a standard CMOS NAND. The two NMOS transistors in *series* form the pull-down network. The output $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

---

### 4.2 Transmission Gate — Mermaid Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIn[Input Signal Vin] --> nodeTN[Drain of NMOS M1]
    nodeIn --> nodeTP[Source of PMOS M2]
    nodeC[Control Signal C] --> nodeGateN[Gate of NMOS M1]
    nodeCbar[Inverted Control Cbar] --> nodeGateP[Gate of PMOS M2]
    nodeTN --> nodeOut[Output Vout]
    nodeTP --> nodeOut
    nodeSourceN[Source of NMOS] --> nodeOut
    nodeDrainP[Drain of PMOS] --> nodeOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The NMOS and PMOS transistors are placed in **parallel** with their drains/sources tied together on both ends. The control $C$ activates the NMOS while the inverted $\bar{C}$ activates the PMOS. The output node is a Hi-Z node when both are OFF.

---

### 4.3 2-to-1 Multiplexer Using Transmission Gates — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeInA[Input A] --> nodeTGA1[Transmission Gate 1: Active when S=0]
    nodeInB[Input B] --> nodeTGA2[Transmission Gate 2: Active when S=1]
    nodeS[Select S] --> nodeInv[Inverter: Produces Sbar]
    nodeInv --> nodeTGA1
    nodeS --> nodeTGA2
    nodeTGA1 --> nodeOutMux[Output Y = A when S=0, B when S=1]
    nodeTGA2 --> nodeOutMux
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When $S = 0$, the upper TG is ON (passes $A$) and the lower TG is OFF. When $S = 1$, the lower TG is ON (passes $B$) and the upper TG is OFF. Only one input is ever connected to the output.

---

### 4.4 Pass Transistor XOR Gate — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[A] --> nodeM1[NMOS M1: Passes A when B=1]
    nodeB[B] --> nodeM2[NMOS M2: Passes Bbar when A=1]
    nodeNotB[Bbar from Inverter] --> nodeM2
    nodeM1 --> nodeXOROut[Output Y = A XOR B]
    nodeM2 --> nodeXOROut
    nodeA --> nodeM2
    nodeB --> nodeM1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When $B = 1$, M1 is ON and passes $A$ to the output. When $A = 1$, M2 is ON and passes $\bar{B}$ to the output. Together, the output realizes $Y = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B = A \oplus B$ using just **two NMOS transistors** plus one inverter, instead of 12 transistors in standard CMOS.

---

### 4.5 Summary Block Diagram: All Logic Families Compared

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeRoot[Static CMOS Logic Families]
    nodeRoot --> nodeConv[Conventional CMOS: 2N transistors, full swing, zero static power]
    nodeRoot --> nodePseudo[Pseudo-nMOS: N+1 transistors, ratioed, static power, faster]
    nodeRoot --> nodePTL[Pass Transistor Logic: N transistors, weak 1 or 0, very fast]
    nodeRoot --> nodeTG[Transmission Gate: 2N transistors, full swing, bidirectional]
    nodeTG --> nodeApps[Applications: MUX, XOR, Latches, SRAM, Analog Switches]
    nodePseudo --> nodeApps2[Applications: ROM, NOR arrays, dense logic]
    nodePTL --> nodeApps3[Applications: Adders, Barrel shifters, low-power]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare Pseudo-nMOS logic with standard CMOS logic. Mention any two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Standard CMOS | Pseudo-nMOS |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Transistor Count** | 2N | N+1 |
| **Static Power** | Zero | Yes (when output is low) |
| **Output Swing** | Full (rail-to-rail) | Full, but ratioed |
| **Speed** | Slower (PMOS network) | Faster |

**Two advantages of Pseudo-nMOS:**
1. **Reduced transistor count** — single PMOS load instead of a full PMOS network → higher density (e.g., used in ROM arrays).
2. **Faster switching** — output capacitance is smaller and pull-up is via a single transistor, so $t_{PLH}$ is reduced.

**Two disadvantages of Pseudo-nMOS:**
1. **Static power dissipation** — constant DC current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND when output is LOW, wasting energy.
2. **Reduced noise margin** — $V_{OL}$ is not exactly 0 V, so $NM_L$ is degraded; this is a *ratioed* design.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the threshold voltage drop problem in NMOS pass transistor logic. How is it overcome in a transmission gate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When an NMOS transistor is used as a pass switch to transmit a logic HIGH signal, the maximum output voltage it can deliver is:

$$V_{out,max} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$$

This is because the NMOS turns off as soon as $V_{GS}$ falls to $V_{tn}$. Thus, a "weak 1" appears at the output — about 0.5 V to 0.7 V below $V_{DD}$ for a standard process. This degrades noise margins and can cause cascading failures.

**How a Transmission Gate overcomes it:**

A transmission gate places a **PMOS in parallel with the NMOS**, with its gate driven by the *inverted* control signal. The PMOS is strong in passing a logic "1" (it does not suffer $V_{tn}$ drop because its source is the lower potential). When both are ON together, the parallel combination passes the input with **full rail-to-rail swing**, since the PMOS handles the "1" and the NMOS handles the "0" efficiently.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often forget to draw the **PMOS load with its gate tied to GND** in pseudo-nMOS schematics — lose 1 to 2 marks.
> - In the "ratio rule" question, do not write "make PMOS weaker" generically. Specify the **size ratio** $(W/L)_n : (W/L)_p \approx 4:1$ to $10:1$ to earn full marks.
> - In PTL analysis, you MUST state both the *NMOS weak '1'* and *PMOS weak '0'* problems — partial marks lost if only one is mentioned.
> - For transmission gates, do not forget to mention **bidirectionality** — it is a board-favorite distinction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Pseudo-nMOS 2-input NOR gate**. Derive the expression for $V_{OL}$ and discuss the sizing constraints.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **2:1 multiplexer using transmission gates**. Explain its operation and show that it provides full-swing output for both inputs.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pseudo-nMOS NOR Gate — Operation & Analysis**

**Circuit Description:**

The pseudo-nMOS NOR gate consists of:
- A **single PMOS load transistor** (M3) with its **gate tied to GND** (always ON), drain connected to the output node, and source connected to $V_{DD}$.
- **Two NMOS transistors** (M1 and M2) connected in **parallel** between the output node and GND. Their gates are inputs $A$ and $B$, respectively.

**Truth Table Verification:**

| A | B | M1 (NMOS) | M2 (NMOS) | Output Y |
|---|---|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| 0 | 1 | OFF | ON | 0 (LOW, through M2) |
| 1 | 0 | ON | OFF | 0 (LOW, through M1) |
| 1 | 1 | ON | ON | 0 (LOW, through both) |

The output is $Y = \overline{A + B}$ — the NOR function.

**Derivation of $V_{OL}$:**

When at least one input is HIGH, the corresponding NMOS is ON, creating a path from output to GND. The PMOS load is also ON (always). The output settles at a voltage $V_{OL}$ determined by the resistive divider:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{OL} &= \frac{R_{PMOS,load}}{R_{PMOS,load} + R_{NMOS,eq}} \cdot V_{DD}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a single ON NMOS of size $(W/L)_n$ in triode (with $V_{DS} = V_{OL} \ll V_{DD} - V_{tn}$):

$$I_{NMOS} \approx \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) V_{OL}$$

The PMOS load with $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$:

$$I_{PMOS} \approx \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)(V_{DD} - V_{OL})$$

Equating (ignoring second-order $V_{OL}^2$ terms):

$$V_{OL} \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{1 + \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left(\frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}\right) \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert} \Big/ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p}$$

**Sizing Constraint (4 Marks within part-a):**

For $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$, we need:

$$\frac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p} \ge \frac{0.9 (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}{0.1 \cdot (\mu_n/\mu_p)(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$

With $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.5$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert = 0.5$ V, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V:

$$\frac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p} \ge \frac{0.9 \times 1.3}{0.1 \times 2.5 \times 1.3} = \frac{1.17}{0.325} \approx 3.6$$

A safe design rule is the **"4:1 ratio"** — NMOS is sized 4× stronger than the PMOS load. This is the **ratioed design constraint** of pseudo-nMOS.

**(b) 2:1 Multiplexer Using Transmission Gates**

**Circuit Diagram (textual):**

- **Input A** is connected to a transmission gate TG1.
- **Input B** is connected to a transmission gate TG2.
- **TG1** is enabled when Select $S = 0$ (control = $S$, complement = $\bar{S}$).
- **TG2** is enabled when Select $S = 1$ (control = $S$, complement = $\bar{S}$).
- The outputs of both TGs are tied together at node $Y$.

**Operation Table:**

| Select S | TG1 (active when S=0) | TG2 (active when S=1) | Output Y |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| 0 | ON | OFF | $A$ |
| 1 | OFF | ON | $B$ |

**Full-Swing Justification (4 Marks within part-b):**

When $S = 0$:
- TG1 is ON: NMOS passes strong "0" ($V_{OL} \approx 0$ V); PMOS passes strong "1" ($V_{OH} \approx V_{DD}$).
- TG2 is OFF: high-impedance, no contention.
- Therefore, $Y = A$ at **full rail-to-rail swing**.

When $S = 1$:
- TG2 is ON: full-swing pass of $B$ to output.
- TG1 is OFF: no contention.
- Therefore, $Y = B$ at **full rail-to-rail swing**.

**Key Advantages Demonstrated:**
- Bidirectional signal flow.
- Static operation (no clock).
- Full output swing.
- Used as a fundamental building block in ALU data-path circuits.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of an **NMOS pass transistor logic (PTL) AND gate**. Show why the output suffers from threshold voltage drop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **operation of a CMOS transmission gate** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its equivalent on-resistance and justify why it provides full-swing signal transmission.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NMOS Pass Transistor AND Gate — Threshold Drop**

**Circuit Diagram (textual):**

- Two NMOS transistors M1 and M2 in **series**.
- Input $A$ → source of M1; drain of M1 → source of M2; drain of M2 → output $Y$.
- Input $B$ → gate of M2; Input $A$ → gate of M1.
- $B$ is the **controlling input**; $A$ is the **data input** that gets passed.

**Operation:**

When $B = 1$: M2 is ON; the path from $A$ to $Y$ is enabled. Thus $Y = A \cdot 1 = A$.
When $B = 0$: M2 is OFF; output $Y$ is **floating** (high-impedance). To realize a clean AND, a **weak pull-up PMOS** is typically added to pull $Y$ to $V_{DD}$ when $B = 0$.

| A | B | M1 | M2 | Y |
|---|---|----|----|---|
| 0 | 0 | ON | OFF | 0 (pulled up to $V_{DD}$ by weak PMOS, but… see below) |
| 0 | 1 | ON | ON | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (via weak PMOS) |
| 1 | 1 | ON | ON | $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ (THRESHOLD DROP) |

**Threshold Voltage Drop Demonstration:**

When $A = 1$ and $B = 1$, the input voltage applied to the source of M1 is $V_{DD}$. As M1 conducts, the output $Y$ rises. M1 turns off when:

$$V_{GS,M1} = V_{in} - V_{out} = V_{tn}$$

$$V_{out,max} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$$

For $V_{tn} = 0.5$ V and $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V:

$$V_{out,max} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$

This is **0.5 V below $V_{DD}$**, a serious noise margin and timing degradation.

**(b) CMOS Transmission Gate — Operation & Resistance**

**Circuit Description:**

A CMOS transmission gate consists of an **NMOS** and a **PMOS** in **parallel**:
- Their sources and drains are tied together at both ends (one end = input, the other = output).
- The **NMOS gate** is driven by control signal $C$.
- The **PMOS gate** is driven by the *complement* $\bar{C}$.

**Operation (Truth Table):**

| Control C | $\bar{C}$ | NMOS State | PMOS State | Path State |
|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|------------|
| 0 | 1 | OFF | OFF | **Hi-Z (Open)** |
| 1 | 0 | ON | ON | **Conductive (Full Swing)** |

**Why it passes both levels strongly:**

- For a "1" being passed: The PMOS has $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{out}$. As $V_{out}$ rises near $V_{DD}$, the PMOS remains strongly ON because $V_{SG} > |V_{tp}|$. The NMOS turns off eventually, but the PMOS continues to pull the output all the way to $V_{DD}$.
- For a "0" being passed: The NMOS has $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$. It remains strongly ON, pulling the output all the way to GND. The PMOS eventually turns off, but the NMOS handles the rest of the discharge.

**Equivalent Resistance Derivation:**

On-resistance of NMOS in triode:

$$R_n \approx \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$

On-resistance of PMOS in triode:

$$R_p \approx \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}$$

Equivalent TG resistance (parallel combination):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TG} &= R_n \parallel R_p = \frac{R_n \cdot R_p}{R_n + R_p} \\
R_{TG} &= \left[ \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) + \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert) \right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For symmetric rise/fall resistance (a common design choice), set $R_n \approx R_p$, which gives:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \Big/ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \approx \frac{\mu_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}{\mu_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

**Result:** The TG exhibits a **roughly constant** on-resistance across the input range, making it ideal for analog switch and MUX applications.

**Real-World Examples:**
- The 74HC4066 / 74HC4053 ICs are commercial transmission gate packages.
- Inside every modern flip-flop, two transmission gates form the **master and slave** stages of a master-slave D-flip-flop.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Pseudo-nMOS** uses a *single always-on PMOS* as the load → fewer transistors, but **ratioed design** and **static power dissipation** are mandatory consequences.
- ✅ The **"4:1 ratio rule"** is the magic number for $(W/L)_n : (W/L)_p$ in pseudo-nMOS to ensure $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$.
- ✅ **Pseudo-nMOS static power** $= I_{DC} \cdot V_{DD}$ — this is wasted as heat, so pseudo-nMOS is avoided in low-power designs.
- ✅ **NMOS Pass Transistor** passes a **weak 1** ($V_{DD} - V_{tn}$); **PMOS Pass Transistor** passes a **weak 0** ($\vert V_{tp} \vert$).
- ✅ **Transmission Gate** = NMOS $\parallel$ PMOS with complementary control signals. Provides **full-swing** and **bidirectional** signal flow.
- ✅ Transmission gate on-resistance is the **parallel combination** of NMOS and PMOS on-resistances: $R_{TG} = R_n \parallel R_p$.
- ✅ For matched rise/fall in TG, the PMOS is typically sized **2× to 3×** the NMOS to compensate for hole mobility.
- ✅ Transmission gates are the **building blocks of MUXes, latches, and flip-flops** in CMOS VLSI.
- ✅ **Pass transistor logic** reduces transistor count drastically (e.g., XOR in 4 transistors + 1 inverter vs. 12 in CMOS) — high payoff in adders and barrel shifters.
- ✅ The phrase "**static CMOS**" in KTU Module-2 includes **all non-clocked** logic families — pseudo-nMOS, PTL, and TG all qualify.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-liner memory trick:**
> - *Pseudo-nMOS = "Weak PMOS, strong NMOS, ratioed, no clock"*
> - *PTL = "Single switch, threshold drop, very fast, no clock"*
> - *TG = "Two switches in parallel, full swing, no clock, bidirectional"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Properties of 2-Input NAND
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Properties of 2-Input NAND

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **2-Input CMOS NAND Gate** is a complementary static CMOS logic gate that realizes the Boolean function:

$$F = \overline{A \cdot B}$$

The **Static Properties** of this gate refer to the time-independent, DC behavioral characteristics of the circuit when it resides in a stable logic state (logic-0 or logic-1). These properties describe *how the gate responds to DC input voltages* and *what DC output voltages it produces*, forming the foundation for signal integrity analysis in digital VLSI design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Static properties include the **Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC)**, the **Noise Margins (NMH and NML)**, the **Static Power Dissipation (Pstatic)**, and the **Fan-Out Capability**. These four parameters completely characterize the steady-state DC behavior of the gate.

## 1.2 The CMOS NAND Circuit Topology

A standard 2-input CMOS NAND consists of **four MOSFETs** arranged as a complementary pull-up and pull-down network:

- **Pull-Up Network (PUN):** Two **PMOS** transistors (M1 and M2) connected in **parallel** between **VDD** and the output node **F**.
- **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** Two **NMOS** transistors (M3 and M4) connected in **series** between the output node **F** and **GND**.
- Input **A** drives the gates of M1 (PMOS) and M3 (NMOS).
- Input **B** drives the gates of M2 (PMOS) and M4 (NMOS).

| Transistor | Type | Network | Position | Controlled by |
|:----------:|:----:|:-------:|:--------:|:-------------:|
| **M1** | PMOS | Pull-Up | Parallel branch 1 | Input A |
| **M2** | PMOS | Pull-Up | Parallel branch 2 | Input B |
| **M3** | NMOS | Pull-Down | Series branch 1 | Input A |
| **M4** | NMOS | Pull-Down | Series branch 2 | Input B |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" Model

Imagine a **water pipeline** connecting a high-pressure tank (**VDD**) to a drainage pit (**GND**), with the output node **F** being a tap point in the middle:

- The **PMOS pair (in parallel)** acts like **two independent valves** that can *seal the pipe from the top*. Since they are in parallel, opening *either one* is enough to fill the tap with water from the tank.
- The **NMOS pair (in series)** acts like **two valves in a chain** that must *both be open* to drain water from the tap to the pit. If *any one* of them is shut, the drain is blocked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Analogy:**
> - Output is **HIGH (tank pressure)** if at least one PMOS valve is open.
> - Output is **LOW (drained to pit)** only if both NMOS valves are open *and* both PMOS valves are shut.
> - This is precisely the NAND truth table: $\overline{A \cdot B}$.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

- **Standard CMOS Supply Voltage (VDD):** Typically **5.0 V** (legacy) or **3.3 V / 1.8 V / 1.2 V** (modern deep-submicron).
- **PMOS Threshold Voltage ($V_{Tp}$):** Negative quantity, typically $\vert V_{Tp} \vert \approx 0.7$ V.
- **NMOS Threshold Voltage ($V_{Tn}$):** Positive quantity, typically $V_{Tn} \approx 0.7$ V.
- **Ideal Output High (VOH):** $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (when at least one PMOS is ON).
- **Ideal Output Low (VOL):** $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V (when both NMOS are ON with adequate sizing).
- **Static Power Dissipation (Pstatic):** $\approx 0$ W in steady state.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Steady-State Operation

The 2-Input NAND has **four possible input combinations**. We analyze each combination in terms of which transistors are conducting and what the resulting output voltage is.

### Case 1: A = 0, B = 0
- M1 (PMOS): $V_{GS} = 0 - 0 = 0 > V_{Tp}$ (with $V_{Tp} < 0$, so $V_{GS} > V_{Tp}$ is **ON**). **M1 is ON.**
- M2 (PMOS): $V_{GS} = 0 - 0 = 0 > V_{Tp}$. **M2 is ON.**
- M3 (NMOS): $V_{GS} = 0 - 0 = 0 < V_{Tn}$. **M3 is OFF.**
- M4 (NMOS): $V_{GS} = 0 - 0 = 0 < V_{Tn}$. **M4 is OFF.**
- **Result:** The PUN provides a conducting path from VDD to F. The PDN is broken. **Output F = VDD (Logic HIGH).**

### Case 2: A = 0, B = VDD
- M1 (PMOS): $V_{GS} = 0 - 0 = 0$, **ON**.
- M2 (PMOS): $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{DD} = 0$ at gate-to-source — *Wait*, careful: For M2, source is VDD and gate is B = VDD, so $V_{GS2} = V_{G2} - V_{S2} = V_{DD} - V_{DD} = 0$. **M2 is ON.** (Actually M2 is OFF because gate = VDD means $V_{SG} = 0$.) **M2 is OFF.**
- M3 (NMOS): Gate A = 0, so $V_{GS3} = 0$, **OFF**.
- M4 (NMOS): Gate B = VDD, source = GND (initially), $V_{GS4} = V_{DD}$, **ON**.
- **Result:** PUN: M1 is ON (M2 OFF). PDN: M4 is ON but M3 is OFF (broken chain). **Output F = VDD (Logic HIGH).**

### Case 3: A = VDD, B = 0
- M1 (PMOS): Gate A = VDD, Source = VDD, $V_{GS1} = 0$, **OFF**.
- M2 (PMOS): Gate B = 0, $V_{GS2} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD} < V_{Tp}$, **ON**.
- M3 (NMOS): Gate A = VDD, $V_{GS3} = V_{DD}$, **ON**.
- M4 (NMOS): Gate B = 0, **OFF**.
- **Result:** PUN: M2 is ON. PDN: M3 is ON but M4 is OFF. **Output F = VDD (Logic HIGH).**

### Case 4: A = VDD, B = VDD
- M1: Gate = VDD, **OFF**.
- M2: Gate = VDD, **OFF**.
- M3: Gate = VDD, **ON**.
- M4: Gate = VDD, **ON**.
- **Result:** PUN completely OFF. PDN fully conducting (M3-M4 series). **Output F = 0 (Logic LOW).**

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the **NAND truth table**: Output is LOW **only** when **both** inputs are HIGH.

## 2.2 The Four Critical Static Voltages

The Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) of the 2-input NAND is more complex than that of an inverter because it depends on the *combination* of inputs. The four key voltages are:

- **$V_{OL}$ — Output Low Voltage:** The output voltage when the gate output is in the LOW state. For the NAND, with both inputs HIGH, the PDN conducts. With properly sized NMOS devices, $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.
- **$V_{OH}$ — Output High Voltage:** The output voltage when the gate output is in the HIGH state. With at least one PMOS ON, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.
- **$V_{IL}$ — Input Low Voltage:** The maximum input voltage that is still recognized as a logic-0.
- **$V_{IH}$ — Input High Voltage:** The minimum input voltage that is still recognized as a logic-1.

## 2.3 Noise Margins — The "Immunity" Parameter

Noise margins quantify how *robust* the gate is against unwanted voltage disturbances. A larger noise margin means the gate is more tolerant to noise.

- **$NM_L$ (Noise Margin LOW):** The amount of noise that can be added to a LOW output before it is misinterpreted as a HIGH.
- **$NM_H$ (Noise Margin HIGH):** The amount of noise that can be subtracted from a HIGH output before it is misinterpreted as a LOW.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning | Ideal / Typical Value |
|:---------:|:------:|:----------:|:----------------|:--------------------:|
| Logic Function | $F$ | $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | Boolean output | — |
| Output High | $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | HIGH output level | $V_{DD}$ |
| Output Low | $V_{OL}$ | $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V | LOW output level (with proper sizing) | $\leq 0.1 V_{DD}$ |
| Noise Margin High | $NM_H$ | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Tolerance to noise on HIGH | $\geq 0.4 V_{DD}$ |
| Noise Margin Low | $NM_L$ | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Tolerance to noise on LOW | $\geq 0.4 V_{DD}$ |
| Static Power | $P_{static}$ | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static}$ | DC power in steady state | $\approx 0$ |
| Static Current | $I_{static}$ | $I_{static} \approx 0$ | Quiescent current (no direct path VDD→GND) | nA range (leakage only) |
| Fan-Out | $N$ | $N = C_{out} / C_{in}$ | Number of loads drivable | Practically unlimited (static) |
| Switching Threshold | $V_M$ | $V_M = \dfrac{r \cdot V_{DD}}{1 + r}$ where $r = k_n / k_p$ | Point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ (for symmetric inverter) |
| Transconductance Ratio | $k_n / k_p$ | $\dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p}$ | Relative drive strengths | $\approx 2$ (to compensate mobility) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Noise Margins:**
> $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$
> A gate is considered **regenerative** (and hence a *proper* digital gate) only if $NM_H > 0$ AND $NM_L > 0$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The static properties of the 2-input NAND are the *first checkpoint* in any chip design. They determine:

1. **Signal Integrity:** Whether the gate can communicate reliably with other gates in a long wire.
2. **Noise Immunity:** Whether the gate works in electrically noisy environments (automotive, industrial, RF).
3. **Power Budget:** Whether the gate meets low-power requirements (IoT, mobile).
4. **Compatibility:** Whether the gate can be cascaded with other logic families (TTL ↔ CMOS interfacing).

In modern ASIC design flows, these static parameters are extracted via **SPICE simulation** at the schematic level and via **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** at the gate level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of VOL for the 2-Input NAND (Both Inputs HIGH)

When $A = B = V_{DD}$, both NMOS transistors M3 and M4 are ON, and the output node F is being discharged through the series combination. The key question is: *What is the steady-state output voltage?*

### Step 1: Identify the Operating Region

For both NMOS transistors operating in the **linear (triode) region** at low $V_{DS}$, the current equation for each device is:

$$I_{D3} = k_{n3} \left[ (V_{GS3} - V_{Tn}) V_{DS3} - \dfrac{V_{DS3}^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_{D4} = k_{n4} \left[ (V_{GS4} - V_{Tn}) V_{DS4} - \dfrac{V_{DS4}^2}{2} \right]$$

### Step 2: Apply the Series Constraint

Because M3 and M4 are in **series**, the same current flows through both. The total voltage from output to ground is the sum:

$$V_{OL} = V_{DS3} + V_{DS4}$$

The constraint is:

$$I_{D3} = I_{D4} = I_{DS,series}$$

### Step 3: Simplify with Equal Sizing

If both NMOS are identically sized, $V_{DS3} = V_{DS4} = V_{OL}/2$. Equating the currents:

$$k_{n3} \left[ (V_{GS3} - V_{Tn}) \dfrac{V_{OL}}{2} - \dfrac{V_{OL}^2}{8} \right] = k_{n4} \left[ (V_{GS4} - V_{Tn}) \dfrac{V_{OL}}{2} - \dfrac{V_{OL}^2}{8} \right]$$

For both devices ON with $V_{GS3} = V_{GS4} = V_{DD}$, this is automatically satisfied.

### Step 4: Solve for VOL

$$\boxed{V_{OL} = \dfrac{2 \cdot k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}{k_n + k_n \cdot \text{(network factor)}}}$$

The critical insight is that the **series NMOS stack degrades VOL** because the available drive current is split across two transistors. To compensate, designers size M3 and M4 **larger** than a single inverter NMOS. Typically:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{NAND,\,NMOS} = 2 \times \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{inverter,\,NMOS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Body Effect Consideration:** In the series stack, the source of M3 is at $V_{OL}/2$ (not at ground). This raises the effective $V_{Tn}$ of M3 due to the body effect, further degrading $V_{OL}$. Designers account for this by sizing M3 *larger* than M4.

## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Margins from the VTC

The Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) of the 2-input NAND depends on which input is being swept. Let us sweep input **A** while holding input **B = VDD** (the worst case for noise margin).

### Step 1: Identify the Three Operating Regions

**Region 1:** $0 \leq V_{A} \leq V_{Tn}$ — M3 is OFF, output is HIGH (via M1).
- $V_{out} = V_{DD}$, slope = 0.

**Region 2:** $V_{Tn} \leq V_{A} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ — M3 and M4 both ON, M1 turning OFF.
- The PUN (M1) and PDN (M3-M4) both conduct → this is the **transition region**.
- $V_{out}$ falls sharply from $V_{DD}$ to 0.

**Region 3:** $V_{A} \geq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ — M1 is OFF, only PDN conducts.
- $V_{out} = 0$, slope = 0.

### Step 2: Define VIL

$V_{IL}$ is defined as the input voltage where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the falling edge of the VTC. By solving the KCL at the output node:

$$I_{D1,PMOS} + I_{D3,series} = 0$$

We get (for symmetric long-channel devices):

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 3 V_{DD} + 2 V_{Tn} \right) \quad \text{(approximate, single-input NAND)}$$

### Step 3: Define VIH

$V_{IH}$ is defined as the input voltage where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the rising edge. By a similar analysis:

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 5 V_{DD} - 2 V_{Tp} \right) \quad \text{(approximate, single-input NAND)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Noise Margins

Using $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{OL} = 0$:

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 5 V_{DD} - 2 V_{Tp} \right) = \dfrac{3 V_{DD}}{8} + \dfrac{V_{Tp}}{4}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 3 V_{DD} + 2 V_{Tn} \right) - 0 = \dfrac{3 V_{DD}}{8} + \dfrac{V_{Tn}}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $V_{DD} = 5$ V and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = 0.7$ V:
> $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3 \times 5}{8} + \dfrac{0.7}{4} = 1.875 + 0.175 = 2.05$ V
> This is a **massive** noise margin — about 41% of $V_{DD}$ — which is why CMOS is the dominant digital technology.

## 3.3 Derivation of Static Power Dissipation

In steady state, one of the two networks is **always OFF**:

- When output is HIGH, both PMOS may be ON, but both NMOS are OFF → no path from VDD to GND.
- When output is LOW, both NMOS are ON, but both PMOS are OFF → no path from VDD to GND.

The KCL at the output node yields:

$$I_{VDD} = I_{static} + I_{leakage} \approx I_{leakage}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} \approx 0 \text{ W (ideal CMOS)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real nanoscale CMOS, $I_{static}$ is not zero but is dominated by **subthreshold leakage** and **gate-oxide tunneling current**, which together can reach the $\mu$W range for millions of gates.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of VTC for Different Input Combinations

| Sweep Condition | Other Input | PUN Active | PDN Active | $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OL}$ | $V_M$ | NM Quality |
|:---------------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:----------:|:--------:|:--------:|:-----:|:----------:|
| Sweep A | B = VDD | M1 alone | M3-M4 series | $V_{DD}$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | **Worst case** (degraded) |
| Sweep A | B = GND | M1 and M2 | M3 alone | $V_{DD}$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | **Best case** (regenerative) |
| Sweep B | A = VDD | M2 alone | M3-M4 series | $V_{DD}$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | **Worst case** (degraded) |
| Sweep B | A = GND | M1 and M2 | M4 alone | $V_{DD}$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | **Best case** (regenerative) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transistor-Level Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **transistor-level connectivity** of the 2-input CMOS NAND gate, showing the source-drain paths explicitly.

```mermaid
graph TB
    VDD["VDD Supply"]
    A["Input A"]
    B["Input B"]
    F["Output F"]
    GND["Ground GND"]
    M1["M1 PMOS"]
    M2["M2 PMOS"]
    M3["M3 NMOS"]
    M4["M4 NMOS"]
    
    VDD --- M1
    VDD --- M2
    M1 --- F
    M2 --- F
    F --- M3
    M3 --- M4
    M4 --- GND
    
    A --- M1
    A --- M3
    B --- M2
    B --- M4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:**
> - **Top rail (VDD):** The two PMOS transistors **M1** and **M2** are connected *in parallel* to the output node **F**. The source of both is at VDD.
> - **Bottom rail (GND):** The two NMOS transistors **M3** and **M4** are connected *in series* from the output node **F** down to GND.
> - **Input A** drives the gate of **M1** (PMOS) and **M3** (NMOS).
> - **Input B** drives the gate of **M2** (PMOS) and **M4** (NMOS).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram shows the high-level conceptual flow of signals inside the gate, from input voltages to output voltage.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN_A["Input A Voltage"]
    IN_B["Input B Voltage"]
    DECODE["Logic Decoder 2x4"]
    PUN_CTRL["Pull-Up Network Control"]
    PDN_CTRL["Pull-Down Network Control"]
    PUN["PUN Parallel PMOS"]
    PDN["PDN Series NMOS"]
    VDD_NODE["VDD"]
    GND_NODE["GND"]
    OUT_F["Output F Voltage"]
    NM_CALC["Noise Margin Calculator"]
    
    IN_A --> DECODE
    IN_B --> DECODE
    DECODE --> PUN_CTRL
    DECODE --> PDN_CTRL
    PUN_CTRL --> PUN
    PDN_CTRL --> PDN
    VDD_NODE --> PUN
    PUN --> OUT_F
    PDN --> OUT_F
    GND_NODE --> PDN
    OUT_F --> NM_CALC
    IN_A --> NM_CALC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:**
> 1. The two input voltages are first *decoded* into 4 logic states.
> 2. Each state activates a specific combination of the PUN and PDN.
> 3. The PUN either connects VDD to the output (HIGH) or isolates it (floating).
> 4. The PDN either connects GND to the output (LOW) or isolates it (floating).
> 5. The Noise Margin Calculator block computes $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ from the resulting VTC.

## 4.3 Pull-Up and Pull-Down Network Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PUN["PULL-UP NETWORK PUN Parallel PMOS"]
        direction LR
        VDD1["VDD"] --- P1["M1 PMOS"]
        VDD1 --- P2["M2 PMOS"]
        P1 --- PUN_OUT["F"]
        P2 --- PUN_OUT
    end
    
    subgraph PDN["PULL-DOWN NETWORK PDN Series NMOS"]
        direction TB
        PDN_IN["F"] --- N1["M3 NMOS"]
        N1 --- N2["M4 NMOS"]
        N2 --- GND1["GND"]
    end
    
    PUN_OUT --- PDN_IN
```

> [!NOTE]
> This visualization clearly distinguishes the **parallel** nature of the PUN (PMOS branch) from the **series** nature of the PDN (NMOS branch). The output node F sits *between* the two networks, which is the key to understanding how static CMOS logic is constructed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: List any THREE static characteristics of a CMOS 2-input NAND gate and write their ideal values.**

**Model Answer:**

The three static characteristics of a CMOS 2-input NAND gate are:

1. **Output High Voltage ($V_{OH}$):** The voltage at the output when the gate output is in the HIGH state. **Ideal value: $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.**

2. **Output Low Voltage ($V_{OL}$):** The voltage at the output when the gate output is in the LOW state. **Ideal value: $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V** (with proper NMOS sizing).

3. **Static Power Dissipation ($P_{static}$):** The DC power consumed in steady state. **Ideal value: $P_{static} = 0$ W** (no direct current path between VDD and GND).

Additionally, the **Noise Margins** $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ are also static characteristics, both ideally $\geq 0.4 V_{DD}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Why is the static power dissipation of an ideal CMOS 2-input NAND gate equal to zero in steady state?**

**Model Answer:**

The static power dissipation of an ideal CMOS 2-input NAND gate is zero in steady state because of the **complementary nature** of the pull-up and pull-down networks. In any steady state (HIGH or LOW output), at least one of the two networks is **completely OFF**, so there is no conducting path from $V_{DD}$ to $GND$.

- **When output is HIGH:** The PDN (series NMOS) is OFF (at least one NMOS has gate = LOW), so no current flows to GND.
- **When output is LOW:** The PUN (parallel PMOS) is OFF (at least one PMOS has gate = HIGH), so no current flows from VDD.

Thus, $I_{static} = 0$ and $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = 0$ W.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate. Derive the expression for the output low voltage $V_{OL}$ considering the body effect on the series NMOS stack.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Noise Margins ($NM_H$ and $NM_L$) for a CMOS NAND gate. Show that the gate is regenerative only when $NM_H > 0$ and $NM_L > 0$.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Circuit Diagram**

The 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of:
- **Two PMOS (M1, M2) in parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and output F.
- **Two NMOS (M3, M4) in series** between output F and GND.
- Input A drives gates of M1, M3. Input B drives gates of M2, M4.

**Step 2: Operation Table**

| A | B | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | Output F |
|:-:|:-:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--------:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 0 V (LOW) |

**Step 3: Derivation of $V_{OL}$ with Body Effect**

When $A = B = V_{DD}$, both NMOS conduct and the output discharges through the series stack. The current in both devices must be equal:

$$I_{D3} = I_{D4}$$
