topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi-Output Domino Logic**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Output Domino Logic (MODL)** is an advanced CMOS dynamic logic design technique in which a *single* evaluation (pull-down/pull-up) network is structured to generate **two or more Boolean function outputs** at different electrical nodes within the same physical network. The technique exploits the fact that intermediate nodes of a series-parallel MOS network inherently implement *partial / sub-functions* of the variables applied to the gate, which can be buffered and used as legitimate outputs.

MODL belongs to the family of **domino CMOS logic** and is formally introduced in KTU Module 2 to demonstrate how transistor-level restructuring can reduce **area, switching capacitance, and dynamic power** without altering the logical function.

## 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Branching River Dam"**
>
> Imagine a hydroelectric dam (VDD source) feeding a river. The river splits into two main channels:
> - **Channel-1** has 3 small dams in series (valves A, B, C).
> - **Channel-2** branches off in the *middle* of Channel-1 (after valve A, before B) and goes straight to the ocean (GND).
>
> Water level at the **end of Channel-1** represents function $f_1 = \overline{A \cdot B \cdot C}$ — the ocean is reached only when *all three* valves open.
> Water level at the **branching point** of Channel-2 represents $f_2 = \overline{A}$ — water escapes if *only* valve A opens, *regardless* of B and C.
>
> In a single physical river network, we can **measure water levels at multiple points** to obtain different Boolean functions. MODL does the same thing with CMOS — it taps voltage at *different nodes* of one pull-down network to get multiple outputs.

## 🎯 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dynamic Node** | A capacitive node precharged to VDD every clock cycle |
| **Unateness** | A function is *positive unate* in $x_i$ if replacing $x_i$ by 0 → 1 never decreases $f$ |
| **Monotonic Transition** | A node may switch in **only one direction** during the evaluation phase (here, HIGH → LOW) |
| **Shared Evaluation Network** | The pull-down/pull-up tree that is common to two or more output functions |
| **Footless / Footed Transistor** | A series transistor at the bottom used to suppress leakage during precharge |

## 🏷️ Syllabus Tag

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — CMOS Subsystem Design**
> Learning outcome: *Design and analyse complex dynamic CMOS gates using multi-output domino logic, including transistor sharing for high-performance datapath blocks.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Why MODL Exists — The Problem With Wide Domino Gates

Standard **domino CMOS** has a well-known drawback: as the number of inputs (fan-in) grows, the **stack height** of the NMOS pull-down network increases. This leads to:

- Increased **parasitic capacitance** at the dynamic node
- Slower **evaluation delay** due to series resistance
- Larger **clock load** on the precharge/evaluate transistors
- Higher **dynamic power dissipation** ($P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MODL attacks this problem by **horizontally slicing** the wide function into multiple narrower, *transistor-shared* sub-functions whose outputs are combined externally. Two or more outputs are produced by the **same** evaluation tree.

## ⚙️ Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Precharge Phase** (CLK = 0): The PMOS precharge transistor turns ON. Both output nodes $N_1$ (top) and $N_2$ (intermediate) are pulled up to $V_{DD}$. All NMOS pull-down devices are OFF.
2. **Evaluate Phase** (CLK = $V_{DD}$): The precharge transistor turns OFF; the foot/eval transistor turns ON. Now, depending on input vectors, *some* internal nodes may discharge to GND.
3. **Discharge Path Analysis**:
   - $N_1$ discharges only when *all* transistors in the *longest* path from $N_1$ to GND are ON.
   - $N_2$ (an intermediate node) discharges when *all* transistors in its *shorter* path to GND are ON.
4. **Output Buffering**: Static CMOS inverters convert the dynamic (inverted) levels into non-inverting rail-to-rail outputs, completing the domino pipeline.

## 📐 Mathematical Conditions for Valid MODL

For two outputs $f_1$ and $f_2$ of a shared NMOS pull-down tree to be **valid MODL**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition 1 — Monotonicity (Single-Direction Switching):**
> Every dynamic node must transition in **at most one direction** (HIGH→LOW for NMOS pull-down) during evaluation. This is the standard domino constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition 2 — Positive Unateness:**
> All Boolean functions $f_i$ must be **positive unate** in every input variable that controls a shared NMOS transistor. Formally:
> $$f_i(x_1, \dots, x_{k-1}, 1, x_{k+1}, \dots, x_n) \;\geq\; f_i(x_1, \dots, x_{k-1}, 0, x_{k+1}, \dots, x_n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition 3 — Containment of Conducting Paths:**
> Every conducting path from $N_2$ to GND must **include** a conducting path from $N_1$ to GND. Equivalently, the pull-down network of $N_1$ must be a *superset* (in the series-parallel sense) of the pull-down network of $N_2$.

If these three conditions are satisfied, MODL is **functionally correct** and free of charge-sharing / race hazards.

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Use `\vert` instead of $\vert$ in any LaTeX enclosed in tables.**

| Symbol / Term | Expression | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Power dissipated charging/discharging node | W |
| Evaluation Delay | $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$ | RC delay of pull-down network | s |
| Effective Resistance | $R_{eq} = n \cdot R_{n}$ where $n$ = stack height | Resistance of series NMOS chain | $\Omega$ |
| Transistor Count Saved | $\Delta T = \sum_{i} T_{i}^{sep} - T_{shared}$ | Area benefit of MODL | # |
| Capacitance Ratio | $C_{N_2}/C_{N_1} < 1$ | Intermediate node is smaller → faster | unitless |
| Noise Margin | $NM_L = \vert V_{IL} - V_{OL} \vert$ | Robustness against noise | V |
| Unateness Test | $\partial f / \partial x_i \in \{0, +1\}$ | Function must be monotone in $x_i$ | — |
| Contention Ratio | $I_{ratio} = I_{drive,n} / I_{drive,p}$ | Ratio for up/down drive strength | — |

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

MODL is widely used in:
- **High-performance microprocessors** (ALU datapath, adder trees)
- **Manchester Carry Chains** (generate $G = AB$ and propagate $P = A \oplus B$ from one shared tree)
- **Content-Addressable Memories (CAM)** for match-line evaluation
- **Carry-save multipliers** where multiple intermediate sums/carrys share input transistors

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 🎯 Worked Example: Implementing $f_1 = AB + C$ and $f_2 = AB$ via MODL

We will design a single MODL gate that simultaneously realises two Boolean functions using **one shared** pull-down network. The derivation is fully algebraic and shows the transistor-count advantage.

### 🔢 Step 1 — Define the Two Target Functions

$$f_1(A, B, C) = A \cdot B + C$$

$$f_2(A, B) = A \cdot B$$

Both functions share the product term $A \cdot B$. This common sub-expression is the **structural leverage** that MODL exploits.

### 🔢 Step 2 — Construct the Standard (Non-MODL) Implementation

In **standard dynamic logic**, each function needs its own gate:

> **Gate-1 (for $f_1$):**
> Pull-down network = $A$ in series with $B$, **in parallel** with $C$.
> Transistor count = 3 NMOS (A, B, C) + 1 PMOS precharge + 1 NMOS evaluate = **5 NMOS + 1 PMOS**

> **Gate-2 (for $f_2$):**
> Pull-down network = $A$ in series with $B$.
> Transistor count = 2 NMOS (A, B) + 1 PMOS precharge + 1 NMOS evaluate = **4 NMOS + 1 PMOS**

> **Total Standard Cost:**
> $$T_{std} = 5 + 4 = 9 \text{ NMOS transistors} \quad (+ 2 \text{ PMOS precharge})$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Restructure the Logic for MODL Compatibility

Notice that $f_2 = A \cdot B$ is the *partial product* that already appears in $f_1$. If we expose the **internal node** between transistors $A$ and $B$ in Gate-1's network, that node will discharge to GND **only when** $A=1$ AND $B=1$ — exactly the condition for $f_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MODL insight: $f_2$ is a **node-discharge function** of an internal node of the $f_1$ network. The same physical NMOS stack serves *both* outputs.

### 🔢 Step 4 — Build the MODL Network Topology

Let node $N_1$ be the **top output** (dynamic node of $f_1$).
Let node $N_2$ be the **intermediate node** between NMOS A and NMOS B.

The complete pull-down network from $N_1$ to GND is:

$$N_1 \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\{A \text{ series } B\}}_{\text{stack to } N_2} \;\longrightarrow\; GND \quad \Vert \quad N_1 \;\longrightarrow\; C \;\longrightarrow\; GND$$

Where "$\Vert$" denotes the parallel combination of the two discharge paths.

### 🔢 Step 5 — Precharge Behaviour (CLK = 0)

With CLK low, the PMOS precharge device $M_P$ is ON:

$$V(N_1) \;\longrightarrow\; V_{DD}$$

$$V(N_2) \;\longrightarrow\; V_{DD} \quad \text{(charged through the OFF NMOS stack since A = B = 0)}$$

### 🔢 Step 6 — Evaluate Behaviour (CLK = $V_{DD}$)

The precharge PMOS turns OFF; the evaluate NMOS turns ON. The discharge of each node is governed by its own path:

**Discharge of $N_1$:**
$$N_1 \text{ discharges if } \bigl[(A = 1) \land (B = 1)\bigr] \;\lor\; (C = 1)$$

$$\Rightarrow\; \overline{f_1} = AB + C \quad \text{at node } N_1$$

**Discharge of $N_2$:**
$$N_2 \text{ discharges if } (A = 1) \land (B = 1) \quad \text{(the only path from } N_2 \text{ to GND)}$$

$$\Rightarrow\; \overline{f_2} = AB \quad \text{at node } N_2$$

### 🔢 Step 7 — Buffering the Outputs

Add two static CMOS inverters at $N_1$ and $N_2$ to restore full logic swing and fan-out drive:

$$\text{Out}_1 = \overline{\overline{f_1}} = f_1 = AB + C$$

$$\text{Out}_2 = \overline{\overline{f_2}} = f_2 = AB$$

### 🔢 Step 8 — Transistor Count Comparison

| Implementation | NMOS Count | PMOS Count | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard (2 separate gates) | $5 + 4 = 9$ | $1 + 1 = 2$ | 11 |
| **MODL (shared network)** | $\mathbf{3 + 1 = 4}$ | $\mathbf{1}$ | **5** |
| **Savings** | $\mathbf{5 \text{ NMOS}}$ | $\mathbf{1 \text{ PMOS}}$ | **6 (≈ 55 %)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The savings arise because the $(A, B)$ NMOS pair is **reused** for both outputs and the precharge PMOS is **shared** across the same dynamic node.

### 🔢 Step 9 — Verify Monotonicity & Unateness Conditions

Check **Condition 2 (Positive Unateness)** for $f_1$ and $f_2$:

$$\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial A} = B, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial B} = A, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial C} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{All } \in \{0, +1\} \;\checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial A} = B, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial B} = A \quad \Rightarrow \text{Both } \in \{0, +1\} \;\checkmark$$

Check **Condition 3 (Path Containment)**: The path $A \to B$ from $N_2$ to GND is a *sub-path* of the path $A \to B$ from $N_1$ to GND. $\checkmark$

All conditions are satisfied ⇒ the MODL design is **legally correct**.

### 🔢 Step 10 — Delay Comparison

The intermediate node $N_2$ has **smaller parasitic capacitance** than $N_1$ (it sees only 1 NMOS drain + 1 NMOS gate + wiring). Hence:

$$t_{pd, N_2} = 0.69 \, R_{B} \, C_{N_2} \quad < \quad t_{pd, N_1} = 0.69 \, (R_A + R_B) \, C_{N_1}$$

So $f_2$ becomes available *faster* than $f_1$ — a useful timing skew in pipelined datapaths.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Mermaid Block Diagram: MODL Architecture

> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `inA`), no reserved keywords are used, all labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]`**

### Q1. Define Multi-Output Domino Logic. State any **two** essential conditions for a valid MODL implementation. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Multi-Output Domino Logic (MODL)** is a CMOS dynamic design technique in which a single pull-down (or pull-up) network is shared among two or more Boolean functions, with the additional outputs tapped from **intermediate nodes** of the same transistor tree.
>
> Two essential conditions are:
> 1. **Monotonicity Condition:** All dynamic nodes must switch in only one direction (HIGH → LOW for NMOS pull-down) during the evaluation phase.
> 2. **Positive Unateness:** The output functions must be *positive unate* in every input variable controlling a shared NMOS transistor — no complemented inputs are allowed in the pull-down path.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### Q2. Compare MODL with a standard single-output domino CMOS gate in terms of (i) transistor count, (ii) speed, and (iii) monotonicity constraints. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standard Domino | MODL |
|---|---|---|
| Transistor Count | Higher (one full network per output) | **Lower** (network is *shared*) |
| Speed | Slower for wide fan-in | **Faster** (smaller intermediate-node capacitance) |
| Monotonicity | Required | **Required + Unateness + Path Containment** |

> MODL provides area/power savings at the cost of **stricter design constraints**.

---

## 📚 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]`

> **Q.A (a)** Explain the operating principle of **Multi-Output Domino Logic** with the help of a circuit diagram. Clearly state the role of intermediate nodes. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q.A (b)** Implement the functions $f_1 = AB + C$ and $f_2 = AB$ using a single MODL gate. Draw the transistor schematic and compute the transistor-count saving. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. MODL is built on the **domino CMOS** structure: a clocked PMOS precharges a dynamic node to $V_{DD}$ during $\overline{CLK}$, while an NMOS evaluate transistor enables discharge during CLK.
2. In MODL, the pull-down network is *deliberately* designed as a **series-parallel tree** in which multiple internal nodes are accessible.
3. Each internal node $N_i$ implements a *partial* function of the inputs — specifically, the conjunction of all transistors between that node and GND.
4. The dynamic node $N_1$ (top) implements the *full* function: $f_1 = $ "all paths from $N_1$ to GND".
5. A second output $f_2$ is obtained by attaching an inverter to an **intermediate node** $N_2$ that lies on a sub-path of the $N_1$ network. Hence $f_2 \subseteq f_1$ in the monotonic (H→L) sense.
6. The technique saves transistors because the precharge PMOS, evaluate NMOS, and shared series transistors are reused.

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

See **Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation** above.

- Network: A in series with B (with $N_2$ between them), C in parallel with the entire A-B branch.
- Transistor count = 3 NMOS (A, B, C) + 1 NMOS (foot) + 1 PMOS (precharge) + 2 inverters (4+4 = 8 transistors) = **17 transistors total** for the MODL gate.
- Standard two-gate implementation = 9 NMOS + 2 PMOS + 4 inverters (16 transistors) = **27 transistors total**.
- **Savings = 10 transistors (≈ 37 %)** in the entire gate-level implementation (counting all MOSFETs including inverter pairs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - Forgetting to **include the foot transistor** in the transistor-count comparison.
> - Drawing $N_2$ at the *wrong* position (it must lie **between** two series NMOS, not in a parallel branch).
> - Missing the **path-containment** check — students often assert MODL validity without verifying it.
> - Confusing MODL with **dual-output domino** (DODL) — DODL uses two separate networks; MODL uses one.

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set C]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **monotonicity** and **positive unateness** constraints in MODL. Why are they necessary? Illustrate with an example where the unateness condition is violated. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q.B (b)** Design a MODL implementation of a 1-bit **full adder** generating both **Sum** ($S = A \oplus B \oplus C$) and **Carry-out** ($C_{out} = AB + BC + CA$) using a 2-stage MODL structure. Justify the choice of intermediate functions. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

1. **Monotonicity Constraint:** All dynamic nodes must transition in a single direction (H→L for NMOS pull-down) during the evaluation phase. This prevents **race-through** of glitches into subsequent logic stages. If a node could rise during evaluation, it would cause erroneous pulses to propagate down the domino chain.
2. **Positive Unateness Constraint:** Every function $f_i$ realised by the shared tree must satisfy $\partial f_i / \partial x_k \in \{0, +1\}$ for every input $x_k$. This is necessary because NMOS transistors only respond to *high* gate voltages — they cannot be controlled by complemented inputs directly.
3. **Why Both Are Necessary:** Monotonicity guarantees **noise-immune** charge sharing; unateness guarantees **legal NMOS pull-down structure**. Together, they ensure the gate is hazard-free and transistor-realizable.
4. **Counter-example (unateness violated):** Consider the function $f = A + \overline{B}$. This is *negative unate* in $B$ — flipping $B$ from 0 to 1 *decreases* $f$. The pull-down network would require a PMOS in the discharge path, which is illegal in pure NMOS dynamic logic. The function must be **inverted and restructured** (e.g., $\overline{f} = \overline{A} \cdot B$, realised as a separate gate).

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

The 1-bit full adder is implemented in **two MODL stages**.

> **Stage-1 — MODL Gate producing $G$ and $\bar{P}$:**
> - $G = A \cdot B$ (generate)
> - $\bar{P} = \overline{A \oplus B} = AB + \overline{A}\,\overline{B}$ (propagate complement)
> - Shared NMOS tree: branches implement AB and $(\bar{A}\bar{B} + AB)$.
> - $G$ comes from the intermediate node of the AB series branch.
> - $\bar{P}$ comes from the dynamic top node.

> **Stage-2 — MODL Gate producing $S$ and $C_{out}$:**
> - $C_{out} = G + P \cdot C_{in}$
> - $S = P \oplus C_{in}$
> - The intermediate $P$ signal from Stage-1 is fed as the controlling variable to the Stage-2 shared network.
> - $C_{out}$ is obtained at the top dynamic node; $S$ is obtained at the XOR-style intermediate node.

This 2-stage MODL full adder typically uses **~ 24 transistors** versus **~ 40 transistors** in static CMOS — saving **~ 40 % area** and significantly reducing the critical-path delay through transistor sharing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - Treating $S$ and $C_{out}$ as outputs of a *single* MODL stage — incorrect; they require **two cascaded** stages.
> - Forgetting that $\bar{P}$ (propagate-complement) is the natural NMOS function; $P$ itself is not directly available without an extra inverter.
> - Ignoring the **clock distribution** requirement of dynamic logic (every MODL stage needs a clock signal and a foot transistor).
> - Not justifying the *choice* of $G$ and $\bar{P}$ as intermediate functions (they are the natural positive-unate decompositions of $S$ and $C_{out}$).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **MODL = One shared dynamic network ⇒ Multiple Boolean outputs.**
- The shared network must be **monotonic** (single-direction switching) and **positive unate** in every shared input.
- Intermediate nodes are *intentional* output taps — they implement **partial conjunctions** of the input stack.
- **Transistor-count savings** typically range from **30 % to 50 %** for two-output MODL gates versus equivalent separate-gate implementations.
- **Speed advantage** comes from reduced node capacitance on intermediate outputs.
- **Design rule:** Place the *more frequently used* output at the *intermediate* node (smaller capacitance ⇒ faster).
- **Caveat:** Every MODL stage needs clock distribution → contributes to clock loading.
- MODL is **not** a separate logic family — it is a *transistor-sharing optimisation* of standard domino CMOS.
- The **path-containment** check (Condition 3) is the most-frequently-skipped validation in KTU answers; always verify it.
- Common KTU examples: full adders, Manchester carry chains, parity generators, content-addressable memories.
- MODL gates must be **cascaded carefully** — output of one MODL stage feeds the next, and the *unateness* property must be preserved across stages.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *"design using MODL"*, always include (1) the SOP/unate form, (2) the transistor schematic with *both* output nodes labelled, (3) the unateness proof, and (4) a transistor-count comparison table. Examiners award the full 14 marks only when all four are present.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual-Rail Domino Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dual-Rail Domino Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dual-Rail Domino Logic** is a *differential dynamic CMOS logic style* in which every logic variable **X** is represented by **two complementary signal wires**, namely **X (true rail)** and **X̄ (complement rail, often written as X_b)**. Unlike single-rail (standard) domino, the logical complement is generated intrinsically by the pull-down network (PDN) structure rather than by a discrete output inverter, thereby removing the inverting-output restriction of conventional domino logic.

In a Dual-Rail Domino gate, during the **precharge (Φ = 0)** phase, both output nodes (Out and Out_b) are precharged to **V_DD**. During the **evaluate (Φ = 1)** phase, the pull-down network evaluates the input condition. If the inputs satisfy the function `F`, the node **Out** discharges to **GND** (LOW) while **Out_b** stays at **V_DD** (HIGH). Conversely, if the inputs satisfy `F̄`, the **Out_b** node discharges while **Out** stays HIGH. Because both true and complement outputs are generated *monotonically* (only falling transitions during evaluation), the gate can directly cascade into another dual-rail domino gate without violating monotonicity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a *dual-rail signal* like a **two-track railway switch**. Track A represents the TRUE answer and Track B represents the FALSE answer. Both tracks start lifted up (precharged). When the train (input data) arrives, depending on which direction it needs to go, **only one track lowers down** to let it through — the other remains lifted. A passenger watching either track instantly knows the answer *and* its complement. Single-rail domino is like having only Track A and needing a mirror to see Track B (which is slow and adds delay). Dual-rail gives you **both views natively, simultaneously, and with no inversion penalty**.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics (Single-Line Summary)

- **Differential signaling:** True and complement outputs are both computed in one gate.
- **No output inverter required:** Removes the *non-inverting buffer* needed in standard domino for cascading.
- **Monotonic transitions preserved:** Both rails only make **falling transitions** during evaluation — safe for cascading.
- **Static logic compatibility:** Since both polarities exist, the output can be treated as a static signal (after a sense amplifier or latch) for interfacing with standard CMOS.
- **Area overhead:** Approximately **2× the transistors and wiring** of single-rail domino for the same logical function.

## 1.4 Physical / Operating Constants Referenced

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| V_DD | Supply Voltage | **1.0 V – 1.8 V** (modern processes) |
| V_Tn | NMOS Threshold | **~0.4 V** |
| C_L | Output Load Capacitance | **10 fF – 100 fF** per node |
| t_pd | Evaluation Delay | **~100 ps – 300 ps** |
| T_clk | Clock Period | **~1 ns – 2 ns** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

### Phase 1 — Precharge (Φ = 0, clock LOW)

1. PMOS precharge transistors **M_p1** and **M_p2** turn ON.
2. Both output nodes **Out** and **Out_b** are pulled up to **V_DD** through these PMOS devices.
3. The NMOS evaluation transistor (footer) is **OFF**, blocking any discharge path to GND.
4. The PDN is **isolated** from GND, so input signals cannot discharge either node yet.
5. Both rails sit at HIGH — this is the *quiescent / reset state*.

> [!NOTE]
> During precharge, both Out and Out_b are **simultaneously HIGH**. This violates the usual digital invariant "one rail HIGH, one LOW," but it is *acceptable* because dual-rail logic relies on the **transitions** in the evaluate phase, not the absolute precharge values, for correct downstream cascading.

### Phase 2 — Evaluate (Φ = 1, clock HIGH)

1. Precharge PMOS transistors **turn OFF**, isolating Out and Out_b from V_DD.
2. The NMOS footer transistor turns ON, connecting the PDN to **GND**.
3. The PDN (a differential NMOS tree) evaluates the inputs.
4. **Exactly one of two complementary conditions** will be true:
   - If **F(inputs) = 1** → the left PDN branch conducts → **Out discharges** to GND, **Out_b remains HIGH**.
   - If **F̄(inputs) = 1** → the right PDN branch conducts → **Out_b discharges** to GND, **Out remains HIGH**.
5. Both rails undergo **monotonic falling transitions only** (never rising during evaluate).
6. Output is now **stable and differential**, ready to drive the next domino stage.

## 2.2 Why Dual-Rail? — The "Why" Behind the Design

### The Problem with Single-Rail Domino

Standard domino has an **inverting output**. To compute `F AND G`, you cannot directly cascade because each gate inverts. You must add a static CMOS inverter after each domino gate to restore non-inverting behavior. This **inverter adds delay, area, and power**, and worse, it creates a **non-monotonic** intermediate signal that **breaks cascading**.

### The Dual-Rail Solution

By computing **both F and F̄ in the same gate**, dual-rail domino:

- **Eliminates the external inverter** → faster, smaller, lower power.
- **Preserves monotonicity on both rails** → gates cascade safely without buffers.
- **Provides a differential signal** → excellent noise immunity, suited for **domino adders, multipliers, SRAM sense amplifiers, and high-speed datapaths**.

### The Trade-off

- **2× area and wiring** per gate.
- **2× clock loading** (two precharge transistors and two output nodes).
- **Higher dynamic power** due to both rails switching.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| **#** | **Concept / Formula** | **Expression / Symbol** | **Use / Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dual-Rail Signal Representation | $X = (X,\; \bar{X})$ | Each variable uses 2 wires |
| 2 | Precharge Condition | $\Phi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; Out = V_{DD},\; \bar{Out} = V_{DD}$ | Both nodes high in reset |
| 3 | Evaluate Condition (F true) | $\Phi = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; Out \rightarrow 0,\; \bar{Out} = V_{DD}$ | Left rail falls |
| 4 | Evaluate Condition (F̄ true) | $\Phi = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \bar{Out} \rightarrow 0,\; Out = V_{DD}$ | Right rail falls |
| 5 | Evaluate Delay | $t_{pd} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{DSAT}}$ | First-order RC delay |
| 6 | Power Dissipation (Dynamic) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Per switching event |
| 7 | Total Dual-Rail Power | $P_{total} \approx 2 \cdot P_{single\text{-}rail}$ | Both rails active |
| 8 | Logical Completeness | $F + \bar{F} = 1$ | One branch always evaluates |
| 9 | Monotonicity Condition | $\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \le 0 \text{ during evaluate}$ | Falling only — no glitches |
| 10 | Sense Amp Decision Time | $t_{sa} = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{sense}}$ | Static conversion to CMOS levels |
| 11 | Charge Sharing Limit | $\Delta V_{out} \le \frac{C_{PDN}}{C_{PDN} + C_L} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Must be $<$ V_Tn for safety |
| 12 | Noise Margin (differential) | $NM_{diff} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Improved vs single-ended |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The differential nature of dual-rail means the **noise margin is calculated as a differential quantity**, often giving **2× improvement** over single-ended signaling at the cost of doubled interconnect.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **High-speed carry-skip and carry-select adders** (e.g., 64-bit adders in modern CPUs).
- **Multipliers and ALUs** in superscalar processors (Intel Pentium, AMD Ryzen datapaths).
- **Content-Addressable Memories (CAMs)** where match lines are differential.
- **SRAM sense amplifiers** — dual-rail domino sense amps are industry standard.
- **Phase-locked loops (PLLs)** for fine-grain timing recovery.
- **Read channels of disk drives** and high-speed serial link front-ends.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Discharge Delay of a Dual-Rail Domino Gate

We derive the **evaluation delay** for a dual-rail domino gate discharging one of its output nodes through an NMOS pull-down network treated as a single equivalent transistor.

**Step 1 — Define the discharge current.**

For a saturated NMOS transistor in the pull-down network, the drain current (using the **square-law model**) is given by:

$$
I_{DSAT} = \frac{k_n}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}
$$

where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ is the process transconductance parameter.

**Step 2 — Define the load capacitance to be discharged.**

The output node sees a lumped load:

$$
C_L = C_{gate,\,next} + C_{wire} + C_{diffusion}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the basic RC discharge relation.**

The time to discharge the output from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (the switching threshold) is approximately:

$$
t_{pd} \;\approx\; \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{DSAT}}
$$

where $\Delta V = V_{DD}/2$ for a 50% switching point.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pd} &\;\approx\; \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{\dfrac{k_n}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}} \\
\\
t_{pd} &\;\approx\; \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The logic here is: **bigger transistors (larger W/L) ⇒ faster discharge**; **larger loads (bigger C_L) ⇒ slower discharge**; **lower V_DD ⇒ quadratically slower** because both ΔV shrinks and (V_GS − V_Tn) shrinks.

**Step 5 — Dual-Rail modification.**

Because dual-rail gates have **two output nodes**, each with its own precharge PMOS and PDN branch, the effective per-rail capacitance is approximately:

$$
C_{L,\text{eff}} = C_{L} + C_{inter\text{-}rail}
$$

where $C_{inter\text{-}rail}$ is the coupling capacitance between the two differential output lines. The **differential delay** (time between the two rails transitioning) is typically the same as the single-rail value, but **absolute** delay is roughly:

$$
t_{pd,\text{dual-rail}} \;\approx\; t_{pd,\text{single-rail}} \cdot (1 + \eta)
$$

where $\eta$ is a small overhead factor ($\eta \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$) capturing the inter-rail coupling.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Noise Margin

For a differential pair of signals $(V_{out},\; V_{\bar{out}})$, the differential voltage is:

$$
V_{diff} \;=\; V_{out} - V_{\bar{out}}
$$

**Step 1 — At logic '1' (F true):**

$$
V_{diff,\text{high}} \;=\; V_{out,\text{HIGH}} - V_{\bar{out},\text{LOW}} \;\approx\; V_{DD} - 0 \;=\; V_{DD}
$$

**Step 2 — At logic '0' (F̄ true):**

$$
V_{diff,\text{low}} \;=\; V_{out,\text{LOW}} - V_{\bar{out},\text{HIGH}} \;\approx\; 0 - V_{DD} \;=\; -V_{DD}
$$

**Step 3 — Differential noise margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_{\text{diff}} &= \frac{\vert V_{diff,\text{high}} - V_{diff,\text{low}} \vert}{2} \\
\\
&= \frac{\vert V_{DD} - (-V_{DD}) \vert}{2} \\
\\
&= V_{DD}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** The differential noise margin **scales directly with V_DD**, whereas single-ended noise margin is limited by transistor thresholds (typically **0.3 × V_DD**). This is why high-speed differential circuits (LVDS, dual-rail domino) are preferred in noisy environments.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge Sharing Worst Case

Suppose the PDN is partially ON during evaluate, creating a charge-sharing path between an internal node (initial voltage $V_x$) and the output node (precharged to $V_{DD}$).

**Step 1 — Charge conservation:**

$$
C_x \cdot V_x + C_L \cdot V_{DD} \;=\; (C_x + C_L) \cdot V_{\text{final}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for final voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{final}} \;=\; \frac{C_x \cdot V_x + C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_x + C_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop on the output:**

$$
\Delta V_{out} \;=\; V_{DD} - V_{\text{final}} \;=\; \frac{C_x}{C_x + C_L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_x)
$$

**Step 4 — Safety constraint (must stay above V_Tn):**

$$
\Delta V_{out} \;\le\; V_{DD} - V_{Tn}
$$

> Substituting:

$$
\frac{C_x}{C_x + C_L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_x) \;\le\; V_{DD} - V_{Tn}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the safe ratio $C_x/C_L$:**

$$
\frac{C_x}{C_L} \;\le\; \frac{V_{DD} - V_{Tn}}{V_x - V_{Tn}}
$$

> **Logic:** To keep the output safely above the NMOS threshold (avoiding false evaluation), the **internal node capacitance must be small relative to the load capacitance**. This is why dual-rail domino gates typically have a **keeper transistor** or careful sizing to avoid charge-sharing failures.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single-Rail vs Dual-Rail Domino

| **Parameter** | **Single-Rail Domino** | **Dual-Rail Domino** | **Ratio / Comment** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output rails per gate | 1 | 2 | **2× wiring** |
| External inverter needed | **Yes** (for cascading non-inverting function) | **No** | **Delay saved** |
| Transistors per gate (typical) | $N+3$ | $2N+6$ | **~2×** |
| Power (dynamic) | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ | $\approx 2\alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ | **~2×** |
| Cascading depth | Limited by monotonicity + buffers | Direct cascading | **Easier** |
| Noise margin (differential) | $\approx 0.3 V_{DD}$ | $\approx V_{DD}$ | **~3.3× better** |
| Logical completeness | Single boolean | Both true and complement | **Information-doubling** |
| Layout density | High | Medium | **Trade-off** |
| Typical use | Datapath ALUs | Sense amps, fast adders | **Application-specific** |
| Susceptibility to charge sharing | Moderate | Higher (two nodes) | **Needs keeper** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dual-Rail Domino AND/NAND Gate (Generic Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clkA["Clock Phi"]:::clk
    prA["PMOS Precharge Left"]:::pmos
    prB["PMOS Precharge Right"]:::pmos
    evalA["NMOS Footer"]:::nmos
    pdnL["Left PDN branch evaluates F"]:::pdn
    pdnR["Right PDN branch evaluates F_bar"]:::pdn
    outA["Out - True Rail"]:::node
    outB["Out_b - Complement Rail"]:::node
    nextA["Next Dual Rail Gate Input True"]:::next
    nextB["Next Dual Rail Gate Input Complement"]:::next

    clkA --> prA
    clkA --> prB
    clkA --> evalA
    prA --> outA
    prB --> outB
    evalA --> pdnL
    evalA --> pdnR
    pdnL --> outA
    pdnR --> outB
    outA --> nextA
    outB --> nextB

    classDef clk fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3d85c6,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#6aa84f,color:#000
    classDef pdn fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#c27ba0,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#e69138,color:#000
    classDef next fill:#d0e0e3,stroke:#45818e,color:#000
```

> The diagram illustrates: **one clock** drives three transistors (two precharge PMOS, one NMOS footer). The **PDN splits into two parallel branches**: the left implements function F (drives Out LOW when F=1), the right implements F̄ (drives Out_b LOW when F̄=1). Outputs cascade directly into the next dual-rail gate — **no inverter needed**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Operational Phases of a Dual-Rail Domino Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Clock Edge Arrives"]):::entry
    phCheck{Is Phi equal to 0 ?}:::decision
    prech["Precharge Phase Both Out and Out_b pulled to VDD through PMOS transistors"]:::phase
    eval["Evaluate Phase PMOS OFF, NMOS footer ON, PDN connects to GND"]:::phase
    chkF{Does F of inputs equal 1 ?}:::decision
    disOut["Left PDN conducts Out discharges to GND, Out_b remains at VDD"]:::evalstep
    disOutB["Right PDN conducts Out_b discharges to GND, Out remains at VDD"]:::evalstep
    stable["Both rails are now monotonic and complementary - output is valid"]:::stable
    next["Output drives next dual-rail domino stage directly - no inverter needed"]:::stable

    start --> phCheck
    phCheck -- Yes --> prech --> chkF
    phCheck -- No --> eval --> chkF
    chkF -- Yes --> disOut --> stable
    chkF -- No --> disOutB --> stable
    stable --> next

    classDef entry fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3d85c6,color:#000
    classDef evalstep fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#c27ba0,color:#000
    classDef stable fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Dual-Rail Domino in a Datapath

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputReg["Input Register Bank True and Complement pairs stored"]:::reg
    drGate1["Dual Rail Domino Gate 1 e.g. AND of A and B outputs F1 and F1_bar"]:::dr
    drGate2["Dual Rail Domino Gate 2 e.g. OR of F1 and C outputs F2 and F2_bar"]:::dr
    drGate3["Dual Rail Domino Gate 3 e.g. XOR of F2 and D outputs OUT and OUT_bar"]:::dr
    senseAmp["Differential Sense Amplifier Converts dynamic to static CMOS levels"]:::sa
    outputReg["Static CMOS Output Register"]:::reg
    clkBus["Global Clock Phi - drives all precharge and footer transistors"]:::clk

    inputReg --> drGate1 --> drGate2 --> drGate3 --> senseAmp --> outputReg
    clkBus -.precharge and evaluate.-> drGate1
    clkBus -.precharge and evaluate.-> drGate2
    clkBus -.precharge and evaluate.-> drGate3

    classDef reg fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef dr fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3d85c6,color:#000
    classDef sa fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#c27ba0,color:#000
    classDef clk fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Each `Dual Rail Domino Gate` block represents a full differential dynamic stage. The dotted lines from the clock bus represent the **global precharge/evaluate distribution** — a critical timing path in any high-speed dual-rail design. The **Sense Amplifier** is the interface that converts the monotonic dynamic differential signal back into a static full-swing CMOS signal for downstream static logic.

## 4.4 Schematic Symbol Table (Transistor-Level Mapping)

| **Transistor** | **Type** | **Gate Signal** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|---|
| M_p1 | PMOS | Φ (clock) | Precharges **Out** to V_DD |
| M_p2 | PMOS | Φ (clock) | Precharges **Out_b** to V_DD |
| M_eval | NMOS | Φ (clock) | Footer — enables evaluate phase |
| M_1 ... M_k | NMOS | Inputs A, B, ... | Pull-down network evaluating F |
| M_1' ... M_k' | NMOS | Inputs (complement) | Pull-down network evaluating F̄ |
| M_keeper (optional) | Weak PMOS | Feedback from Out/Out_b | Holds precharge against leakage |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**"Differentiate between single-rail and dual-rail domino logic. Mention any two advantages of dual-rail domino."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Single-Rail Domino:**
> - Uses **only one output rail** per gate.
> - Output is **inverting**; requires an external static CMOS inverter for cascading non-inverting functions.
> - The added inverter creates a **non-monotonic intermediate**, breaking direct cascading.

> **Dual-Rail Domino:**
> - Uses **two output rails**: $Out$ (true) and $\bar{Out}$ (complement).
> - Both true and complement outputs are generated **intrinsically** by the differential PDN.
> - **No external inverter needed** — gates cascade directly.

> **Two Advantages of Dual-Rail Domino:**
> 1. **Eliminates the inverting-output restriction**, enabling direct cascading of domino gates and reducing critical-path delay.
> 2. **Higher differential noise margin** (≈ V_DD) compared to single-ended (≈ 0.3 V_DD), improving robustness in noisy high-speed environments.
> 3. *(Bonus)* Provides both polarities of the function in a single gate, simplifying sense-amplifier interfacing.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is the role of the precharge and evaluation phases in a dual-rail domino gate? Why are both rails precharged to V_DD?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of Precharge (Φ = 0):**
> - The PMOS precharge transistors (M_p1, M_p2) turn ON and pull **both Out and Out_b to V_DD**.
> - The NMOS footer is OFF, so the PDN is **isolated from GND** — inputs cannot discharge the outputs.
> - This **resets** the gate to a known initial state every clock cycle.

> **Role of Evaluate (Φ = 1):**
> - Precharge PMOS turn OFF; the NMOS footer turns ON, **enabling the PDN**.
> - The PDN evaluates the inputs. Exactly **one rail (Out or Out_b) will discharge** based on whether F or F̄ is true.
> - The result is a **monotonic falling transition** — safe for cascading into the next dual-rail gate.

> **Why both rails precharged to V_DD:**
> - Because only one rail needs to fall during evaluate (the other stays HIGH), precharging both ensures that **both true and complement outputs are simultaneously valid HIGH at the start of evaluate**.
> - This guarantees **monotonicity on both rails** (only falling transitions), which is the *fundamental requirement* for cascading dynamic domino logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Module 2]**

**"Explain the structure and operation of a Dual-Rail Domino Logic gate with a neat circuit diagram. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of standard single-rail domino logic. Also derive the expression for its evaluation delay."** *(14 Marks)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Structure of Dual-Rail Domino Gate — 7 Marks**

1. **Circuit Components** (1 mark):
   - 2 PMOS precharge transistors (M_p1, M_p2) — gated by Φ.
   - 1 NMOS evaluation footer (M_eval) — gated by Φ.
   - 2 PDN branches: left implements F, right implements F̄.
   - 2 output nodes: **Out** and **Out_b**.

2. **Circuit Diagram** (2 marks):

```
        VDD              VDD
         |                |
        [Mp1]            [Mp2]      ← PMOS precharge (gated by Φ)
         |                |
         |---Out          |---Out_b
         |                |
       [PDN: F]         [PDN: F̄]    ← NMOS pull-down networks
         |                |
         +-----[Meval]---+           ← NMOS footer (gated by Φ)
                |
               GND
```

3. **Operation** (2 marks):
   - **Precharge (Φ = 0):** Mp1, Mp2 ON → Out = Out_b = V_DD. Meval OFF.
   - **Evaluate (Φ = 1):** Mp1, Mp2 OFF. Meval ON. If F = 1, left PDN conducts → Out → 0, Out_b stays V_DD. If F̄ = 1, right PDN conducts → Out_b → 0, Out stays V_DD.

4. **Overcoming Single-Rail Limitations** (2 marks):
   - **Removes external inverter** → saves delay, area, power.
   - **Monotonic differential outputs** → cascades safely.
   - **Higher noise margin** → robust in noisy environments.
   - **Both polarities available** → simplifies sense-amp interface.

**(b) Evaluation Delay Derivation — 7 Marks**

1. **Model the discharge** as an RC network: Output capacitance $C_L$ discharged by NMOS pull-down current $I_{DSAT}$. (1 mark)

2. **Square-law current:** (1 mark)
$$
I_{DSAT} = \frac{k_n}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}
$$

3. **Discharge time to V_DD/2:** (2 marks)
$$
t_{pd} \;\approx\; \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{I_{DSAT}}
$$

4. **Final expression:** (1 mark)
$$
t_{pd} \;\approx\; \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}}
$$

5. **Interpretation & dual-rail correction:** (2 marks)
   - Larger W/L → faster; larger C_L → slower.
   - Dual-rail overhead: $t_{pd,\text{dual}} \approx t_{pd,\text{single}} \cdot (1 + \eta)$ where $\eta \approx 0.05–0.15$.
   - Real-world typical $t_{pd} \approx 100–300$ ps in modern 28 nm processes.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**"With necessary diagrams, explain the concept of Dual-Rail Domino Logic. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications in modern VLSI design."** *(14 Marks)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Concept and Circuit Explanation — 7 Marks**

1. **Definition:** Dual-rail domino is a differential dynamic CMOS logic style where each variable X is represented as $(X, \bar{X})$. (1 mark)

2. **Circuit Structure:** (2 marks)
   - Two PMOS precharge devices, one NMOS footer, two complementary PDN branches.
   - Precharge (Φ=0): Both Out and Out_b = V_DD.
   - Evaluate (Φ=1): Exactly one rail discharges based on the inputs.

3. **Comparison Table:** (2 marks)
   - Single-rail: 1 output, inverting, needs inverter, lower noise margin.
   - Dual-rail: 2 outputs, non-inverting natively, no inverter, higher noise margin.

4. **Monotonicity Principle:** (2 marks)
   - Both rails only make falling transitions during evaluate.
   - This guarantees safe cascading in dynamic logic.

**(b) Advantages, Disadvantages, and Applications — 7 Marks**

1. **Advantages:** (3 marks)
   - **No output inverter** → faster, smaller.
   - **High differential noise margin** (~V_DD vs ~0.3 V_DD).
   - **Both polarities generated simultaneously** → simpler sense-amp design.
   - **Suitable for high-speed cascading** without buffering.

2. **Disadvantages:** (2 marks)
   - **2× area** (wires + transistors).
   - **2× dynamic power** (both rails switch).
   - **Charge-sharing risk** — needs keeper transistors.
   - **Clock loading doubled** → higher clock tree power.

3. **Applications:** (2 marks)
   - **Carry-skip and carry-select adders** in CPUs.
   - **SRAM sense amplifiers**.
   - **High-speed ALUs and multipliers**.
   - **Content-Addressable Memories (CAMs)**.
   - **Read channels in disk drives / serial links**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "inverting" vs "non-inverting":** Many students wrongly say "dual-rail domino is non-inverting" — but each **individual rail IS still inverting**. It is the **pair together** that provides both polarities. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the NMOS footer:** Students often draw only precharge PMOS, forgetting the evaluation NMOS footer transistor. This is essential — marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating "monotonic" transitions:** The entire reason dual-rail works for cascading is the monotonic falling-only behavior. Without this statement, the answer is incomplete — marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping charge-sharing analysis:** KTU frequently asks about charge-sharing in dynamic logic. Students who skip the $\Delta V_{out}$ formula lose **2 marks**.
> 5. **Writing inverter-based delay formula instead of RC discharge:** Many confuse CMOS inverter delay (which uses $\tau = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$) with the discharge delay formula. Use $t_{pd} \approx C_L V_{DD} / I_{DSAT}$ — marks lost: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dual-Rail Domino Logic**

- ✅ Dual-rail domino represents every signal as $(X, \bar{X})$ — **two wires per variable**.
- ✅ **No external inverter needed** — the complement is generated intrinsically by the differential PDN.
- ✅ Both outputs are **precharged to V_DD** during Φ = 0 (reset phase).
- ✅ During **evaluate (Φ = 1)**, exactly **one rail discharges** while the other **stays HIGH**.
- ✅ Only **falling (monotonic) transitions** occur during evaluate → enables **direct cascading**.
- ✅ Two precharge PMOS + one NMOS footer + two PDN branches (F and F̄) → basic topology.
- ✅ **Advantages:** higher noise margin (~V_DD differential), removes inverting-output bottleneck, both polarities available.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** ~2× area, ~2× dynamic power, charge-sharing risk, clock loading doubled.
- ✅ **Keeper transistor** often required to maintain precharge against leakage and charge sharing.
- ✅ **Evaluation delay formula:** $t_{pd} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{Tn})^2}$.
- ✅ **Differential noise margin** = $V_{DD}$, vs ~$0.3 V_{DD}$ for single-ended → **~3× improvement**.
- ✅ **Applications:** high-speed adders/multipliers, SRAM sense amplifiers, CAMs, ALUs, read channels.
- ✅ **Static conversion:** use a **sense amplifier** at the output to convert dynamic differential to static CMOS levels.
- ✅ **Monotonicity rule:** $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} \le 0$ during the entire evaluate phase — never rising.
- ✅ **Charge-sharing safety condition:** $\Delta V_{out} \le V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ — keeps output safely above the NMOS threshold.
- ✅ **Examiner's red flags to avoid:** forgetting the footer transistor, confusing inverting vs non-inverting rails, skipping monotonicity statement, using inverter delay formula instead of discharge formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NP Domino Logic (NORA)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** NP Domino Logic (NORA)

# NP Domino Logic (NORA) – Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **NORA (NO-RAce) Domino Logic**, also called **NP Domino Logic**, is an advanced dynamic CMOS logic family introduced by Pfennings and Wu (1984). It merges the architecture of **NP-CMOS logic** and **Domino CMOS logic** by cascading alternating **n-block** (n-tree logic) and **p-block** (p-tree logic) dynamic stages in a pipeline, all driven by a single clock ($\phi$) and its complement ($\overline{\phi}$). The technique eliminates the race-through (charge redistribution) problem inherent in classical CMOS domino logic, enabling true **pipelined operation** at high clock frequencies.

In simpler terms, NORA is a clever way of "pipelining" dynamic logic gates. Standard Domino only allows **n-type** pull-down logic blocks, which means every stage evaluates in the same clock half. NORA flips the script — it allows the designer to alternate between **n-type logic** (pull-down) and **p-type logic** (pull-up) stages, where one half of the pipeline is precharging while the other is evaluating, and vice-versa.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Two-Way Escalator" Analogy**
> Imagine a shopping mall escalator:
> - **Standard Domino Logic** = a single escalator going *only up*. All people (data) must ride in the same direction during the same half of the day, causing congestion.
> - **NORA (NP Domino)** = a **two-way escalator**. People riding *up* on one section can step onto a section going *down* on the next step. The two halves of the system alternate — one is "precharging" (resetting) while the other "evaluates" (transports people). There is **no race** because the two halves never push data in conflicting directions simultaneously.

The "NO-RAce" name literally means: the structure is designed such that the dynamic node and the output node of adjacent stages **never toggle in a way that creates a short-circuit race condition** between the precharge transistor and the pull-up/pull-down network.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Design Metrics

- **Single Clock Family:** Uses only $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$ (no extra delayed clock needed like in standard domino).
- **Pipeline Depth Scaling:** $N$ cascaded stages = $N/2$ logical levels in one clock period.
- **Static Power:** Zero static power in true NORA (no DC path between $V_{DD}$ and GND during steady state).
- **Logic Capacity:** Both **non-inverting (n-block)** and **inverting-equivalent (p-block)** functions are realized without external inverters between stages.
- **Charge-Sharing Sensitivity:** Moderate — the dynamic node is sensitive to leakage and capacitive coupling, but precharge PMOS in each cell restores the full-swing $V_{DD}$.

## 1.4 NORA in the KTU Module-2 Context

In the **Digital CMOS Design** syllabus, NORA is presented as the **pipelined, race-free evolution of Domino Logic**. It is the bridge between:
- Classical Dynamic Logic (precharge–evaluate, single clock).
- True Pipeline Architectures (multi-stage, single-clock, race-free operation).

The topic is **high-yield** for KTU University Exams, often appearing as a 7-mark or 14-mark Part B question under Module 2 ("Combinational and Sequential MOS Logic Circuits").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# NP Domino Logic (NORA) – Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks

A NORA stage is built from two complementary dynamic cells, shown schematically in **Figure 1** (Section 4):

| Cell Type | Driver Network | Precharge Device | Evaluate Device | Output Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Block (n-Cell)** | nMOS pull-down logic tree | PMOS (to $V_{DD}$) | nMOS (to GND) | **True (non-inverted)** of logic |
| **p-Block (p-Cell)** | pMOS pull-up logic tree | nMOS (to GND) | PMOS (to $V_{DD}$) | **Inverted** of logic |

> [!NOTE]
> The **p-block** uses a pMOS logic network. During precharge, the dynamic node is pulled to **GND** (via nMOS precharge). During evaluate, if the pMOS network conducts, the node is pulled up to $V_{DD}$. Because of this, p-block logic is **logically equivalent to an inverting function** — which is exactly what is needed to feed the next n-block stage.

## 2.2 Operational Phases (Clock Cycle)

A NORA pipeline alternates between two global phases driven by clock $\phi$:

### Phase 1 — $\phi = 0$ (Precharge Half)
- All **n-block** stages: dynamic node $\to V_{DD}$ (PMOS precharge ON). Output of next stage = '1' (since inverter sees '1' on dynamic node).
- All **p-block** stages: dynamic node $\to 0V$ (nMOS precharge ON). Output of next stage = '1'.

> **Net result:** Every stage in the pipeline produces a **HIGH** output during precharge, which is the **safe idle state**.

### Phase 2 — $\phi = 1$ (Evaluate Half)
- All **n-block** stages: PMOS precharge OFF, nMOS evaluate ON. If the nMOS logic tree conducts $\Rightarrow$ dynamic node discharges $\Rightarrow$ output = '0'. If not, dynamic node remains HIGH $\Rightarrow$ output = '1'.
- All **p-block** stages: nMOS precharge OFF, pMOS evaluate ON. If the pMOS logic tree conducts $\Rightarrow$ dynamic node charges $\Rightarrow$ output = '0'. If not, dynamic node remains LOW $\Rightarrow$ output = '1'.

## 2.3 The "NO RAce" Property — Why It Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Race Condition Definition:** A race exists in a clocked logic family if the output of a stage can change **twice** within one clock period (once during precharge, once during evaluate) before being latched — leading to false evaluation.

In **standard Domino**:
- Output can only make **one** transition per cycle (0→1 during precharge, then 1→0 conditionally during evaluate). It is intrinsically race-free — but only because every stage is **n-type pull-down**.

In **NORA**, the architecture alternates **n-blocks** and **p-blocks**:
- During $\phi = 0$: every dynamic node is **precharged to a known value** ($V_{DD}$ for n-blocks, $0V$ for p-blocks). The static CMOS inverters in each cell then drive their outputs HIGH.
- During $\phi = 1$: the logic networks evaluate, but **no path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND** because precharge and evaluate transistors are never ON simultaneously within the same cell.

Thus, the output of each stage makes **at most one transition per cycle** — exactly the domino property — hence the name **NO-RAce**.

## 2.4 Cascading / Pipeline Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **NORA Pipeline Cascading Rule**
> - An **n-block** cell (true logic) is followed by a **p-block** cell (inverting logic), and vice versa.
> - The static inverter inside each cell buffers the dynamic node, providing drive for the next stage.
> - In the steady-state evaluate phase, the chain of alternating n/p cells propagates the logic function from the first stage to the last — all within a single $\phi = 1$ half-period.

The result is a **pipeline** of $N$ NORA cells that processes one logic operation per clock cycle with full $V_{DD}$ swing at every output.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic node precharge voltage (n-block) | $V_{pre,n} = V_{DD}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Dynamic node precharge voltage (p-block) | $V_{pre,p} = 0$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Output HIGH during precharge | $V_{out,pre} = V_{DD}$ | V (both cell types) |
| 4 | Output after evaluate (n-block, conducting) | $V_{out,eval} = 0$ | V |
| 5 | Output after evaluate (n-block, non-conducting) | $V_{out,eval} = V_{DD}$ | V |
| 6 | Max pipeline depth per clock half-period | $N_{max} = \left\lfloor t_{half} / t_{pd,cell} \right\rfloor$ | stages |
| 7 | Effective logic depth per clock cycle | $N_{eff} = N_{max} / 2$ | logical gates |
| 8 | Static power dissipation | $P_{static} = 0$ (ideal) | Watts |
| 9 | Dynamic power (per cell) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts |
| 10 | Min clock period | $T_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{pd,cell} + t_{setup}$ | seconds |
| 11 | Charge-sharing voltage drop | $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{node}}{C_{node} + C_{L}}$ | V |
| 12 | Noise margin (HIGH) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | V |
| 13 | Noise margin (LOW) | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | V |
| 14 | Throughput | $f_{clk} = 1/T_{min}$ | Hz |
| 15 | Transistor count reduction vs Static CMOS | $\approx 25\%$ to $40\%$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exam Purposes:** Memorize rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, and 11. The charge-sharing equation (row 11) is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How NORA Is Used |
|---|---|
| **High-Speed Adders** | Carry-lookahead / Manchester carry chains in NORA pipeline form (e.g., AMD Athlon, Pentium classic ALUs). |
| **Microprocessor Datapaths** | Wide datapath busses where full-swing, race-free, pipelined logic is required. |
| **DRAM Sense Amplifiers** | Adjacent-stage driving structure is conceptually identical to NORA cascading. |
| **Signal Processing ASICs** | Pipelined multipliers and FIR filters in DSPs. |
| **Modern FinFET / 3nm Designs** | Concept survives — replaced by "domino" or "zipper" logic in scaled nodes due to leakage. |

## 2.7 Comparison Table: NORA vs Standard Domino vs NP-CMOS

| Parameter | Standard Domino | NP-CMOS (Static) | **NORA (NP Domino)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logic function per stage | Non-inverting | Both polarities possible | Both polarities (alt. cells) |
| Cascade requirement | Inverter-free | Static logic | Alternates n/p cells |
| Race-free? | Yes (intrinsic) | Yes (static) | **Yes (NO-RAce)** |
| Pipelineable? | No (single half-cycle) | No (full cycle) | **Yes (per half-cycle)** |
| Static power | $\approx 0$ | Small (leakage) | $\approx 0$ |
| Charge sharing risk | Yes | No | Yes (mitigated by precharge size) |
| Transistor count (per gate) | $N+3$ (n-tree + 2) | $2N+2$ | $N+3$ (similar to domino) |
| KTU exam weight | High | Medium | **High** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# NP Domino Logic (NORA) – Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Exhaustive Operation Analysis — A 2-Stage NORA Pipeline

Let us analyse the canonical NORA pipeline shown in **Figure 2 (Section 4)** — an n-block stage driving a p-block stage — for the logic function $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ implemented in the p-block, with inputs $A, B$ arriving from the n-block's output.

### Step 1: Identify Cell Types and Node Names

- **Stage 1 (n-block):** Implements $F_n = A \cdot B$ (true AND). Nodes: dynamic node $X_1$, output $Out_1$.
- **Stage 2 (p-block):** Implements $F_p = \overline{A \cdot B}$ (logically equivalent to NAND). Nodes: dynamic node $X_2$, output $Y$.

### Step 2: Transistor-Level Description

**Stage 1 (n-block):**
- PMOS precharge $M_{p1}$: gate $=\phi$, source $=V_{DD}$, drain $=X_1$.
- nMOS evaluate $M_{e1}$: gate $=\phi$, source $=GND$, drain $=$ top of n-tree.
- nMOS logic tree: $M_{A}$, $M_{B}$ in series (AND function).
- Static CMOS inverter: $\text{Inv}_1$ between $X_1$ and $Out_1$.

**Stage 2 (p-block):**
- nMOS precharge $M_{n2}$: gate $=\overline{\phi}$, source $=GND$, drain $=X_2$.
- PMOS evaluate $M_{ep2}$: gate $=\overline{\phi}$, source $=V_{DD}$, drain $=$ top of p-tree.
- pMOS logic tree: $M_{A}'$, $M_{B}'$ in series (pMOS series = NAND function when active).
- Static CMOS inverter: $\text{Inv}_2$ between $X_2$ and $Y$.

### Step 3: Analyse Phase $\phi = 0$ (Precharge)

- $M_{p1}$ ON (PMOS with gate LOW) $\Rightarrow X_1$ charges to $V_{DD}$.
- $M_{e1}$ OFF (nMOS with gate LOW) $\Rightarrow$ n-tree isolated from GND.
- $\text{Inv}_1$ sees $V_{DD}$ on input $\Rightarrow Out_1 = 0$ V (LOW). 

  > **WAIT** — Re-examining: an inverter with input HIGH gives output LOW. But standard Domino output during precharge is **HIGH** because there is a **static (ratioed) keeper** or because the precharged node drives a high-to-low transition into the buffer. Let us correct this with the **standard CMOS Domino** convention used in NORA:

- In NORA, the dynamic node is precharged HIGH in n-block, but the **buffer** inside the n-cell is an **inverter**, so the output goes LOW. However, the **external next stage** (a p-block) only responds to its own precharge phase, so this is consistent. The key invariant: **during precharge, all dynamic nodes are restored to a defined state**.

- $M_{n2}$ ON (nMOS with gate HIGH, since $\overline{\phi}=1$) $\Rightarrow X_2$ discharges to GND.
- $M_{ep2}$ OFF (PMOS with gate HIGH) $\Rightarrow$ p-tree isolated from $V_{DD}$.
- $\text{Inv}_2$ sees GND on input $\Rightarrow Y = V_{DD}$ (HIGH).

### Step 4: Analyse Phase $\phi = 1$ (Evaluate)

- $M_{p1}$ OFF, $M_{e1}$ ON.
- If $A = 1$ AND $B = 1$: n-tree conducts $\Rightarrow X_1$ discharges through $M_{e1}$ to GND.
- If $A = 0$ OR $B = 0$: n-tree does not conduct $\Rightarrow X_1$ stays at $V_{DD}$ (held by precharge capacitance $C_{X1}$).
- $Out_1 = \text{Inv}_1(X_1)$ $\Rightarrow$ the logic function is delivered to Stage 2.

- Simultaneously, $M_{n2}$ OFF, $M_{ep2}$ ON.
- $Out_1$ (i.e., $A$ and $B$ logically) feeds the gates of $M_{A}'$, $M_{B}'$ (pMOS tree of Stage 2).
- If both $Out_1$ signals are LOW (i.e., $A=0$ or $B=0$): at least one pMOS in series is ON $\Rightarrow X_2$ charges to $V_{DD}$ $\Rightarrow Y = 0$ V.
- If both $Out_1$ signals are HIGH ($A=1$ and $B=1$): pMOS series is OFF $\Rightarrow X_2$ stays at GND $\Rightarrow Y = V_{DD}$ (HIGH).

This implements $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ = NAND, completing the 2-stage pipeline logic.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Charge-Sharing Voltage Drop

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic KTU 3-mark question**: "Derive the expression for voltage drop due to charge sharing in a dynamic logic gate."

Consider an n-block NORA cell with:
- Precharged dynamic node $X$ at $V_{DD}$.
- Total capacitance on $X$ = $C_X$ (precharge drain cap + interconnect).
- An additional internal node $Z$ (within the logic tree) with capacitance $C_Z$ initially at $0$ V.

When the evaluate clock arrives, suppose the conducting path connects $X$ to $Z$ (and **not** to GND — otherwise $X$ just discharges to 0 V and charge sharing is irrelevant). Charge redistributes between $C_X$ and $C_Z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{before}} &= C_X \cdot V_{DD} + C_Z \cdot 0 = C_X \cdot V_{DD} \\
Q_{\text{after}}  &= (C_X + C_Z) \cdot V_{X,\text{final}} \\
\text{By charge conservation:} \quad Q_{\text{before}} &= Q_{\text{after}} \\
C_X \cdot V_{DD} &= (C_X + C_Z) \cdot V_{X,\text{final}} \\
V_{X,\text{final}} &= V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_X}{C_X + C_Z} \\
\therefore \quad \Delta V &= V_{DD} - V_{X,\text{final}} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_Z}{C_X + C_Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The voltage drop is **zero** if $C_Z = 0$ (no parasitic cap) and approaches $V_{DD}$ if $C_Z \gg C_X$. To minimize $\Delta V$, designers either:
- Increase $C_X$ by adding a deliberate "bleeder" capacitor.
- Ensure the logic tree is sized so $C_X \gg C_Z$ in the worst case.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Minimum Clock Period of a NORA Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **typical 7-mark KTU Part B question** for Module 2.

Let a NORA pipeline have $N$ cascaded cells, alternating n/p. Let each cell's worst-case propagation delay be $t_{pd}$. Let the setup time of the input latch be $t_{su}$.

The clock has two halves: precharge (duration $t_{\phi 0}$) and evaluate (duration $t_{\phi 1}$), with $t_{\phi 0} + t_{\phi 1} = T_{clk}$.

For race-free operation:
- $t_{\phi 0} \geq t_{pd}$ of any single cell (to fully precharge all dynamic nodes).
- $t_{\phi 1} \geq (N/2) \cdot t_{pd} + t_{su}$ (to evaluate the entire logic chain).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{min} &= t_{\phi 0} + t_{\phi 1} \\
        &= t_{pd} + \left(\dfrac{N}{2}\right) \cdot t_{pd} + t_{su} \\
        &= t_{pd} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{N}{2}\right) + t_{su}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **balanced design** ($t_{\phi 0} = t_{\phi 1} = T_{min}/2$):

$$
\dfrac{T_{min}}{2} = \max\left\{t_{pd}, \; \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot t_{pd} + t_{su}\right\}
$$

If $N \geq 2$ (typical case):

$$
\dfrac{T_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot t_{pd} + t_{su}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
T_{min} = N \cdot t_{pd} + 2 t_{su}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** A 2-stage NORA pipeline ($N=2$) can complete one full logic operation per clock cycle, exactly like a static CMOS pipeline, but with **fewer transistors**.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 6-stage NORA pipeline has $t_{pd,cell} = 0.4$ ns per cell and $t_{su} = 0.1$ ns. Compute the maximum clock frequency.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 6 \text{ stages} \\
t_{\phi 1} &= \left(\dfrac{N}{2}\right) \cdot t_{pd} + t_{su} = 3 \cdot 0.4 + 0.1 = 1.3 \text{ ns} \\
t_{\phi 0} &= t_{pd} = 0.4 \text{ ns} \\
T_{min} &= t_{\phi 0} + t_{\phi 1} = 0.4 + 1.3 = 1.7 \text{ ns} \\
f_{max} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{min}} = \dfrac{1}{1.7 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 588.2 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $f_{max} \approx 588$ MHz.

## 3.5 Noise Margin Derivation (Brief)

For a NORA cell, the noise margin is determined by the static CMOS inverter inside the cell. The HIGH noise margin is:

$$
NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}
$$

where $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (ideal precharge) and $V_{IH}$ is the inverter's input-high threshold. For a symmetric inverter with $k_n = k_p$:

$$
V_{IH} \approx \dfrac{1}{8}\left(5V_{DD} - 2V_t\right) \;\text{(approximate)}
$$

This is identical to standard CMOS noise margins, confirming that NORA preserves robust static-CMOS-like noise immunity at its buffered outputs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# NP Domino Logic (NORA) – Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level NORA Cell Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph NBLK["N BLOCK CELL"]
        PHI["Clock phi"] -- gate --> MPRE_N["PMOS Precharge"]
        VDD1["VDD"] -- source --> MPRE_N
        MPRE_N -- drain --> X1["Dynamic Node X1"]
        PHI -- gate --> MEVAL_N["NMOS Evaluate"]
        MEVAL_N -- source --> GND1["GND"]
        MEVAL_N -- drain --> NTREE["nMOS Logic Tree"]
        NTREE -- gate --> INA["Input A"]
        NTREE -- gate --> INB["Input B"]
        NTREE -- drain --> X1
        X1 -- in --> INV1["Static CMOS Inverter"]
        INV1 -- out --> OUT1["Output Out1 non inverting"]
    end

    subgraph PBLK["P BLOCK CELL"]
        PNOT["Clock phi_bar"] -- gate --> MPRE_P["NMOS Precharge"]
        GND2["GND"] -- source --> MPRE_P
        MPRE_P -- drain --> X2["Dynamic Node X2"]
        PNOT -- gate --> MEVAL_P["PMOS Evaluate"]
        VDD2["VDD"] -- source --> MEVAL_P
        MEVAL_P -- drain --> PTREE["pMOS Logic Tree"]
        PTREE -- gate --> INC["Input C from Out1"]
        PTREE -- gate --> IND["Input D from Out1"]
        PTREE -- drain --> X2
        X2 -- in --> INV2["Static CMOS Inverter"]
        INV2 -- out --> OUT2["Output Out2 inverting"]
    end

    OUT1 -- drives --> PTREE
    style NBLK fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style PBLK fill:#fff0e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style X1 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
    style X2 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **N BLOCK CELL** on the left precharges $X_1$ to $V_{DD}$ when $\phi=0$ and evaluates the nMOS tree when $\phi=1$.
- The **P BLOCK CELL** on the right precharges $X_2$ to $0$ V when $\overline{\phi}=1$ (i.e., $\phi=0$) and evaluates the pMOS tree when $\overline{\phi}=0$ (i.e., $\phi=1$).
- The two cells share the **same clock** family and pipeline data from left to right.

## 4.2 NORA 2-Stage Pipeline: AND followed by NAND

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input A"] -- n-tree gate --> STAGE1["STAGE 1 n block AND"]
    B["Input B"] -- n-tree gate --> STAGE1
    PHI["Clock phi"] -- precharge and evaluate --> STAGE1
    STAGE1 -- Out1 drives p-tree --> STAGE2["STAGE 2 p block NAND"]
    PHIbar["Clock phi_bar"] -- precharge and evaluate --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 -- Y --> YNODE["Y equals A.B complement"]

    style STAGE1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d7a,stroke-width:2px
    style STAGE2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#7a3d00,stroke-width:2px
    style YNODE fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Clock-Phase Timing Diagram (Mermaid Gantt Equivalent)

```mermaid
gantt
    title NORA Clock Phases and Pipeline Activity
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Clock phi
    phi equals 0 precharge half :a1, 0, 5
    phi equals 1 evaluate half   :a2, 5, 10

    section N Block Stage
    Precharging X1 to VDD    :n0, 0, 5
    Evaluating nMOS tree      :n1, 5, 10

    section P Block Stage
    Precharging X2 to 0V     :p0, 0, 5
    Evaluating pMOS tree      :p1, 5, 10

    section Pipeline Throughput
    One logic result per 10 ns :t0, 0, 10
```

## 4.4 Functional State-Machine of a NORA Cell

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Precharge
    Precharge : Precharge Dynamic Node to VDD for n block, 0V for p block
    Precharge --> Evaluate : phi transitions 0 to 1
    Evaluate : Logic Tree Active, Output Transitions Conditionally
    Evaluate --> Hold : phi transitions 1 to 0
    Hold : Dynamic Node Held by Parasitic Capacitance
    Hold --> Precharge : Next Clock Cycle
    Evaluate --> [*] : Optional Reset for Test
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Fallback:** Detailed transistor-level stick diagrams of NORA cells are not well-suited to Mermaid's text-based rendering. The block-level functional architecture above (4.1 – 4.4) faithfully represents the **clock distribution, logic-block interaction, precharge/evaluate sequence, and pipeline structure** that KTU examiners expect students to draw in their answer sheets.

```
          |
        |-+|
        | |<- Mp1 (PMOS precharge, gate = phi)
        |-+|
          |----------+----------+----------+
          |          |          |          |

          |          |          |          |

          +------|     |---------+ Out1 (true logic output)
                 |_____|         |

                                 |-+

        +-----+    +-----+        |
   A ---|     |----|     |---------+   (series nMOS = AND function)

```
