For 1 Mbit = $2^{20}$ bitlines, total dynamic power ≈ $10 \text{ } \mu\text{W} \times 2^{20} \approx 10.5$ W (worst-case full toggle). In practice, with realistic $\alpha < 0.05$ and activity patterns, the actual power is much lower, around **100–500 mW**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Memory Cell Comparison (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

| Parameter | 6T SRAM | 1T1C DRAM | NOR ROM | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell Size ($F^2$)** | 120–150 | 6–8 | 6–10 | 4–5 |
| **Access Time** | < 1 ns | 10–50 ns | 10–100 ns | 25–200 µs (page read) |
| **Volatile?** | Yes | Yes | No (mask) | No |
| **Refresh Needed?** | No | Yes (64 ms) | No | No |
| **Endurance (Writes)** | Unlimited | $> 10^{15}$ | N/A | $10^4$–$10^6$ cycles |
| **Power per Bit (Read)** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Process Complexity** | Standard CMOS | Special cap. | Standard | Floating-gate |
| **Use Case** | CPU Cache | Main Memory | Firmware, BIOS | SSDs, USB |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Memory Chip Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Address Bus] --> B[Row Decoder]
    A --> C[Column Decoder]
    D[Control Logic CS WE OE] --> E[Timing Control]
    B --> F[Memory Array Rows]
    F --> G[Bitline Pairs]
    G --> H[Column MUX]
    H --> I[Sense Amplifier Bank]
    I --> J[Write Driver Bank]
    J --> K[Data I/O Buffers]
    E --> B
    E --> C
    E --> I
    E --> J
    C --> H
    L[Precharge Circuit] --> G
    E --> L
    M[External Data Bus] --> K
    K --> M
```

### 4.2 Schematic: The 6T SRAM Cell (Transistor-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    WL[WordLine WL] --- N5
    N5[Access NMOS M5] --- Q
    WL2[WordLine WL] --- N6
    N6[Access NMOS M6] --- QBAR
    BL[Bitline BL] --- N5
    BLBAR[Bitline BL_bar] --- N6
    Q[Storage Node Q] --- M1
    M1[Pull-Down NMOS M1] --- GND
    Q --- M2
    M2[Pull-Up PMOS M2] --- VDD
    QBAR[Storage Node Q_bar] --- M3
    M3[Pull-Down NMOS M3] --- GND
    QBAR --- M4
    M4[Pull-Up PMOS M4] --- VDD
    Q --- M4
    QBAR --- M2
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Read Operation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Address Valid] --> S2[Step 2 Precharge BL and BL_bar to VDD]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Row Decoder Activates Wordline WL]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Access Transistors Turn ON]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Charge Sharing Develops DeltaV on Bitline]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Sense Enable SE Triggers Sense Amplifier]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Sense Amp Latches Data to Full Rail]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Column Decoder Routes to Output Pad]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Output Enable OE Activates Data Bus]
```

### 4.4 DRAM Architecture: Sense Amplifier Placement

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Memory Array Top Half] --> SA
    B[Memory Array Bottom Half] --> SA
    SA[Shared Sense Amplifier Center]
    SA --> EQ[Equalize Circuit]
    SA --> BLT[Bitline True BL]
    SA --> BLC[Bitline Complement BL_bar]
    EQ --> BLT
    EQ --> BLC
    SA --> OUT[Output to Column MUX]
```

### 4.5 Row Decoder Tree (3-to-8 Decoder Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[A2 A1 A0 Address Inputs] --> PD[Pre-decoder 3-to-8 AND]
    PD --> R0[Wordline 0]
    PD --> R1[Wordline 1]
    PD --> R2[Wordline 2]
    PD --> R3[Wordline 3]
    PD --> R4[Wordline 4]
    PD --> R5[Wordline 5]
    PD --> R6[Wordline 6]
    PD --> R7[Wordline 7]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Expectation:** When asked to "draw the memory architecture," the **MUST-HAVE** blocks are: **Address buffer → Row Decoder → Array → Sense Amplifier → Column Decoder → Output Buffer**. Skipping any of these loses 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term **"bit-cell"** in a semiconductor memory. List the four main components of a memory chip and their functions.

**Model Answer:**
A *bit-cell* is the fundamental storage element of a semiconductor memory that holds one bit (0 or 1) of information. The four main components are:
1. **Memory Array** — A matrix of bit-cells organized in rows and columns storing the data.
2. **Address Decoders (Row & Column)** — Decode the input address and activate the corresponding wordline and bitlines.
3. **Sense Amplifiers** — Detect and amplify the small voltage swing on the bitlines to full digital levels during a read.
4. **Control/Write Logic** — Manages read/write timing, precharge, and write-driver activation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM in terms of cell structure, volatility, and refresh requirement.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SRAM (6T) | DRAM (1T1C) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | 6 transistors (cross-coupled latches) | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor |
| **Volatility** | Volatile (loses data on power-off) | Volatile (loses data on power-off) |
| **Refresh** | **Not required** | **Required every ≈ 64 ms** |
| **Density** | Low (≈ 120 $F^2$) | High (≈ 6 $F^2$) |
| **Speed** | Fast (< 1 ns) | Slower (10–50 ns) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: SRAM Cell Design** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Draw the schematic of a standard **6T SRAM cell** and explain its **Read, Write, and Hold** operations with neat waveforms. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Cell Ratio (CR)** condition for **read stability** and explain what happens if the cell ratio is chosen too small. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic and Operations:**
- The 6T SRAM cell consists of two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1, M2) and (M3, M4) forming a bistable latch, plus two NMOS access transistors M5 and M6 controlled by the wordline WL.
- **Hold:** WL = 0 V, M5 and M6 OFF. The latch retains its state as long as $V_{DD}$ is supplied.
- **Read:** BL and $\overline{BL}$ are precharged to $V_{DD}$. WL is pulsed HIGH. A small differential voltage (≈ 100–200 mV) develops on the bitlines depending on stored data. Sense amp amplifies this to full rail.
- **Write:** WL is pulsed HIGH. The write driver forces BL and $\overline{BL}$ to complementary values. The access transistors overpower the latch pull-ups, flipping the cell.

**(b) Cell Ratio Derivation:**
As derived in Section 3.1, the read stability requires the pull-down NMOS to be stronger than the access transistor. The cell ratio is:

$$CR = \frac{(W/L)_{pull\text{-}down}}{(W/L)_{access}} > 1.2$$

**If CR is too small** (e.g., CR = 0.8): During a read, the access transistor can pull the storage `0` node above the switching threshold of the opposite inverter. This causes a **destructive read** — the cell flips its state, losing the stored data. The read operation becomes unreliable. Designers ensure CR ≥ 1.5 with process corner margins.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: DRAM, ROM, and Sense Amplifiers** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **1T1C DRAM cell** operation during Read and Write. Derive the expression for the bitline voltage swing. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **NOR ROM** and **NAND ROM** architectures. Draw their cell structures and explain why NAND has higher density. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 1T1C DRAM Operation:**
- **Write:** Wordline WL → HIGH, access transistor ON. Bitline voltage ($V_{DD}$ for `1`, 0 for `0`) charges the storage capacitor $C_S$.
- **Read:** Bitline is precharged to $V_{DD}/2$. WL → HIGH. Charge sharing occurs between $C_S$ and bitline capacitance $C_{BL}$. Final voltage:
$$V_f = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{stored} + \frac{C_{BL}}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$
- The bitline swing is typically **100–250 mV**, requiring a sense amplifier.
- A periodic **refresh** (every 64 ms) rewrites the cell to compensate for leakage.

**(b) NOR vs NAND ROM:**

| Aspect | NOR ROM | NAND ROM |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | One NMOS per cell, source to GND, drain to bitline | NMOS transistors in series (NAND string) |
| **Density** | Lower (10 $F^2$) | Higher (4–5 $F^2$) |
| **Random Read** | Fast (supports random access) | Slower (page-based, serial) |
| **Write/Erase** | Slow (per-byte) | Fast (per-page/block) |
| **Application** | BIOS, code execution | SSDs, USB, mass storage |

**NAND is denser** because multiple cells share the same bitline contact, source line, and wordline routing — eliminating per-cell isolation overhead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Cell Ratio" and "Pull-Down Ratio":** The Cell Ratio (CR) is for read stability, the Pull-Down Ratio (PDR) is for writability. Mixing them up loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Hold" state:** Many students describe only Read and Write, forgetting the Hold (standby) state. KTU expects all **three** operations to be mentioned. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Drawing the sense amp inside the cell:** The sense amplifier is **peripheral** circuitry, NOT inside the memory cell. Drawing it inside loses marks.
> 4. **Units in power calculation:** Writing $P_{dyn} = 10$ without specifying **µW** or **mW** loses **1 mark**. Always include units.
> 5. **Skipping the charge-sharing equation in DRAM:** A 14-mark DRAM question **always** requires the $\Delta V_{BL}$ derivation. Skipping it = lose 5 marks.
> 6. **Wrong index for ROM comparison:** Writing "NOR is denser than NAND" loses 3 marks. The truth is **NAND is denser** but slower in random access.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **6T SRAM cell** has **2 cross-coupled inverters + 2 access NMOS** = 6 transistors.
- ☐ **Cell Ratio** $CR = (W/L)_{pull\text{-}down} / (W/L)_{access}$ must be **> 1.2** for read stability.
- ☐ **Pull-Down Ratio** $PDR = (W/L)_{access} / (W/L)_{pull\text{-}up}$ must be **< 1.8** for writability.
- ☐ **DRAM** = **1T + 1C**, requires **refresh every 64 ms**, bitline swing ≈ **100–250 mV**.
- ☐ **DRAM cell area ≈ 6 $F^2$**, **SRAM cell area ≈ 120 $F^2$**.
- ☐ **Bitline swing** $\Delta V_{BL} = \dfrac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{stored}$.
- ☐ **NOR ROM** is fast random-read; **NAND ROM** is high-density page-read.
- ☐ **Sense amplifier** amplifies a small **differential** bitline voltage to full CMOS levels.
- ☐ **Row decoder** selects one wordline; **Column decoder/MUX** routes the bitline to the output pad.
- ☐ **Address lines required** = $\log_2(\text{number of words})$, **independent of word width**.
- ☐ **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$; **Static power** $P_{stat} = I_{leak} V_{DD}$.
- ☐ **Memory hierarchy (fastest to slowest):** Register → SRAM Cache → DRAM → NAND Flash → HDD.
- ☐ A **memory cell** is **NOT** the same as a **memory word**. Cell = 1 bit; Word = N bits.
- ☐ For a 1 Mbit memory, the number of address lines is **20** (since $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$).
- ☐ **Refresh** in DRAM is a periodic **Read + Rewrite** operation; it consumes **≈ 1–2%** of total memory bandwidth.

---

# 1. SRAM – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Static Random-Access Memory (SRAM)** is a *volatile* semiconductor memory that stores each bit of data in a bistable latch built from cross-coupled CMOS inverters. As long as the power supply $V_{DD}$ is maintained, the stored value remains *static* without the need for periodic refresh cycles (which distinguishes it from DRAM). Data is accessed via a randomly-addressable word-organized array.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Examiner Language):** *"An SRAM cell is a bistable flip-flop based memory element using cross-coupled inverters whose state is preserved as long as $V_{DD}$ is connected — making it the fastest on-chip memory structure used for cache, registers, and look-up tables."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Two Door Puzzle":** Imagine two heavy swinging doors (the cross-coupled inverters) that are connected so that pushing one open automatically *locks* the other shut, and vice versa. A passerby (the access transistor) can briefly unlock the doors (the *wordline* rises) to read which one is open, or to push them into a new position (write). However, the doors only stay in their current position if the building still has power — pull the mains, and both doors swing to the center and the memory is lost. This is exactly how an SRAM cell works.

## 1.3 Why SRAM Matters in CMOS Design

SRAM is the **workhorse memory of every modern microprocessor**, occupying the largest silicon real estate in:
- **L1 / L2 / L3 CPU caches**
- **Register files** inside the ALU
- **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** of FPGAs
- **Buffers & FIFOs** in networking ASICs

The dominant cell in production chips today is the **6T (six-transistor) CMOS SRAM cell** because it offers the best *density–stability–speed* trade-off.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Benchmark Metrics (always bolded in KTU answers):**
> - **Cell area** is reported in terms of **F²** (where F = minimum feature size)
> - **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** measured in **Volts**
> - **Access time $t_a$** measured in **nanoseconds**
> - **Read/Write power** in **µW/cell**
> - **Cell Ratio (β)** and **Pull-up Ratio (γ)** are dimensionless design parameters

## 1.4 Cell Evolution & Topological Variants

| Cell Type | Transistor Count | Use Case |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **6T Cell** | 6 | Industry standard, L1 cache |
| **4T Loadless Cell** | 4 | High-density, requires special process |
| **5T Cell** | 5 | Single-ended read, small area |
| **8T / 10T Cell** | 8 / 10 | Differential read, ultra-low voltage operation |
| **DICE / ST-DICE** | 10 / 12 | Radiation-hardened (aerospace) |

For KTU 2024 Module 3, the **6T SRAM cell is the primary focus** of every derivable question.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6T SRAM Cell — Anatomy

A standard 6T cell consists of two CMOS inverters cross-coupled to form a bistable latch, plus two NMOS access transistors gated by the Wordline (WL):

- **M1, M2** → Driver NMOS of the two inverters
- **M3, M4** → Pull-up PMOS loads
- **M5, M6** → Access NMOS transistors

The two storage nodes are the internal nodes **Q** and **$\bar{Q}$**. The two complementary bitlines are **BL** and **$\overline{BL}$** (also called BLB).

## 2.2 Modes of Operation

A 6T SRAM cell has **three distinct operating modes** (KTU examiner favourite):

1. **Hold Mode** — WL = 0; cell is isolated, retaining its data.
2. **Read Mode** — WL = 1; BL and $\overline{BL}$ are precharged, then a small differential voltage develops across them depending on stored data.
3. **Write Mode** — WL = 1; one of the bitlines is driven low to flip the cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** A *robust* SRAM cell must be **read-stable** (data must not flip during read) **and** *write-able* (data must flip during write) — these two requirements create a fundamental design tension solved by careful transistor sizing.

## 2.3 Critical Sizing Ratios (The "Heart" of SRAM Design)

The *cell ratio* controls read stability; the *pull-up ratio* controls write-ability.

### 2.3.1 Cell Ratio (β)
$$
\beta = \frac{(W/L)_{M1}}{(W/L)_{M5}} = \frac{(W/L)_{driver}}{(W/L)_{access}}
$$

- For the cell to be **read-stable**, β must be **large** (typically **β ≥ 1.5** to **2.5** in production designs).
- Physically: a *weaker* access transistor prevents the storage node from rising above the trip point of the opposite inverter during read.

### 2.3.2 Pull-up Ratio (γ)
$$
\gamma = \frac{(W/L)_{M3}}{(W/L)_{M5}} = \frac{(W/L)_{pullup}}{(W/L)_{access}}
$$

- For the cell to be **write-able**, γ must be **small** (typically **γ ≤ 1.5** to **1.8**).
- A *weaker* pull-up PMOS allows the bitline to overpower the latch and flip it.

## 2.4 Static Noise Margin (SNM)

**SNM** is defined as the *maximum DC noise voltage* that can be tolerated at the storage nodes without flipping the cell. It is the *side of the largest square that can be inscribed inside the cross-coupled inverter butterfly curve*.

$$
SNM = V_{TH} - \frac{1}{k+1} \cdot \left[ \frac{V_{DD} - \frac{2k+1}{k+1} \cdot V_{TH} + \frac{k}{(k+1)^2} \cdot V_{DD}}{...} \right]
$$

For a symmetric cell with matched driver / access sizing, the **classical Seevinck formula** (commonly tested):

$$
SNM = V_{TH,n} - \left( \frac{1}{k+1} \right)\left[ \frac{V_{DD} - \frac{V_{TH,n}}{r} - V_{TH,n}}{1 + \frac{1}{r} - \frac{V_{TH,n}}{V_{DD}}} \right]
$$

where $k = \frac{k_n}{k_p} = \frac{\mu_n (W/L)_n}{\mu_p (W/L)_p}$ is the *transconductance ratio*.

> [!TIP]
> **Design rule of thumb:** For $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TH} = 0.35\,\text{V}$, the SNM in hold mode is roughly **0.3 V**, and in read mode it degrades to about **0.15–0.20 V** if β is poorly chosen.

## 2.5 Bitline Voltage Development (Read)

During a read, both bitlines are precharged to $V_{DD}$. When WL rises, one of the storage nodes (say Q = 0) is connected to its bitline (BL). The bitline capacitance discharges by:

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{C_{cell}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

where $C_{BL}$ is the bitline capacitance (hundreds of fF typically). The sense amplifier detects this $\Delta V_{BL}$ (typically only **~100 mV** is enough).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Ratio | $\beta = (W/L)_{M1} / (W/L)_{M5}$ | Dimensionless; **β > 1 for read stability** |
| Pull-up Ratio | $\gamma = (W/L)_{M3} / (W/L)_{M5}$ | Dimensionless; **γ < 2 for write-ability** |
| Bitline ΔV (Read) | $\Delta V_{BL} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Volts |
| Bitline Capacitance | $C_{BL} = N_{cols} \cdot C_{cell} + C_{wire}$ | Farads |
| Hold SNM (symmetric) | $SNM_{hold} = V_{TH,n} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{k+1}}\right)$ | Volts; $k = k_n/k_p$ |
| Read SNM (degraded) | $SNM_{read} \approx SNM_{hold} - \Delta V_{q}$ | Volts; $\Delta V_q$ ≈ 0.1 V typ. |
| Read Current | $I_{read} = k_{n,access} \cdot \left( (V_{DD} - V_T)V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right)$ | Amps |
| Sense Amp Sensitivity | $V_{sense,min} \approx \dfrac{V_{TH,SA}}{\text{DC gain}}$ | Volts; ~30 mV |
| Access Time | $t_a = 0.69 \cdot R_{BL} \cdot C_{BL}$ | Seconds; $R_{BL}$ = bitline R |
| Static Power | $P_{leak} = N \cdot I_{off} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Watts; key metric in nanometer CMOS |
| Dynamic Write Energy | $E_w = C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Joules; per bitline |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** All absolute-value bars above are written as `\vert` so that the column pipes of the markdown table are not parsed as math delimiters.

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Industry

A modern smartphone SoC may contain **~500 million SRAM cells** in its L3 cache alone. A 5% reduction in cell area translates to **millions of dollars** in manufacturing savings per wafer. Every design parameter (β, γ, SNM) is therefore a *knob* carefully tuned to extract maximum performance, minimum area, and minimum leakage.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Read Stability Criterion for 6T Cell

**Setup:** Suppose the cell stores `Q = 0` and `$\bar{Q}$ = $V_{DD}$`. Wordline rises, connecting BL to storage node Q (which is at 0 V). The access transistor M5 and the driver M1 form a *voltage divider* between BL ($V_{DD}$) and ground.

**Step 1:** Define the storage node Q potential during read. The NMOS M5 is in saturation (because $V_{DS5} = V_{DD}$), while M1 is in the linear (triode) region:

$$
V_{Q,read} = V_{DD} - V_{TH,n} \quad \text{(if M5 is saturated)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at node Q. Let $I_1$ = current through M1 (driver) and $I_5$ = current through M5 (access):

$$
I_5 = I_1
$$

**Step 3:** For M5 (saturation):
$$
I_5 = \frac{1}{2} k_{n5} \left( V_{DD} - V_{Q} - V_{TH,n} \right)^2
$$

**Step 4:** For M1 (triode, since $V_{Q}$ is small):
$$
I_1 = k_{n1} \left[ (V_{Q} - V_{TH,n})V_{Q} - \frac{V_{Q}^2}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Equate and solve for $V_Q$ in the limit $V_Q \ll V_{TH,n}$:
$$
\frac{1}{2} k_{n5} (V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{TH,n})^2 \approx k_{n1} (V_Q - V_{TH,n}) V_Q
$$

**Step 6:** Read-flip condition is when $V_Q$ exceeds the trip point of the *opposite* inverter (M3-M2). For a symmetric inverter, trip-point $\approx V_{DD}/2$. The classical "read-stability" inequality is:

$$
\frac{(W/L)_{M1}}{(W/L)_{M5}} \ge \beta_{min}
$$

where

$$
\beta_{min} = \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{TH,n})^2}{2 \cdot V_{TH,n} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TH,n})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TH,n} = 0.35\,\text{V}$:**
> $\beta_{min} = \dfrac{(1.0 - 0.7)^2}{2(0.35)(0.65)} = \dfrac{0.09}{0.455} \approx 1.98$
> Hence designers use $\beta \geq 2$ as a safe lower bound.

## 3.2 Derivation: Write-Ability Criterion

**Setup:** The cell stores `Q = $V_{DD}$` and `$\bar{Q}$ = 0`. To write, BL is pulled to 0 V and WL rises. The pull-up M3 must be *weaker* than the series combination of M5 + M2 so that $\bar{Q}$ can be pulled below the trip-point of the (M1–M4) inverter, thereby flipping Q low.

**Step 1:** Node `$\bar{Q}$` is pulled low by BL through M5. M2 (the opposite driver) is ON (since Q is high). The contention is between M3 (pulling up) and the series of M5+M2 (pulling down).

**Step 2:** For successful write, the voltage at $\bar{Q}$ must drop below $V_{TH,n}$ of M1. Using the standard current equation in saturation:

$$
I_{M3} < I_{M5} = I_{M2}
$$

**Step 3:** M3 (PMOS, in saturation):
$$
I_{M3} = \frac{1}{2} k_{p3} (0 - V_{DD} - V_{TH,p})^2
$$

**Step 4:** M5 + M2 (both NMOS, series; roughly approximated as a single equivalent transistor of width reduced):
$$
I_{M5} = I_{M2} = k_{n5} \left[ (V_{DD} - V_{TH,n}) V_{\bar{Q}} - \frac{V_{\bar{Q}}^2}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** The classical write criterion becomes:

$$
\frac{(W/L)_{M5}}{(W/L)_{M3}} > \gamma_{min}
$$

with

$$
\gamma_{min} = \frac{\mu_p / \mu_n}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{TH,p}}{V_{DD} - 2V_{TH,n}} \right)^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> For typical $0.18\,\mu\text{m}$ process ($\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$), $\gamma_{min} \approx 1.4$. Designers choose $\gamma \leq 1.5$ to comfortably write the cell.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bitline ΔV in Read (Charge-Sharing)

**Step 1:** Pre-charge phase: BL and $\overline{BL}$ both at $V_{DD}$.

**Step 2:** WL rises; suppose Q=0 is connected to BL. The cell-side capacitance $C_{cell}$ (≈ drain capacitance of M1 + bitline diffusion cap) was at 0 V, while BL was at $V_{DD}$.

**Step 3:** Apply charge conservation (no transistor path to ground yet, only to Q):
$$
Q_{initial} = C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD} + C_{cell} \cdot 0 = C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**Step 4:** After charge sharing:
$$
Q_{final} = (C_{BL} + C_{cell}) \cdot V_f
$$

**Step 5:** Equate:
$$
C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD} = (C_{BL} + C_{cell}) \cdot V_f
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{C_{BL}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**Step 6:** The drop in BL voltage:
$$
\Delta V_{BL} = V_{DD} - V_f = \frac{C_{cell}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** If $C_{BL} = 500\,\text{fF}$ and $C_{cell} = 5\,\text{fF}$ and $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$:
> $\Delta V_{BL} = \dfrac{5}{505} \cdot 1.0 \approx 9.9\,\text{mV}$
> This is then *amplified* by the sense amplifier to a full-swing digital level.

## 3.4 Derivation: Hold-SNM (Seevinck Closed-Form)

For a **symmetric 6T cell** in hold mode (WL = 0), the SNM (side of the inscribed square in the butterfly plot) is given by the Seevinck equation:

$$
SNM_{hold} = V_{TH,n} - \frac{1}{k+1}\left[ V_{DD} - \frac{V_{TH,n}}{k-1} \right]
$$

**Step 1:** Derive the right-side edge of the butterfly. Let $V_{in}$ = noise injected at Q, $V_{out}$ = resulting noise at $\bar{Q}$. The two VTCs cross.

**Step 2:** Define the slope of the lower segment of one VTC, which is approximately:
$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \approx -k \quad \text{(in the transition region)}
$$

**Step 3:** The two VTCs are mirror images. The side of the largest square that fits is:
$$
SNM = V_{TH,n} - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{TH,n}) - V_{TH,n}/k}{1 + k}
$$

**Step 4:** For matched devices ($k = 1$, i.e., $\mu_n (W/L)_n = \mu_p (W/L)_p$), this simplifies to:
$$
SNM_{hold} = V_{TH,n} - \frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{TH,n}}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** With $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $V_{TH,n} = 0.35\,\text{V}$:
$$
SNM_{hold} = 0.35 - \frac{1.0 - 0.7}{2} = 0.35 - 0.15 = 0.20\,\text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **read SNM** is always **lower** than the **hold SNM** because the access transistor *partially loads* the storage node, raising its voltage and shrinking the butterfly.

## 4.2 SRAM Read Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start Read Cycle"] --> B["Precharge BL and BLB to VDD"]
    B --> C["Assert Wordline WL to VDD"]
    C --> D["Storage Node Q drives one bitline low"]
    D --> E["Small Differential DeltaV develops on BL pair"]
    E --> F["Sense Amplifier Latches Data"]
    F --> G["Output Valid Qout"]
    G --> H["Deassert WL - Cell Isolated"]
    H --> I["End Read Cycle"]
```

## 4.3 SRAM Write Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start Write Cycle"] --> B["Drive Bitlines to New Data"]
    B --> C["BL pulled to D data or VDD for one"]
    C --> D["Assert Wordline WL to VDD"]
    D --> E["Access Transistor Overpowers Latch"]
    E --> F{"Contention Resolved"}
    F -->|Cell Flips| G["New Data Stored in Latch"]
    F -->|Cell Fails| H["Write Failure - Increase Driver"]
    G --> I["Deassert WL"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["End Write Cycle"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM in terms of refresh, area, speed, and power.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Storage element** | Cross-coupled latch (6 transistors) | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor |
| **Refresh needed?** | No | Yes (every ~64 ms) |
| **Cell area** | Large (~120 F²) | Small (~6 F²) |
| **Access time** | Very fast (~1–5 ns) | Slower (~50–100 ns) |
| **Static power** | Higher (leakage in cell) | Lower |
| **Dynamic power** | Lower per access | Higher (refresh) |
| **Application** | Cache, registers | Main memory |
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Volatile |

> [!NOTE]
> Mentioning *at least 4 points* fetches full 3 marks. A small comparison table is the fastest way to score.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Static Noise Margin (SNM) of an SRAM cell. Why is read-SNM smaller than hold-SNM?**

**Model Answer:**
**SNM** is defined as the *maximum DC noise voltage* that can be applied to the storage nodes of an SRAM cell *without flipping the stored data*. It is graphically measured as the *side of the largest square that can be inscribed inside the cross-coupled inverters' butterfly plot*.

**Why read-SNM < hold-SNM:**
During a *read* (WL = 1), the access transistor M5/M6 turns ON and *partially loads* the low-storage node. The low node rises from 0 V to a small positive value (~$V_{Q,read}$), shrinking the bistable region's butterfly width. In *hold* mode, M5/M6 are OFF, so the storage node is undisturbed and the full VDD swing is available — yielding a larger SNM.

> [!TIP]
> Always draw a *small sketch* of the butterfly plot and label the two SNMs to fetch the additional *presentation* mark.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question offers an **internal choice**. Below we provide both alternatives.
