```mermaid
    nodeG["Continuous Power Draw"]
    nodeH["Capacitor Node Charge Storage"]
    nodeI["Pass Transistor Write"]
    nodeJ["Periodic Refresh Required"]
    nodeK["Sensitive to Leakage Coupling"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
```

---

## Diagram 4: Sequential CMOS Pipeline Timing Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["FLIP FLOP 1 Launch Edge"]
    nodeB["COMBINATIONAL LOGIC BLOCK"]
    nodeC["FLIP FLOP 2 Capture Edge"]
    nodeD["TIMING BUDGET"]
    nodeE["t clk to Q"]
    nodeF["t p logic"]
    nodeG["t su"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** Each pipeline stage's timing budget must satisfy $t_{clk \to Q} + t_{p,logic} + t_{su} \leq T_{clk}$. This is the **critical path** constraint that limits maximum clock frequency in any synchronous CMOS system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate clearly between a **CMOS latch** and a **CMOS flip-flop**. Highlight their triggering behavior and typical application contexts.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Latch | Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Triggering | **Level-sensitive** (transparent when enable is active) | **Edge-triggered** (captures on CLK edge) |
| Transparency | Output follows input while enabled | Output is isolated except at clock edge |
| Implementation | Single stage with enable/clock control | Master-Slave or pulse-triggered structure |
| Use Case | Temporary data hold, asynchronous interfaces | Synchronous registers, pipeline stages |
| Hazard Risk | Susceptible to **race-through** when enable is wide | Edge-triggered, race-free by construction |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for triggering difference, 1 mark for application/table summary.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the concept of **regenerative feedback** in CMOS bistable circuits. Why is it essential for static memory operation?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Regenerative feedback is the technique of connecting two inverters in a **positive feedback loop** such that the output of each drives the input of the other. This creates a system with **two stable equilibrium points** corresponding to the stored binary states (Q = 1 or Q = 0).

It is essential because:
1. **Noise immunity:** Any small perturbation at either node is amplified and driven back to the nearest rail.
2. **Leakage compensation:** Sub-threshold leakage that would otherwise discharge a dynamic node is overcome by the active driving inverters.
3. **Metastability recovery:** When the circuit is pushed away from a stable state (e.g., during a write), the loop gain ($A_v^2 \gg 1$) ensures fast regeneration to a valid rail.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark concept, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark significance (any two of the three points above).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **7 + 7** across two sub-parts. Internal choice requires *two entirely different* questions of comparable cognitive demand.

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Static CMOS SR Latch built using cross-coupled NOR gates**. Derive the characteristic equation and explain all four input combinations. *(7 Marks)*

**Q. (b)** Design and explain a **CMOS D-Latch using transmission gates**. Discuss its advantages over the NAND-based SR latch implementation. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Solution

#### Q.A (a) — CMOS SR Latch Using Cross-Coupled NOR Gates (7 Marks)

**Circuit Structure:**
The latch consists of **two 2-input CMOS NOR gates** whose outputs are cross-coupled to the inputs of the other.

- Let NOR-1 output = $Q$, NOR-2 output = $\overline{Q}$.
- Inputs to NOR-1: $R$ and $\overline{Q}$.
- Inputs to NOR-2: $S$ and $Q$.

**Logic Operation (Truth Table):**

| S | R | Q (Next) | $\overline{Q}$ (Next) | State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Q (Previous) | $\overline{Q}$ (Previous) | **Hold** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **Reset** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **Set** |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Invalid / Forbidden** |

**Characteristic Equation Derivation:**

The next state $Q_{next}$ is determined by the present state $Q$, $S$, and $R$:

$$
Q_{next} = S + \overline{R} \cdot Q
$$

with the constraint that $S \cdot R = 0$ (forbidden state).

**CMOS Implementation Detail:**
- Each NOR gate is implemented as **2 PMOS in series** (pull-up) and **2 NMOS in parallel** (pull-down).
- For 180nm technology, typical W/L ratios: PMOS $\frac{4\mu m}{0.18\mu m}$, NMOS $\frac{2\mu m}{0.18\mu m}$.
- Static power dissipation: **Zero in hold state** (since one of the pull-up/pull-down paths is always off).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to mention the **forbidden state** ($S = R = 1$) and lose 1 mark. Also, failing to specify that cross-coupling is what makes it *bistable* (rather than combinational) costs another mark.

---

#### Q.A (b) — CMOS D-Latch Using Transmission Gates (7 Marks)

**Circuit Architecture:**

A D-latch has a single data input $D$ and produces $Q = D$ when enabled, and $Q$ holds when disabled.

1. **Transmission Gate 1 (TG1):** Passes $D$ to internal node $X$ when CLK = 1.
2. **Inverter Pair (cross-coupled):** Inverter-1 drives Inverter-2's input; Inverter-2's output feeds back to Inverter-1's input → forms bistable storage.
3. **Transmission Gate 2 (TG2):** Provides the feedback path during CLK = 0 to keep the bistable loop intact.

**Operating Phases:**

- **CLK = 1 (Transparent):** TG1 is ON, TG2 is OFF. The data $D$ propagates to the storage node, and $Q = D$. The bistable is being overwritten.
- **CLK = 0 (Hold):** TG1 is OFF, TG2 is ON. The cross-coupled inverters form a closed loop, retaining the last value of $D$.

**Output Driver:** A final buffer inverter provides rail-to-rail $Q$ output with adequate drive strength.

**Advantages over NAND-based SR latch:**

| Feature | TG-based D-Latch | NAND SR Latch |
|---|---|---|
| Single Data Input | Yes (no $S$/$R$ ambiguity) | No (requires both) |
| Forbidden State | **None** | $S=R=0$ is forbidden |
| Static Power | Near-zero in hold | Some contention in hold |
| Race Condition | Absent (single input) | Possible when both $S,R$ transition |
| Layout Area | Slightly larger (TG needs both NMOS+PMOS) | More compact |
| Controllability | Easier (single $D$ line) | Requires careful $S$/$R$ coordination |

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$
Q_{next} = D \quad \text{when } CLK = 1
$$

$$
Q_{next} = Q_{current} \quad \text{when } CLK = 0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Marks are frequently deducted for omitting the role of **TG2 in closing the feedback loop during hold**. Without TG2, the inverters cannot retain state because the input is isolated — the feedback path *must* be re-established every cycle.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**Q. (a)** With suitable waveforms, explain the **master-slave configuration** used to construct an **edge-triggered D flip-flop** from two level-sensitive latches. *(7 Marks)*

**Q. (b)** Define **setup time** ($t_{su}$) and **hold time** ($t_h$) for a CMOS flip-flop. Derive the **maximum clock frequency** equation for a pipelined datapath. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution

#### Q.B (a) — Master-Slave Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop (7 Marks)

**Architecture:**

Two D-latches are cascaded:
- **Master Latch:** Enabled when CLK = 1.
- **Slave Latch:** Enabled when $\overline{CLK}$ = 1 (i.e., when CLK = 0).

The output of the Master drives the input of the Slave. The final $Q$ output comes from the Slave.

**Waveform Analysis (Timing Diagram):**

| Time Interval | CLK | Master State | Slave State | $Q_M$ (Master Output) | $Q$ (Final Output) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_0 \to t_1$ | 0 | Hold | Transparent | Previous value | New = $Q_M$ at $t_0$ |
| $t_1 \to t_2$ | 1 | Transparent | Hold | Follows $D$ | Holds previous $Q$ |
| $t_2 \to t_3$ | 0 | Hold | Transparent | Latches at $t_2$ | $Q_M$ updates |
| $t_3 \to t_4$ | 1 | Transparent | Hold | Follows new $D$ | Holds |

**Critical Observation:**

- During the **entire CLK-high interval**, the output $Q$ is **frozen** at its value from the previous falling edge.
- Output $Q$ changes **only at the falling edge of CLK** (i.e., negative-edge-triggered).
- This makes the design race-free: data must be stable only in a narrow window around the active edge.

**Why Two Phases?**

If both latches were transparent simultaneously, the input $D$ would pass straight to $Q$, defeating the purpose of edge-triggering. The **opposite clock phases** ensure that *only one latch is transparent at a time*, breaking the direct path.

---

#### Q.B (b) — Setup Time, Hold Time, and Maximum Clock Frequency (7 Marks)

**Definitions:**

- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** The minimum time interval **before the active clock edge** during which the data input $D$ must be held stable for the flip-flop to reliably capture it.

$$
D(t_{edge} - t_{su}) = D_{valid}
$$

- **Hold Time ($t_h$):** The minimum time interval **after the active clock edge** during which the data input $D$ must remain stable.

$$
D(t_{edge} + t_h) = D_{valid}
$$

**Derivation of Maximum Clock Frequency:**

Consider a pipeline: FF1 → combinational logic → FF2.

**Step 1:** Data arrives at FF2's input after:

$$
t_{arrival} = t_{clk \to Q,FF1} + t_{p,logic}
$$

**Step 2:** For FF2 to capture this data at the next clock edge:

$$
t_{arrival} \leq T_{clk} - t_{su,FF2}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging:

$$
T_{clk} \geq t_{clk \to Q,FF1} + t_{p,logic} + t_{su,FF2}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum operating frequency:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{clk \to Q,FF1} + t_{p,logic} + t_{su,FF2}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For $t_{clk \to Q} = 80\,\text{ps}$, $t_{p,logic} = 320\,\text{ps}$, $t_{su} = 60\,\text{ps}$:

$$
T_{clk,min} = 80 + 320 + 60 = 460\,\text{ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} \approx 2.174\,\text{GHz}
$$

**Hold Time Violation Check:**

The hold constraint is:

$$
t_{clk \to Q,FF1} + t_{p,logic,min} \geq t_{h,FF2}
$$

This is a **race-condition constraint** and is independent of clock frequency — it must be satisfied at *any* frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **setup time constraint** (frequency-limiting) with **hold time constraint** (race-condition). Setup violation = slower design; hold violation = total design failure at any speed.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 4 Introduction**

- ✅ **Sequential CMOS** = combinational logic + state retention mechanism.
- ✅ **Two storage paradigms:** Static (cross-coupled inverters) vs. Dynamic (capacitor charge).
- ✅ **Latch** = level-sensitive; **Flip-flop** = edge-triggered.
- ✅ **Regenerative feedback** ($A_v^2 \gg 1$) ensures bistability and noise immunity.
- ✅ **Clocked CMOS (C²MOS)** = static CMOS logic + clocked transistors to break feedback during writes.
- ✅ **TSPC** = True Single-Phase Clocking — uses only one clock phase, no $\overline{CLK}$ required.
- ✅ **Master-Slave** = two latches in opposite clock phases → edge-triggered.
- ✅ **Pulse-triggered FF** = single latch + narrow pulse generator → fast, low-power.
- ✅ **Setup time constraint** → determines **maximum clock frequency**.
- ✅ **Hold time constraint** → determines **race-free operation**, independent of frequency.
- ✅ **Dynamic storage** requires **refresh** due to sub-threshold and gate leakage.
- ✅ **Static storage** has **no leakage concern** but draws more area and (in pseudo-nMOS) static power.
- ✅ **Forbidden state** in SR latches ($S = R = 1$ for NOR, $S = R = 0$ for NAND) must be excluded.
- ✅ **Transmission gates** combine NMOS + PMOS in parallel for full-rail signal passing.
- ✅ **Pipeline timing equation:** $T_{clk} \geq t_{clk \to Q} + t_{p,logic} + t_{su}$.
- ✅ **Energy per transition:** $E \approx \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^2$ (fundamental switching energy).
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** = key figure of merit for flip-flop comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wire Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wire Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wire Geometry?

In deep-submicron CMOS design, the **physical dimensions and topology of metal interconnect wires** — their **width (W)**, **length (L)**, **thickness (t)**, **spacing (S)**, and **layer assignment** — are collectively termed **Wire Geometry**. As transistors have scaled into the nanometer regime, interconnect (wire) parasitics have begun to **dominate** the total propagation delay, power dissipation, and signal integrity of a digital circuit. Therefore, the *shape*, *size*, and *routing hierarchy* of wires are first-class design variables in modern VLSI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire Geometry** is the study of how the *physical dimensions and routing configuration* of interconnects influence the **resistance (R)**, **capacitance (C)**, **inductance (L)**, and ultimately the **delay, power, and noise** of a CMOS circuit. The 2024 KTU syllabus treats this as a vital bridge between the *device* level (transistors) and the *chip* level (system performance).

### 1.2 Hierarchical Wire Classification

Modern CMOS processes provide **multiple metal layers** (often 6–12 in 2024-era nodes), and engineers assign wires to these layers based on their electrical role:

| Layer Type | Typical Use | Wire Geometry Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Local (Poly / Metal-1)** | Cell-to-cell within a gate | Short, narrow, very resistive |
| **Semi-Global (Mid-Metal)** | Block-to-block routing | Moderate length and width |
| **Global (Top-Metal)** | Chip-wide clock / data buses | Wide, thick, low resistance, designed for minimum delay |

> [!NOTE]
> Top-metal layers in 2024 processes use **copper (Cu) with low-k dielectric** ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2.2$–$2.7$) and exhibit *thicker cross-sections* to reduce resistance, while local layers use thinner aluminum-like wires with higher resistance but finer pitch.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway System"

Think of a CMOS chip as a **city**, transistors as **buildings**, and wires as the **road network**:

- **Local streets (narrow Metal-1):** Connect houses on the same block — short, many turns, slow traffic.
- **Arterial roads (semi-global):** Connect neighborhoods — wider, faster.
- **Highways (thick top-metal):** Carry massive traffic (clock, data buses) across the entire city — built wide, thick, and straight for **minimum travel time**.

Just as a city planner chooses *road width, length, and routing* to minimize commute time, the VLSI designer chooses **wire geometry** to minimize **propagation delay, power, and signal integrity violations**.

### 1.4 Why Wire Geometry Matters — Scaling Perspective

With each technology node, the following geometric trends emerge:

- Transistor gate length $L_g$ **shrinks** (~0.7× per node).
- Wire **widths and pitches shrink** (~0.7× per node).
- Chip area **grows**, so global wire lengths actually **increase**.
- Result: **wire delay grows** while gate delay shrinks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The intrinsic gate delay of a 2024-node CMOS inverter is on the order of **a few picoseconds**, but a poorly-sized 5 mm global wire can have a delay of **hundreds of picoseconds** to **nanoseconds** — i.e., wire delay is the *bottleneck*.

### 1.5 Key Geometric Variables

The fundamental variables that govern interconnect behavior are:

- $W$ — wire **width** (µm or nm)
- $L$ — wire **length** (µm or mm)
- $t$ — wire **thickness** (height of metal)
- $H$ — dielectric thickness to substrate
- $S$ — spacing to **neighboring wires**
- $\rho$ — metal **resistivity** (Ω·m)
- $\varepsilon_{ox}$ — dielectric permittivity between wire and substrate

These variables feed directly into the **resistance** ($R$), **capacitance** ($C$), and **delay** ($\tau$) expressions covered in the next section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wire Resistance — The Geometric Foundation

The DC resistance of a uniform rectangular conductor is given by:

$$
R \;=\; \rho \,\frac{L}{A} \;=\; \rho \,\frac{L}{W \cdot t}
$$

where $A = W \cdot t$ is the cross-sectional area. Because IC wires are uniform in cross-section, we introduce a more practical quantity — the **sheet resistance**:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\rho}{t} \quad \text{(units: } \Omega / \square \text{)}
$$

The resistance of a wire of length $L$ and width $W$ is then:

$$
R \;=\; R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $L/W$ is the **number of "squares"** that the wire contains. A 10 µm long, 0.5 µm wide wire consists of $L/W = 20$ squares. Each square contributes $R_s$ ohms.

**Typical sheet resistance values (2024-era processes):**

| Layer | Sheet Resistance $\boldsymbol{R_s}$ |
|---|---|
| Polysilicon (silicided) | ~5 – 10 Ω/□ |
| Metal-1 (thin) | ~0.07 – 0.1 Ω/□ |
| Mid-metal | ~0.05 – 0.08 Ω/□ |
| Top-metal (thick Cu) | ~0.02 – 0.05 Ω/□ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** *Doubling* the width of a wire **halves** its resistance but also **increases** its capacitance to the substrate and to neighbors. This trade-off is the heart of wire geometry optimization.

## 2.2 Wire Capacitance — Three Components

A wire over a silicon substrate has three distinct capacitance components:

1. **Area (parallel-plate) capacitance to substrate:**
   $$C_{area} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \, W \, L}{H_{ox}}$$
2. **Fringing capacitance to substrate** (from wire edges):
   $$C_{fringe} \approx \varepsilon_{ox} \cdot L$$
3. **Lateral (coupling) capacitance to neighboring wires:**
   $$C_{couple} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \, t \, L}{S}$$

The **total wire capacitance** is:

$$
C_{total} \;=\; C_{area} + C_{fringe} + 2 C_{couple}
$$

The factor of 2 in the coupling term accounts for *two* neighbors (one on each side).

> [!WARNING]
> In modern (deep-submicron) processes, the **coupling capacitance** between adjacent wires is often **the dominant component** (60–80 % of the total), not the area capacitance. This is why *wire spacing* and *routing* matter as much as *wire width*.

## 2.3 Distributed RC Line Model

A long wire is modeled as a **distributed RC ladder** with per-unit-length resistance $r$ (Ω/µm) and capacitance $c$ (F/µm):

$$
r = \frac{R_s}{W} \qquad c = c_{area} + c_{fringe} + 2 c_{couple}
$$

For a wire of total length $L$:

$$
R_{wire} = r \cdot L \qquad C_{wire} = c \cdot L
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every high-yield formula** you need to solve KTU examination questions on wire geometry. Memorize the form, units, and the limiting cases.

| \# | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Critical Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wire resistance | $R = R_s \cdot L / W$ | Ω | $L/W$ = number of squares |
| 2 | Sheet resistance | $R_s = \rho / t$ | Ω/□ | Process parameter |
| 3 | Area capacitance | $C_{area} = \varepsilon_{ox} W L / H$ | F | Parallel-plate to substrate |
| 4 | Fringing capacitance | $C_{fringe} \approx \varepsilon_{ox} L$ | F | Edge-field term |
| 5 | Coupling capacitance | $C_{coup} = \varepsilon_{ox} t L / S$ | F | Dominant in deep-submicron |
| 6 | Lumped-RC delay | $\tau_D = 0.69 \, R_{eq} C_{eq}$ | s | Used for short wires |
| 7 | Distributed-RC delay | $\tau_D = 0.38 \, R_{wire} C_{wire}$ | s | Used for long, unbuffered wires |
| 8 | Elmore delay (n-segment ladder) | $\tau_D = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R C$ | s | Sum of path-weighted capacitances |
| 9 | Elmore delay (continuous line) | $\tau_D = \frac{1}{2} r c L^{2}$ | s | Limit as $n \to \infty$ |
| 10 | Optimal repeater count | $n_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_{wire} C_{wire}}{2 R_{tr} C_{tr}}}$ | — | Bakoglu's result |
| 11 | Optimal repeater size | $s_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_{tr} C_{wire}}{R_{wire} C_{tr}}}$ | — | Bakoglu's result |
| 12 | Miller-coupling delay factor | $1 \pm \frac{C_{coup}}{C_{total}}$ | — | '+' for in-phase, '−' for out-of-phase |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The number 0.38 for distributed RC lines** comes from the Elmore delay of a uniform ladder in the limit of infinite segments, multiplied by 0.69 (the 50 %-to-90 % rise-time conversion). This constant is a **board-exam favorite**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Wire Geometry

The wire geometry framework underpins nearly every physical-design tool in industry:

- **Place-and-route tools** (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC) automatically size and layer-assign wires.
- **Clock-tree synthesis** uses wire geometry to balance skew across a chip.
- **Power-delivery networks** rely on wide top-metal wires to minimize IR drop.
- **Signal-integrity analysis** uses wire geometry to estimate crosstalk and ground bounce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Elmore Delay of an n-Stage Uniform RC Ladder

This derivation is the **most important** in Module 4 because it ties together wire geometry, resistance, and delay.

### Setup

Consider a wire modeled as a **cascade of $n$ identical RC segments**, each with resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$, driven by a source with output resistance $R_{tr}$ and terminated into a load $C_L$:

- Total wire resistance: $R_w = nR$
- Total wire capacitance: $C_w = nC$

> The intermediate nodes are labeled $1, 2, \ldots, n$ (with $n$ = last node driving $C_L$).

### Definition of Elmore Delay

For any RC tree, the Elmore delay from input $i$ to output $j$ is defined as:

$$
\tau_{D} \;=\; \sum_{k} R_{ik} \, C_k
$$

where $R_{ik}$ is the **common resistance** shared between the input-to-output path and the path from input to node $k$, and $C_k$ is the total capacitance at node $k$.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Resistances to the source (from input to each node):**

The resistance from the source to the $k^{th}$ intermediate node is:

$$
R_{source \to k} = R_{tr} + kR
$$

**Step 2 — Capacitances at each node:**

The capacitance at the $k^{th}$ intermediate node is $C$ (one segment), and at the output node is $C + C_L$.

**Step 3 — Identify the common resistance $R_{ik}$ for each capacitance:**

For an inverting path (the input drives the output through all $n$ segments), the common resistance from input to each capacitance $C_k$ is:

$$
R_{ik} = R_{tr} + kR
$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all capacitances:**

$$
\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (R_{tr} + kR)\,C \;+\; (R_{tr} + nR)(C_L)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the arithmetic sum:**

The sum $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$, hence:

$$
\tau_D = n R_{tr} C + R_{tr} C_L + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R C + n R C_L
$$

**Step 6 — Group terms (assume $C_L \to 0$ for clarity; a common KTU simplification):**

$$
\tau_D \;=\; R_{tr}\,(nC) \;+\; \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\,RC
$$

**Step 7 — Take the continuous limit (replace $R = rL/n$ and $C = cL/n$):**

$$
R \cdot C = \frac{rL}{n} \cdot \frac{cL}{n} = \frac{rcL^2}{n^2}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\tau_D = R_{tr} c L + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \frac{rcL^2}{n^2}
$$

As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\frac{n(n+1)}{2n^2} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau_D \;=\; R_{tr} c L \;+\; \frac{1}{2} r c L^{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** For a long, unbuffered wire, the delay is **quadratic in length** ($L^2$). The first term is the *driver* delay, the second is the *wire* delay. Since the wire term grows as $L^2$, doubling the wire length **quadruples** the delay — this is why **repeaters** are essential for long global interconnects.

**Step 8 — Convert to rise time (50 % → 90 %) using the standard multiplier 0.69:**

$$
t_{rise} \;=\; 0.69 \, \tau_D
$$

For a wire whose own resistance dominates ($R_{tr} c L \ll \tfrac{1}{2} r c L^2$):

$$
t_{rise} \;\approx\; 0.69 \cdot \frac{1}{2} r c L^{2} \;\approx\; 0.38 \, r c L^{2}
$$

This is the **famous 0.38** constant quoted in textbooks and KTU boards.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Wire Sizing (Width that Minimizes Delay)

Consider a single wire segment of length $L$ driven by a CMOS inverter with output resistance $R_{tr}$ and loaded by a fixed load capacitance $C_L$ at the far end.

**Step 1 — Express the total resistance and capacitance as functions of $W$:**

$$
R_w(W) = \frac{R_s L}{W} \qquad C_w(W) = c_0 W L + c_f L
$$

where $c_0$ is the *area* capacitance per unit area ($\varepsilon_{ox}/H$) and $c_f$ is the *fringing* per unit length (independent of $W$).

**Step 2 — Approximate the Elmore delay** (lumped approximation, valid when $R_w$ is comparable to $R_{tr}$):

$$
\tau_D(W) = \left(R_{tr} + \frac{R_s L}{2W}\right) \cdot \left( c_0 W L + c_f L + C_L \right)
$$

> The factor $1/2$ in $R_s L / (2W)$ accounts for the *average* resistance seen charging the distributed capacitance.

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $W$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d \tau_D}{dW} = 0
$$

Expanding the product:

$$
\tau_D = R_{tr}(c_0 W L + c_f L + C_L) + \frac{R_s L}{2W}(c_0 W L + c_f L + C_L)
$$

$$
\tau_D = R_{tr} c_0 W L + R_{tr}(c_f L + C_L) + \frac{R_s L}{2}(c_0 L + \frac{c_f L + C_L}{W})
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d\tau_D}{dW} = R_{tr} c_0 L - \frac{R_s L}{2} \cdot \frac{c_f L + C_L}{W^2}
$$

Setting to zero:

$$
R_{tr} c_0 L = \frac{R_s L}{2} \cdot \frac{c_f L + C_L}{W^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W$:**

$$
W_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_s (c_f L + C_L)}{2 R_{tr} c_0}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A *wider* wire reduces resistance (good) but increases area capacitance (bad). The optimum width balances these two opposing effects. The driver resistance $R_{tr}$ acts as a reference: a *weaker* driver (larger $R_{tr}$) tolerates a *narrower* optimal wire.

**Step 5 — Limiting case $C_L \gg c_f L$ (load-dominated):**

$$
W_{opt} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{R_s C_L}{2 R_{tr} c_0}}
$$

In this regime, the optimal width depends *only* on the load, the driver strength, and the technology, not on the wire length — a very useful KTU result.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimal Repeater Insertion (Bakoglu's Method)

For a long wire of length $L$, insert $k$ equally-spaced **identical** repeaters (inverters), each with drive resistance $R_{tr}$ and input capacitance $C_{tr}$.

**Step 1 — Segment length:** Each segment has length $L/k$.

**Step 2 — Segment parasitics:**

$$
R_{seg} = \frac{R_s L}{k W} \qquad C_{seg} = c L / k
$$

**Step 3 — Total delay** (sum of $k$ identical stages):

$$
\tau_D(k) = k \cdot 0.69 \left[ R_{tr} \left( c L / k + C_{tr} \right) + \frac{R_s L}{2 k W} \left( c L / k \right) \right]
$$

$$
\tau_D(k) = 0.69 \left[ R_{tr} (c L + k C_{tr}) + \frac{R_s c L^{2}}{2 k W} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $k$:**

$$
\frac{d\tau_D}{dk} = 0.69 \left[ R_{tr} C_{tr} - \frac{R_s c L^{2}}{2 k^{2} W} \right] = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$:**

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_s c L^{2}}{2 R_{tr} C_{tr} W}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The optimum number of repeaters scales as the **square root of the wire length**, and the minimum delay then scales **linearly** with $L$ — a dramatic improvement over the unbuffered $L^{2}$ dependence.

## 3.4 Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A 0.13 µm CMOS wire has $R_s = 0.08 \,\Omega/\square$, $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $H_{ox} = 0.4$ µm, $W = 0.4$ µm, $t = 0.5$ µm, $L = 2000$ µm. The driver resistance is $R_{tr} = 500 \,\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Wire resistance:**

$$
R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 0.08 \cdot \frac{2000}{0.4} = 400 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Area capacitance:**

$$
C_{area} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} W L}{H_{ox}} = \frac{(3.45 \times 10^{-11})(0.4 \times 10^{-6})(2000 \times 10^{-6})}{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
C_{area} = 3.45 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 0.4 \cdot 2000 = 2.76 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{F} = 27.6 \, \text{fF}
$$

**Step 3 — Distributed delay (driver-dominated case):**

$$
\tau_D = 0.69 \, R_{tr} C_{area} = 0.69 \cdot 500 \cdot 27.6 \times 10^{-15} = 9.52 \, \text{ps}
$$

**Step 4 — If wire-resistance dominates (using $0.38 RC$):**

$$
\tau_D = 0.38 \cdot 400 \cdot 27.6 \times 10^{-15} = 4.2 \, \text{ps}
$$

The total is the **sum**, approximately $\tau_{total} \approx 13.7$ ps.

> [!NOTE]
> This step-by-step numerical pattern is **exactly** what KTU examiners expect in Part-B solutions. Always show *all* intermediate numerical values.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wire Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since a literal cross-section is impractical in Mermaid, the following diagram models the **interconnect stack as a functional architecture** showing the geometric and electrical roles of each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["GLOBAL ROUTING LAYER M9-M10 THICK CU"]
        G1["WIDTH 10X  RESISTANCE 0.03 OHMS PER SQ"]
        G2["USED FOR CLOCK AND POWER"]
    end
    subgraph A2["SEMI GLOBAL LAYER M3-M6"]
        S1["WIDTH 4X  RESISTANCE 0.07 OHMS PER SQ"]
        S2["USED FOR BLOCK TO BLOCK"]
    end
    subgraph A3["LOCAL ROUTING LAYER M1-M2 AND POLY"]
        L1["WIDTH 1X  RESISTANCE 0.1 OHMS PER SQ"]
        L2["USED FOR INTRA CELL ROUTING"]
    end
    subgraph A4["SUBSTRATE AND ACTIVE"]
        SB1["HEAVILY DOPED WELL  GROUND PLANE"]
    end
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Distributed RC Ladder Model

The standard lumped-segment model used to compute the **Elmore delay** is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["VIN"] --> n1["R SEG 1"]
    n1 --> n2["C SEG 1 TO GND"]
    n1 --> n3["R SEG 2"]
    n3 --> n4["C SEG 2 TO GND"]
    n3 --> n5["R SEG 3"]
    n5 --> n6["C SEG 3 TO GND"]
    n5 --> n7["R SEG N"]
    n7 --> n8["C SEG N TO GND"]
    n7 --> n9["C LOAD AT OUTPUT"]
    n7 --> n10["VOUT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In this diagram, **R SEG K** represents the resistance of the $k^{th}$ wire segment and **C SEG K TO GND** is the segment capacitance to the substrate. The **right-most capacitor** is the load (gate) capacitance of the receiving gate.

## 4.3 Repeater-Insertion Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["START WITH LONG WIRE OF LENGTH L"] --> B["MEASURE RC PER UNIT LENGTH"]
    B --> C["COMPUTE UNBUFFERED DELAY 0.38 R C"]
    C --> D{"DELAY MEETS TIMING BUDGET"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["USE UNBUFFERED WIRE"]
    D -- "NO" --> F["COMPUTE K OPT USING BAKOGLU FORMULA"]
    F --> G["COMPUTE REPEATER SIZE S OPT"]
    G --> H["INSERT K REPEATERS EQUALLY SPACED"]
    H --> I["VERIFY DELAY AND SKEW"]
    I --> J["END OF REPEATER INSERTION"]
```

## 4.4 Crosstalk Coupling Model

When two adjacent wires run in parallel, their coupling capacitance $C_c$ produces **crosstalk**. The Mermaid block below shows the equivalent circuit for the **victim** wire driven by $V_{victim}$ and the **aggressor** by $V_{agg}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["DRIVER AGGRESSOR  RA1"] --> W1["WIRE AGGRESSOR"]
    W1 --> Cc["COUPLING CAPACITANCE CC BETWEEN WIRES"]
    Cc --> W2["WIRE VICTIM"]
    A2["DRIVER VICTIM  RA2"] --> W2
    W2 --> Cg1["CG TO SUBSTRATE"]
    W2 --> CL1["LOAD CAPACITANCE OF VICTIM GATE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Miller effect** multiplies the effective coupling capacitance seen by the victim:
> - When aggressor and victim switch in **opposite directions**: effective $C = 2 C_c$ (worst case delay).
> - When they switch in the **same direction**: effective $C = 0$ (no impact on delay).

## 4.5 Wire-Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["WIRE GEOMETRY DECISION"] --> B{"LENGTH OF WIRE"}
    B -- "SHORT LESS THAN 50 UM" --> C["USE MINIMUM WIDTH 1X"]
    B -- "MEDIUM 50 TO 500 UM" --> D["TAPER OR USE 2X WIDTH"]
    B -- "LONG MORE THAN 500 UM" --> E["INSERT REPEATERS AND WIDEN"]
    C --> F["VALIDATE TIMING"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{"TIMING MET"}
    G -- "YES" --> H["FINALIZE ROUTING"]
    G -- "NO" --> I["ITERATE WIDTH OR REPEATER COUNT"]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "sheet resistance" of an interconnect wire. Why is it a useful metric in VLSI wire geometry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Sheet resistance $R_s$ is defined as the resistance of a square sheet of uniform thickness: $R_s = \rho / t$, with units of **Ω per square (Ω/□)**.
- **(1 mark)** It is useful because the resistance of any wire can be written as $R = R_s (L/W)$, where $L/W$ is the *number of squares*. This makes resistance calculation **independent of absolute dimensions** for uniform wires.
- **(1 mark)** It is a **process-defined parameter**, set by the metal layer and its thickness, and is **directly measurable** on a test structure using a four-point probe.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is fringing capacitance? How does it differ from the parallel-plate (area) capacitance of a wire?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Fringing capacitance arises from the **electric field lines** that bulge out from the *edges* of a wire, terminating on the substrate or on neighboring conductors. It is proportional to wire length: $C_f \approx \varepsilon_{ox} L$.
- **(1 mark)** Parallel-plate capacitance arises from the **vertical field** between the *bottom* of the wire and the substrate: $C_{pp} = \varepsilon_{ox} W L / H$. It scales with the wire's *area* (W·L).
- **(1 mark)** Fringing becomes *more significant* as wires become *narrower* (small W), because the perimeter-to-area ratio grows. In deep-submicron processes, fringing often **dominates** the area term.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal Choice: Attempt **ONE** of Question A or Question B. Each question carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.*

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Elmore delay** of a uniform RC ladder with $n$ segments, each of resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$, driven by a source of resistance $R_{tr}$ and loaded with capacitance $C_L$. State the limiting form for $n \to \infty$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 0.18 µm CMOS wire has $R_s = 0.08 \,\Omega/\square$, $W = 0.5$ µm, $L = 1500$ µm, and a total per-unit-length capacitance $c = 0.25$ fF/µm. The driver resistance is $R_{tr} = 250$ Ω and the load capacitance is $C_L = 50$ fF. Calculate (i) the wire resistance, (ii) the wire capacitance, (iii) the Elmore delay, and (iv) the 50%–90% rise time.

**Model Solution (a):**
1. The Elmore delay of an RC tree is $\tau_D = \sum_k R_{ik} C_k$ where $R_{ik}$ is the common resistance on the path from input to node $k$.
2. For a uniform $n$-segment ladder, the resistance from the source to node $k$ is $R_{ik} = R_{tr} + kR$.
3. The capacitance at each intermediate node is $C$ (one segment); the capacitance at the output is $C_L$.
4. Summing over $k = 1$ to $n$:

   $$
   \tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (R_{tr} + kR) C + (R_{tr} + nR) C_L
   $$

5. Using $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2$:

   $$
   \tau_D = n R_{tr} C + R_{tr} C_L + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R C + n R C_L
   $$

6. Taking $n \to \infty$ and using $R = rL/n$, $C = cL/n$:

   $$
   \tau_D = R_{tr} c L + \frac{1}{2} r c L^{2}
   $$

   The delay is **quadratic** in length.

**Model Solution (b):**

(i) Wire resistance:

$$
R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 0.08 \cdot \frac{1500}{0.5} = 240 \, \Omega
$$

(ii) Wire capacitance:

$$
C_{wire} = c \cdot L = 0.25 \times 10^{-15} \times 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 0.375 \, \text{pF} = 375 \, \text{fF}
$$

(iii) Elmore delay (using the lumped form with average resistance $R/2$):

$$
\tau_D = R_{tr}(C_{wire} + C_L) + \frac{R}{2} C_{wire} = 250 \cdot (375 + 50)\text{f} + 120 \cdot 375\text{f}
$$

$$
\tau_D = 250 \cdot 425 \times 10^{-15} + 120 \cdot 375 \times 10^{-15} = 106.25 \, \text{ps} + 45 \, \text{ps} = 151.25 \, \text{ps}
$$

(iv) Rise time:

$$
t_{rise} = 0.69 \cdot \tau_D = 0.69 \cdot 151.25 = 104.4 \, \text{ps}
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **wire tapering** in VLSI. Why is uniform wire sizing generally not optimal for delay, and derive the condition for the optimal wire width $W_{opt}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 10 mm global interconnect in a 90 nm CMOS process has $R_w = 50$ Ω/mm and $C_w = 0.4$ pF/mm. A minimum-sized inverter has $R_{tr} = 5$ kΩ and $C_{tr} = 4$ fF. Using Bakoglu's repeater model, find (i) the optimum number of repeaters, (ii) the optimum size of each repeater, and (iii) the resulting minimum delay.

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** *Wire tapering* refers to deliberately making a wire **wider** as it carries current over long distances, to reduce its resistance. The width is chosen to balance the resistance penalty (which decreases with $W$) against the capacitance penalty (which increases with $W$).
2. **Why uniform sizing is not optimal (2 marks):** A uniform narrow wire has high resistance and therefore high *wire* delay (quadratic in $L$); a uniform wide wire has very large capacitance, which also slows down transitions through the driver. The optimum is a *balance*.
3. **Derivation (3 marks):** The total Elmore delay of a wire with driver resistance $R_{tr}$ and load $C_L$ is:

   $$
   \tau_D(W) = \left(R_{tr} + \frac{R_s L}{2W}\right) (c_0 W L + c_f L + C_L)
   $$

   Differentiating with respect to $W$ and equating to zero:

   $$
   W_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_s (c_f L + C_L)}{2 R_{tr} c_0}}
   $$

   This is the **optimal width** that minimizes the total delay.

**Model Solution (b):**

Total wire resistance and capacitance:

$$
R_w = 50 \times 10 = 500 \, \Omega \qquad C_w = 0.4 \times 10 = 4 \, \text{pF}
$$

(i) Optimum number of repeaters (Bakoglu):

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_w C_w}{2 R_{tr} C_{tr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{500 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-15}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-11}}} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07
$$

Therefore use **$k = 7$ repeaters**.

(ii) Optimum repeater size (relative to minimum):

$$
s_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{R_{tr} C_w}{R_w C_{tr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{5000 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-12}}{500 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-15}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{10^{4}} = 100
$$

Each repeater should be **100× the minimum size** (which is impractical — in practice, use a cascade of progressively larger inverters, *logical effort tapering*).

(iii) Minimum delay per segment:

$$
R_{seg} = R_w / k = 500 / 7 = 71.4 \, \Omega \qquad C_{seg} = C_w / k = 4/7 = 0.571 \, \text{pF}
$$

Effective driver (with $s = 100$):

$$
R_{tr,eff} = R_{tr}/s = 50 \, \Omega \qquad C_{tr,eff} = s \cdot C_{tr} = 400 \, \text{fF}
$$

Delay per segment:

$$
\tau_{seg} = 0.69 \big( R_{tr,eff} \cdot (C_{seg} + C_{tr,eff}) + \tfrac{R_{seg}}{2} C_{seg} \big)
$$

$$
\tau_{seg} = 0.69 \cdot (50 \cdot 0.971 \text{p} + 35.7 \cdot 0.571 \text{p}) = 0.69 \cdot (48.55 + 20.4) \, \text{ps} = 47.7 \, \text{ps}
$$

Total delay:

$$
\tau_{total} = k \cdot \tau_{seg} = 7 \cdot 47.7 = 334 \, \text{ps}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** — Common places where students *lose marks* on this type of question:
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor** when computing 50%–90% rise times. The Elmore delay is the **first moment** of the impulse response; the *actual* rise time requires the 0.69 multiplier.
> 2. **Mixing up the Elmore formula** by adding $C_L$ inside the summation *twice*, leading to an incorrect $nR_{tr}C_L + nRC_L$ term.
> 3. **Ignoring the factor of 2 in the coupling capacitance** in the total wire capacitance.
> 4. **Using $R$ instead of $R/2$** as the average resistance of a distributed line. The $1/2$ factor comes from the *centroid* of the linear resistance distribution.
> 5. **Not stating the units explicitly** in numerical answers. KTU boards *always* require units in the final answer.
> 6. **Omitting the limiting case** of the Elmore delay when asked for "the continuous limit." Always state the $\tfrac{1}{2} r c L^2$ form.
> 7. **Confusing sheet resistance with bulk resistivity** in the very first Part-A question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> A high-density, rapid-revision checklist for last-minute review:

- **Wire geometry** = study of *physical dimensions* (W, L, t, S, H) of interconnects and their impact on R, C, and delay.
- **Sheet resistance:** $R_s = \rho / t$ in Ω/□. Wire resistance: $R = R_s (L/W)$.
- **Capacitance components:** area ($C_a = \varepsilon_{ox} W L / H$), fringing ($C_f \approx \varepsilon_{ox} L$), coupling ($C_c = \varepsilon_{ox} t L / S$). In modern processes, **coupling dominates**.
- **Lumped RC delay:** $\tau_D = 0.69 R_{eq} C_{eq}$.
- **Distributed RC delay (continuous limit):** $\tau_D = 0.38 r c L^2$ — **quadratic in length**.
- **Elmore delay of $n$-segment ladder:** $\tau_D = R_{tr} c L + \tfrac{1}{2} r c L^2$.
- **Optimal wire width:** $W_{opt} = \sqrt{R_s (c_f L + C_L) / (2 R_{tr} c_0)}$.
- **Optimal repeater count (Bakoglu):** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{R_w C_w / (2 R_{tr} C_{tr})}$ — scales as $\sqrt{L}$.
- **Optimal repeater size:** $s_{opt} = \sqrt{R_{tr} C_w / (R_w C_{tr})}$.
- **Minimum buffered delay** scales *linearly* with $L$ (versus $L^2$ unbuffered).
- **Crosstalk / Miller effect:** coupling capacitance $C_c$ produces effective $C = 2 C_c$ (opposite switching) or $0$ (same switching).
- **Layer hierarchy:** local (Poly, M1) → semi-global (M2–M5) → global (top metals). Wider and thicker with each tier.
- **Constant to memorize:** 0.69 (rise time), 0.38 (distributed RC rise time), ½ (centroid of distributed R).
- **Always** show units in numerical answers and label diagrams clearly.
- **Always** state assumptions (e.g., $C_L \to 0$, $n \to \infty$, lumped vs distributed).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interconnect Modelling** is the analytical and electrical characterisation of the metallic (typically polysilicon, copper, or aluminium) wires that route signals, power, and clock between transistors on a VLSI die. As CMOS feature sizes scale deep into the nanometer regime (≤ 65 nm), interconnect parasitic effects — namely **resistance (R)**, **capacitance (C)**, and **inductance (L)** — become the dominant determinants of circuit delay, dynamic power dissipation, and signal integrity.

The three parasitic components are formally defined as:

| Parasitic | Symbol | Physical Origin | KTU Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistance | R | Finite conductivity of metal/doped polysilicon wire | Ω (Ohm) |
| Capacitance | C | Coupling between the wire, substrate, and neighbouring wires | F (Farad) |
| Inductance | L | Self and mutual magnetic flux linkage at high frequency switching | H (Henry) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Interconnect parasitics determine the **delay, power, and noise** characteristics of deep-submicron CMOS designs. They are no longer second-order effects and *must* be considered at every level of digital design abstraction.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a VLSI chip as a **large, multi-storey office building**:
- **Transistors** = light switches on each floor.
- **Interconnect wires** = the long electrical cables running through the building's walls and ceilings.
- **Resistance (R)** is the *opposition to current flow* in the wire — like water flowing through a narrow pipe.
- **Capacitance (C)** is the *ability to store electrical charge* — like a tiny invisible water tank attached to the wire. The wire and the ground plane form two plates of a parallel-plate capacitor.
- **Inductance (L)** is the *tendency to oppose sudden changes in current* — like the inertia of a heavy flywheel. It becomes significant only when the current switches extremely fast (high di/dt), which happens in long global wires and clock networks.

When a designer orders a "wire" from the silicon foundry, the foundry is actually delivering a **distributed R, C, and L network** — a transmission line — that profoundly affects how fast a digital signal can travel from one logic gate to another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants used throughout this topic:**
> - Resistivity of copper (Cu): $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m}$
> - Resistivity of aluminium (Al): $\rho \approx \mathbf{2.65 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m}$
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m}$
> - Relative permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_r \approx \mathbf{3.9}$
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ m/s}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wire Resistance (R)

The DC resistance of a uniform interconnect segment of length $L$, width $W$, and thickness $t$ is governed by Ohm's law applied to a uniform conductor:

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t}$$

**Why this matters in VLSI:**
- As wires are made thinner (smaller $t$ and $W$) with each technology node, $R$ **increases linearly**.
- The same wire, made *narrower* on a finer process, becomes a worse conductor.
- Long global wires (clock, data buses) can have resistances in the **kΩ range**.

#### 2.1.1 Sheet Resistance (R□)

A VLSI-favoured metric is the **sheet resistance** $R_{\square}$, defined as the resistance of a square sheet of the material:

$$R_{\square} = \frac{\rho}{t} \quad [\Omega/\square]$$

> The number of "squares" of a rectangular wire is simply $n = L/W$. The total resistance is then the elegant product:

$$R = R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{W} = R_{\square} \cdot n_{\square}$$

**Why "squares"?** Any square of side $x$ cut from a uniform conducting film has resistance $\rho/t$, regardless of $x$. This makes resistance estimation trivial for layout designers — just count the squares between two points.

#### 2.1.2 Skin Effect at High Frequencies

At GHz clock frequencies, current tends to crowd near the **surface** of the conductor, effectively reducing the cross-section through which current flows. The skin depth $\delta$ is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}}$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency and $\mu$ is the permeability. The effective AC resistance becomes:

$$R_{ac} \approx \frac{\rho \cdot L}{2(W + t)\cdot \delta} \quad \text{for } \delta \ll W, t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume R is constant with frequency. In modern GHz designs, R increases with $\sqrt{f}$ due to skin effect, which can degrade clock distribution networks.

---

### 2.2 Wire Capacitance (C)

The capacitance of an interconnect to its surroundings is the **sum of three physically distinct contributions**:

#### 2.2.1 The Three-Capacitance Model

1. **Area Capacitance (C_a)** — parallel-plate capacitance between the *bottom face* of the wire and the substrate:

$$C_a = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot W \cdot L}{t_{ox}}$$

2. **Fringing Capacitance (C_f)** — capacitance due to the *electric field lines* that fringe from the sides of the wire to the substrate. For a wire of height $h$ and width $W$:

$$C_f \approx \varepsilon_{ox} \cdot L \cdot \left[0.77 + 1.06 \left(\frac{W}{t_{ox}}\right)^{0.25} + 1.06 \left(\frac{t_{ox}}{W}\right)^{0.5}\right]$$

3. **Lateral (Coupling) Capacitance (C_l)** — capacitance between the *side faces* of the wire and an adjacent neighbour at distance $S$:

$$C_l = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot t \cdot L}{S}$$

The **total capacitance per unit length** is:

$$C_{tot} = C_a/L + 2C_f/L + C_l/L = c_a + 2c_f + c_l \quad [F/m]$$

where lowercase symbols denote per-unit-length values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inter-layer Dielectric (ILD):** The dielectric used between metal layers in modern processes is typically **SiO₂** ($\varepsilon_r = 3.9$) or low-$\kappa$ dielectrics ($\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ – $2.7$) in advanced nodes to reduce capacitance.

#### 2.2.2 Miller Coupling Effect

When two adjacent wires switch in **opposite** directions (a 0→1 transition on one neighbour while the other goes 1→0), the effective capacitance seen by the driver is **doubled** because the coupling capacitance behaves as a Miller multiplier:

$$C_{eff, Miller} = 2 \cdot C_l$$

This is a critical source of **crosstalk-induced delay** in deep-submicron designs.

---

### 2.3 Wire Inductance (L)

Inductance arises from magnetic flux linkage when current flows through a wire. Two forms matter in VLSI:

#### 2.3.1 Self-Inductance

For a long straight wire of length $\ell$ and radius $r$ (with $\ell \gg r$), the internal + external self-inductance is:

$$L_{self} = \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\frac{2\ell}{r}\right) - 1\right]$$

For on-chip wires, the **external inductance** (field outside the wire) dominates, giving roughly $L \approx 1\ \text{nH/mm}$.

#### 2.3.2 Mutual Inductance

Two parallel wires carrying currents $i_1$ and $i_2$ experience magnetic coupling:

$$M = \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\frac{2\ell}{D}\right) - 1 + \frac{D}{\ell}\right]$$

where $D$ is the centre-to-centre spacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When does inductance matter?** Inductance is negligible when:
> - Wires are **short** (resistance dominates)
> - Switching frequencies are **low** (capacitance dominates)
> 
> Inductance becomes critical for **global wires** (clock trees, data buses) in multi-GHz designs where the wire length approaches $\lambda/10$ of the signal's electrical wavelength.

---

### 2.4 The RC Delay and Elmore's Model

For most on-chip wires, the **lumped RC model** is a reasonable first-order approximation. The fundamental time constant is:

$$\tau_{RC} = R \cdot C$$

For a **distributed RC line** (a wire modelled as many infinitesimally small RC segments), Elmore's delay model gives the 50% propagation delay as:

$$t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{tot} \cdot C_{tot} = 0.69 \cdot (r \cdot \ell) \cdot (c \cdot \ell) = 0.69 \, r \, c \, \ell^2$$

> **The single most important KTU takeaway:** On-chip delay scales with the **square of wire length**, $\ell^2$. Doubling the wire length *quadruples* the delay — this is why long global wires are designed with buffers (repeaters).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wire resistance | $R = \rho L / (Wt)$ | Ω | Thinner wire → higher R |
| 2 | Sheet resistance | $R_{\square} = \rho / t$ | Ω/□ | Material property, geometry-free |
| 3 | Resistance from squares | $R = R_{\square} \cdot (L/W)$ | Ω | $n = L/W$ squares |
| 4 | Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho / (\omega \mu)}$ | m | Decreases with $\sqrt{f}$ |
| 5 | Area capacitance (per unit length) | $c_a = \varepsilon_{ox} W / t_{ox}$ | F/m | Dominant for wide wires |
| 6 | Lateral capacitance (per unit length) | $c_l = \varepsilon_{ox} t / S$ | F/m | Dominant for narrow, dense wires |
| 7 | Total capacitance | $C_{tot} = c_a \ell + 2c_f \ell + c_l \ell$ | F | Sum of three contributions |
| 8 | Miller effective capacitance | $C_{eff} = 2 C_l$ | F | For opposite-switching neighbours |
| 9 | Self-inductance | $L = \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi}\left[\ln(2\ell/r) - 1\right]$ | H | Logarithmic in length |
| 10 | Mutual inductance (parallel wires) | $M = \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi}\left[\ln(2\ell/D) - 1 + D/\ell\right]$ | H | Decreases with spacing $D$ |
| 11 | Lumped RC time constant | $\tau = RC$ | s | First-order delay |
| 12 | Distributed RC delay (Elmore) | $t_p = 0.69\, r\, c\, \ell^2$ | s | Delay $\propto \ell^2$ |
| 13 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Ω | Transmission-line property |
| 14 | Propagation velocity | $v = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | m/s | Approaches $c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ |
| 15 | Wire is inductive if | $\ell \gtrsim t_{rise} \cdot c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | m | Long wire + fast edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These equations are used daily in static timing analysis (STA), power grid design, signal integrity verification, and physical synthesis in industry tools such as Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC, and Mentor Calibre.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wire Resistance Equation

We begin from the microscopic definition of resistance in a uniform conductor of length $L$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 1** — Identify the cross-section of a rectangular on-chip wire.
The wire has width $W$ and thickness $t$, so the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the current flow is $A = W \cdot t$.

**Step 2** — Substitute into the resistance formula.

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t}$$

**Step 3** — Introduce the sheet resistance concept. Factor the expression to expose $\rho/t$:

$$R = \left(\frac{\rho}{t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right) = R_{\square} \cdot n_{\square}$$

**Step 4** — Interpretation. $R_{\square}$ depends only on the material and its thickness (the fabrication process), while $n_{\square}$ depends only on the *layout shape* of the wire. A layout designer need only count the number of squares in the drawn polygon to estimate the wire resistance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Area Capacitance

We model the wire (top plate), the substrate (bottom plate), and the SiO₂ between them (dielectric) as a parallel-plate capacitor.

**Step 1** — Recall the parallel-plate capacitance formula for a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and plate area $A$ separated by distance $d$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A}{d}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the wire geometry. The plate area is the bottom face of the wire, $A = W \cdot L$. The separation is the oxide thickness $t_{ox}$. The dielectric is SiO₂, so $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

**Step 3** — Write the per-unit-length form (divide both sides by $L$):

$$c_a = \frac{C_a}{L} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot W}{t_{ox}} \quad [F/m]$$

**Step 4** — For an aluminium wire with $W = 1\ \mu m$, $t_{ox} = 0.1\ \mu m$, $\varepsilon_r = 3.9$:

$$c_a = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.345\ pF/mm$$

---

### 3.3 Elmore Delay Derivation for a Distributed RC Line

This is the **most important derivation** in this module for KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Set up the distributed model.** A wire of length $\ell$ is modelled as a chain of $n$ identical RC segments, each with resistance $r\ell/n$ and capacitance $c\ell/n$, where $r$ and $c$ are the per-unit-length resistance and capacitance.

**Step 2 — Step-response of one segment.** The unit-step response of a single RC low-pass filter is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Cascade delay accumulation.** For the first segment, the 50% delay (when $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$) is:

$$t_{p,1} = 0.69 \cdot R_1 C_1$$

**Step 4 — Elmore's theorem.** Elmore (1948) proved that for a tree-structured RC network, the dominant-pole approximation of the step response delay from input node to any output node $k$ is:

$$t_{pd,k} = \sum_{i} R_{ik} \cdot C_i$$

where $R_{ik}$ is the *common resistance* in the path from the input to both node $i$ and node $k$, and $C_i$ is the capacitance at node $i$.

**Step 5 — Apply to a uniform distributed line.** Discretise the wire into $n$ nodes, each with capacitance $c\ell/n$, separated by resistors $r\ell/n$. For the $j$-th node from the driver, the common path resistance is $R_{ik} = r \cdot j\ell/n$.

**Step 6 — Sum the Elmore expression in the limit $n \to \infty$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pd} &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left(\frac{r \ell}{n}\right) \cdot j \cdot \left(\frac{c \ell}{n}\right) \\
&= \frac{r c \ell^2}{n^2} \sum_{j=1}^{n} j \\
&= \frac{r c \ell^2}{n^2} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Take the continuum limit ($n \to \infty$):**

$$t_{pd} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{r c \ell^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right) = \frac{r c \ell^2}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Final Elmore delay for a distributed RC line** (the 50% propagation delay to the far end):

$$t_{p, Elmore} = 0.69 \cdot R_{tot} \cdot \frac{C_{tot}}{2} = \frac{0.69 \cdot r c \ell^2}{2}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 5 mm long, 1 µm wide, 0.5 µm thick aluminium interconnect runs over a 0.2 µm thick SiO₂ layer. Given:
- $\rho_{Al} = 2.65 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$
- $\varepsilon_r(SiO_2) = 3.9$
- Neglect fringing and lateral capacitance.

**Find:** (a) Wire resistance, (b) Area capacitance, (c) RC time constant, (d) Elmore delay.

**(a) Resistance:**

$$R = \frac{2.65 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.325 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-13}} = 265\ \Omega$$

**(b) Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854 \times 5 \cdot 10^{-21}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{172.6 \times 10^{-21}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.863\ pF$$

**(c) RC time constant:**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 265 \cdot 0.863 \times 10^{-12} = 228.7\ ps$$

**(d) Elmore delay:**

$$t_p = 0.69 \cdot \frac{R \cdot C}{2} = 0.69 \cdot \frac{228.7\ ps}{2} = 78.9\ ps$$

---

### 3.5 When Does Inductance Become Important? — Analytical Criterion

A wire transitions from "RC-dominated" to "LC/inductive" behaviour when its **unloaded flight time** $t_{flight} = \ell / v$ approaches the signal rise time $t_{rise}$:

$$t_{flight} = \ell \sqrt{LC} = \ell \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$

The rule of thumb used in industry and the KTU syllabus is:

$$\ell_{crit} \approx \frac{t_{rise}}{10} \cdot \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

For SiO₂ ($\varepsilon_r = 3.9$) and $t_{rise} = 100\ ps$:

$$\ell_{crit} \approx \frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{10} \cdot \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{3.9}} \approx 1.51\ mm$$

> Wires **longer than ~1.5 mm** at 100 ps rise time begin to exhibit inductive effects.

This is the *exact* structure that static timing analysers model internally for long wires.
