## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define sheet resistance. A 0.5 µm thick polysilicon interconnect has a sheet resistance of 8 Ω/□. Find the resistance of a wire of length 200 µm and width 2 µm.

**Model Answer:**

Sheet resistance $R_{\square}$ is the resistance of a square sheet of conducting material, independent of the absolute size of the square. It is defined as:

$$R_{\square} = \frac{\rho}{t} \quad [\Omega/\square]$$

Number of squares: $n = L/W = 200/2 = 100$ squares.

$$R = R_{\square} \cdot n = 8 \times 100 = 800\ \Omega$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List the three components of on-chip interconnect capacitance and state which one dominates for **(a)** a wide, isolated global wire, and **(b)** two closely-spaced narrow minimum-width wires.

**Model Answer:**

The three components are:
1. **Area capacitance ($C_a$)** — bottom face to substrate
2. **Fringing capacitance ($C_f$)** — side faces to substrate
3. **Lateral coupling capacitance ($C_l$)** — between adjacent wires

- **(a)** Wide isolated global wire: **Area capacitance $C_a$ dominates** because $W$ is large.
- **(b)** Closely-spaced narrow minimum-width wires: **Lateral capacitance $C_l$ dominates** because $S$ is very small, making $C_l = \varepsilon_{ox} t / S$ large. In modern processes at the 45 nm node and below, $C_l$ can exceed $C_a$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Elmore delay of a uniformly distributed RC interconnect line of length $\ell$, with per-unit-length resistance $r$ and per-unit-length capacitance $c$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4 mm long wire has $r = 0.075\ \Omega/\mu m$ and $c = 0.12\ fF/\mu m$. Compute the 50% propagation delay using the Elmore formula. Comment on the dependence of delay on wire length.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Discretise the wire into $n$ identical RC segments, each with resistance $r\ell/n$ and capacitance $c\ell/n$. By Elmore's theorem, the 50% delay from input to the $k$-th node is:

$$t_{pd,k} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ik} C_j$$

For a uniform line and the far-end node:

$$t_p = \frac{rc\ell^2}{n^2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}j = \frac{rc\ell^2}{n^2}\cdot\frac{n(n+1)}{2} \xrightarrow{n\to\infty} \frac{rc\ell^2}{2}$$

Including the 0.69 factor for the 50% point of the step response:

$$\boxed{t_p = 0.69 \cdot \frac{rc\ell^2}{2}}$$

**(b)** Convert units: $r = 0.075\ \Omega/\mu m = 75\ \Omega/m$, $c = 0.12\ fF/\mu m = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}\ F/m$, $\ell = 4\ mm = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ m$.

$$rc\ell^2 = 75 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 1.44 \times 10^{-13}\ s^2/\Omega\cdot F \cdot \Omega = 144\ ps$$

$$t_p = 0.69 \times 144/2 = 49.7\ ps$$

**Comment:** Delay grows as $\ell^2$. Doubling the wire length quadruples the delay. This is why **buffer insertion (repeaters)** is mandatory for long global wires — splitting a long wire into $N$ segments reduces the delay to roughly $O(\ell^2 / N)$.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 – Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three-capacitance model for on-chip interconnects. Derive the area capacitance for an aluminium wire of width $W = 1\ \mu m$, oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 0.1\ \mu m$, length $L = 1\ mm$, with $\varepsilon_r = 3.9$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the Miller effect in interconnect capacitance? Two parallel metal-2 wires each 500 µm long, 0.5 µm wide, 0.5 µm thick, separated by 0.2 µm, run over a 0.1 µm oxide. Compute the lateral coupling capacitance and the Miller-doubled effective load when both wires switch in opposite directions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *See Section 2.2 for the full conceptual model.* The three contributions are $C_a$ (parallel-plate to substrate), $C_f$ (fringing field to substrate), and $C_l$ (lateral to neighbours).

For area capacitance:

$$C_a = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r W L}{t_{ox}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.9 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.345\ pF$$

**(b)** The Miller effect arises because the voltage across the coupling capacitor can swing by $2 V_{DD}$ when adjacent wires switch in opposite directions, doubling the effective charge transfer.

Lateral capacitance:

$$C_l = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot t \cdot L}{S} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_l = \frac{1.726 \times 10^{-20}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.63 \times 10^{-14}\ F \approx 86.3\ fF$$

Miller-doubled effective load:

$$C_{eff} = 2 C_l \approx 172.6\ fF$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor** in Elmore's delay. The formula $t_p = rc\ell^2/2$ is the *time constant*; the *50% delay* needs the 0.69 multiplier.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — $\Omega/\mu m$ vs $\Omega/m$, $fF$ vs $pF$. Always convert per-unit-length values to **SI units (m, F, Ω)** before computing.
> 3. **Ignoring fringing capacitance** for narrow wires. At sub-100 nm nodes, fringing and lateral capacitance together often *exceed* the area capacitance.
> 4. **Mis-stating the inductance criterion** — students often claim "inductance always matters" or "inductance never matters". The correct statement is: *inductance matters when $\ell \gtrsim t_{rise} \cdot c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$*.
> 5. **Sheet resistance unit confusion** — $R_{\square}$ is in **Ω per square** (a dimensionless count multiplied by Ω). Do not write "Ω" alone.
> 6. **Mixing up self and mutual inductance** — mutual inductance requires *current flow in a return path*; the on-chip return path is the ground/power network.
> 7. **Missing the $2 C_f$ factor** — the total fringing contribution is doubled because a wire has *two* side faces.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential things to remember before the KTU exam:**

- **Wire resistance:** $R = \rho L / (Wt) = R_{\square} \cdot n_{\square}$. Sheet resistance is the layout engineer's best friend.
- **Capacitance has 3 parts:** Area ($C_a$), Fringing ($C_f$, $\times 2$ for two sides), Lateral ($C_l$). Modern processes are **lateral-capacitance-dominated**.
- **Miller effect:** Opposite-switching neighbours double the effective load capacitance: $C_{eff} = 2 C_l$.
- **Elmore delay (distributed RC line):** $t_p = 0.69 rc\ell^2 / 2$. Delay grows **quadratically** with wire length.
- **Repeaters are mandatory** for long global wires because they convert the $\ell^2$ dependence into an $\ell$ dependence.
- **Inductance matters** only when $\ell \gtrsim t_{rise} \cdot c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. For 100 ps rise time in SiO₂, this is roughly 1.5 mm.
- **Skin effect:** At high frequency, AC resistance increases as $\sqrt{f}$ because current crowds near the wire surface.
- **Three interconnect abstraction models:** Lumped RC (short), Distributed RC (medium), Transmission line RLC (long, fast).
- **Cu vs Al:** Copper has lower resistivity ($\rho = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$ vs Al's $2.65 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$) — used in modern processes to combat rising R.
- **Low-$\kappa$ dielectrics:** $\varepsilon_r < 3.9$ used in advanced nodes to reduce capacitance and crosstalk.
- **Crosstalk** is a direct consequence of $C_l$ and is the primary signal-integrity concern in deep-submicron design.
- **Master the unit conversions** — most KTU exam mistakes are dimensional, not conceptual.
- **The $\ell^2$ scaling** is the single most-tested insight — expect it in nearly every KTU Module-4 question on interconnects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interconnect Impact: Delay, Energy, Crosstalk, and Inductive Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interconnect Impact: Delay, Energy, Crosstalk, and Inductive Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In modern deep-submicron CMOS technology (sub-90 nm), the **interconnect** — the metal wiring that connects transistors — is no longer a passive, ideal element. It behaves as a **distributed R, L, and C network** that significantly degrades circuit performance.

**Interconnect Impact** is the collective effect of parasitic resistance ($R_w$), parasitic capacitance (which splits into **ground capacitance** $C_g$ and **coupling capacitance** $C_c$), and **parasitic inductance** ($L_w$) on the four fundamental metrics of digital design:

1. **Interconnect Delay** — slowing down of signal propagation along a wire.
2. **Interconnect Energy** — extra dynamic and short-circuit power dissipated in the wires.
3. **Crosstalk** — unintended electrical coupling (noise + delay) between adjacent wires.
4. **Inductive Effects** — voltage drop and ringing caused by $L \frac{di}{dt}$ on wide, long global wires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As feature size shrinks, gate delay decreases (≈ 30%/generation), but **wire delay INCREASES** due to narrower, taller, and longer wires. By 45 nm and below, wire delay **dominates** gate delay. Hence this topic carries heavy weightage in Module 4.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building Water-Pipe Network"

Think of your chip as a **large apartment complex**:
- The **transistors** are the **water pumps** (active, fast).
- The **interconnect wires** are the **pipes** carrying water (signal) from one pump to another.

Now, in this analogy:
- **Resistance (R)** = pipe friction. Water arrives slower because of friction along the pipe walls.
- **Capacitance (C)** = the pipe is slightly elastic. It takes time to "fill" before water flows out the other end.
- **Coupling Capacitance (C_c)** = two parallel pipes lying close together. If you suddenly turn on water in Pipe A, Pipe B "jiggles" (crosstalk).
- **Inductance (L)** = the inertia of the water column. When you slam the valve shut, the water hammer causes a pressure surge (voltage overshoot/ringing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For *local* wires (≤ 10 µm), only RC matters. For *intermediate* wires, coupling C dominates. For *global* wires (clock, power, data buses > 1 mm), **inductance** can no longer be ignored.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (45 nm) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sheet resistance (Cu, top metal) | $R_\square$ | **0.05 – 0.1** | $\Omega/\square$ |
| Wire capacitance (area) | $c_a$ | **30 – 100** | aF/µm² |
| Coupling capacitance | $c_c$ | **50 – 200** | aF/µm |
| Wire inductance | $l_w$ | **0.5 – 5** | pH/µm |
| Velocity of signal on wire | $v$ | $\approx c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Permittivity of SiO₂ | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **3.9 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |

> [!TIP]
> The **dielectric constant of SiO₂** ($\varepsilon_r = 3.9$) and the **velocity of light in vacuum** ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) are the two constants you must memorize for any interconnect analysis.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Interconnects (KTU Standard Classification)

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (and Weste-Harris convention), interconnects are classified by length:

| Class | Length | Dominant Parasitic | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | ≤ 10 µm | R + C_g | Delay, drive strength |
| **Semi-global** | 10 µm – 1 mm | C_c (coupling) | **Crosstalk** |
| **Global** | ≥ 1 mm | R, C_c, **L** | **Inductance**, delay, skew |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interconnect Resistance

A wire of length $\ell$, width $w$, thickness $t$, and resistivity $\rho$ has a total resistance:

$$R_w = \rho \cdot \frac{\ell}{w \cdot t}$$

To make this independent of $\rho$, the industry uses **sheet resistance** $R_\square = \rho / t$, measured in **ohms per square**:

$$R_w = R_\square \cdot \frac{\ell}{w}$$

**Key Insight:** As $w$ shrinks in advanced nodes, $R_w$ **increases** for the same length — this is why wire delay gets worse with scaling.

## 2.2 Interconnect Capacitance

The total capacitance on a wire has three components:

$$C_{total} = C_{area} + C_{fringe} + 2 \cdot C_{coupling}$$

- $C_{area}$ — parallel-plate capacitance to the substrate (ground).
- $C_{fringe}$ — side-wall fringing field capacitance.
- $2 \cdot C_{coupling}$ — capacitance to the *two* neighboring wires (left and right). The factor 2 arises because we count both neighbors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern processes, $C_{coupling}$ is often **larger than** $C_{area}$ because wires are tall and narrow with small horizontal spacing. This is the root cause of all crosstalk.

## 2.3 Lumped vs Distributed RC Model

A long wire is naturally a **distributed** RC network. KTU expects you to know both views.

### (a) Lumped RC Model (Short Wires)
For a wire with total $R_w$ and $C_w$, lumped at the output:

$$\tau_{lumped} = R_w \cdot C_w$$

### (b) Distributed RC Model (Long Wires)
For a uniform wire, divide it into $N$ identical RC segments. The **Elmore delay** (1954, used universally in CMOS timing) gives the 50% propagation delay:

$$\tau_{50\%} = 0.69 \cdot R_w \cdot C_w \quad \text{(for a single-step driver)}$$

For a wire driven by a gate of resistance $R_d$:

$$\tau_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot (R_d \cdot C_w + R_w \cdot C_w / 2 + R_d \cdot C_{L,\text{load}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.69** comes from the Elmore approximation of a step response; the **1/2** for the distributed R·C term is because the resistance is shared (some of the C is "pre-charged" through less R).

## 2.4 Crosstalk — Two Coupled Wires

Consider two adjacent wires (victim and aggressor) with coupling capacitance $C_c$.

### (a) Crosstalk Delay Effect
When the aggressor switches in the *opposite* direction to the victim, the effective Miller capacitance seen by the victim driver is:

$$C_{eff} = C_g + 2 \cdot C_c \quad \text{(opposite switching — WORST case)}$$

$$C_{eff} = C_g + 0 \cdot C_c = C_g \quad \text{(same direction switching — BEST case)}$$

### (b) Crosstalk Noise (Glitch on Static Victim)
If the victim is quiet (held high or low) and the aggressor switches with amplitude $V_{DD}$ through a coupling capacitance $C_c$ to a victim capacitance $C_v$ (to ground), the peak noise voltage on the victim is:

$$V_{noise} = \frac{C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD}$$

The **coupling factor** is:

$$k_c = \frac{C_c}{C_v + C_c}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU frequently tests: "If $C_v = 100$ fF and $C_c = 100$ fF, what is the noise voltage for $V_{DD} = 1$ V?" Answer: **0.5 V** — which can flip a static logic level!

## 2.5 Inductive Effects (RLC Wires)

Inductance becomes important when the **wire length approaches the signal wavelength** or when signals have **fast edges** ($t_r < 2.5 \cdot t_{flight}$, where $t_{flight}$ is the line delay).

Characteristic impedance of an RLC line:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_w}{C_w}}$$

For a lossless line, the **propagation velocity** is:

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_w \cdot C_w}}$$

The condition for the line to be "electrically long" is:

$$\ell > \frac{t_r}{2.5 \cdot \sqrt{L_w C_w}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often say "inductance is always negligible on-chip." This is **wrong** for wide, thick global wires (clock distribution, power grid) operating at GHz frequencies. Always justify your answer with the $t_r$ vs $t_{flight}$ inequality.

## 2.6 Energy & Power Dissipation in Interconnects

The energy required to charge a wire capacitance $C_w$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ is:

$$E_w = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_w \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

For a wire switching at frequency $f$:

$$P_w = C_w \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

For $N$ such wires, the **interconnect power** can exceed the gate power in data buses.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every equation that has appeared in KTU 2024 Scheme past papers for this module. **Memorize the column "When to Use"** — it is the key to picking the right formula in the exam.

| # | Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_w = R_\square \cdot \ell / w$ | Wire resistance | Given sheet resistance |
| 2 | $C_w = c_a \cdot \ell \cdot w + 2 c_f \cdot \ell + 2 c_c \cdot \ell$ | Total wire capacitance | Single isolated wire |
| 3 | $\tau_{lumped} = 0.69 R_w C_w$ | Lumped RC delay | Short wire |
| 4 | $\tau_{pd} = 0.69 (R_d C_w + R_w C_w / 2 + R_d C_L)$ | Distributed Elmore delay | Long wire with driver $R_d$ |
| 5 | $C_{eff} = C_g + 2 C_c$ | Worst-case coupling capacitance | Opposite switching |
| 6 | $C_{eff} = C_g$ | Best-case coupling capacitance | Same-direction switching |
| 7 | $V_{noise} = (C_c / (C_v + C_c)) \cdot V_{DD}$ | Peak crosstalk noise | Static victim |
| 8 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L_w / C_w}$ | Characteristic impedance | Inductive analysis |
| 9 | $v = 1 / \sqrt{L_w C_w}$ | Propagation velocity | RLC line |
| 10 | $t_{flight} = \ell / v$ | Flight time on line | RLC line |
| 11 | $E_w = \frac{1}{2} C_w V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per transition | Energy calculation |
| 12 | $P_w = C_w V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power in wire | Power calculation |
| 13 | $\ell > t_r / (2.5 \sqrt{L_w C_w})$ | Electrically long criterion | When to use RLC |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in **Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Innovus, and Mentor Calibre** for Static Timing Analysis (STA), Signal Integrity (SI) checks, and IR-drop analysis. Mastery of these equations = mastery of modern chip design flow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Elmore Delay of a Distributed RC Line

**Problem Setup:** A uniform wire of total resistance $R_w$ and total capacitance $C_w$ is driven by a step source with source resistance $R_d$. Find the 50% propagation delay $\tau_{pd}$.

### Step 1 — Discretize the Wire
Divide the wire into $N$ identical segments. Segment $i$ has resistance $R_w / N$ and capacitance $C_w / N$.

### Step 2 — Define the Transfer Function
For each segment, the Laplace-domain transfer from input to node $i$ is:

$$H_i(s) = \prod_{k=1}^{i} \frac{1}{1 + s R_k C_k}$$

The overall transfer function from source to output (node $N$) is:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{\left(1 + s R_d C_1 / N\right) \cdots \left(1 + s (R_d + R_w) C_N / N\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Expand Denominator
Expand the denominator to get the **Elmore delay**, which is the sum of all time constants divided by $-1$ times the coefficient of $s$ divided by the constant term:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{i} R_k$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Distributed Values
Each $C_i = C_w / N$ and $R_k = R_d + (k-1) R_w / N$. The double sum becomes:

$$\tau_D = \frac{C_w}{N} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{i} \left(R_d + (k-1) \frac{R_w}{N}\right)$$

$$\tau_D = \frac{C_w}{N} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(i R_d + \frac{R_w}{N} \cdot \frac{(i-1)i}{2}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Sum of Integers
Using $\sum_{i=1}^{N} i = N(N+1)/2 \approx N^2/2$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{N} i^2 \approx N^3/3$ for large $N$:

$$\tau_D = \frac{C_w}{N} \left( \frac{N^2}{2} R_d + \frac{R_w}{N} \cdot \frac{N^3}{6} \right)$$

$$\tau_D = \frac{R_d C_w}{2} + \frac{R_w C_w}{6}$$

### Step 6 — Add the Load Capacitance Contribution
If there is a load capacitance $C_L$ at the end, it sees the full source + wire resistance:

$$\tau_{load} = (R_d + R_w) C_L$$

### Step 7 — Combine All Terms
The **Elmore propagation delay** is:

$$\tau_{pd} = 0.69 \left( \frac{R_d C_w}{2} + \frac{R_w C_w}{6} + (R_d + R_w) C_L \right)$$

For $C_L = 0$ and $R_d \ll R_w$:

$$\tau_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot \frac{R_w C_w}{2}$$

> This matches the **"half-RC"** rule of thumb for a distributed line.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Crosstalk Noise Voltage on a Static Victim

**Problem Setup:** Victim line is at $V_{DD}$ (high), with capacitance $C_v$ to ground. Aggressor line is at 0 V and switches to $V_{DD}$ with rise time $t_r$. The two lines are coupled by $C_c$. Find the peak voltage induced on the victim.

### Step 1 — Write the Charge Conservation Equation
Let $V_v$ be the induced victim voltage, $V_a = V_{DD}$ the aggressor final voltage.

**Total charge on victim node before switching:** $Q_{before} = C_v \cdot V_{DD} + C_c \cdot (V_{DD} - 0) = V_{DD}(C_v + C_c)$

**Total charge on victim node after switching:** $Q_{after} = C_v \cdot V_v + C_c \cdot (V_v - V_{DD})$

### Step 2 — Apply Conservation ($Q_{before} = Q_{after}$)
$$V_{DD}(C_v + C_c) = C_v V_v + C_c V_v - C_c V_{DD}$$

$$V_{DD} C_v + V_{DD} C_c + V_{DD} C_c = C_v V_v + C_c V_v$$

$$V_{DD} C_v + 2 V_{DD} C_c = V_v (C_v + C_c)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_v$
$$V_v = \frac{C_v + 2 C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD}$$

### Step 4 — Subtract the Original Bias (Final Step)
The *change* in victim voltage (the noise) is $\Delta V_v = V_v - V_{DD}$:

$$\Delta V_v = \frac{C_v + 2 C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD} - V_{DD} = \frac{C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD}$$

$$\boxed{V_{noise} = \frac{C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD}}$$

> This is the **Kushner / Sakurai** formula that KTU uses verbatim.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: When to Use the Inductive (RLC) Model

**Problem Setup:** A clock wire of length $\ell = 5$ mm has $L_w = 1$ nH/mm and $C_w = 0.2$ pF/mm. The rise time is $t_r = 50$ ps. Determine whether the RLC model is required.

### Step 1 — Compute Per-Unit-Length Quantities
$$L_w = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ H/mm} = 10^{-12} \text{ H/µm}$$

$$C_w = 0.2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/mm} = 2 \times 10^{-16} \text{ F/µm}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Propagation Velocity
$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_w C_w}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{-16}}}$$

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-28}}} = \frac{1}{1.414 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 7.07 \times 10^{13} \text{ µm/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Flight Time
$$t_{flight} = \frac{\ell}{v} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \text{ µm}}{7.07 \times 10^{13} \text{ µm/s}} \approx 70.7 \text{ ps}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Criterion
We require $t_r < 2.5 \cdot t_{flight}$ for inductive effects to matter:

$$2.5 \cdot t_{flight} = 2.5 \times 70.7 = 176.75 \text{ ps}$$

Since $t_r = 50$ ps $< 176.75$ ps, **the wire is electrically long and inductance must be modeled**.

> [!TIP]
> This derivation structure is exactly what KTU examiners want in a 14-mark question. Practice writing all three steps with explicit numbers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Driving a Large Load

**Scenario:** A minimum-size inverter ($R_{eq,n} = 10$ kΩ) must drive a 5 mm global wire ($R_w = 200$ Ω, $C_w = 2$ pF) and a load capacitance $C_L = 1$ pF.

| Configuration | Delay Formula | Numerical Value (ps) |
|---|---|---|
| Lumped RC: $\tau = R_{eq} C_w$ | $0.69 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-12}$ | **13,800** |
| Elmore (distributed): $\tau = 0.69 (R_{eq} C_w / 2 + R_w C_w / 6 + (R_{eq}+R_w) C_L)$ | $0.69 (10{,}000 \cdot 1\text{p} + 200 \cdot 0.33\text{p} + 10{,}200 \cdot 1\text{p})$ | $0.69 (10{,}000 + 66 + 10{,}200) \text{ ps} \approx \mathbf{13{,}984 \text{ ps}}$ |
| With buffer inserted (4 cascaded inverters, optimal sizing) | $0.69 \cdot N \cdot t_{inv,min}$ | **≈ 200** |

> The 70× speedup from buffering is the **single most important practical lesson** in interconnect design: *never* drive a large RC load directly — always use a tapered (buffered) chain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interconnect Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CMOS Inverter Driver] -->|R_d| B[Lumped RC Section]
    B --> C[Distributed RC Line]
    C --> D[Coupling Capacitor C_c]
    C --> E[Ground Capacitor C_g]
    C --> F[Series Inductance L_w]
    C --> G[Load Capacitance C_L]
    D -->|Aggressor| H[Crosstalk Analysis Module]
    E --> I[Delay Analysis Module]
    F --> J[Inductive Analysis Module]
    I --> K[Elmore Delay tau_pd]
    H --> L[Noise Voltage V_noise]
    J --> M[Characteristic Impedance Z_0]
    K --> N[Timing Report]
    L --> O[Signal Integrity Report]
    M --> P[Reflection Coefficient]
    N --> Q[KTU Final Output: Delay, Energy, Crosstalk, L]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The single distributed RC line simultaneously feeds three analysis modules. The KTU exam asks you to compute (a) the delay, (b) the crosstalk noise, and (c) the inductive ringing. All three come from the same physical wire.

## 4.2 RC Tree Structure for Elmore Delay

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Vin] --> rd[Source R_d]
    rd --> n1[Node 1]
    n1 --> r1[R_w / N]
    n1 --> c1[C_w / N]
    r1 --> n2[Node 2]
    n2 --> r2[R_w / N]
    n2 --> c2[C_w / N]
    r2 --> n3[Node N]
    n3 --> cN[C_w / N]
    n3 --> cL[Load C_L]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the classic RC ladder. The Elmore delay is the sum $\sum_i C_i \cdot R_{path,i}$ where $R_{path,i}$ is the resistance from the source to node $i$.

## 4.3 Crosstalk Mechanism — Two Coupled Lines

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph W1[Aggressor Wire]
        a1[Aggressor Driver] --> a2[Aggressor Receiver]
    end
    subgraph W2[Victim Wire]
        v1[Victim Driver] --> v2[Victim Receiver]
    end
    a2 -.C_c coupling.-> v2
    v2 -.feedback.-> a2
    style a1 fill:#ffd9b3
    style v1 fill:#b3d9ff
```

> **Color code:** Orange = aggressor (noisy), Blue = victim (clean). The double arrow shows bidirectional coupling.

## 4.4 Inductive Line with Termination

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Driver V_s, R_d] --> tl[Distributed RLC Line, length ell, Z_0]
    tl --> term[Termination R_T]
    term --> gnd[GND]
```

> **Design rule:** To suppress reflections, choose $R_T = Z_0 = \sqrt{L_w / C_w}$. Mismatched termination → overshoot/undershoot on clock lines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEVT501, Module 4]`
**Define interconnect delay. With a neat sketch, explain the Elmore delay model for a distributed RC line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Interconnect delay is the time taken for a signal to propagate from the driver output to the receiver input through the parasitic resistance and capacitance of the wire. For a uniform distributed RC line of total resistance $R_w$ and capacitance $C_w$ driven by a source resistance $R_d$ and terminated by load $C_L$, the **Elmore delay** (50% propagation delay) is:

$$\tau_{pd} = 0.69 \left( R_d C_w + \frac{R_w C_w}{2} + (R_d + R_w) C_L \right)$$

The Elmore model uses the first moment of the impulse response to estimate the dominant time constant. It is widely used in Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools because it is additive over RC trees.

**Sketch (must include):** A driver $R_d$ in series with a ladder of $N$ RC segments, ending in $C_L$, with the input and output waveforms showing 50% crossing points.

*(Examiner's tip: Mention the 0.69 factor explicitly — many students forget it and lose 1 mark.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEVT501, Module 4]`
**What is crosstalk? Derive the expression for the peak noise voltage induced on a static victim line due to switching of an adjacent aggressor line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Crosstalk** is the unintended electrical coupling between adjacent signal lines through their mutual (coupling) capacitance $C_c$. It manifests as (a) extra delay on switching victims and (b) noise glitches on static victims.

**Derivation:** Let victim be static at $V_{DD}$ with capacitance $C_v$ to ground, and aggressor switch from 0 to $V_{DD}$. Using charge conservation before and after the transition:

$$V_{noise} = \frac{C_c}{C_v + C_c} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Practical implications:**
- If $C_c = C_v$, the noise is **50% of $V_{DD}$** — enough to flip a logic level.
- Crosstalk is reduced by **increasing spacing**, **shielding** (grounded lines on both sides), or **using differential signaling**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **Derive the Elmore propagation delay of a 5-segment uniform RC ladder driven by a gate of resistance $R_d = 500$ Ω. Take $R_w = 1$ kΩ total and $C_w = 1$ pF total. The load capacitance is 0.5 pF. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-segment values:**
$$R_k = R_w / N = 1000 / 5 = 200 \text{ Ω per segment}$$

$$C_k = C_w / N = 1 \text{ pF} / 5 = 0.2 \text{ pF per segment}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance seen by each node (from source):**
- Node 1: $R_{path,1} = R_d = 500$ Ω
- Node 2: $R_{path,2} = R_d + R_1 = 500 + 200 = 700$ Ω
- Node 3: $R_{path,3} = 500 + 400 = 900$ Ω
- Node 4: $R_{path,4} = 500 + 600 = 1100$ Ω
- Node 5: $R_{path,5} = 500 + 800 = 1300$ Ω

**Step 3 — Apply Elmore sum:**
$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{5} C_i \cdot R_{path,i}$$

$$\tau_D = 0.2\text{p} \times (500 + 700 + 900 + 1100 + 1300)$$

$$\tau_D = 0.2 \text{ pF} \times 4500 \text{ Ω} = 900 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 — Add the load capacitance contribution:**
The load $C_L = 0.5$ pF sees the full path resistance $R_d + R_w = 500 + 1000 = 1500$ Ω:

$$\tau_{load} = 1500 \text{ Ω} \times 0.5 \text{ pF} = 750 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 5 — Total Elmore delay (50% crossing):**
$$\tau_{pd} = 0.69 \times (\tau_D + \tau_{load}) = 0.69 \times (900 + 750) \text{ ps}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{pd} = 0.69 \times 1650 \text{ ps} = 1138.5 \text{ ps} \approx 1.14 \text{ ns}}$$

#### (b) **Explain the phenomenon of crosstalk in CMOS VLSI interconnects. With a suitable circuit model, show that the worst-case delay increase is 2× the nominal delay. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Crosstalk Definition:** Crosstalk is the electrical interference between two adjacent signal lines coupled through a parasitic capacitance $C_c$. It produces two effects:
1. **Crosstalk delay** — change in victim propagation delay.
2. **Crosstalk noise** — unintended voltage glitch on a static victim.

**Circuit Model:** Consider a victim line with driver $R_{dv}$, ground capacitance $C_g$, load $C_L$, and coupling capacitance $C_c$ to an aggressor driver $R_{da}$.

**Case 1 — Aggressor Quiet (Nominal Delay):**
The victim sees only $C_g + C_L$:

$$\tau_{nom} = 0.69 \cdot R_{dv} (C_g + C_L)$$

**Case 2 — Aggressor Switches in Opposite Direction (Worst Case):**
The voltage difference between the two lines is $2 V_{DD}$. The charge transferred through $C_c$ is doubled — this is the **Miller effect**. The effective capacitance seen by the victim is:

$$C_{eff} = C_g + 2 C_c + C_L$$

$$\tau_{worst} = 0.69 \cdot R_{dv} (C_g + 2 C_c + C_L)$$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{\tau_{worst}}{\tau_{nom}} = \frac{C_g + 2 C_c + C_L}{C_g + C_L} = 1 + \frac{2 C_c}{C_g + C_L}$$

**Special case:** If $C_g = C_L = 0$ (coupling-dominated wire):

$$\frac{\tau_{worst}}{\tau_{nom}} = 1 + 2 = 3$$

If $C_g + C_L = 2 C_c$ (typical layout): the ratio is $1 + 1 = 2$.

Hence, **worst-case delay is approximately 2× the nominal delay** in a typical well-designed layout, justifying the KTU standard claim.

**Case 3 — Aggressor Switches in Same Direction (Best Case):**
Both lines move in the same direction, $V_{DD}$ is across neither side of $C_c$ effectively:

$$C_{eff} = C_g + C_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{best} = \tau_{nom}$$

> **Mitigation techniques:** (i) Increase wire spacing; (ii) Insert grounded shield lines; (iii) Use staggered (skewed) clock routing; (iv) Use differential signaling; (v) Limit maximum adjacent switching time.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) **Discuss the inductive effects in on-chip interconnects. Under what conditions does the RLC model become necessary? Derive the characteristic impedance of a lossless RLC line. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Why Inductance Matters:**
In deep-submicron CMOS, global interconnects (clock distribution, power grid, wide data buses) are wide, thick, and long. The frequency of operation is in the GHz range. Under these conditions:
- Skin effect reduces the effective cross-section.
- The magnetic flux linkage through the wire loop is no longer negligible.
- The voltage drop $V_L = L \, di/dt$ becomes comparable to the IR drop.

**Conditions for RLC Model (Electrically Long Line):**
A wire must be modeled as a transmission line (RLC) when its **flight time** $t_{flight}$ is comparable to the **signal rise time** $t_r$. The precise criterion is:

$$\ell > \frac{t_r}{2.5 \cdot \sqrt{L_w C_w}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad t_{flight} > \frac{t_r}{2.5}$$

If $t_r < 2.5 t_{flight}$, the wire is **electrically long** and inductive effects (overshoot, undershoot, ringing, reflections) become significant.

**Derivation of Characteristic Impedance:**
Consider a lossless RLC line with per-unit-length parameters $L$ and $C$. Write the telegrapher's equations:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}, \quad \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

Assume a traveling wave solution $V(x,t) = V_0 e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$:

$$-jk V_0 = -j\omega L I_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_0}{I_0} = \frac{\omega L}{k}$$

From the second equation, $k = \omega \sqrt{LC}$. Therefore:

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_0} = \frac{\omega L}{\omega \sqrt{LC}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_w}{C_w}}}$$

For typical global wires: $Z_0 \approx 50 \text{ Ω}$. To avoid reflections, **terminate the line with $R_T = Z_0$**.

---

#### (b) **A clock distribution wire is 10 mm long with $L_w = 1$ nH/mm and $C_w = 0.3$ pF/mm. The clock has a rise time of 100 ps. Determine: (i) the propagation velocity, (ii) the flight time, (iii) whether the RLC model is required, and (iv) the characteristic impedance. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\ell = 10 \text{ mm}$
- $L_w = 1 \text{ nH/mm} = 10^{-9} \text{ H/mm}$
- $C_w = 0.3 \text{ pF/mm} = 0.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/mm}$
- $t_r = 100 \text{ ps} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s}$

**(i) Propagation Velocity:**
$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_w C_w}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-9} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-22}}} = \frac{1}{1.732 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$v \approx 5.77 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm/s} = 5.77 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Flight Time:**
$$t_{flight} = \frac{\ell}{v} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{5.77 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm/s}} = 1.732 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s} \approx 173.2 \text{ ps}$$

**(iii) RLC Criterion:**
$$2.5 \times t_{flight} = 2.5 \times 173.2 = 433 \text{ ps}$$

Since $t_r = 100 \text{ ps} < 433 \text{ ps}$, the wire is **electrically long** and the **RLC model is required**.

**(iv) Characteristic Impedance:**
$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_w}{C_w}} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-9}}{0.3 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{3333.3} \approx 57.74 \text{ Ω}$$

> **Engineering action:** The clock driver must be terminated with a 58 Ω resistor (or buffer chain sized to match) to suppress reflections that would otherwise cause timing jitter and double-clocking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor** in Elmore delay → lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_{eff} = C_g + 2C_c$ (worst) and $C_g + 0$ (best)** → many students write "$C_{eff} = C_g + C_c$" which is **always wrong**; KTU expects you to know the Miller multiplier is 2 (opposite) or 0 (same).
> 3. **Quoting "inductance is negligible on-chip"** without checking the $t_r$ vs $t_{flight}$ condition → 0 marks for "when to use RLC" questions.
> 4. **Using $R_w = \rho \ell / A$ instead of $R_w = R_\square \cdot \ell / w$** when given $R_\square$ → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing RC ladders but not labeling the per-segment values** ($R_w/N$, $C_w/N$) → lose 1 mark in sketches.
> 6. **In crosstalk noise, forgetting to subtract the original $V_{DD}$** when the victim is at $V_{DD}$ → noise becomes wrong by a factor of $V_{DD}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam)**

- [ ] **Interconnect Delay:** Use **Elmore delay** $\tau_{pd} = 0.69 (R_d C_w + R_w C_w / 2 + (R_d + R_w) C_L)$. The 0.69 is from the 50% crossing of an exponential step response.
- [ ] **Distributed vs Lumped:** Lumped RC overestimates delay for long wires. Always check if the wire is "electrically long" ($t_r < 2.5 R_w C_w$).
- [ ] **Coupling Capacitance:** In modern nodes, $C_c > C_g$ because wires are tall and narrow. This is the *physical root* of all crosstalk.
- [ ] **Miller Effect:** Worst-case coupling doubles the effective capacitance ($C_{eff} = C_g + 2C_c$). Best-case is $C_{eff} = C_g$.
- [ ] **Crosstalk Noise:** $V_{noise} = (C_c / (C_v + C_c)) \cdot V_{DD}$. If $C_c = C_v$, the noise is 50% of $V_{DD}$ — a critical failure mode.
- [ ] **Inductive Criterion:** RLC is needed when $t_r < 2.5 t_{flight}$ OR $\ell > t_r / (2.5 \sqrt{L_w C_w})$. Always check this before assuming RC.
- [ ] **Characteristic Impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L_w / C_w}$. Terminate with $R_T = Z_0$ to kill reflections.
- [ ] **Energy in Wires:** $E_w = \frac{1}{2} C_w V_{DD}^2$. Bus-level interconnects can dominate total chip power.
- [ ] **Wire Classification:** Local (RC), Semi-global (coupling C), Global (RLC). Map this to the formula you choose.
- [ ] **Practical Fixes for Long Wires:** (i) Buffer insertion with optimal taper ($f = e \approx 2.718$); (ii) Shielding with grounded lines; (iii) Increased spacing; (iv) Twisted or interleaved routing.
- [ ] **Sheet Resistance:** $R_w = R_\square \cdot (\ell / w)$. Use this form whenever $R_\square$ is given.
- [ ] **KTU Traps:** Never write $C_{eff} = C_g + C_c$ — that is the **average** case, never asked. KTU asks only for **worst** (×2) or **best** (×0).
- [ ] **Single Most Important Equation:** $\tau_{pd} = 0.69 (R_d C_w + R_w C_w / 2 + (R_d + R_w) C_L)$ — appears in 80% of interconnect problems.
- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** Identify wire class → Choose model (RC or RLC) → Pick the right formula → Carry units → Box the final answer.

**Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE examination!** Aim for a clean, well-labeled sketch and a fully-numbered derivation. Examiners reward *clarity* and *method* as much as the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effective Resistance and Elmore Delay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT501 - DIGITAL CMOS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effective Resistance and Elmore Delay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Effective Resistance ($R_{\text{eff}}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> The **Effective Resistance** of a CMOS logic gate is defined as the ratio of the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ to the average short-circuit current that flows from the supply to ground when the input is switched (or equivalently, the ratio of $V_{DD}$ to the average discharging/charging current delivered to the output load capacitance).

Mathematically, the effective resistance is given by:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} i_{DS}(t)\,dt} = \frac{V_{DD}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This resistance is a **linearized, single-number representation** of a highly nonlinear, time-varying MOS transistor. It lets engineers treat the transistor as a simple resistor in hand analysis.

**Why we need it:** A MOSFET is a voltage-controlled current source whose current $i_{DS}(t)$ changes with $V_{GS}(t)$ and $V_{DS}(t)$ throughout the transient. To estimate the gate delay $t_p \approx 0.69 R_{\text{eff}} C_L$ (a first-order RC model), we must collapse this nonlinear behavior into one equivalent resistance.

## 1.2 Elmore Delay ($\tau_D$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Elmore Delay** is a first-order approximation of the 50% propagation delay of an **RC tree network**. For a tree with $N$ nodes, it is computed as the sum of products of every capacitance in the tree and the **resistance on the unique path from the input source to that capacitance**.

$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i\,i}\,C_{i}$$

where $R_{i\,i}$ is the **shared path resistance** (or "resistance seen by capacitor $C_i$ looking back toward the source").

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Building with Staircases**
> Imagine a multi-floor building where water (signal) flows down from the roof tank (input) to each floor. The pipes (resistors) restrict water flow, and every floor has a holding tank (capacitor). Water takes time to fill each floor's tank.
> - The **flow restriction at each floor** depends on **all the pipes above it** (shared path).
> - The **time to fill the ground floor** depends on every pipe + every tank in the chain.
> Elmore delay says: "Total fill time ≈ sum over all tanks of (size of that tank × sum of all pipes above it)." It is **not** exact, but it is the **best closed-form estimate** for an RC tree.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Highlighted)

- **$V_{DD}$:** Nominal supply voltage, typically **1.8 V** in 180 nm CMOS (KTU syllabus baseline).
- **$C_L$:** Total load capacitance at the output node, measured in **femtofarads (fF)**.
- **$t_p$:** Propagation delay, measured in **picoseconds (ps)** for modern nodes.
- **$\gamma$:** Body-effect coefficient (process parameter, dimensionless, typically **0.4 – 0.6**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Effective Resistance

The current through a saturated/linear NMOS transistor during the output fall transition is:

$$i_{DS}(t) = \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)\left[(V_{GS} - V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1:** Apply a ramp input from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ at the gate. The output is initially charged to $V_{DD}$, so $V_{DS}$ (output voltage) is at $V_{DD}$ at $t=0$.
- **Step 2:** As the input rises, the transistor enters **saturation** first, then moves to the **linear/triode region** as the output falls.
- **Step 3:** Compute the average current $\langle i_{DS} \rangle$ integrated over the entire fall time $t_{\text{fall}}$.
- **Step 4:** Define $R_{\text{eff}} \triangleq V_{DD}/\langle i_{DS} \rangle$, which yields a **single lumped resistor** that predicts correct *average* charging/discharging time.

### Two Common Definitions

- **Input-independent (or "natural") $R_{\text{eff}}$:** Used for static CMOS; current is driven by $V_{DD}$ at the output.
$$R_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$
- **Output-dependent $R_{\text{eff}}$:** Used in pass-transistor and pseudo-NMOS logic, where the output voltage itself affects $V_{DS}$.

## 2.2 Elmore Delay — The Logic Behind the Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a "first moment" approximation?**
> The exact 50% delay of an RC tree requires solving a transcendental equation (closed-form only for $N \le 2$). Elmore (1948) observed that for a **monotonically rising step response** (no overshoot), the dominant-pole approximation is excellent. The delay equals the **first moment of the impulse response** — the centroid of the step response's probability density.

### Derivation of the Path-Resistance Concept

For node $i$ in an RC tree, the resistance that contributes to its discharge is **the sum of all resistances on the path from node $i$ back to the source**. Denote this as $R_{i\,i}$ (resistance from source → node $i$):

$$R_{i\,i} = \sum_{k \in \text{path}(src \to i)} R_k$$

Then Elmore's first-moment delay is:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \cdot R_{i\,i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **exact to first order**, has **at most 1.5% error** for balanced RC ladders, and is **monotone**: increasing any $C_i$ or $R_k$ *always* increases $\tau_D$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_{\text{eff}} = V_{DD}/\langle I \rangle$ | Effective transistor resistance | kΩ range | Delay estimation |
| $t_p = 0.69 R_{\text{eff}} C_L$ | First-order gate delay (no parasitics) | ps | Quick hand analysis |
| $t_p = 0.69(R_{\text{eff}} C_{\text{par}} + (R_{\text{eff}}+R_{\text{wire}})C_L)$ | Delay with internal + wire resistance | ps | Buffered interconnects |
| $\tau_D = \sum_i C_i R_{i\,i}$ | Elmore delay of an RC tree | ps | Multi-node interconnects |
| $R_{i\,i} = \sum_{k \in \text{path}} R_k$ | Path resistance seen by $C_i$ | Ω | Required for Elmore |
| $\tau_{\text{lumped}} = R C$ | Single-stage RC delay | ps | Driver + load only |
| $\tau_{\text{ladder},n} \approx n^2 RC/2$ | Uniform $n$-stage RC ladder Elmore delay | ps | Long interconnect |
| $I_{D0} = \mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{DD}-V_t)V_{DSAT}$ | Velocity-saturated saturation current | A | Deep submicron current |
| $R_{\text{eff}} = V_{DSAT}/(I_{D0}/2)$ | Velocity-saturated effective resistance | Ω | Nanometer regimes |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these tools are used in industry:**
> - **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** tools like Synopsys PrimeTime compute Elmore delays as a fast pre-pass before full SPICE.
> - **Place-and-Route engines** use $R_{\text{eff}}$ look-up tables per cell to size buffers on long wires.
> - **High-speed links** (DDR, SerDes) require accurate Elmore modeling of multi-tap pre-emphasis drivers.
> - **Power-grid IR-drop** analysis uses the same tree mathematics to estimate worst-case voltage droop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Resistance of a Saturated NMOS (Velocity-Saturated)

Consider an NMOS transistor in **velocity saturation**, with gate driven by a step from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ and output initially at $V_{DD}$ (high).

**Step 1 — Write the saturation current (velocity-saturated model):**

$$I_{D,\text{sat}} = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{GS} - V_t) V_{DSAT}$$

During the entire transient, $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ (after the step), so:

$$I_{D,\text{sat}} = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{DD} - V_t) V_{DSAT}$$

**Step 2 — As the output discharges, the transistor enters the linear region:**

The linear-region current with $V_{DS} = V_{\text{out}}(t)$ becomes:

$$I_{D,\text{lin}}(t) = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) \left[(V_{DD} - V_t)V_{\text{out}}(t) - \frac{V_{\text{out}}^{2}(t)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Average current during a full discharge from $V_{DD}$ to $0$:**

Average over $V_{\text{out}}$ from $V_{DD}$ down to $0$ (uniform weight — by substitution $dV_{\text{out}}/dt$):

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \frac{1}{V_{DD}} \int_0^{V_{DD}} \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)\left[(V_{DD} - V_t)V - \frac{V^2}{2}\right] dV$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \frac{\mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox}(W/L)}{V_{DD}}\left[\frac{(V_{DD}-V_t)V^2}{2} - \frac{V^3}{6}\right]_{0}^{V_{DD}}$$

Substitute the limits:

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \frac{\mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox}(W/L)}{V_{DD}}\left[\frac{(V_{DD}-V_t)V_{DD}^{2}}{2} - \frac{V_{DD}^{3}}{6}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $V_{DD}$:**

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right) V_{DD}\left[\frac{V_{DD}-V_t}{2} - \frac{V_{DD}}{6}\right]$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$\frac{V_{DD}-V_t}{2} - \frac{V_{DD}}{6} = \frac{3(V_{DD}-V_t) - V_{DD}}{6} = \frac{2V_{DD} - 3V_t}{6} = \frac{V_{DD}}{3} - \frac{V_t}{2}$$

So:

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right) V_{DD}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{3} - \frac{V_t}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the definition of $R_{\text{eff}}$:**

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{\langle I_D \rangle} = \frac{1}{\mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)\left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{3} - \dfrac{V_t}{2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplification in velocity-saturation regime:** If $V_{DSAT} \ll V_{DD}-V_t$ (deep-submicron), the average current is approximated as $I_{D0}/2$, where $I_{D0}$ is the saturation current with $V_{DS} = V_{DSAT}$. This gives the clean formula $R_{\text{eff}} = V_{DSAT}/(I_{D0}/2) = 2 V_{DSAT}/I_{D0}$ used in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Elmore Delay of a 3-Stage RC Ladder

Consider a **uniform RC ladder** with input at node 1, capacitors $C$ at nodes 1, 2, 3, and series resistors $R$ between adjacent nodes, with a ground capacitor $C$ at node 3 (output).

**Network topology:**
- $R_1$ between source and node 1
- $R_2$ between node 1 and node 2
- $R_3$ between node 2 and node 3
- $C_1, C_2, C_3$ at nodes 1, 2, 3 (all equal to $C$)

**Step 1 — Compute path resistances $R_{i\,i}$:**

- $R_{1\,1} = R_1$ (only $R_1$ lies between source and node 1)
- $R_{2\,2} = R_1 + R_2$ (both lie on the path source → node 2)
- $R_{3\,3} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ (all three lie on the path source → node 3)

**Step 2 — Apply the Elmore formula:**

$$\tau_D = C_1 R_{1\,1} + C_2 R_{2\,2} + C_3 R_{3\,3}$$

$$\tau_D = C R_1 + C (R_1 + R_2) + C (R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$$

**Step 3 — Group like terms:**

$$\tau_D = 3 C R_1 + 2 C R_2 + 1 \cdot C R_3$$

**Step 4 — For a uniform ladder ($R_k = R$ for all $k$):**

$$\tau_D = C R (3 + 2 + 1) = 6 C R$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $n$ uniform stages:**

The coefficients form the sequence $n, (n-1), \ldots, 1$, whose sum is $n(n+1)/2$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{D,\text{uniform}} = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R C \approx \frac{n^{2} R C}{2} \text{ for large } n}$$

> [!TIP]
> This **quadratic growth** in $n$ is the fundamental reason long interconnects must be **buffered** — inserting inverters breaks a long RC chain into shorter, faster pieces.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a 3-section distributed RC interconnect. Each section has $R = 100\,\Omega$ and $C = 50\,\text{fF}$. The driving NMOS has $R_{\text{eff}} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$. Compute the total high-to-low propagation delay using the Elmore model.

**Step 1 — Driver (lumped) RC delay for input capacitance of line:**

The line's input capacitance is $C_1 = 50\,\text{fF}$. The driver sees:

$$\tau_{\text{driver}} = R_{\text{eff}} \cdot C_1 = 1000 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 50\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 2 — Elmore delay of the 3-section line from its input:**

From the formula derived in §3.2 with $n=3$, $R=C=R$:

$$\tau_{\text{line}} = 6 R C = 6 \times 100 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 30\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 3 — Total Elmore delay (driver + line, assuming line input capacitance is included once):**

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{eff}} C_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{3} C_i R_{i\,i}$$

Recompute carefully including the driver:

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{eff}}(C_1+C_2+C_3) + C_2(R_{\text{eff}}+R_1) + C_3(R_{\text{eff}}+R_1+R_2)$$

Substituting values:

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = 1000(150\,\text{fF}) + 50\,\text{fF}(1100) + 50\,\text{fF}(1200)$$

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = 150\,\text{ps} + 55\,\text{ps} + 60\,\text{ps} = 265\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to 50% propagation delay:**

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times \tau_{\text{total}} = 0.69 \times 265 \approx 183\,\text{ps}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Effective Resistance Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Voltage Step VDD] --> B[Gate of NMOS]
    B --> C[Transistor Current IDS of t]
    C --> D[Average Current Integrator]
    D --> E[Compute Iavg over Discharge Time]
    E --> F[Apply Reff = VDD divided by Iavg]
    F --> G[Single Lumped Resistor Model]
    G --> H[Use in Delay Equation tp = 0.69 Reff CL]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of an RC Tree Used in Elmore Delay

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Voltage Source Vin] --> R1[Resistor R1]
    R1 --> N1[Node 1 with Capacitor C1]
    R1 --> R2[Resistor R2]
    R2 --> N2[Node 2 with Capacitor C2]
    R2 --> R3[Resistor R3]
    R3 --> N3[Node 3 Output Capacitor C3]
    N1 --> G1[Ground]
    N2 --> G2[Ground]
    N3 --> G3[Ground]
```

## 4.3 CMOS Inverter with Effective Resistance Annotation

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Vin] --> MN[GATE NMOS]
    IN --> MP[GATE PMOS]
    VDD[Supply VDD] --> MP
    MP --> OUT[Output Node Vout]
    MN --> OUT
    OUT --> CL[Load Capacitor CL]
    CL --> GND[Ground]
    MN -. Effective Resistance Reff .- CALC[Average Current Calculator]
    CALC --> DELAY[Propagation Delay tp]
    CL --> DELAY
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Long Interconnect Buffer Insertion Strategy

```mermaid
graph LR
    GATE[Logic Gate Driver] --> BUF1[Inverter Buffer 1]
    BUF1 --> W1[Wire Section 1 RC Ladder]
    W1 --> BUF2[Inverter Buffer 2]
    BUF2 --> W2[Wire Section 2 RC Ladder]
    W2 --> BUF3[Inverter Buffer 3]
    BUF3 --> W3[Wire Section 3 RC Ladder]
    W3 --> LOAD[Final Load Capacitance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural insight:** The Elmore delay of a long wire grows as $n^2$ in the number of segments $n$. By inserting $k$ buffers, the wire is broken into $k+1$ pieces, each of length $n/(k+1)$, and the total delay scales as $n^2/k$ — a **linear speedup** at the cost of area and power.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define effective resistance of a MOS transistor and state why it is introduced in CMOS delay analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Effective resistance $R_{\text{eff}}$ of a MOS transistor is defined as the ratio of the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ to the average discharging (or charging) current delivered by the transistor to the load capacitance during a full output transition.

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} i_{DS}(t)\,dt}$$

**Why introduced (2 marks):**
1. A MOS transistor is a nonlinear, voltage-dependent current source whose current varies during the transient.
2. Hand calculation of delay requires a **simple linear RC** model: $t_p = 0.69 R C_L$.
3. $R_{\text{eff}}$ collapses the complex nonlinear behavior into a single equivalent resistance that reproduces the **average** switching current, enabling quick first-order delay estimation.
4. It also allows the use of standard **timing analyzer libraries** where each cell has a single $R_{\text{eff}}$ value.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Elmore delay? Mention one key property that makes it useful for digital design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Elmore delay is a first-order approximation of the 50% propagation delay of an RC tree network. It is computed as the weighted sum of every node capacitance, with the weight being the path resistance from the source to that node:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i R_{i\,i}$$

**Key property (1 mark):** Elmore delay is **monotone** — increasing any $C_i$ or $R_k$ *always* increases $\tau_D$. This guarantees that optimization heuristics (e.g., wire sizing, buffer insertion) will not produce surprising degradations, which is critical for static timing analysis (STA) tools.

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the effective resistance of a velocity-saturated NMOS transistor discharging a load capacitance. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a uniform 4-stage RC ladder interconnect driven by a CMOS inverter, compute the Elmore delay. Each segment has $R = 200\,\Omega$, $C = 100\,\text{fF}$, and the driver $R_{\text{eff}} = 800\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
- Step input applied at the gate, switching instantly from $0$ to $V_{DD}$.
- Output node initially charged to $V_{DD}$.
- Velocity-saturation regime: $V_{DSAT} \ll V_{DD} - V_t$.
- Body effect neglected ($V_{SB} = 0$).
- Channel-length modulation ignored ($\lambda = 0$).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The saturation current in velocity saturation:

$$I_{D0} = \mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{DD} - V_t) V_{DSAT}$$

In velocity saturation, the drain current remains approximately constant at $I_{D0}$ as long as $V_{DS} > V_{DSAT}$. The average discharge current over a full swing is:

$$\langle I_D \rangle = \frac{I_{D0}}{2}$$

(half of the peak, because the current starts at $I_{D0}$ when $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$ and tapers as the transistor enters triode, with the mean being $I_{D0}/2$).

Substituting into the effective resistance definition:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{\langle I_D \rangle} = \frac{2 V_{DD}}{I_{D0}} = \frac{2 V_{DD}}{\mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{DD} - V_t) V_{DSAT}}$$

Equivalently, using $I_{D0} = I_{D,\text{sat}}$ with $V_{DSAT}$ replaced:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2 V_{DSAT}}{\mu_{\text{eff}} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{DD} - V_t) V_{DSAT}} \cdot V_{DD} = \frac{2 V_{DD}}{I_{D0}}}$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Elmore delay of 4-stage ladder with $C_1=C_2=C_3=C_4=C$, $R_1=R_2=R_3=R_4=R$:**

From the derived formula in §3.2:

$$\tau_{\text{ladder}} = C R \sum_{k=1}^{4} k = C R \cdot 10 = 10 R C$$

Substituting values:

$$\tau_{\text{ladder}} = 10 \times 200 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} = 2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s} = 200\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 2 — Add the driver contribution.** The driver must charge the **total line capacitance** $4C = 400\,\text{fF}$:

$$\tau_{\text{driver}} = R_{\text{eff}} \cdot (4C) = 800 \times 400 \times 10^{-15} = 320\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 3 — Cross terms (capacitors downstream of $C_1$ also see the driver resistance):**

The total Elmore delay for the driver + line combination:

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{eff}}(C_1+C_2+C_3+C_4) + C_2 R_1 + C_3(R_1+R_2) + C_4(R_1+R_2+R_3)$$

$$= 800(400\,\text{fF}) + 100\,\text{fF}(200) + 100\,\text{fF}(400) + 100\,\text{fF}(600)$$

$$= 320\,\text{ps} + 20\,\text{ps} + 40\,\text{ps} + 60\,\text{ps} = 440\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 4 — 50% propagation delay:**

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times 440\,\text{ps} \approx 304\,\text{ps}$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of Elmore delay for an RC tree. Show that for a uniform $n$-stage RC ladder, the Elmore delay is $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}RC$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-input NAND gate drives a 50 fF load through 200 µm of polysilicon interconnect. The interconnect is modeled as a 5-section RC ladder, each with $R = 50\,\Omega$, $C = 10\,\text{fF}$. The NAND's pull-down network has an effective resistance of $600\,\Omega$. Calculate the high-to-low propagation delay using Elmore delay. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**RC Tree sketch (3 marks):** See SECTION 4 §4.2 for the canonical diagram.

**Elmore delay statement (2 marks):** The Elmore delay of an RC tree is defined as the first moment of its impulse response, computed as:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i R_{i\,i}$$

where $R_{i\,i}$ is the sum of resistances on the unique path from the input source to node $i$.

**Derivation for $n$-stage uniform ladder (2 marks):**

In a uniform ladder, every series resistor = $R$, every node capacitor = $C$, and the path resistance to node $k$ is $R_{k\,k} = kR$ (since $k$ resistors lie between source and node $k$).

Summing over $k = 1$ to $n$:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C \cdot kR = RC \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = RC \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_D = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}RC}$$

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Elmore delay of the 5-section ladder alone:**

Using the uniform-ladder formula with $n=5$:

$$\tau_{\text{ladder}} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} R C = 15 R C = 15 \times 50 \times 10 \times 10^{-15} = 7.5\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 2 — Driver contribution to charge the line's total capacitance:**

Total line capacitance $= 5C = 50\,\text{fF}$:

$$\tau_{\text{driver-cap}} = R_{\text{eff}} \times 5C = 600 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 30\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 3 — Add the final load capacitance seen through the entire line:**

The 50 fF load at the far end of the ladder sees the full path: $R_{\text{eff}} + 5R$:

$$\tau_{\text{load}} = 50\,\text{fF} \times (600 + 5 \times 50) = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 850 = 42.5\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 4 — Full Elmore sum (driver + 5 line caps + final load):**

Using $C_0$ for the load, $C_k = C$ for $k=1..5$:

$$\tau_D = R_{\text{eff}}(5C + C_0) + \sum_{k=2}^{5} C \cdot [(k-1)R] + C_0 \cdot (5R)$$

Capacitors $C_1$ to $C_5$ each see $R_{\text{eff}}$; the load sees $R_{\text{eff}} + 5R$:

$$\tau_D = 600 \times (50\,\text{fF} + 50\,\text{fF}) + 50\,\text{fF} \times 250 = 600 \times 100\,\text{fF} + 12.5\,\text{ps}$$

$$\tau_D = 60\,\text{ps} + 12.5\,\text{ps} = 72.5\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 5 — 50% propagation delay:**

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times 72.5\,\text{ps} \approx 50\,\text{ps}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the cross terms:** Many students compute only $R_{\text{eff}} C_{\text{total}}$ and ignore the resistance path contributions for downstream capacitors. **Full Elmore sum is mandatory.**
> 2. **Mixing lumped and distributed:** Do NOT multiply $R_{\text{eff}}$ by every capacitor. The driver resistor contributes once to the first capacitor and once to the *path* of every downstream capacitor.
> 3. **Unit errors:** $C$ must always be in **farads (F)** for SI consistency, not fF. Convert at the start: $50\,\text{fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$.
> 4. **Elmore ≠ exact delay:** The 50% delay is **0.69 × Elmore**, not the Elmore value itself. Marks are lost when this factor is omitted.
> 5. **For velocity-saturated $R_{\text{eff}}$, the average current is $I_{D0}/2$**, not $I_{D0}$. Wrong factor of 2 → wrong answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Effective Resistance & Elmore Delay)**
> - **$R_{\text{eff}}$** is a single linearized resistor equivalent to a nonlinear transistor for **average-current** delay calculation.
> - **Velocity-saturated $R_{\text{eff}}$** formula: $R_{\text{eff}} = 2V_{DD}/I_{D0}$ where $I_{D0} = \mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{DD}-V_t)V_{DSAT}$.
> - **Long-channel $R_{\text{eff}}$** formula: $R_{\text{eff}} = 1/[\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{DD}/3 - V_t/2)]$.
> - **Elmore delay** is the **first-moment (centroid)** approximation of an RC tree's step response.
> - **Elmore formula:** $\tau_D = \sum_i C_i R_{i\,i}$, with $R_{i\,i}$ = sum of resistances on the path from source to node $i$.
> - **Uniform $n$-stage ladder:** $\tau_D = n(n+1)RC/2 \approx n^2 RC/2$ for large $n$ — **quadratic** with length.
> - **50% propagation delay** is $0.69 \times$ Elmore delay (first-order RC step response).
> - **Buffering** breaks a long wire into $k+1$ shorter ladders, reducing delay from $n^2RC$ to $n^2RC/k$ — **linear speedup**.
> - **Monotonicity** of Elmore is a key property exploited by STA tools.
> - **Common KTU mistakes:** missing cross terms, wrong factor of 2 in $R_{\text{eff}}$, forgetting the 0.69 factor, mixing units.
> - **Industry use:** Static timing analysis, wire sizing, buffer insertion, IR-drop analysis, pre-emphasis design in high-speed serial links.
